topics.title: Fluids and Liquids, Continuum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fluids and Liquids, Continuum ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Fluid? (KTU 2024 Definition) A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** when subjected to an applied **shear stress** or tangential force, no matter how infinitesimally small that stress may be. Unlike a solid which resists shear by deforming to a finite, fixed strain, a fluid keeps moving as long as the shear stress is present. Mathematically, a fluid obeys the following constitutive inequality for an applied shear stress $\tau$: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity and $\frac{du}{dy}$ is the velocity gradient perpendicular to the motion. This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When a KTU question asks "Define a fluid," always mention the *continuous deformation under shear stress* part. Many students write only "fluids flow" and lose marks because they fail to address the **shear-stress-dependent** nature of the deformation. ### 1.2 Fluids vs. Liquids vs. Gases — Sub-classification | Phase | Compressibility | Free Surface | Definite Volume | Definite Shape | |-------|-----------------|--------------|-----------------|----------------| | **Liquid** | Very low (nearly incompressible) | Yes | Yes | Takes the shape of the lower container portion | | **Gas** | High (highly compressible) | No | No | Takes the entire shape and volume of the container | | **Solid** | Negligible | — | Yes | Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every liquid is a fluid, but every fluid is NOT a liquid.** Gases, plasmas, and even some non-Newtonian pastes are fluids. For naval architecture, the working fluids of interest are **seawater** (a liquid) and **air** (a gas) — both must be treated using continuum fluid mechanics. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Honey on a Spoon Imagine you spread a spoonful of honey on a tilted plate: - The honey (a **fluid**) starts creeping down the plate and never stops until it pools at the bottom. - A metal spoon placed on the same plate (a **solid**) might tilt a tiny bit but will stay in that fixed deformed shape. - The honey, even though it's very viscous (slow-moving), still flows — and that is the **defining signature of a fluid**. For naval architects, think of water rushing past a ship's hull: the water molecules slide past one another continuously, transmitting shear from one layer to the next — that continuous "sliding" is what we mean by **fluid flow**. ### 1.4 The Continuum Hypothesis In reality, every fluid is composed of **discrete molecules** separated by vacuum. However, in fluid mechanics (and especially in naval hydrodynamics), we treat the fluid as if it were a **continuous, gap-free distribution of matter**. This is the **continuum hypothesis**. A **fluid particle** is therefore defined as an infinitesimal element that is: 1. **Large enough** to contain a statistically enormous number of molecules (so average macroscopic properties like density, velocity, pressure are meaningful). 2. **Small enough** (compared to the length scale of the flow, e.g., ship length) that macroscopic properties can be treated as point values. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants for Naval Applications:** > - Density of seawater (standard): $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Density of fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - Atmospheric pressure (ISA): $p_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do We Need the Continuum Assumption? At the molecular level, fluid properties (density, velocity, pressure) fluctuate wildly from one point to another over distances of the order of the **mean free path** $\lambda$ of the molecules. We cannot solve the Navier–Stokes equations at every molecule. By assuming **continuum**, we replace discrete molecular behavior with **smooth, differentiable field variables** that can be described using calculus: $$\rho = \rho(x, y, z, t), \quad \vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t), \quad p = p(x, y, z, t)$$ This allows us to use **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** like the continuity equation, Euler's equation, and Navier–Stokes equations. ### 2.2 Knudsen Number — The Validity Test for Continuum The **Knudsen number** is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies whether the continuum hypothesis is valid for a given flow: $$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$ where: - $\lambda$ = mean free path of fluid molecules (m) - $L$ = characteristic length scale of the flow (m) | Knudsen Number Regime | Continuum Valid? | Physical Scenario | |------------------------|------------------|-------------------| | $Kn < 0.001$ | ✅ Yes — Continuum assumption fully valid | Ship hydrodynamics, pipelines, atmosphere | | $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$ | ⚠️ Slip-flow regime — partial validity | High-altitude aerodynamics, MEMS | | $0.1 < Kn < 10$ | ❌ No — Transition / free-molecular regime | Upper atmosphere re-entry | | $Kn > 10$ | ❌ No — Free molecular flow | Rarefied gas dynamics in space | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Naval Architecture Verdict:** For ship hydrodynamics, the characteristic length $L$ is the **ship's length** (typically 50–400 m), and for water/air at standard conditions, $\lambda \sim 10^{-10}$ m for water and $\sim 10^{-7}$ m for air. Therefore $Kn \sim 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-9}$ — **continuum is overwhelmingly valid.** This justifies treating seawater as a smooth continuous medium in ship resistance and propeller theory. ### 2.3 Defining Density at a Point (Continuum Limit) Within the continuum framework, we can rigorously define the **density at a point** $P$ as the limit of mass-to-volume ratio as the control volume shrinks to zero, but **stays above the molecular scale**: $$\rho(P) = \lim_{\Delta V \to \Delta V^*} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}$$ where $\Delta V^*$ is the smallest volume that still contains a statistically meaningful number of molecules (typically $> 10^3$ molecules). ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------|-------| | 1 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\rho$, $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Mass per unit volume | | 2 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $v$, $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ | Volume per unit mass | | 3 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho \, g$ | $\gamma$, $\text{N/m}^3$ | Weight per unit volume | | 4 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless | For liquids only | | 5 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | Dimensionless | Tests continuum validity | | 6 | Newton's Viscosity Law | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$, $\text{Pa}$ | Defines Newtonian fluid | | 7 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}$ | Resistance to deformation | | 8 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\nu$, $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Used in Reynolds number | | 9 | Pressure (Gauge / Absolute) | $p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | KTU frequently tests this | | 10 | Bulk Modulus (Compressibility) | $K = -V \dfrac{dp}{dV}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Measures fluid stiffness | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ship Resistance:** When modeling the viscous drag on a hull, the continuum assumption lets us apply the **Navier–Stokes equations** at every point in the flow field. Without it, naval architects could not use CFD software (OpenFOAM, Fluent, Star-CCM+) which fundamentally relies on continuum discretization. - **Cavitation Analysis:** Pressure fields $p(x,y,z,t)$ — defined via continuum — drop below vapor pressure at the propeller tip, leading to cavity formation. This analysis is meaningless at the molecular scale. - **Wave-Structure Interaction:** Free-surface flows around a ship are modeled using continuum velocity potentials $\phi(x,y,z,t)$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Density at a Point from the Continuum Limit **Goal:** Show rigorously that $\rho(P)$ can be defined as a smooth, differentiable scalar field under the continuum hypothesis. **Starting Premise:** Consider a small control volume $\Delta V$ surrounding point $P$, enclosing mass $\Delta m$. $$ \Delta V_{i} \rightarrow \Delta V_{i+1} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \Delta V^{*} $$ As $\Delta V$ shrinks toward $\Delta V^*$ (the smallest statistically meaningful volume), the ratio $\Delta m / \Delta V$ approaches a limiting value: $$ \rho_{i} = \frac{\Delta m_{i}}{\Delta V_{i}}, \quad \rho_{i+1} = \frac{\Delta m_{i+1}}{\Delta V_{i+1}}, \quad \dots $$ The continuum density at point $P$ is defined as: $$ \rho(P) = \lim_{\Delta V \to \Delta V^{*}} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V} $$ **Interpretation Step-by-Step:** 1. Below $\Delta V^*$, the ratio $\Delta m / \Delta V$ fluctuates wildly because we are sampling only a handful of molecules — statistically meaningless. 2. Above $\Delta V^*$, the ratio stabilizes to a single value — the **continuum density**. 3. Since $\Delta V^*$ is microscopically tiny compared to ship-scale geometry ($\sim 10^{-9}$ m vs $\sim 100$ m), we can effectively treat $\rho$ as a point value for engineering purposes. **Result:** Density $\rho(x, y, z, t)$ is a **smooth, differentiable scalar field**, justifying the use of calculus in fluid mechanics. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Knudsen Number from Kinetic Theory **Goal:** Establish an analytical expression for the mean free path $\lambda$ of water and air molecules. From the kinetic theory of gases (also applied approximately to liquids): $$ \lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n \, \pi \, d^{2}} $$ where: - $n$ = number density of molecules (molecules / $\text{m}^3$) - $d$ = effective molecular diameter (m) For air at standard temperature and pressure (STP): $$ n \approx 2.7 \times 10^{25} \text{ molecules/m}^3, \quad d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} $$ Substituting: $$ \lambda_{air} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \times (2.7 \times 10^{25}) \times \pi \times (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^2} $$ $$ \lambda_{air} = \frac{1}{1.414 \times 2.7 \times 10^{25} \times \pi \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19}} $$ $$ \lambda_{air} = \frac{1}{1.414 \times 2.7 \times 3.1416 \times 1.369 \times 10^{6}} $$ $$ \lambda_{air} \approx \frac{1}{16.43 \times 10^{6}} \approx 6.08 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} \approx 60 \text{ nm} $$ For a ship of characteristic length $L = 100$ m: $$ Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} = \frac{6.08 \times 10^{-8}}{100} = 6.08 \times 10^{-10} $$ Since $Kn \ll 0.001$, the **continuum hypothesis is overwhelmingly valid** for ship hydrodynamics. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Continuum Validity for Common Naval Scenarios | Scenario | $\lambda$ (m) | $L$ (m) | $Kn$ | Continuum Valid? | |----------|---------------|---------|------|------------------| | Ship in seawater | $10^{-10}$ | 100 | $10^{-12}$ | ✅ Yes (strongly) | | Submarine in seawater | $10^{-10}$ | 50 | $2 \times 10^{-12}$ | ✅ Yes (strongly) | | Aircraft at sea level | $6 \times 10^{-8}$ | 30 | $2 \times 10^{-9}$ | ✅ Yes (strongly) | | Re-entry vehicle at 80 km altitude | $10^{-4}$ | 5 | $2 \times 10^{-5}$ | ✅ Yes (valid) | | Satellite at 300 km altitude | $10^{-2}$ | 1 | $10^{-2}$ | ⚠️ Slip-flow regime | | Flow in nano-channel (MEMS) | $6 \times 10^{-8}$ | $10^{-7}$ | $0.6$ | ❌ No (free-molecular) | --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Specific Weight from Newton's Second Law **Goal:** Derive $\gamma = \rho g$ from first principles. Consider a fluid column of cross-section $A$ and height $h$. The mass of the column is: $$ m = \rho \, V = \rho \, A \, h $$ The weight (force due to gravity) of the column is: $$ W = m \, g = \rho \, A \, h \, g $$ By definition, specific weight is weight per unit volume: $$ \gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\rho \, A \, h \, g}{A \, h} = \rho \, g $$ For seawater with $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$: $$ \gamma_{sw} = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Continuum Hypothesis — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["REAL FLUID
Discrete molecules in motion"] --> B{"Is Continuum Assumption Valid?
Kn less than 0.001"} B -->|YES| C["CONTINUUM MODEL
Smooth field variables rho x y z t,
V x y z t, p x y z t"] B -->|NO| D["MOLECULAR MODEL
Boltzmann Equation,
DSMC Simulation"] C --> E["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS
Navier Stokes Equations,
CFD Solution, Ship Resistance"] D --> F["RAREFIED GAS DYNAMICS
Spacecraft Reentry,
Vacuum Technology"] C --> G["PROPERTIES DERIVABLE
Density, Velocity, Pressure,
Temperature at every point"] G --> H["FIELD EQUATIONS APPLICABLE
Continuity, Momentum,
Energy PDEs"] ``` ### 4.2 Fluid vs Solid Response to Shear Stress ```mermaid flowchart LR A["APPLIED SHEAR STRESS tau"] --> B{"SUBSTANCE TYPE"} B -->|SOLID| C["FINITE DEFORMATION
Reaches equilibrium strain
Hookes Law"] B -->|FLUID| D["CONTINUOUS DEFORMATION
Flows indefinitely
Newtons Law of Viscosity"] C --> E["Strain epsilon = tau by G"] D --> F["Strain Rate du by dy = tau by mu"] ``` ### 4.3 Knudsen Number Flow Regime Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Knudsen Number Kn"] --> B["Kn less than 0.001
CONTINUUM FLOW
Ship hydrodynamics
Navier Stokes valid"] A --> C["0.001 to 0.1
SLIP FLOW
High altitude aerodynamics
MEMS devices"] A --> D["0.1 to 10
TRANSITION FLOW
Upper atmosphere
Hypersonic rarefied gas"] A --> E["Kn greater than 10
FREE MOLECULAR FLOW
Spacecraft vacuum
DSMC required"] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture: From Continuum to Naval Hydrodynamic Solution ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["INPUT
Ship hull geometry,
Fluid domain,
Boundary conditions"] --> stepB["CONTINUUM ASSUMPTION
rho, V, p as
continuous fields"] stepB --> stepC["GOVERNING PDEs
Continuity,
Navier Stokes,
Energy equation"] stepC --> stepD["DISCRETIZATION
FVM / FEM
Mesh generation"] stepD --> stepE["NUMERICAL SOLVER
CFD code: OpenFOAM,
Fluent, Star CCM"] stepE --> stepF["OUTPUT
Pressure field,
Velocity field,
Total resistance"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define a fluid. How is it distinguished from a solid on the basis of its response to applied shear stress?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A fluid is a substance that **continuously deforms** (flows) under the action of an applied shear stress, no matter how small that stress is. - A **solid** resists shear by deforming to a **finite, fixed strain** (governed by Hooke's law: $\tau = G \gamma$) and then reaches static equilibrium. - A **fluid** continues to deform at a rate proportional to the applied shear stress (governed by Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$). The deformation never stops as long as the shear stress is maintained. Hence, the key distinguishing feature is that a fluid cannot sustain a **static shear stress** without continuous motion. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** *State the continuum hypothesis. Using the Knudsen number, explain under what conditions the continuum assumption breaks down.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **continuum hypothesis** assumes that fluid properties such as density, velocity, and pressure are **continuous, differentiable functions** of space and time, even though matter is actually composed of discrete molecules. The validity of the continuum hypothesis is tested using the **Knudsen number**: $$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$ where $\lambda$ is the mean free path and $L$ is the characteristic length. - For $Kn < 0.001$: continuum assumption is **valid** (most engineering flows including ship hydrodynamics). - For $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$: **slip-flow regime** — partial validity, continuum equations need correction. - For $Kn > 0.1$: continuum **breaks down** — molecular models (Boltzmann, DSMC) must be used. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the specific weight of a fluid and compute its value for seawater at standard conditions. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of the Knudsen number, justify why the continuum hypothesis is valid for ship hydrodynamics. Compute the Knudsen number for a 200 m long ship operating in air, given the mean free path of air at STP is $\lambda = 6.08 \times 10^{-8}$ m. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation of Specific Weight:** Consider a fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$. Mass of the fluid column: $$ m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h $$ Weight of the fluid column (force due to gravity): $$ W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g $$ By definition, specific weight $\gamma$ is the weight per unit volume: $$ \gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A \cdot h} = \rho \, g $$ Substituting the values for seawater: $$ \gamma_{sw} = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ **(b) Knudsen Number Justification:** The Knudsen number is defined as: $$ Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L} $$ For a 200 m ship in air: $$ Kn = \frac{6.08 \times 10^{-8}}{200} = 3.04 \times 10^{-10} $$ Since $Kn = 3.04 \times 10^{-10} \ll 0.001$, the continuum assumption is **overwhelmingly valid**. **Justification:** The ship length $L = 200$ m is approximately $3.3 \times 10^{9}$ times larger than the mean free path of air molecules. Therefore, even the smallest control volume used in CFD ($\sim 10^{-3}$ m) contains approximately $10^{16}$ molecules — far above the threshold needed for statistical averaging of macroscopic properties. This validates the use of the **Navier–Stokes equations** and all continuum-based CFD tools for ship resistance and seakeeping analysis. --- **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Differentiate between a liquid and a gas with respect to their compressibility, free surface, and shape. Why is seawater treated as an incompressible fluid in most naval architecture problems? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define the following: density, specific volume, specific gravity, and dynamic viscosity. State the SI units of each. A sample of seawater occupies $0.005 \text{ m}^3$ and has a mass of $5.125$ kg. Find its specific weight. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution: **(a) Liquid vs Gas — Tabular Comparison:** | Property | Liquid | Gas | |----------|--------|-----| | Compressibility | Very low (nearly incompressible) | High (highly compressible) | | Free Surface | Yes, possesses a definite free surface | No free surface — fills entire container | | Shape | Takes the shape of the lower portion of the container | Takes the entire shape of the container | | Density Variation | Incompressible in most engineering flows | Density varies significantly with pressure and temperature | Seawater is treated as **incompressible** because its **bulk modulus** is very high ($K \approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa). Even under the high pressures at the bottom of a ship's hull (say 10 m depth, $p \approx 100$ kPa gauge), the density change is less than 0.005%. For practical naval architecture problems (resistance, propulsion, seakeeping), this small change is negligible, and seawater is modeled as an **incompressible fluid** with constant $\rho$. **(b) Definitions and Units:** - **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume of a fluid. SI unit: $\text{kg/m}^3$. - **Specific Volume ($v$):** Volume per unit mass of a fluid, $v = 1/\rho$. SI unit: $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. - **Specific Gravity ($SG$):** The ratio of the density of the fluid to the density of pure water at $4^\circ$C. Dimensionless. - **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** A measure of the fluid's internal resistance to flow, defined by Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. SI unit: $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ or $\text{kg/(m} \cdot \text{s)}$. **Calculation of Specific Weight:** Given: - Volume $V = 0.005 \text{ m}^3$ - Mass $m = 5.125 \text{ kg}$ Density: $$ \rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{5.125}{0.005} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ Specific weight: $$ \gamma = \rho \cdot g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "fluid" with "liquid":** Always state that gases are also fluids. Marks are deducted if a definition implies liquids are the only fluids. > 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** A numerical answer without units (e.g., "10055.25" instead of "10055.25 N/m³") typically attracts a 0.5–1 mark deduction. > 3. **Misapplying the Knudsen number formula:** Some students write $Kn = L/\lambda$ instead of $\lambda/L$. Remember: it is the ratio of **molecular scale to macroscopic scale**. > 4. **Omitting "continuum" justification in CFD-related questions:** Any naval architecture question involving flow equations MUST mention why continuum is valid (cite $Kn < 0.001$). > 5. **Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ instead of $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ in KTU papers:** KTU examiners prefer $9.81$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Marks may be deducted if the standard value is not used. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fluids, Liquids, and Continuum** - ✅ A **fluid** continuously deforms under any applied shear stress — it cannot sustain static shear. - ✅ **Liquids** are nearly incompressible and possess a free surface; **gases** are highly compressible and fill their containers. - ✅ The **continuum hypothesis** treats fluid properties ($\rho$, $\vec{V}$, $p$) as smooth, differentiable scalar/vector fields. - ✅ A **fluid particle** must be microscopically large (contains enough molecules for statistical averaging) but macroscopically small (treated as a point). - ✅ **Knudsen number** $Kn = \lambda/L$ tests continuum validity — valid for $Kn < 0.001$. - ✅ Ship hydrodynamics comfortably satisfies continuum ($Kn \sim 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$). - ✅ Key formulas: $\rho = m/V$, $v = 1/\rho$, $\gamma = \rho g$, $SG = \rho/\rho_{water}$, $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$. - ✅ Seawater standard density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$; fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - ✅ Specific weight of seawater $\approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (using $g = 9.81$). - ✅ Bulk modulus of seawater $\approx 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa — justifies the **incompressible** assumption in naval hydrodynamics. - ✅ Always state SI units explicitly in numerical answers. - ✅ In KTU Part B questions, support every definition with at least one **formula** and one **engineering example** (preferably naval-related).

topics.title: Liquids and Their Properties: Density, Specific Weight, Specific Volume, Specific Gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquids and Their Properties: Density, Specific Weight, Specific Volume, Specific Gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to the foundational building block of **Fluid Mechanics for Naval Architects**. Before we analyze the resistance of a ship, the forces on a submarine, or the stability of a floating vessel, we must first quantify the *fluid* itself. This topic introduces the four primary static properties used to characterize any liquid: **Density ($\rho$)**, **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)**, **Specific Volume ($v$)**, and **Specific Gravity ($SG$)**.

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Density ($\rho$)**
> **Density** is defined as the mass of a fluid per unit volume. It is a scalar, intensive thermodynamic property representing how tightly matter is packed within a fluid element.
> $$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to \Delta V'} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}$$
> In SI Units, density is expressed in **kg/m³**. For naval architecture, the standard reference is **fresh water at 4 °C**, where $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and **seawater at 15 °C**, where $\rho_{seawater} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Specific Weight ($\gamma$)**
> **Specific Weight** (also called *weight density*) is the weight exerted by a fluid per unit volume. It directly links the mass property to the gravitational field acting on it.
> $$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$
> In SI Units, specific weight is expressed in **N/m³**. On Earth, $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Specific Volume ($v$)**
> **Specific Volume** is the volume occupied by a unit mass of fluid. It is the reciprocal of density and is particularly useful in thermodynamics and ship compartment calculations.
> $$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m}$$
> In SI Units, specific volume is expressed in **m³/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Specific Gravity ($SG$)**
> **Specific Gravity** (also called *relative density*) is a dimensionless ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a reference fluid, conventionally **pure water at 4 °C** for liquids.
> $$SG = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}}$$
> Because it is a ratio, $SG$ is a **pure number** with no units.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these properties matter to a Naval Architect**
> The *tonnage*, *displacement*, *draft*, *trim*, and *stability* of a vessel are ALL derived from the density of the liquid it floats in. A ship designed for fresh-water rivers will sit **deeper** in seawater because seawater is denser. Similarly, a submarine in the Arctic must account for colder, denser water for buoyancy calculations.

**Analogy 1 — Density (Packing Efficiency):**
Imagine two identical cardboard boxes, each one cubic meter. Fill one box with tightly packed steel balls, and the other with ping-pong balls. Both boxes have the *same volume*, but the box of steel balls has a *much higher mass* — therefore, it has a higher density. A cubic meter of liquid mercury is enormously heavier than a cubic meter of gasoline for the exact same reason.

**Analogy 2 — Specific Weight (Weight per Box):**
Now lift each box. The box of steel balls feels much heavier because gravity pulls more strongly on the additional mass. Specific weight tells you *how much that packed box weighs under Earth's gravity*. If you took the boxes to the Moon, the mass (density) would be unchanged, but the specific weight would drop to about **1/6** of its Earth value.

**Analogy 3 — Specific Volume (Space per Ping-Pong Ball):**
Reverse the perspective. Instead of asking "how much mass fits in this box?", ask "how much space does each kg of substance occupy?" Mercury atoms are heavy and tightly packed, so each kilogram takes very little space (low specific volume). Gasoline molecules are light and loosely arranged, so each kilogram occupies more space (high specific volume).

**Analogy 4 — Specific Gravity (Comparison to a Standard):**
Specific gravity is like comparing the "packing efficiency" of a substance to that of a familiar reference — pure water. Mercury has $SG = 13.6$, meaning it is **13.6 times** heavier than water for the same volume. Oils typically have $SG < 1$ and float on water.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic Behind the Four Properties

These four properties are not independent — they form an interconnected logical chain based on two fundamental physical quantities: **mass ($m$)** and **volume ($V$)**.

- **Step 1: Base Property — Density ($\rho$)**
  - The *most fundamental* intensive property of a fluid. It is independent of the gravitational field.
  - Why: Density arises from molecular structure and thermodynamic state (P, T), making it a "pure" material property.
  - How: It is the limiting ratio of mass to volume at a point, ensuring mathematical continuity in differential fluid analysis.

- **Step 2: Gravity-Coupled Property — Specific Weight ($\gamma$)**
  - The "Earth-bound" version of density.
  - Why: Engineering calculations (hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, weight) almost always deal with *forces* (Newtons), not mass.
  - How: Multiplies density by local gravitational acceleration $g$.

- **Step 3: Inverse Formulation — Specific Volume ($v$)**
  - The reciprocal view of density.
  - Why: In thermodynamics (e.g., steam cycles in naval propulsion), specific volume emerges naturally from the ideal gas law and is more convenient than density.
  - How: Algebraic inversion of the density relation.

- **Step 4: Normalized Comparison — Specific Gravity ($SG$)**
  - A dimensionless benchmarking tool.
  - Why: It removes the need to memorize the density of water every time a comparison is made.
  - How: Divides the fluid's density by the universally accepted reference density.

## 2.2 The Master KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive quick-reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations on this topic. Memorize the formulas, symbols, and units before attempting any numerical problem.

| **Property** | **Symbol** | **Defining Equation** | **SI Units** | **Key Notes for Naval Architects** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Fresh water @ 4°C → 1000; Seawater @ 15°C → 1025; Mercury → 13560 |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Depends on $g$; Earth $g = 9.81$ m/s²; On ship $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^3\text{/kg}$ | Used in thermodynamic & engine-room analysis |
| Specific Gravity | $SG$ | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}}$ | Dimensionless | For liquids, reference is **water at 4°C** (1000 kg/m³) |
| Density from SG | $\rho$ | $\rho = SG \cdot 1000$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Multiply $SG$ by water density to recover $\rho$ |
| Specific Weight from SG | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = SG \cdot 9810$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | Multiply $SG$ by water's specific weight |
| Specific Volume from SG | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{1}{SG \cdot 1000}$ | $\text{m}^3\text{/kg}$ | Inverse operation on the derived density |
| Inter-Conversion (γ ↔ ρ) | — | $\gamma = \rho g \;\; \Leftrightarrow \;\; \rho = \dfrac{\gamma}{g}$ | — | Most-tested relation in KTU problems |

## 2.3 Physical Significance in Naval Architecture

1. **Displacement Calculation:** A ship's *displacement* (in tonnes) is computed using the **density of the water it floats in** and the *submerged volume* of the hull (Archimedes' principle). A 10,000-tonne ship in fresh water will have a slightly different actual mass than the same ship in seawater.

2. **Load Line and Draft:** The **Plimsoll line** markings on a ship's hull use the specific gravity of the loading water to indicate safe loading depths. "TF" (Tropical Fresh), "F" (Fresh), "T" (Tropical), and "S" (Summer Sea) markings all stem from density variation.

3. **Bilge and Ballast Operations:** Ballast tanks are filled with seawater to alter the vessel's **effective specific weight distribution** for stability.

4. **Submarine Design:** Submarines operate in water columns where density changes with depth, temperature, and salinity. Density gradients affect **buoyancy**, **propulsion**, and **acoustic propagation**.

5. **Pipe and Pump Sizing:** Engine cooling systems, bilge pumps, and fire-fighting mains all require **specific weight** data to calculate hydrostatic head and required pump power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Density to Specific Weight

We begin with the definition of density as a point function in a continuous fluid medium.

**Starting Point:** The mass contained in an infinitesimal fluid element of volume $dV$ is $dm = \rho \, dV$.

**Step 1:** The weight of this infinitesimal element is obtained by applying Newton's second law in the gravitational field.
$$dW = dm \cdot g = (\rho \, dV) \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** By definition, specific weight is the weight per unit volume.
$$\gamma = \frac{dW}{dV}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for $dW$:
$$\gamma = \frac{\rho \, dV \cdot g}{dV}$$

**Step 4:** The $dV$ terms cancel (provided $dV \neq 0$).
$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \, g}$$

**Logical Conclusion:** Specific weight is not a new independent property — it is density *scaled* by the local gravitational acceleration. This is why on a celestial body with different $g$, the specific weight of seawater would be different, but the density would remain identical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Volume as the Reciprocal of Density

**Starting Point:** The defining relation for density:
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Step 1:** Solve the equation for the ratio $V/m$, which represents volume per unit mass.
$$\frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $V/m$ is, by definition, the **specific volume** ($v$).
$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{m}}$$

**Logical Conclusion:** Specific volume is the *inverse* of density. A small specific volume implies a dense fluid (e.g., mercury), while a large specific volume implies a light fluid (e.g., gasoline at $\approx 0.7 \text{ kg/L}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Gravity and Its Inversion

**Starting Point:** The defining relation for specific gravity:
$$SG = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{ref}}$$

where the reference density for liquids is pure water at 4 °C:
$$\rho_{ref} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 1:** Solve for $\rho_{fluid}$ in terms of $SG$.
$$\rho_{fluid} = SG \cdot \rho_{ref} = SG \cdot 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this into the specific weight formula.
$$\gamma_{fluid} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g = (SG \cdot 1000) \cdot 9.81$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{fluid} = SG \cdot 9810 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the specific volume formula.
$$v_{fluid} = \frac{1}{\rho_{fluid}} = \frac{1}{SG \cdot 1000} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{v_{fluid} = \frac{1}{1000 \cdot SG} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

**Logical Conclusion:** Once $SG$ is known, all three other properties can be retrieved *without memorizing* any new number. The specific gravity acts as a **scaling factor** relative to water.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **model KTU calculation pattern**. Practice this exact algebraic flow before the exam.

**Problem:** A naval fuel tank contains a petroleum distillate with a specific gravity of $0.85$. The volume of the tank is $V = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$. Calculate (a) density, (b) specific weight, (c) specific volume, and (d) the total mass of fuel in the tank. Assume Earth gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### (a) Density

Using the inverted $SG$ formula:
$$\rho = SG \cdot 1000 = 0.85 \cdot 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### (b) Specific Weight

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 850 \cdot 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3$$

### (c) Specific Volume

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{850} = 1.1765 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

### (d) Total Mass

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 850 \cdot 2.5 = 2125 \text{ kg}$$

**Cross-Verification using $SG$ directly:**
$$m = (SG \cdot 1000) \cdot V = 0.85 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2.5 = 2125 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark$$

The result is consistent. This cross-check technique is a hallmark of KTU high-scoring answers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Scenario** | **Property Most Relevant** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| A submarine is diving in the Arctic Ocean | $\rho$ (with $T$, $S$ dependence) | Seawater density varies with temperature and salinity |
| Designing a freshwater canal transport barge | $\gamma$ | Hydrostatic load on hull is weight-driven |
| Sizing a fuel oil expansion tank in an engine room | $v$ | Thermal expansion calculations need volume per kg |
| Reading a hydrometer at port customs | $SG$ | Hydrometers measure $SG$ directly (dimensionless) |
| Archimedes' principle for buoyancy | $\rho$ or $\gamma$ | Buoyant force is $\gamma \cdot V_{submerged}$ or $\rho g \cdot V_{submerged}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — The Property Inter-Relationship

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical derivation chain** between the four fluid properties, from the most fundamental (Density) to the most derived (Specific Volume from $SG$).


The following diagram maps the **algorithmic flow** a student should follow when solving a fluid properties numerical question in the KTU examination.

This diagram highlights how **density is the central property** in marine operations, with the others derived from it depending on application context.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
**Define the following with their SI units: (i) Specific Weight, (ii) Specific Volume, (iii) Specific Gravity.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** It is defined as the weight of a fluid per unit volume. Mathematically, $\gamma = \rho g$, where $\rho$ is the density and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity. **SI Unit: N/m³.**

**(ii) Specific Volume ($v$):** It is defined as the volume occupied by a unit mass of a fluid. Mathematically, $v = 1/\rho = V/m$. **SI Unit: m³/kg.**

**(iii) Specific Gravity ($SG$):** It is defined as the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of pure water at 4 °C (a standard reference liquid). Mathematically, $SG = \rho_{fluid}/\rho_{water @ 4°C}$. **SI Unit: Dimensionless (no units).**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Distinguish between density and specific weight. Why is specific gravity a dimensionless quantity?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Density ($\rho$)** | **Specific Weight ($\gamma$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mass per unit volume | Weight per unit volume |
| Formula | $\rho = m/V$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| Depends on $g$? | **No** | **Yes** |
| SI Unit | kg/m³ | N/m³ |
| Nature | Independent of location | Varies with gravitational field |

**Why $SG$ is dimensionless:** Specific gravity is defined as a *ratio of two densities* (fluid density to water density). Both numerator and denominator have identical units (kg/m³). When the units cancel out, the resulting quantity has no units — hence it is **dimensionless**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern, each Part B question in Module 1 is for **14 marks** and is typically divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You will be given an **internal choice**, so two independent question alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A naval fuel oil has a specific gravity of $0.92$. Compute its (i) density, (ii) specific weight, and (iii) specific volume in SI units. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular barge has a flat bottom of dimensions $20 \text{ m} \times 8 \text{ m}$. When fully loaded, the barge sits with a draft (submerged depth) of $1.5 \text{ m}$ in seawater of density $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Determine the total displacement mass and the buoyant force acting on the barge. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Given:** $SG = 0.92$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water @ 4°C} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Density:**
$$\rho = SG \cdot 1000 = 0.92 \cdot 1000 = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Specific Weight:**
$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 920 \cdot 9.81 = 9025.2 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**(iii) Specific Volume:**
$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{920} = 1.087 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $L = 20 \text{ m}$, $B = 8 \text{ m}$, Draft $T = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1: Submerged Volume of Barge (V_sub):**
$$V_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot T = 20 \cdot 8 \cdot 1.5 = 240 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Displacement Mass (m_d):**
By Archimedes' principle, the mass of water displaced equals the displacement mass:
$$m_d = \rho_{seawater} \cdot V_{sub} = 1025 \cdot 240 = 246000 \text{ kg} = 246 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Buoyant Force (F_B):**
$$F_B = m_d \cdot g = 246000 \cdot 9.81 = 2,413,260 \text{ N} \approx 2.413 \text{ MN}$$

**Final Answers:** $m_d = 246,000 \text{ kg}$ and $F_B = 2.413 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A liquid has a specific weight of $8720 \text{ N/m}^3$. Calculate its (i) specific gravity, (ii) density, and (iii) the mass of $0.4 \text{ m}^3$ of this liquid. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain how a **hydrometer** works on the principle of specific gravity. Why does a hydrometer float deeper in lighter liquids? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Given:** $\gamma = 8720 \text{ N/m}^3$, $V = 0.4 \text{ m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Specific Gravity:**
$$SG = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma_{water}} = \frac{8720}{9810} = 0.8889 \approx 0.889$$

**(ii) Density:**
$$\rho = \frac{\gamma}{g} = \frac{8720}{9.81} = 888.89 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Cross-check using $SG$:** $\rho = 0.889 \cdot 1000 = 889 \text{ kg/m}^3$ ✓ (minor rounding difference)

**(iii) Mass of 0.4 m³:**
$$m = \rho \cdot V = 888.89 \cdot 0.4 = 355.56 \text{ kg}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Working of a Hydrometer:**

A hydrometer is a calibrated glass instrument consisting of a hollow bulb at the bottom (loaded with mercury or lead shot for stability) and a thin graduated stem at the top. When gently lowered into a liquid, it floats in an upright position based on **Archimedes' principle**.

The buoyant force $F_B = \rho_{liquid} \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$ balances the weight of the hydrometer $W = m_h \cdot g$. Therefore:

$$\rho_{liquid} \cdot V_{submerged} = m_h$$

$$V_{submerged} = \frac{m_h}{\rho_{liquid}}$$

Since the mass of the hydrometer $m_h$ is constant, the submerged volume is *inversely proportional* to the liquid's density. The graduated stem reads the level at the liquid surface, which directly indicates the **specific gravity**.

**Why it floats deeper in lighter liquids:**
- If $\rho_{liquid}$ is **smaller** (lighter liquid), then $V_{submerged}$ must be **larger** to displace enough liquid weight to balance $W$.
- Hence, the hydrometer sinks deeper in lighter liquids.
- Conversely, in denser liquids (e.g., seawater), the hydrometer floats higher because a smaller submerged volume provides the same buoyant force.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Many students lose **2 to 3 marks** by writing density as $\text{kg/L}$ or as $\text{g/cm}^3$ without converting to $\text{kg/m}^3$. The KTU 2024 Scheme strictly requires SI base units. Always convert before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting $g$:** Specific weight problems often expect the *weight* in Newtons. If the question asks for "weight," remember to multiply the mass by $g$. A common error is reporting mass when the question requires weight.
> 3. **Wrong Reference for $SG$:** For *liquids*, the reference is **pure water at 4 °C** with $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. For *gases*, the reference is **air at standard conditions**. Do not interchange them.
> 4. **Hydrometer Direction:** A hydrometer floats **higher** in *denser* liquids, not deeper. Reversing this is a classic 1-mark deduction error.
> 5. **Displacement Confusion:** Remember that *displacement* is a **mass** (in tonnes) AND a **volume** (in m³). Be precise with the term "displacement tonnage" versus "displacement volume."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid-Fire Recall)**

- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume, $\rho = m/V$, SI unit **kg/m³**, freshwater @ 4°C = 1000, seawater @ 15°C ≈ 1025.
- **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume, $\gamma = \rho g$, SI unit **N/m³**, freshwater = 9810, seawater ≈ 10055.
- **Specific Volume ($v$):** Volume per unit mass, $v = 1/\rho$, SI unit **m³/kg**.
- **Specific Gravity ($SG$):** Dimensionless ratio to **water at 4°C**, $SG = \rho_{fluid}/1000$.
- **Master Inversion:** $\rho = SG \cdot 1000$, $\gamma = SG \cdot 9810$, $v = 1/(SG \cdot 1000)$.
- **Conversion Triplet:** $\rho \leftrightarrow \gamma$ via $g$; $\rho \leftrightarrow v$ via inversion; $\rho \leftrightarrow SG$ via water reference.
- **Naval Relevance:** Displacement, draft, Plimsoll line, ballast, submarine buoyancy, fuel tank design.
- **Hydrometer Rule:** Floats *higher* in denser liquids, *deeper* in lighter liquids.
- **Earth Gravity:** Always use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified.
- **KTU 2024 Trend:** Questions often combine property calculation with a small real-world naval application (e.g., fuel tank, ballast tank, barge). Practice these hybrid problems.
- **Dimensionless Property:** $SG$ is the *only* dimensionless property among the four — a frequent 1-mark conceptual question.
- **Don't Mix:** Density and specific weight are *not* the same. Density is mass-based; specific weight is force-based.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity and Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viscosity and Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity** is the physical property of a real fluid that represents its internal resistance to deformation (or flow) under an applied shear stress. It quantifies the fluid's tendency to resist relative motion between its adjacent layers.

In the rigorous terminology of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** syllabus for *Fluid Mechanics for Naval Architects*, viscosity is defined as the **shear stress per unit rate of shear strain** in a flowing fluid. Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant $\mu$ that links the internal friction force to the velocity gradient in a fluid continuum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Naval Architects:** A ship's hull moving through seawater experiences a **viscous drag force** that constitutes 60–80% of the total resistance for a typical displacement vessel. Without an accurate understanding of viscosity, one cannot predict powering requirements, fuel consumption, or the frictional component of the **ITTC 1978 resistance prediction method**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Honey vs. Water Demonstration

Imagine pouring two liquids on an inclined plank:
- **Water** flows rapidly and spreads out almost instantly. Its molecules slide past each other with little resistance. → **Low viscosity**.
- **Honey** (or cold molasses) creeps down slowly because the internal molecular bonds cling stubbornly to each other. → **High viscosity**.

If you could "freeze" the motion of fluid and look at two neighboring layers, the faster layer is trying to drag the slower one along, while the slower one is dragging the faster one back. This molecular "friction" between layers is precisely what we call **viscosity**.

| Fluid Type | Approximate Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Practical Feel |
|------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------|
| **Air** (20 °C) | $1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ | Invisible flow |
| **Water** (20 °C) | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | Thin, free-flowing |
| **Seawater** (15 °C) | $1.138 \times 10^{-3}$ | Slightly thicker than fresh water |
| **Lubricating oil (SAE 30)** | $\approx 0.29$ | Syrupy |
| **Heavy fuel oil (bunker C)** | $\approx 1.0$–$5.0$ | Very thick, used in slow-speed marine diesels |
| **Pitch / Bitumen** | $\approx 10^{8}$ | Solid-like at room temperature |

## 1.3 Two Faces of Viscosity

Fluid mechanics recognizes **two distinct forms** of the viscosity property. Both must be mastered for KTU university-level problem solving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** Also called *absolute viscosity*. It is the direct measure of a fluid's internal shear resistance. **SI unit: Pa·s (Pascal-second) or N·s/m²**. **CGS unit: Poise (P)**. $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$.
>
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** Defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density. **SI unit: m²/s**. **CGS unit: Stokes (St)** or **centiStokes (cSt)**. $1 \text{ St} = 1 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. It represents the fluid's momentum diffusivity and is the parameter most commonly used in the **Reynolds Number** calculation that governs boundary layer transition on ship hulls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Internal Shear in a Moving Fluid

Consider a fluid confined between two parallel plates separated by a small distance $dy$.

- The **lower plate is stationary**.
- The **upper plate is moved** at a constant velocity $U$ in the positive $x$-direction.
- The fluid in contact with the lower plate has velocity $0$ (the **no-slip condition**).
- The fluid in contact with the upper plate has velocity $U$.

Because of viscosity, the velocity of the fluid changes linearly from $0$ at the bottom to $U$ at the top. The fluid behaves as if it is composed of infinitesimally thin laminae (layers), each sliding over the one below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experiment by Isaac Newton (1687):** Newton proposed that the shear stress $\tau$ between two fluid layers is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ perpendicular to the direction of flow.

This is the foundation of classical fluid mechanics and is what separates an **ideal (inviscid) fluid** from a **real (viscous) fluid**. For naval architecture, **no fluid is truly inviscid** — even air and water have measurable, non-zero viscosity that produces real-world resistance on a ship.

## 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Governing Equation

Newton's Law of Viscosity is expressed as the following constitutive equation:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where each term has a precise physical meaning:

- $\tau$ = **Shear stress** (Pa or N/m²) — the tangential force per unit area transmitted between adjacent fluid layers.
- $\mu$ = **Dynamic (absolute) viscosity** (Pa·s) — the proportionality constant; a fluid property.
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = **Velocity gradient** (s⁻¹) — the rate of change of velocity in the direction normal to flow; also called the **rate of shear strain** or **shear rate**.

For a fluid where the velocity profile is non-linear (as in pipe flow or boundary-layer flow), the shear stress at any point $y$ is given by the more general form:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{d}{dy}\left( u(y) \right)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the complete, board-exam-ready list of equations, definitions, and unit conversions for the topic **"Viscosity and Newton's Law of Viscosity"**. Memorize this verbatim for the KTU End Semester Examination.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | SI Unit | CGS Unit |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau$ | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Pa = N/m² | dyne/cm² |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dfrac{du}{dy}}$ | Pa·s | Poise (P) |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s | Stokes (St) |
| 4 | Newton's Law — General | $\tau_{yx}$ | $\tau_{yx} = \mu \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | Pa | dyne/cm² |
| 5 | Shear Force on Area | $F$ | $F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | N | dyne |
| 6 | Poiseuille Conversion | — | $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | — | — |
| 7 | Stokes Conversion | — | $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | — | — |
| 8 | Viscosity of Water (20 °C) | $\mu_w$ | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | Pa·s | $\approx 1.002$ cP |
| 9 | Viscosity of Seawater (15 °C) | $\mu_{sw}$ | $1.138 \times 10^{-3}$ | Pa·s | $\approx 1.138$ cP |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (for ships) | $Re$ | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In ship model testing at towing tanks (such as the **IIT Madras towing tank** or the **Cooperative Research Ships (Cooperative Research Ships, CRS)**), the **Reynolds Number** of the model and prototype must be matched (or corrected using the **ITTC 1978 friction line** $\Rightarrow C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$). The Reynolds number itself is built upon the kinematic viscosity $\nu$, so a precise understanding of viscosity is non-negotiable for any Naval Architect.

## 2.4 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

A fluid that **obeys** Newton's Law of Viscosity with a constant $\mu$ is called a **Newtonian fluid**. If $\mu$ varies with the shear rate (or shear history), the fluid is **non-Newtonian**.

| Fluid Class | Behaviour of $\mu$ | Examples (Marine/Naval Relevance) |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Newtonian** | $\mu$ = constant | Water, air, gasoline, diesel oil, most simple liquids |
| **Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)** | $\mu$ decreases with shear rate | Polymer-thickened bilge-cleaning fluids, some drill muds |
| **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | $\mu$ increases with shear rate | Concentrated sand-water slurries, quick-sand |
| **Bingham plastic** | Behaves as solid until yield stress exceeded | Toothpaste, drilling mud, grease |
| **Thixotropic** | $\mu$ decreases with time at constant shear | Some marine paints, sediments |
| **Rheopectic** | $\mu$ increases with time at constant shear | Rare; some gypsum suspensions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Seawater, fresh water, air, and all standard marine fuels at moderate temperatures are **Newtonian fluids**. Do not apply non-Newtonian models (power-law, Bingham, etc.) to these unless the question explicitly states the fluid is non-Newtonian. This is a frequent KTU board-error.

## 2.5 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

The viscosity response to temperature is **fundamentally opposite** for liquids and gases:

- **For liquids** (including water, oils, and fuels): $\mu$ **decreases sharply** with increasing temperature. Heating water from 0 °C to 100 °C drops its viscosity by roughly a factor of **7**.
- **For gases** (including air): $\mu$ **increases** with increasing temperature, but only weakly (the kinetic theory of gases predicts $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naval-Architecture Insight:** This is why **warm tropical seawater** ($\approx 28$ °C) has *lower* viscosity ($\mu \approx 0.85 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) than **cold polar seawater** ($\approx 0$ °C, $\mu \approx 1.79 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). A ship operating in cold Arctic waters will therefore experience a higher frictional resistance than the same ship in equatorial waters — a non-trivial effect for ice-class vessels and global fleet routing.

A commonly used empirical correlation for the dynamic viscosity of fresh water (valid 0 °C $\le T \le$ 100 °C) is:

$$\mu(T) = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 + 0.0337 T + 0.000221 T^2 \right)^{-1}$$

where $T$ is in °C and $\mu$ is in Pa·s.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity from a Force Balance

Consider a fluid element in the form of a thin rectangular block bounded by two horizontal planes separated by a small vertical distance $dy$ and having a top surface of area $dA$. The block is sheared by the relative motion of the surrounding fluid.

> We will derive the expression $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ rigorously from a control-volume momentum balance.

**Step 1 — Define the velocity field.**
Assume a one-dimensional, steady, laminar flow in the $x$-direction. The velocity varies only in the $y$-direction:

$$u = u(y)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-slip condition at the lower plane.**
At the lower plane ($y$), the fluid velocity is $u(y)$. At the upper plane ($y + dy$), the fluid velocity is $u(y + dy)$.

**Step 3 — Express the velocity at the upper plane using a Taylor series expansion.**
Expand $u(y + dy)$ about the point $y$:

$$u(y + dy) = u(y) + \frac{du}{dy} dy + \frac{1}{2!} \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} (dy)^2 + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Truncate the series for an infinitesimally small layer.**
Since $dy \rightarrow 0$, the higher-order terms become negligible, and we obtain:

$$u(y + dy) - u(y) = \frac{du}{dy} dy$$

**Step 5 — Identify the relative velocity between the two faces of the fluid element.**

$$\Delta u = \frac{du}{dy} dy$$

**Step 6 — Define the rate of shear strain (shear rate).**
The rate of shear strain $\dot{\gamma}$ is the relative velocity per unit separation distance:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\Delta u}{dy} = \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 7 — State Newton's Postulate.**
Newton postulated that the shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain, with a constant of proportionality $\mu$:

$$\tau \propto \dot{\gamma}$$

$$\tau = \mu \, \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute the shear rate back to obtain Newton's Law of Viscosity.**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is the final, fundamental form of Newton's Law of Viscosity, valid for all Newtonian fluids.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Viscous Force on a Submerged Surface

For a flat plate of area $A$ in contact with a fluid of viscosity $\mu$, the **viscous (skin-friction) force** on the plate can be derived by direct application of Newton's law.

**Step 1 — Write the local shear stress at a generic point.**

$$\tau(y) = \mu \frac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{y}$$

**Step 2 — Express the differential viscous force on an infinitesimal area $dA$.**

$$dF = \tau(y) \, dA = \mu \frac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{y} dA$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the total wetted area $A$ to obtain the total viscous drag.**

$$F = \int_A \tau(y) \, dA = \int_A \mu \frac{du}{dy}\bigg|_{y} dA$$

For the classic **linear Couette flow** (between two parallel plates with the upper plate moving at velocity $U$ and the gap $h$ being constant), the velocity profile is:

$$u(y) = U \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the velocity gradient.**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total shear force.**

$$F = \mu \frac{U}{h} A$$

**Step 6 — Express the corresponding shear stress.**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

This is the **Couette-flow solution** — a recurring result in KTU board problems and an important building block for the analysis of journal bearings and certain lubrication systems in marine shaft lines.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU 3-Mark Style Numerical

> **[Problem]** A 1.2 m × 0.8 m flat plate is towed through seawater at 5 m/s. The boundary layer velocity profile at the trailing edge is approximated by $u/U = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ where $\delta = 0.02$ m. Calculate the **total skin-friction drag force** on the plate. Use $\mu_{\text{seawater}} = 1.138 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Compute the local velocity gradient from the power-law profile.**

$$u = U \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{\delta} \cdot \frac{1}{7} \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7 - 1} = \frac{U}{7\delta} \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{-6/7}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the shear stress at the wall ($y = 0$).**

$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{7\delta} \left(\frac{0}{\delta}\right)^{-6/7}$$

The expression $\left(\dfrac{0}{\delta}\right)^{-6/7}$ is formally infinite, so we must use the actual wall-gradient obtained by differentiating $u/U = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ and substituting $y \to 0^+$:

$$\left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{U}{7\delta} \cdot \lim_{y \to 0^+} \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{-6/7}$$

This limit diverges for $y \to 0^+$, which physically means the wall shear stress in a 1/7-power-law turbulent boundary layer is a known constant derived from empirical integration:

$$\tau_w = 0.0288 \, \rho U^2 \, Re_x^{-1/7}$$

For the purpose of this KTU-style worked example (which examines the underlying Newton's law, not the full turbulent integration), we will evaluate the **local shear stress at a representative $y = 0.001$ m** and integrate numerically.

**Step 3 — Plug in the representative values.**

- $U = 5$ m/s
- $\delta = 0.02$ m
- $\mu = 1.138 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Plate area $A = 1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96$ m²

At $y = 0.001$ m:

$$\left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0.001} = \frac{5}{7 \times 0.02} \left(\frac{0.001}{0.02}\right)^{-6/7} = \frac{5}{0.14} \times (0.05)^{-0.857}$$

Computing $(0.05)^{-0.857}$:

$$\ln(0.05) = -2.996 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -0.857 \times (-2.996) = 2.568 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{2.568} \approx 13.05$$

Therefore:

$$\left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0.001} = 35.714 \times 13.05 \approx 466.0 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the local wall shear stress.**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 1.138 \times 10^{-3} \times 466.0 \approx 0.530 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Estimate the total drag force over the plate area.**

$$F \approx \tau \cdot A = 0.530 \times 0.96 \approx 0.509 \; \text{N}$$

> **[Note for students]** The above used a *single-point* representative shear stress. A full KTU 14-mark answer would require integrating the 1/7-power-law profile across the plate, or invoking the **Schlichting integral momentum equation**. The result above is intentionally simplified to demonstrate how Newton's Law of Viscosity is applied as the constitutive starting point.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Concept Map of Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PROPERTY: VISCOSITY] --> B[Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    A --> C[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    A --> D[Newtonian Behaviour]
    A --> E[Non-Newtonian Behaviour]
    B --> F[SI Unit: Pa s]
    B --> G[CGS Unit: Poise P]
    B --> H[Maritime Use: Frictional Drag Calculation]
    C --> I[SI Unit: m squared per s]
    C --> J[CGS Unit: Stokes St]
    C --> K[Maritime Use: Reynolds Number for Model Testing]
    D --> L[Newton Law: tau equals mu times du over dy]
    L --> M[Constant mu independent of shear rate]
    E --> N[Shear Thinning Pseudoplastic]
    E --> O[Shear Thickening Dilatant]
    E --> P[Bingham Plastic]
    E --> Q[Thixotropic Time Dependent]
    H --> R[Ship Hull Resistance]
    H --> S[Lubrication of Marine Shaft Bearings]
    K --> R
    K --> T[ITTC 1978 Friction Line]
    R --> U[Fuel Consumption Estimate]
    T --> U
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Couette Flow Velocity Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLATE_SYSTEM[Couette Flow Apparatus]
        direction TB
        P1[Lower Plate: Stationary, y equals 0] --> P2[Upper Plate: Moving at U, y equals h]
        P1 -. No slip at wall .-> F1[Fluid Layer at y equals 0: u equals 0]
        F1 --> F2[Fluid Layer at y equals h over 2: u equals U over 2]
        F2 --> F3[Fluid Layer at y equals h: u equals U]
        P2 -. No slip at wall .-> F3
    end
    PLATE_SYSTEM --> EQ[Velocity profile: u over U equals y over h]
    EQ --> TAU[Shear stress: tau equals mu times U over h]
    TAU --> F[Shear force: F equals mu times A times U over h]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Velocity Profile Visualization

```
  Upper Plate  ───────────────────► U (free stream)
     |     |     |     |     |     |
     |  →  |  →  |  →  |  →  |  →  |   <- Fluid layers (laminae)
     |     |     |     |     |     |      each moving at a different
     |  →  |  →  |  →  |  →  |  →  |      speed → "shear"
     |     |     |     |     |     |
  Lower Plate  ───────────────────► 0  (no-slip)
     └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘
      y=0   y=δ/4 y=δ/2 y=3δ/4 y=δ

  ⇒ Linear velocity profile for Couette flow
  ⇒ Slope du/dy = U/δ
  ⇒ τ = μ(U/δ)   (Newton's Law applied)
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Block — When to Use Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given a Fluid Mechanics Problem Involving Viscosity] --> Q1{Is density rho in the governing equation?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> KV[Use Kinematic Viscosity nu equals mu over rho]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the problem about pipe flow or Reynolds number?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> KV
    Q2 -- No --> DV[Use Dynamic Viscosity mu directly in Newton law tau equals mu du over dy]
    KV --> R1[Example: Reynolds number Re equals V L over nu]
    KV --> R2[Example: Navier Stokes equation contains nu]
    DV --> R3[Example: Couette flow shear stress]
    DV --> R4[Example: Journal bearing analysis]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Conceptual Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCNST302]**
> **Q1.** Define **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**. State their SI units and give one physical significance of each in naval architecture.

**Model Answer:**

- **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the proportionality constant in Newton's Law of Viscosity that represents a fluid's internal resistance to shear deformation. **SI unit: Pa·s (N·s/m²)**. *Physical significance:* Used directly in calculating skin-friction drag on a ship hull ($F_D = \mu A \, \frac{du}{dy}$).
- **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$):** Defined as $\nu = \mu / \rho$, it represents the fluid's momentum diffusivity. **SI unit: m²/s**. *Physical significance:* Used in the **Reynolds Number** $Re = VL/\nu$, which determines the onset of turbulent boundary-layer flow on a ship's hull — critical for model testing and the ITTC 1978 extrapolation method.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCNST302]**
> **Q2.** A fluid of density $\rho = 850$ kg/m³ has a dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.0285$ Pa·s. Calculate its kinematic viscosity in (a) m²/s and (b) centistokes (cSt).

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** 

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.0285}{850} = 3.353 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**(b)** Convert to Stokes: $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \text{ St}$, so

$$\nu = 3.353 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^4 = 0.3353 \text{ St} = 33.53 \text{ cSt}$$

> This fluid corresponds to a **light marine fuel oil (intermediate fuel oil, IFO 30)** — useful context for the student.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCNST302 / Module 1]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. Derive the relationship between shear stress and the velocity gradient for a Newtonian fluid, including the role of the no-slip condition.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A flat plate of length $L = 2.5$ m and width $b = 1.0$ m is stationary in a steady flow of water with free-stream velocity $U = 3.0$ m/s. The velocity profile in the laminar boundary layer is given by the **Blasius exact solution approximation**: $u/U = 2(y/\delta) - (y/\delta)^2$, with $\delta = 5.0$ mm at the trailing edge. Calculate:
> 1. The shear stress distribution $\tau(y)$ inside the boundary layer.
> 2. The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ at the trailing edge.
> 3. The total skin-friction drag force on the plate.
> Use $\mu_{\text{water}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 marks]

1. **Statement:** Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress in a flowing fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient). The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$.
2. **Mathematical form:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$
3. **Derivation:** Starting from the Couette flow assumption with the no-slip condition $u(0) = 0$ and $u(\delta) = U$, performing a Taylor series expansion of the velocity field, truncating at first order, and applying Newton's proportionality postulate, we obtain $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$.
4. **No-slip condition:** This empirical condition (verified experimentally) states that a fluid in direct contact with a solid surface has zero relative velocity with respect to that surface. It is the cornerstone of all viscous-flow theory and is what makes the velocity gradient $\frac{du}{dy}$ non-zero.

**(b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the velocity gradient from the given profile.**

$$u = U \left[ 2 \frac{y}{\delta} - \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^2 \right]$$

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{\delta} \left[ 2 - 2 \frac{y}{\delta} \right] = \frac{2U}{\delta} \left( 1 - \frac{y}{\delta} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress distribution.**

$$\tau(y) = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{2 \mu U}{\delta} \left( 1 - \frac{y}{\delta} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Wall shear stress at $y = 0$.**

$$\tau_w = \tau(0) = \frac{2 \mu U}{\delta} = \frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.0}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.2 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Total skin-friction drag force (integrating wall stress over the plate area).**

For a flat plate in laminar flow, the local wall shear stress varies with $x$ (distance from the leading edge). However, this problem gives a single $\delta$ value at the trailing edge, so we approximate the **average** wall shear stress as $\tau_w$ and multiply by the plate area $A = L \times b = 2.5 \times 1.0 = 2.5$ m²:

$$F_D = \tau_w \times A = 1.2 \times 2.5 = 3.0 \; \text{N}$$

> *(For a more rigorous answer, one would use the Blasius solution $\tau_w(x) = 0.332 \mu U/x^{1/2}$ and integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = L$, yielding $F_D = 0.664 \sqrt{\mu \rho U^3 L \, b^2}$ — but the simplified form above is acceptable for a 7-mark sub-question.)*

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCNST302 / Module 1]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate clearly between **Newtonian** and **Non-Newtonian fluids**. Sketch the shear stress vs. shear rate ($\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$) diagram for at least four classes of non-Newtonian fluids and give one marine/naval example of each.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A submarine is moving submerged in seawater at 8 knots (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s). Estimate the **kinematic viscosity** of the seawater at $5$ °C and $25$ °C, then compute the **Reynolds number** based on the submarine's length $L = 65$ m for both temperatures. Discuss the implications for the boundary-layer regime and the frictional resistance of the submarine.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 marks]

| Class | $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ Shape | Marine Example |
|-------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| **Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)** | Curve concave to the $\dot{\gamma}$ axis, passing through origin | Polymer-thickened bilge cleaning agents |
| **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | Curve convex to the $\dot{\gamma}$ axis, passing through origin | Highly concentrated dredger spoil slurries |
| **Bingham plastic** | Straight line with a yield-stress intercept $\tau_y$ on the $\tau$ axis | Marine drilling muds used in offshore exploration |
| **Thixotropic** | Hysteresis loop; viscosity drops with time at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | Some marine anti-fouling paints |

Newtonian fluids obey $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ with a constant $\mu$ — a *straight line* through the origin. Seawater, fresh water, and marine diesel are all **Newtonian**.

**(b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert 8 knots to m/s.**

$$V = 8 \times 0.5144 = 4.115 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate kinematic viscosity of seawater at 5 °C and 25 °C.**

Using $\mu_w(T) = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 + 0.0337T + 0.000221T^2\right)^{-1}$ Pa·s and $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³:

*At $T = 5$ °C:*

$$\mu_w = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 + 0.1685 + 0.005525\right)^{-1} = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.173^{-1} = 1.528 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\nu_5 = \frac{1.528 \times 10^{-3}}{1025} = 1.491 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \approx 1.49 \; \text{cSt}$$

*At $T = 25$ °C:*

$$\mu_w = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 + 0.8425 + 0.1381\right)^{-1} = 1.792 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.981^{-1} = 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\nu_{25} = \frac{9.05 \times 10^{-4}}{1025} = 8.83 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \approx 0.88 \; \text{cSt}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Reynolds numbers.**

$$Re_5 = \frac{V L}{\nu_5} = \frac{4.115 \times 65}{1.491 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.794 \times 10^{8}$$

$$Re_{25} = \frac{V L}{\nu_{25}} = \frac{4.115 \times 65}{8.83 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.029 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Discussion.**

Both Reynolds numbers are well above the **critical Reynolds number for a submarine** ($Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$), so the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** in both cases. However, the *colder* water ($5$ °C) gives a **40% lower** Reynolds number than the warmer water, implying a **higher frictional resistance coefficient** when using the ITTC 1978 line ($C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10}Re - 2)^2$). Operationally, this means the same submarine consumes **more fuel** in cold polar waters than in warm tropical waters for the same speed and depth — a non-trivial naval-architecture conclusion.

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (compiled from KTU board-evaluation feedback):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\mu$ and $\nu$:** A very common 2-mark error. Remember: $\mu$ has units of Pa·s; $\nu$ has units of m²/s. If the question gives you *density*, you almost certainly need $\nu = \mu/\rho$.
> 2. **Forgetting the no-slip condition:** The no-slip condition is what *creates* a non-zero velocity gradient. If you write $\tau = \mu \, du/dy$ without invoking no-slip, you have explained Newton's law only **halfway** — KTU examiners will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit-conversion mistakes:** $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$, not $1 \text{ Pa·s}$. $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, not $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. These are classic 1-mark-deduction errors.
> 4. **Applying Newton's law to a non-Newtonian fluid:** Seawater, air, water, gasoline, kerosene, and standard marine fuels are **Newtonian** at normal conditions. Do **not** apply a power-law or Bingham model to them.
> 5. **Confusing kinematic-viscosity direction with dynamic-viscosity direction:** $\mu$ measures the *cause* (resistance); $\nu$ measures the *effectiveness of momentum transport*. Memorize one marine-specific example (e.g., $Re_{ship}$) of each.
> 6. **Sign convention:** Shear stress $\tau$ acting on a surface is conventionally taken as **positive** when it acts in the **positive $x$-direction** on a surface whose outward normal is in the **negative $y$-direction**. Getting the sign wrong can flip your answer in 14-mark problems.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Viscosity and Newton's Law of Viscosity**

- **Definition:** Viscosity is the internal resistance of a fluid to shear deformation, captured quantitatively by $\mu$ (dynamic) and $\nu$ (kinematic).
- **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the constitutive equation for all Newtonian fluids.
- **Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$:** Units Pa·s (SI) and Poise (CGS); $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$; for water at 20 °C, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- **Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$; units m²/s (SI) and Stokes (CGS); $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4}$ m²/s.
- **Newtonian Fluids:** Constant $\mu$ — includes water, air, all standard marine fuels at moderate temperatures, and seawater.
- **Non-Newtonian Fluids:** $\mu$ varies with shear rate or time — includes pseudo-plastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic, rheopectic.
- **Temperature Effect:** $\mu$ decreases with $T$ for liquids; $\mu$ increases with $T$ for gases.
- **Naval Relevance:** $\nu$ appears in Reynolds number $Re = VL/\nu$ for ship model testing; ITTC 1978 friction line; boundary-layer transition; skin-friction drag.
- **Seawater Reference:** $\mu_{sw} = 1.138 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 15 °C, $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³.
- **No-Slip Condition:** A fluid in contact with a solid has zero relative velocity — *the* physical reason that $du/dy \neq 0$ and hence $\tau \neq 0$.
- **Couette Flow:** Linear velocity profile $u/U = y/h$, leading to $\tau = \mu U/h$ and $F = \mu A U/h$.
- **High-Yield Conversion Factors:** $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s; $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6}$ m²/s; $1 \text{ kgf·s/m}^2 = 9.81$ Pa·s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
