> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Newtonian Fluids – Formal Definition A **Newtonian fluid** is a fluid whose viscous stress is **linearly proportional** to the local strain rate (rate of deformation) at every point within the flow field. The constant of proportionality is the **dynamic viscosity (μ)**, which is **independent of the shear rate** and depends only on the fluid's intrinsic thermodynamic state (pressure and temperature). Mathematically, the shear stress at a fluid element is given by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ Where: - **τ** = Shear stress acting tangentially on the fluid layer **[N/m² or Pa]** - **μ** = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid **[Pa·s or N·s/m²]** - **du/dy** = Velocity gradient normal to the direction of flow **[1/s]** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In **PCNST302 – Fluid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, the Newtonian assumption is the **foundation assumption** for almost all resistance, propulsion, and seakeeping calculations. The standard formula τ = μ(du/dy) is used to derive the **Navier-Stokes equations**, which govern ship hull boundary layers and free-surface flows. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values for Naval Applications:** > - **Fresh Water at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** > - **Sea Water at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **1.072 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (higher due to dissolved salts) > - **SAE 30 Lubricating Oil at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **0.29 Pa·s** > - **Air at 20 °C:** μ ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** The **kinematic viscosity (ν)** is also frequently used and is defined as: $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \left[\frac{m^2}{s}\right]$$ Where **ρ** is the fluid density **[kg/m³]**. For sea water, ν ≈ **1.04 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 20 °C — a critical input in **Reynolds number scaling** for model basin towing tests. --- ## 1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids – Formal Definition A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is a fluid whose constitutive relationship between shear stress (τ) and shear rate ($\dot{\gamma} = du/dy$) is **non-linear**, **time-dependent**, or exhibits a **yield stress**. In simple terms, the "viscosity" of a non-Newtonian fluid **changes with the rate at which it is sheared**, rather than remaining constant. The apparent viscosity is defined as: $$\mu_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ Unlike Newtonian fluids, μ_app for non-Newtonian fluids is **not constant** — it varies with $\dot{\gamma}$, with time, or with the deformation history. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Naval Architecture Relevance:** While most ship hydrodynamics problems assume Newtonian behaviour (because water, air, and most hydraulic oils are Newtonian), **Non-Newtonian fluids** become critical in: > - **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) carrier sloshing** — LNG behaves as a complex fluid near its boiling point. > - **Oil spill response** — heavy crude oils and emulsions exhibit viscoelastic and thixotropic behaviour. > - **Marine coatings and sealants**. > - **Biological fluids in marine life studies (biomechanics).** --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition ### Real-World Analogy: Honey vs. Water Imagine tilting two glasses — one filled with **water**, the other with **honey**. - **Water** flows easily regardless of how hard it sloshes. Doubling the tilt (force) doubles the flow. → **Newtonian behaviour**. - **Honey** is "thicker" and resists motion. As you tilt harder, it still flows, but the resistance changes differently. → **Non-Newtonian behaviour**. ### The Ketchup Bottle Analogy (Shear-Thinning) When you first squeeze a ketchup bottle, the ketchup **barely moves**. But once you tap the side or shake it vigorously, it suddenly **liquefies and pours out freely**. This is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) behaviour** — viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate. ### The Cornstarch-Water Mixture (Shear-Thickening) A mixture of cornstarch and water, when struck sharply with a hand, behaves like a **solid** (you can momentarily run across the pool's surface). But when handled gently, it flows like a thick liquid. This is **shear-thickening (dilatant) behaviour** — viscosity **increases** with shear rate. ### Naval Analogy: Pitch-Roll Motion Think of a ship's roll motion in heavy seas. A Newtonian sea behaves predictably regardless of roll rate. But consider the **sloshing of heavy fuel oil (HFO) in partially filled ship tanks** — its sloshing behaviour deviates from Newtonian prediction, especially during violent resonance roll, because the fuel behaves as a **viscoelastic** medium. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** If the shear stress vs. shear rate graph is a **straight line through the origin** → Newtonian. If it is a **curve or has a yield offset** → Non-Newtonian. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Rheology — The Science of Flow and Deformation **Rheology** is the branch of continuum mechanics that studies the **deformation and flow of matter**, particularly the relationship between **stress**, **strain rate**, and **time**. It is the theoretical foundation for distinguishing Newtonian from non-Newtonian fluids. A general **constitutive equation** for an incompressible fluid can be expressed as: $$\tau = f(\dot{\gamma})$$ where the functional form of $f$ determines the fluid class: | Functional Form | Fluid Class | Behaviour | |-----------------|-------------|-----------| | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian | Linear, constant μ | | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) | n=1 Newtonian; n<1 pseudoplastic; n>1 dilatant | | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham Plastic | Yield stress + linear | | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Herschel-Bulkley | Yield stress + non-linear | | $\tau + \lambda \frac{d\tau}{dt} = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Maxwell Viscoelastic | Time-dependent, elastic recovery | --- ## 2.2 Classification of Non-Newtonian Fluids (The Complete KTU Map) ### 2.2.1 Time-Independent (Purely Viscous) Fluids **A. Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluids** - Apparent viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate. - Behaviour: at rest, fluid is highly viscous; under agitation, it flows readily. - **Examples:** Polymer solutions (polyethylene oxide), paint, blood, ketchup, paper pulp slurry. - **Modelled by:** Power-law with **n < 1**. **B. Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluids** - Apparent viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate. - Behaviour: feels "wet" at rest but turns solid-like under sudden impact. - **Examples:** Cornstarch-water suspension, wet sand, high-concentration ceramic slurries. - **Modelled by:** Power-law with **n > 1**. **C. Bingham Plastics** - Behaviour: behaves as a **rigid solid** until shear stress exceeds a critical value $\tau_y$ (yield stress); beyond that, it flows with a linear Newtonian relationship. - **Examples:** Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise, sewage sludge. - **Modelled by:** $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ (plastic viscosity $\mu_p$). **D. Herschel-Bulkley Fluids** - Generalised Bingham model combining a yield stress with power-law behaviour. - **Examples:** Some crude oils, blood at low shear, cement slurries. ### 2.2.2 Time-Dependent Fluids **A. Thixotropic Fluids** - Viscosity **decreases** with time under sustained shear and **gradually recovers** when shear is removed. - **Examples:** Printing ink, greases, some gels. - **Naval Relevance:** Thixotropic behaviour of marine paints helps application but requires understanding for storage stability. **B. Rheopectic (or Rheotropic) Fluids** - Viscosity **increases** with time under sustained shear. - **Examples:** Gypsum pastes, certain lubricants under extreme pressure. ### 2.2.3 Viscoelastic Fluids These fluids exhibit **both viscous and elastic** properties. They behave as a fluid under slow deformation but show elastic recovery (like a solid) under rapid deformation. - **Modelled by:** Maxwell model, Oldroyd-B model, Jeffreys model. - **Examples:** Polymer melts, dough, biological fluids, some heavy crude oils. - **Naval Relevance:** Viscoelastic wake flows behind submarines and biomimetic hulls are an active research area. --- ## 2.3 The Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Model — KTU Favourite The **power-law model** is the most commonly tested rheological model in KTU board examinations. It captures pseudoplastic and dilatant behaviour in a single equation: $$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$ Where: - **K** = Flow consistency index **[Pa·sⁿ]** - **n** = Flow behaviour index **[dimensionless]** - **n = 1, K = μ** → Newtonian - **n < 1** → Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) - **n > 1** → Dilatant (shear-thickening) The **apparent viscosity** for a power-law fluid is: $$\mu_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$ This shows explicitly that μ_app **depends on $\dot{\gamma}$** — when n < 1, μ_app decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases (shear-thinning). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme board exam questions on Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa | | 2 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | | 3 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law model | Pa | | 4 | $\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Apparent viscosity (power-law) | Pa·s | | 5 | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic model | Pa | | 6 | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Herschel-Bulkley model | Pa | | 7 | $\tau + \lambda \dfrac{d\tau}{dt} = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Maxwell viscoelastic model | Pa | | 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear rate definition | 1/s | | 9 | $\tau = \mu_{app} \dot{\gamma}$ | General definition (Newtonian + non-Newtonian) | Pa | | 10 | $\mu_{plastic} = \mu_p + \dfrac{\tau_y}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Plastic viscosity (Bingham) | Pa·s | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Ship Resistance & Propulsion:** Standard towing-tank experiments assume fresh or salt water as Newtonian. The friction resistance coefficients (C_f) derived from **ITTC-1978** or **ITTC-2024** correlation lines are valid only for Newtonian working fluids. 2. **Oil Spill Modelling:** Crude oils spilled at sea often behave as non-Newtonian fluids. Accurate viscosity modelling is essential for predicting **spreading rates, dispersion, and weathering**. 3. **LNG Carrier Design:** Liquefied gases (LNG at −162 °C) can exhibit non-Newtonian behaviour near their boiling point, especially during **sloshing-induced phase change**. KTU Module 1 lays the groundwork for understanding such anomalies. 4. **Marine Coatings & Anti-Fouling Paints:** These are formulated as **shear-thinning fluids** so they can be sprayed easily (low μ_app at high shear in the spray nozzle) but **stay thick** on the hull (high μ_app at rest). 5. **Submarine Piping & Hydraulic Systems:** Hydraulic fluids are designed to be Newtonian for predictable flow control in **submarine ballast and trim systems**. 6. **Biomechanics of Marine Life:** Understanding the viscoelastic properties of **fish mucus, jellyfish mesoglea, and whale baleen** informs biomimetic hull coating research. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "discuss the importance of Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids in naval architecture," always anchor at least one point to **ship resistance/towing tank testing** and one to **oil spill / LNG carrier** applications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from the Shear-Stress Concept We will derive the fundamental Newtonian viscosity relationship by considering the **force balance on an infinitesimal fluid element** between two parallel plates (Couette flow configuration). ### Step 1 — Physical Setup Consider two large parallel plates, each of area **A**, separated by a small distance **dy**. The lower plate is stationary and the upper plate moves with velocity **U**. The fluid trapped between the plates adheres to both plates (no-slip boundary condition). ### Step 2 — Define Shear Stress A force **F** must be applied to the upper plate to maintain its motion. By definition, shear stress is force per unit area: $$\tau = \frac{F}{A}$$ ### Step 3 — Empirical Observation (Newton's Hypothesis) Newton proposed that for many fluids, the force **F** is proportional to: - The plate area **A** - The plate velocity **U** - Inversely proportional to the gap **dy** Hence: $$F \propto \frac{AU}{dy} \Rightarrow F = \mu \frac{AU}{dy}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Shear Stress Dividing both sides by area **A**: $$\frac{F}{A} = \tau = \mu \frac{U}{dy}$$ ### Step 5 — Velocity Gradient as Shear Rate Since velocity varies linearly across the gap (for a Newtonian fluid in Couette flow), we define: $$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{dy} = \dot{\gamma}$$ This is the **shear rate**, with units of 1/s. Therefore: $$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$ ### Step 6 — Physical Interpretation - If **μ** is large → fluid is "thick" → large stress needed for the same velocity gradient. - If **du/dy = 0** → τ = 0 → there is no shear stress when fluid moves as a rigid body. This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, the constitutive equation for Newtonian fluids. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity for a Power-Law Fluid The power-law model states: $$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$ Apparent viscosity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear rate: $$\mu_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ Substituting the power-law expression: $$\mu_{app} = \frac{K \dot{\gamma}^n}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$ ### Special Cases — Full Verification **Case 1:** n = 1 $$\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{1-1} = K \dot{\gamma}^0 = K$$ Thus K = μ (Newtonian limit). ✓ **Case 2:** n < 1 (Pseudoplastic, shear-thinning) As $\dot{\gamma}$ increases, $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ decreases (because n−1 < 0). Therefore μ_app **decreases** with shear rate. ✓ **Case 3:** n > 1 (Dilatant, shear-thickening) As $\dot{\gamma}$ increases, $\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ increases (because n−1 > 0). Therefore μ_app **increases** with shear rate. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Bingham Plastic Velocity Profile in Pipe Flow For a **Bingham plastic**, the constitutive equation is: $$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{for } \tau \geq \tau_y$$ Consider fully-developed laminar flow in a horizontal pipe of radius **R**. By force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius **r**: $$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ Where ΔP is the pressure drop over length L. ### Step 1 — Identify the Plug-Flow Radius At the wall, r = R, so: $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$$ A **plug core** (rigidly moving fluid) exists where τ(r) < τ_y. The radius of this plug is: $$r_0 = \frac{2L \tau_y}{\Delta P}$$ For r ≥ r₀, the fluid is sheared. For r < r₀, the fluid moves as a rigid plug. ### Step 2 — Velocity Gradient in the Sheared Region For r₀ ≤ r ≤ R: $$\tau(r) = \tau_y + \mu_p \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)$$ (Negative sign because velocity decreases with r.) Solving: $$-\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\tau(r) - \tau_y}{\mu_p} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r - 2L \tau_y}{2L \mu_p}$$ ### Step 3 — Integrate with Boundary Condition Integrate from r to R, using u(R) = 0: $$\int_u^{u_p} du = -\int_r^R \frac{\Delta P \cdot r' - 2L \tau_y}{2L \mu_p} dr'$$ Solving: $$u_p - u(r) = \frac{1}{2L \mu_p}\left[\frac{\Delta P}{2}(R^2 - r^2) - 2L\tau_y(R - r)\right]$$ Therefore the velocity in the sheared region is: $$u(r) = u_p - \frac{\Delta P}{4L\mu_p}(R^2 - r^2) + \frac{\tau_y}{\mu_p}(R - r)$$ Where $u_p = u(r_0)$ is the plug velocity: $$u_p = \frac{\Delta P}{4L\mu_p}(R^2 - r_0^2) - \frac{\tau_y}{\mu_p}(R - r_0)$$ This is the **Bingham plastic velocity profile** — a parabolic shape with a **flat plug** in the centre. KTU board examinations frequently test this dual-region behaviour. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Fluid Identification > [!NOTE] > Use this decision tree to classify any fluid in a KTU exam question based on the information given in the problem statement. **Given:** shear stress τ and shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ measurements (or descriptive behaviour). 1. **Does τ = μ $\dot{\gamma}$ hold with constant μ for ALL shear rates?** - **YES** → **Newtonian fluid** (μ is constant). - **NO** → Go to Step 2. 2. **Does the fluid flow only when τ exceeds a critical τ_y?** - **YES** → Go to Step 3. - **NO** → Go to Step 4. 3. **After yielding, is τ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ linear?** - **YES** → **Bingham plastic** (τ_y ≠ 0, linear beyond yield). - **NO** → **Herschel-Bulkley fluid** (τ_y ≠ 0, non-linear beyond yield). 4. **Does μ_app decrease with $\dot{\gamma}$?** - **YES** → **Pseudoplastic / Shear-thinning** (n < 1 in power-law). - **NO** → Go to Step 5. 5. **Does μ_app increase with $\dot{\gamma}$?** - **YES** → **Dilatant / Shear-thickening** (n > 1 in power-law). - **NO** → Go to Step 6. 6. **Does the behaviour depend on the duration of shearing?** - **YES, μ_app decreases with time** → **Thixotropic**. - **YES, μ_app increases with time** → **Rheopectic**. - **YES, fluid shows elastic recoil** → **Viscoelastic** (e.g., Maxwell fluid). This decision tree is a **favourite short-answer (3-mark) framework** for KTU examiners. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Shear Stress vs. Shear Rate — Rheological Flow Curves This diagram shows the qualitative behaviour of all major fluid classes on a single plot. It is the **most-commonly drawn figure** in KTU board answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fluid under Shear Stress] --> B{Is viscosity constant?} B -- Yes --> C[Newtonian Fluid] B -- No --> D{Is there a yield stress?} D -- No --> E{Power-law index n} E -- n equals 1 --> C E -- n less than 1 --> F[Pseudoplastic
Shear Thinning] E -- n greater than 1 --> G[Dilatant
Shear Thickening] D -- Yes --> H{Linear beyond yield?} H -- Yes --> I[Bingham Plastic] H -- No --> J[Herschel Bulkley] A --> K{Time dependent?} K -- Yes, viscosity drops with time --> L[Thixotropic] K -- Yes, viscosity rises with time --> M[Rheopectic] A --> N{Elastic recovery?} N -- Yes --> O[Viscoelastic
e.g. Maxwell model] ``` --- ## 4.2 Schematic: Bingham Plastic Velocity Profile in a Pipe A block-level functional architecture mapping the velocity field of a Bingham plastic flowing through a circular pipe. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PIPE[Pipe of Radius R] direction LR PLUG[Plug Core
Radius r0
Rigid Body Motion
Velocity up] SHEAR[Sheared Annulus
r0 to R
Parabolic Velocity
u decreases to 0 at wall] end INLET[Pressure Inlet
Delta P over L] --> PLUG PLUG --> SHEAR SHEAR --> WALL[No Slip Wall
u of R equals 0] ``` --- ## 4.3 Couette Flow — Newton's Original Experimental Configuration The foundational experiment that established the linear shear-stress relationship. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Upper Plate Area A] --> B[Velocity U to the right] B --> C[Fluid Layer dy thick
Linear Velocity Profile] C --> D[Lower Stationary Plate
No Slip Condition] D --> E[Force F applied to upper plate] E --> F[Shear Stress tau equals F over A] F --> G[Newtonian Relation tau equals mu times du by dy] ``` --- ## 4.4 Power-Law Fluid Behaviour Map A schematic representation showing how the flow behaviour index **n** controls the rheological class. ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Power Law Model tau equals K gamma dot to the n] --> CHECK{Value of n} CHECK -- n equals 1 --> NEWTON[Newtonian Fluid
K equals mu
Constant Viscosity] CHECK -- n less than 1 --> PSEUDO[Pseudoplastic
Shear Thinning
Examples Paint Blood] CHECK -- n greater than 1 --> DIL[Dilatant
Shear Thickening
Examples Cornstarch Water] ``` --- ## 4.5 Concept Map — Non-Newtonian Fluid Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Non Newtonian Fluids] --> A[Time Independent] ROOT --> B[Time Dependent] ROOT --> C[Viscoelastic] A --> A1[Shear Thinning
Pseudoplastic
n less than 1] A --> A2[Shear Thickening
Dilatant
n greater than 1] A --> A3[Bingham Plastic
Yield plus Linear] A --> A4[Herschel Bulkley
Yield plus Power Law] B --> B1[Thixotropic
Viscosity drops with time] B --> B2[Rheopectic
Viscosity rises with time] C --> C1[Maxwell Model
Spring and Dashpot in Series] C --> C2[Oldroyd B Model] C --> C3[Kelvin Voigt Model] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Newtonian fluid. Give three examples relevant to naval architecture. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A Newtonian fluid is one in which the shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear deformation, with the constant of proportionality (dynamic viscosity μ) being independent of the shear rate. Mathematically, $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ **Three examples relevant to naval architecture:** 1. **Sea water** — the principal working fluid in towing-tank resistance tests and ship model experiments. 2. **Fresh water** — used as the standard calibration fluid in ITTC-standard model testing. 3. **Air** — used in wind-tunnel tests for superstructure aerodynamic drag and sail aerodynamics. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 mark) + Formula (1 mark) + Examples (1 mark). --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between pseudoplastic and dilatant fluids with one naval/marine example each. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) | Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | |---------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------| | Power-law index | n < 1 | n > 1 | | Apparent viscosity | Decreases with shear rate | Increases with shear rate | | Behaviour under impact | Flows more easily when stirred | Becomes solid-like under impact | | Naval/marine example | Marine anti-fouling paint (sprays easily, stays thick on hull) | Highly concentrated drilling mud in offshore oil rigs under sudden pressure surges | > [!TIP] > Use a **table or a one-liner contrast** — both earn full marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Treatment of Rheological Models **(a)** Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from the Couette flow setup. State clearly the assumptions used. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** For a power-law fluid flowing between two parallel plates separated by a distance h, derive an expression for the **volumetric flow rate per unit width Q** in terms of K, n, h, and the pressure gradient $\frac{dp}{dx}$. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity **Assumptions (state in answer):** 1. The fluid is incompressible. 2. The flow is steady, laminar, and fully developed. 3. The plates are infinitely wide (edge effects neglected). 4. There is no slip at the plates (no-slip boundary condition). 5. Fluid properties (ρ, μ) are constant. 6. The velocity gradient is linear across the gap (true for Newtonian fluid in Couette flow). **Derivation:** Consider two parallel plates of area **A**, gap **dy**, upper plate moving at velocity **U**, lower plate stationary. The force required to drag the upper plate is empirically: $$F = \mu A \frac{U}{dy}$$ Dividing by A: $$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{dy}$$ Since the velocity varies linearly, the velocity gradient equals U/dy, hence: $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, with μ being the **dynamic viscosity**. Units of μ: Pa·s = N·s/m². **(7 marks distributed: 2 for setup + 2 for empirical relationship + 2 for derivation + 1 for units/significance.)** --- #### Part (b) — Volumetric Flow Rate for Power-Law Fluid Between Plates For a power-law fluid: $$\tau = K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$ For plane Poiseuille flow (flow driven by pressure gradient dp/dx), the momentum balance gives: $$\tau(y) = \frac{dp}{dx} y + C_1$$ By symmetry about the centreline (y = 0), τ(0) = 0 → C₁ = 0. Therefore: $$\tau = \frac{dp}{dx} y$$ Equating with the power-law expression: $$\frac{dp}{dx} y = -K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$ (The negative sign arises because u decreases as y moves away from the centre.) Hence: $$\frac{du}{dy} = -\left(\frac{1}{K}\frac{dp}{dx} y\right)^{1/n}$$ Integrate from y = 0 (centreline, maximum velocity u_max) to y: $$u_{max} - u(y) = \int_0^y \left(\frac{1}{K}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right) y'\right)^{1/n} dy'$$ (Assuming dp/dx < 0 for forward flow.) Using the integral identity: $$\int_0^y (y')^{1/n} dy' = \frac{n}{n+1} y^{(n+1)/n}$$ Therefore: $$u_{max} - u(y) = \left(\frac{-1}{K}\frac{dp}{dx}\right)^{1/n} \frac{n}{n+1} y^{(n+1)/n}$$ Apply the no-slip boundary condition u(h) = 0 (where y = h is the plate): $$u_{max} = \left(\frac{-1}{K}\frac{dp}{dx}\right)^{1/n} \frac{n}{n+1} h^{(n+1)/n}$$ So the velocity profile is: $$u(y) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{y}{h}\right)^{(n+1)/n}\right]$$ The **volumetric flow rate per unit width** is: $$Q = \int_0^h u(y) \, dy$$ Using $\int_0^h [1 - (y/h)^{(n+1)/n}] dy$: $$Q = u_{max} \cdot h \cdot \frac{n+1}{2n+1}$$ Substituting u_max: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{n}{2n+1} \left(\frac{-1}{K}\frac{dp}{dx}\right)^{1/n} h^{(2n+1)/n}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently **forget the negative sign** inside the bracket or **lose the factor $\frac{n+1}{2n+1}$** during the final integration. Many also **incorrectly assume parabolic profile** (n=1) and lose 2 marks. Verify by substituting n=1 to recover the classical Hagen-Poiseuille form $Q = \frac{h^3}{12\mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)$ for plane Poiseuille flow. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Treatment **(a)** What is a Bingham plastic? Derive the velocity profile for a Bingham plastic flowing in a circular pipe of radius R under a pressure gradient $\frac{\Delta P}{L}$. Clearly identify the plug-flow radius $r_0$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A power-law fluid with K = 0.4 Pa·sⁿ and n = 0.6 flows in a pipe of radius 0.05 m. If the pressure gradient is −500 Pa/m, calculate the **wall shear stress**, **apparent viscosity at the wall**, and the **maximum velocity** at the centreline. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — Bingham Plastic Velocity Profile in a Pipe A **Bingham plastic** is a fluid that behaves as a rigid solid until the shear stress exceeds a critical yield stress τ_y, after which it flows with a linear viscous response. The constitutive equation: $$\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}, \quad \text{for } \tau \geq \tau_y$$ For flow in a horizontal pipe of radius **R**, momentum balance on a cylindrical element gives: $$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ **Plug-flow radius:** The fluid does not shear until τ reaches τ_y. Setting τ(r₀) = τ_y: $$r_0 = \frac{2L \tau_y}{\Delta P}$$ For r ≥ r₀ (sheared region): $$-\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\tau(r) - \tau_y}{\mu_p} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r - 2L\tau_y}{2L \mu_p}$$ Integrating with u(R) = 0: $$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4L\mu_p}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) - \frac{\tau_y}{\mu_p}(R - r)$$ The plug velocity (constant for 0 ≤ r ≤ r₀): $$u_p = u(r_0) = \frac{\Delta P}{4L\mu_p}(R^2 - r_0^2) - \frac{\tau_y}{\mu_p}(R - r_0)$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem for Power-Law Fluid **Given:** K = 0.4 Pa·sⁿ, n = 0.6, R = 0.05 m, dp/dx = −500 Pa/m. **Step 1 — Wall Shear Stress:** For pipe flow, the wall shear stress is: $$\tau_w = \frac{R}{2}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = \frac{0.05}{2} \times 500 = 12.5 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Wall Shear Rate:** From the power-law model: $$\tau_w = K \dot{\gamma}_w^n$$ $$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{\tau_w}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{12.5}{0.4}\right)^{1/0.6}$$ Compute the ratio: 12.5 / 0.4 = 31.25 Take the logarithm: $\ln(31.25) = 3.4420$ $$\dot{\gamma}_w = \exp\left(\frac{3.4420}{0.6}\right) = \exp(5.7367) \approx 310.1 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Apparent Viscosity at the Wall:** $$\mu_{app,w} = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{12.5}{310.1} \approx 0.0403 \text{ Pa·s}$$ Or equivalently, using $\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$: $$\mu_{app,w} = 0.4 \times (310.1)^{-0.4}$$ $$\log(310.1) = 2.4914 \Rightarrow (310.1)^{-0.4} = 10^{-0.9966} \approx 0.101$$ $$\mu_{app,w} = 0.4 \times 0.101 \approx 0.0403 \text{ Pa·s} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Velocity (Centreline):** For power-law pipe flow: $$u_{max} = \frac{n}{n+1} R \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{0.6}{1.6} \times 0.05 \times 310.1$$ $$u_{max} = 0.375 \times 0.05 \times 310.1 = 5.814 \text{ m/s}$$ **Summary of Results:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Wall shear stress τ_w | **12.5 Pa** | | Apparent viscosity at wall | **0.0403 Pa·s** | | Maximum velocity at centreline | **5.81 m/s** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** > 1. **Common mistake:** Using $u_{max} = \frac{R}{2} \dot{\gamma}_w$ (this is the **Newtonian** result). For a power-law fluid, the correct multiplier is $\frac{n}{n+1}$. Lose 2 marks if wrong. > 2. **Units trap:** Always check units — K must be in **Pa·sⁿ**, not Pa·s. > 3. **Sign convention:** Some students use dp/dx = +500 Pa/m instead of −500 Pa/m, leading to a negative velocity. Use $|dp/dx|$ or be careful with sign. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids** - **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ with **μ constant** at fixed T, P. - **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ has units **m²/s** and is critical for Reynolds number scaling in towing-tank tests. - **Standard values:** μ(water, 20 °C) ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**; μ(air, 20 °C) ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**. - **Power-law model:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ — single equation governing pseudoplastic (n < 1), Newtonian (n = 1), dilatant (n > 1). - **Apparent viscosity:** $\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ — decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ for n < 1, increases for n > 1. - **Bingham plastics** have a **yield stress τ_y** — they behave as solids until τ > τ_y. Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise. - **Herschel-Bulkley** combines yield stress with power-law behaviour — most general time-independent model. - **Thixotropic fluids** recover their viscosity over time after shear removal (e.g., printing ink, marine paints). - **Rheopectic fluids** become more viscous over time under sustained shear (rare; e.g., gypsum pastes). - **Viscoelastic fluids** show both viscous flow and elastic recoil — relevant for polymer solutions, biological fluids, and some crude oils. - **Naval applications** to remember: 1. Towing tank & resistance tests → Newtonian assumption. 2. LNG sloshing → Non-Newtonian near boiling. 3. Crude oil spills → Viscoelastic. 4. Marine paints → Shear-thinning. 5. Submarine hydraulics → Newtonian hydraulic oils. - **Decision tree:** Newtonian ↔ linear τ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ ↔ no yield ↔ no time-dependence. - **Quick checks:** Substitute n = 1 in any power-law formula → must reduce to Newtonian form. - **Units trap:** K has units **Pa·sⁿ**, not Pa·s. Always include the exponent n in the unit label. - **Plug flow:** Occurs in Bingham plastics in pipes — central core moves as rigid body. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In board examinations, always: > 1. Define the model fluid type **first**. > 2. State the **constitutive equation** explicitly. > 3. List **assumptions** clearly in derivations. > 4. Verify limiting cases (set n = 1 for power-law, τ_y = 0 for Bingham). > 5. Connect to at least one **naval/marine application** in any 14-mark question to score the **application/marks-for-understanding** component that KTU examiners reward.

topics.title: Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscosity

**Viscosity (μ)** is the physical property of a fluid that quantifies its internal resistance to deformation (flow) under an applied shear stress. Mathematically, it is the constant of proportionality in **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at a fluid layer (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\mu$ = **Dynamic (absolute) viscosity** (in **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (in **s⁻¹**)

The **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{units: m}^2/\text{s or Stokes (St)}]$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid mass density (in **kg/m³**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Naval Architecture Context**
> Viscosity governs **boundary layer development** on a ship's hull, **frictional resistance (viscous drag)** in model testing, **friction factor** selection in turbulent pipe flow for marine piping systems, and **lubrication behaviour** of marine engine oils and stern-tube lubricants. The **Reynolds number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ is the single most important dimensionless number for naval architects, and it is built directly on viscosity.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine pouring two liquids side-by-side: **honey** and **water**.

- The **honey flows slowly** because its molecules cling to one another strongly (high internal friction = high viscosity).
- The **water flows rapidly** because its molecules slide past each other easily (low internal friction = low viscosity).

Now, warm the honey → it flows much faster. Why? Because the **molecules gain thermal energy, move farther apart, and the cohesive bonds weaken** → viscosity drops sharply with temperature.

For **gases** (like air), the opposite happens: heat them up → viscosity **increases**, because molecules collide more frequently and momentum transfer between layers increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> - **Liquids → Viscosity ↓ as Temperature ↑** (Think: cold honey vs hot honey)
> - **Gases → Viscosity ↑ as Temperature ↑** (Think: heated air thickens in turbulent eddies)
> - **Pressure ↑ → Viscosity ↑ for both** (molecules packed closer → more resistance)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | **Pa·s (N·s/m²)** | poise (P) | 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **m²/s** | stokes (St) | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | g/cm³ | 1 kg/m³ = 10⁻³ g/cm³ |
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | bar | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb:** At **20 °C**, water has $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and air has $\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. For marine fuels (IFO 380 at 50 °C), $\mu \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa·s — about **2000× thicker than water** at the same temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity of Liquids

Liquids are **incompressible** at moderate pressures, and their molecules are tightly packed. Cohesive (van der Waals) forces dominate the momentum transport.

- As **temperature rises**, molecules gain kinetic energy.
- This **overcomes the cohesive forces** holding the molecules together.
- Result: Layers slide more easily → **μ decreases exponentially with T**.

The most widely used empirical correlation is the **Andrade Equation (1930)**:

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{E_a / (RT)}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln \mu = \ln A + \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

where:
- $A$ = Pre-exponential constant (Pa·s), material-specific
- $E_a$ = **Activation energy** for viscous flow (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exponential?** The semi-log plot of $\ln \mu$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line. This linearisation is **frequently asked in KTU 2024 numerical problems**.

A useful **two-point form** (similar to Sutherland's) for liquids is:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

### 2.2 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity of Gases

Unlike liquids, gases are **sparse** — molecules are far apart and rarely interact except through collisions. The dominant momentum-transfer mechanism is **molecular collisions**, not cohesive forces.

- As **T increases**, average molecular velocity $\bar{c} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ increases.
- The mean free path also changes, but the **collision frequency increases** more significantly.
- Result: **Momentum transfer between adjacent layers increases → μ increases**.

The classical result from **Kinetic Theory of Gases** (assuming rigid spheres) is:

$$\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$$

But this over-predicts growth at high T. **Sutherland's Formula (1893)** corrects it with a constant $S$ (Sutherland's constant):

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

where:
- $T_0$ = Reference temperature (commonly **273.15 K** or **288.15 K**)
- $\mu_0$ = Viscosity at $T_0$
- $S$ = **Sutherland's constant** (K), gas-specific

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Values for Air:**
> - $T_0 = 273.15$ K, $\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $S = 110.4$ K
> - This is the **standard atmospheric reference** used in most KTU numericals and in aerodynamic/marine propeller calculations.

### 2.3 Effect of Pressure on Viscosity

**For Liquids:**
- Liquids are nearly incompressible, but high pressure compresses them slightly, increasing $\rho$ and bringing molecules closer.
- This **increases the cohesive forces** → μ increases.
- The **Barus Equation (1893)** gives:

$$\mu_P = \mu_0 \cdot e^{\alpha P}$$

where $\alpha$ = Viscosity-pressure coefficient (Pa⁻¹), typically $1 \times 10^{-8}$ to $1 \times 10^{-9}$ Pa⁻¹ for lubricating oils.

- A more accurate form (Roelands, 1966) used for **high-pressure elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** in marine stern tubes:

$$\log_{10}(\mu + 0.7) = \log_{10}(\mu_0 + 0.7) + \frac{\log_{10}(\mu_P + 0.7) - \log_{10}(\mu_0 + 0.7})}{\log_{10}(P + P_R)} \cdot \log_{10}(P + P_R)$$

**For Gases:**
- Pressure has **minimal effect** on gas viscosity at low-to-moderate pressures (up to ~10 atm).
- This is because $\mu$ depends on **collision dynamics and mean free path**, both of which scale together as density changes.
- However, at very high pressures (e.g., in **submarine ballast systems** at great depths or **high-pressure gas compressors**), gas viscosity **increases with pressure**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity (definition) | $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 2 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\mu = A \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)}$ | Liquids – Andrade's equation | $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 4 | $\ln(\mu_2/\mu_1) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Liquids – two-point form | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \dfrac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$ | Gases – Sutherland's formula | $S$ in K |
| 6 | $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ | Gases – simple kinetic theory (approximate) | $M$ = molar mass |
| 7 | $\mu_P = \mu_0 \cdot e^{\alpha P}$ | Liquids – Barus equation (pressure) | $\alpha$ in Pa⁻¹ |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (uses viscosity) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | VI (Viscosity Index) | Lubricant classification (ASTM D2270) | Empirical, 0–100+ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

1. **Hull Frictional Resistance:** Skin friction coefficient $C_f \propto Re^{-0.2}$ for turbulent flow. A 10 % error in kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (through wrong temperature) can shift the **Effective Power (EHP)** prediction by 5–8 %, which is significant in ship design.
2. **Marine Lubricants:** The **Viscosity Index (VI)** quantifies how little a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature. A high-VI oil (e.g., **150**) is preferred for **stern-tube and main-engine bearings**.
3. **Fuel Oil Pumping:** Heavy fuel oil (HFO, IFO 380) must be heated to **~50–80 °C** to reduce viscosity below **380 cSt** (the "380" in the grade name) for proper atomisation in marine boilers and main engines.
4. **Model Testing (CFD/ITTC):** Reynolds scaling between model and ship uses kinematic viscosity. ITTC-78 correlation: $C_{F,ship} = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Sutherland's Formula from Kinetic Theory

We start from the **Chapman-Enskog kinetic theory** for a dilute monatomic gas. The dynamic viscosity is given by:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c} \lambda$$

where:
- $\bar{c}$ = Mean molecular speed = $\sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$
- $\lambda$ = Mean free path = $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 n}$
- $d$ = Effective molecular diameter
- $n$ = Number density = $\dfrac{N_A \rho}{M}$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\bar{c}$:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 n}$$

**Step 2:** Express density in terms of pressure using ideal gas law $P = \rho R T / M$:

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute density and number density $n = P/(k_B T)$:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 \cdot P/(k_B T)}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify (the $P$ terms cancel, confirming low-pressure independence):

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{M}{RT} \cdot \frac{k_B T}{P} \cdot \frac{PM}{RT} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2}$$

After algebraic reduction (collecting constants and noting that $R = N_A k_B$):

$$\mu = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{\frac{M R T}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the simple kinetic theory result: $\mu_0 \propto \sqrt{T}/d^2$. Now **Sutherland's correction** accounts for the fact that the effective collision diameter $d$ decreases weakly with temperature as molecules become more energetic and penetrate each other's force fields:

$$d^2(T) = d_\infty^2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{T}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute this into the simple theory expression. Taking a reference state ($T_0, \mu_0$) and the general state ($T, \mu$):

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \sqrt{\frac{T}{T_0}} \cdot \frac{1 + S/T_0}{1 + S/T}$$

**Step 7:** To get the standard **$3/2$ power** form, multiply and divide appropriately (Sutherland's original empirical modification):

$$\boxed{\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}}$$

This is the final form. The physical interpretation: the $3/2$ power emerges from the combination of $\sqrt{T}$ velocity growth and the weaker $1/(1+S/T)$ decay of the effective collision cross-section.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 – Gas Viscosity at Ship-Operating Altitude

> **Problem:** A naval reconnaissance drone operates at an altitude where air temperature is **−20 °C**. Using Sutherland's formula with $T_0 = 273.15$ K, $\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, and $S = 110.4$ K, find the dynamic viscosity of air.

**Step 1 – Convert to Kelvin:**

$$T = -20 + 273.15 = 253.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 – Apply Sutherland's formula:**

$$\mu = \mu_0 \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

**Step 3 – Compute each factor:**

$$\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} = \left(\frac{253.15}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (0.9268)^{1.5}$$

Computing:

$$\ln(0.9268) = -0.0760, \quad 1.5 \times (-0.0760) = -0.1140$$

$$e^{-0.1140} = 0.8922$$

**Step 4 – Compute the second factor:**

$$\frac{T_0 + S}{T + S} = \frac{273.15 + 110.4}{253.15 + 110.4} = \frac{383.55}{363.55} = 1.0550$$

**Step 5 – Multiply:**

$$\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.8922 \times 1.0550$$

$$\mu = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.9413 = 1.615 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Result:** $\mu \approx 1.62 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s — about **6 % lower** than at sea-level standard conditions. This directly affects drag and Reynolds number calculations for the drone.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 – Lubricating Oil Viscosity vs Temperature

> **Problem:** A marine cylinder oil has $\mu_1 = 0.15$ Pa·s at $T_1 = 40$ °C and $\mu_2 = 0.04$ Pa·s at $T_2 = 100$ °C. Calculate the activation energy $E_a$ and predict the viscosity at $T_3 = 150$ °C.

**Step 1 – Convert to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 313.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 373.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_3 = 423.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the two-point Andrade form:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 – Compute the LHS:**

$$\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} = \frac{0.04}{0.15} = 0.2667$$

$$\ln(0.2667) = -1.3218$$

**Step 4 – Compute the temperature bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{313.15}$$

$$= 2.6799 \times 10^{-3} - 3.1933 \times 10^{-3} = -5.1340 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 – Solve for $E_a$:**

$$E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(\mu_2/\mu_1)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)} = 8.314 \cdot \frac{-1.3218}{-5.1340 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 8.314 \times 2574.6 = 21{,}405 \text{ J/mol} \approx 21.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 – Predict $\mu_3$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_3}{\mu_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_3} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_3} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{423.15} - \frac{1}{313.15} = 2.3633 \times 10^{-3} - 3.1933 \times 10^{-3} = -8.3000 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\ln(\mu_3/\mu_1) = \frac{21{,}405}{8.314} \times (-8.3000 \times 10^{-4}) = 2574.6 \times (-8.3000 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.137$$

$$\mu_3/\mu_1 = e^{-2.137} = 0.1179$$

$$\mu_3 = 0.15 \times 0.1179 = 0.0177 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Result:** At 150 °C, viscosity drops to **0.0177 Pa·s** (≈ 88 % reduction from 40 °C). This is why **engine oils lose load-carrying capacity** at high temperatures and why **multi-grade oils** with **Viscosity Index Improvers (VIIs)** are essential.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Liquids | Gases |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular spacing | Close (~$10^{-10}$ m) | Far (~$10^{-7}$ m) |
| Dominant force | Cohesive (van der Waals) | Collisional |
| Effect of $\uparrow T$ | $\mu \downarrow$ (exponentially) | $\mu \uparrow$ (mildly) |
| Effect of $\uparrow P$ | $\mu \uparrow$ (exponentially) | $\mu \approx$ const (low P) |
| Best correlation | Andrade (or Vogel) | Sutherland (or Chapman-Enskog) |
| Typical $E_a$ range | 10–40 kJ/mol | N/A (no $E_a$ in same sense) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Viscosity Behaviour Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fluid Sample] --> B{Phase State?}
    B -- Liquid --> C[Andrade Equation]
    B -- Gas --> D[Sutherland Formula]
    C --> E[ln mu vs 1 over T linear plot]
    D --> F[T to power 3 over 2 times T0 plus S bracket]
    C --> G[Viscosity mu decreases with T]
    D --> H[Viscosity mu increases with T]
    G --> I[Marine Lubricant Selection]
    F --> J[Aerodynamic Drag Calculation]
    I --> K[Barus Equation for High Pressure]
    J --> L[Pressure Effect at Very High P]
    K --> M[Stern Tube and Bearing Design]
    L --> N[Submarine Deep Sea System]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Naval Viscosity Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    In1[Service Temperature] --> Engine[Engine Block]
    In2[Service Pressure] --> Engine
    In3[Fluid Type Fuel or Lubricant] --> Engine
    Engine --> Sel{Fuel or Lubricant?}
    Sel -- Lubricant --> VisIdx[Viscosity Index VI Lookup]
    Sel -- Fuel --> HeatReq[Required Heating Power Q dot]
    VisIdx --> Grade[Select ISO VG Grade]
    Grade --> VisT[Apply Andrade to get mu at T]
    HeatReq --> VisT
    VisT --> ReCheck[Reynolds Number Verification]
    ReCheck --> PumpSpec[Pump and Pipe Sizing]
    ReCheck --> Bearing[Boundary Film Thickness in Bearings]
```

### 4.3 Qualitative Trend Curves (Descriptive Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Temperature Increase] --> B[Liquid: Viscosity Down]
    A --> C[Gas: Viscosity Up]
    D[Pressure Increase] --> E[Liquid: Viscosity Up]
    D --> F[Gas: Viscosity Roughly Constant]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read these diagrams in KTU exams:** The arrows are directional cause-effect relationships. When asked to "explain the effect", trace the path from the cause (T or P) through the governing equation to the physical outcome, citing the equation symbol-by-symbol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**"With the help of a suitable graph, explain the effect of temperature on the dynamic viscosity of liquids and gases."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **For Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** with increase in temperature because cohesive (van der Waals) forces between closely packed molecules are weakened by thermal agitation. The variation is **exponential**, given by Andrade's equation $\mu = A \cdot e^{E_a/(RT)}$.
- **For Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with increase in temperature because molecular collision frequency and momentum transfer between adjacent layers rise with temperature. The variation follows Sutherland's formula $\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \dfrac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$.
- **Graph:** Two curves diverging from a common point — one (liquid) falling, the other (gas) rising, both relatively gentle in slope.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**"State Sutherland's formula for the viscosity of a gas. What is the physical significance of Sutherland's constant?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Statement (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

- **Physical significance of $S$ (2 marks):** Sutherland's constant $S$ accounts for the **inter-molecular force field** between gas molecules. It is the temperature at which the effective molecular collision cross-section would theoretically be reduced to **zero** (or equivalently, where $\mu \propto T^{1/2}$ behaviour transitions into the $T^{3/2}$ behaviour). For air, $S = 110.4$ K.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive Sutherland's formula starting from the kinetic theory expression for dynamic viscosity of a gas. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The viscosity of air at 27 °C is $1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Using Sutherland's formula ($T_0 = 273.15$ K, $\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $S = 110.4$ K), determine the temperature at which the viscosity becomes $2.10 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Starting point (1 mark):**
From kinetic theory, dynamic viscosity is:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{3} \rho \bar{c} \lambda$$

**Step 2 — Express mean speed and mean free path (1 mark):**

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 n}$$

**Step 3 — Use ideal gas relations (1 mark):**
$\rho = PM/(RT)$ and $n = P/(k_B T)$. Substituting and simplifying, the $P$ cancels, giving:

$$\mu = \frac{2}{3 \pi d^2} \sqrt{\frac{MRT}{\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Sutherland's correction (2 marks):**
The effective collision cross-section depends on temperature as molecules penetrate each other's force fields:

$$d^2(T) = d_\infty^2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Obtain the ratio (1 mark):**
Forming the ratio at $(T, \mu)$ and $(T_0, \mu_0)$ and combining the $\sqrt{T}$ and $1/(1+S/T)$ dependencies:

$$\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{T_0 + S}{T + S}$$

**Step 6 — State assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Monoatomic, dilute gas; (ii) Rigid spherical molecules modified by Sutherland's soft-sphere correction; (iii) Local thermodynamic equilibrium; (iv) No bulk motion effects (small gradients).

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Substitute known values (2 marks):**

$$2.10 \times 10^{-5} = 1.716 \times 10^{-5} \left(\frac{T}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{273.15 + 110.4}{T + 110.4}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio (1 mark):**

$$\frac{2.10}{1.716} = 1.2238 = \left(\frac{T}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{383.55}{T + 110.4}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange (1 mark):**

$$\left(\frac{T}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = 1.2238 \cdot \frac{T + 110.4}{383.55} = 0.003191 \, (T + 110.4)$$

**Step 4 — Solve iteratively (2 marks):**
Try $T = 330$ K:

$$\left(\frac{330}{273.15}\right)^{3/2} = (1.2081)^{1.5} = e^{1.5 \times \ln 1.2081} = e^{1.5 \times 0.1891} = e^{0.2837} = 1.328$$

RHS: $0.003191 \times (330 + 110.4) = 0.003191 \times 440.4 = 1.405$. LHS < RHS, try higher T.

Try $T = 340$ K:
LHS: $(340/273.15)^{1.5} = (1.2447)^{1.5} = e^{0.2188} = 1.245 \cdot \text{(recheck)}$

Better: $(1.2447)^{1.5}$: $\ln(1.2447) = 0.2188$, $1.5 \times 0.2188 = 0.3282$, $e^{0.3282} = 1.388$.
RHS: $0.003191 \times 450.4 = 1.437$. LHS < RHS still.

Try $T = 350$ K: $(350/273.15)^{1.5} = (1.2813)^{1.5} = e^{0.3730} = 1.452$.
RHS: $0.003191 \times 460.4 = 1.469$. Very close.

**Answer: $T \approx 350$ K = 76.85 °C** (1 mark).

**Step 5 — Engineering significance (1 mark):** At 77 °C — typical of engine-room or supercharger inlet — air viscosity is 22 % higher than at STP. This directly affects Reynolds number, drag, and compressor performance; neglecting it in marine propeller or engine intake design introduces ~3–5 % error in mass-flow predictions.

---

**Q4. Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the effect of pressure on the viscosity of liquids and gases. State the Barus equation and identify **two marine engineering scenarios** where this effect is critical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine lube oil has dynamic viscosity 0.18 Pa·s at 35 °C and 0.085 Pa·s at 75 °C. Use Andrade's equation to find (i) the activation energy $E_a$ and (ii) the viscosity at 120 °C. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Liquids (2 marks):**
Increasing pressure **slightly compresses** the liquid, increasing density and bringing molecules closer. This strengthens cohesive forces and so **viscosity increases** (typically **exponentially** with pressure).

**Step 2 — Gases (1 mark):**
At low-to-moderate pressures (< 10 atm), gas viscosity is **essentially independent of pressure** because mean free path and number density scale oppositely. Only at very high pressures (deep submarine applications, high-pressure compressors) does gas viscosity rise with pressure.

**Step 3 — Barus equation (2 marks):**

$$\mu_P = \mu_0 \cdot e^{\alpha P}$$

where $\alpha$ = pressure-viscosity coefficient (Pa⁻¹), typical values $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-9}$ Pa⁻¹ for lube oils.

**Step 4 — Marine scenarios (2 marks):**
(i) **Stern-tube and main-engine bearings** — film thickness in elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL) is set by high pressure (often >1 GPa in the contact zone). Wrong $\alpha$ ⇒ wrong film thickness ⇒ bearing failure.
(ii) **Hydraulic systems of submarines and offshore rigs** — operating pressures 200–700 bar mean $\mu$ can be 2–3× higher than at atmospheric; flow-rate and pump-power calculations must use the high-pressure $\mu$.

---

**Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark):**
$T_1 = 308.15$ K, $T_2 = 348.15$ K, $T_3 = 393.15$ K.

**Step 2 — Apply the two-point Andrade form (1 mark):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{0.085}{0.18}\right) = \frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{348.15} - \frac{1}{308.15}\right)$$

$$\ln(0.4722) = -0.7506$$

**Step 3 — Compute temperature bracket (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{348.15} - \frac{1}{308.15} = (2.8723 - 3.2452) \times 10^{-3} = -3.729 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_a$ (1 mark):**

$$E_a = 8.314 \times \frac{-0.7506}{-3.729 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.314 \times 2013 = 16{,}737 \text{ J/mol} \approx 16.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Predict $\mu_3$ at 120 °C (2 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{T_3} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{393.15} - \frac{1}{308.15} = (2.5437 - 3.2452) \times 10^{-3} = -7.015 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_3}{0.18}\right) = \frac{16737}{8.314} \times (-7.015 \times 10^{-4}) = 2013 \times (-7.015 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.4122$$

$$\mu_3/0.18 = e^{-1.4122} = 0.2437$$

$$\mu_3 = 0.18 \times 0.2437 = 0.0439 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 6 — Verify by alternative path (1 mark):** From the data, $E_a = 16.7$ kJ/mol is consistent with typical marine lube oils (15–25 kJ/mol). The viscosity at 120 °C (**0.044 Pa·s**) is about 4× lower than at 35 °C — illustrating why **VI improvers** are blended into modern multi-grade marine oils.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the Kelvin conversion** when applying Andrade/Sutherland — instantly loses 1–2 marks in any numerical.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$** in Reynolds number problems — $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is needed for $Re = VL/\nu$ because the velocity and length are the experimental observables, not the stress.
> 3. **Mixing up the trends** (liquids down, gases up with T) — a classic 3-mark question trap. Always state the trend **before** writing the equation.
> 4. **Forgetting units in final answers** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units consistently. Always write "Pa·s", "m²/s", or "K".
> 5. **Dropping the $T_0 + S$ factor** in Sutherland's formula — many students write only the $3/2$ power and lose 2–3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscosity** is the fluid's resistance to shear deformation; $\mu$ = dynamic (Pa·s), $\nu = \mu/\rho$ = kinematic (m²/s).
- **Liquids:** $\mu \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (Andrade: $\mu = A e^{E_a/RT}$). Pressure **increases** $\mu$ (Barus: $\mu_P = \mu_0 e^{\alpha P}$).
- **Gases:** $\mu \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (Sutherland: $3/2$ power with $S$ correction). Pressure has negligible effect at low/moderate $P$.
- **Two-point Andrade** is the most testable form: $\ln(\mu_2/\mu_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- **Sutherland's standard air constants:** $T_0 = 273.15$ K, $\mu_0 = 1.716 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $S = 110.4$ K.
- **Sutherland's constant $S$** represents the temperature at which the simple $\sqrt{T}$ kinetic theory transitions to the $3/2$ power law.
- **Andrade's plot:** $\ln \mu$ vs $1/T$ is a straight line with slope $E_a/R$.
- **Marine relevance:** Hull friction ($C_f \propto Re^{-0.2}$), ITTC-78 line, fuel-oil pre-heating (HFO 380 cSt at 50 °C), VI for lubricants, EHL film thickness in bearings.
- **Reynolds number $Re = \rho V L/\mu$** is the central dimensionless group built on viscosity; misjudging $\mu$ from wrong $T$ corrupts viscous-resistance predictions by up to 5–8 %.
- **Viscosity Index (VI):** Empirical 0–100+ measure of a lubricant's $T$-stability. High-VI oils are critical for **main engine** and **stern-tube** lubrication.
- **Exam tactic:** Always state (a) trend, (b) governing equation, (c) physical reason, (d) naval/marine application — to maximise marks in any "explain" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Viscosity on Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Viscosity on Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In naval hydrodynamics, the **Effect of Viscosity on Floating Bodies** describes the set of resistive (drag) forces that arise because real fluids (water, air) possess **internal friction** (viscosity). When a floating body — such as a ship, pontoon, or barge — moves through a viscous fluid, two principal viscous phenomena occur:

1. **Tangential (shear) stresses** develop along the wetted surface, producing **frictional resistance** ($R_F$).
2. **Normal (pressure) stresses** become asymmetric between the bow and stern of the body, producing **viscous pressure resistance** ($R_{VP}$), also called eddy-making or form resistance.

The total viscous resistance is therefore:

$$R_V = R_F + R_{VP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Viscosity influences not only the drag, but also the **boundary layer growth**, the **point of transition** from laminar to turbulent flow, the **wake geometry**, the **propulsive efficiency**, and the **scaling laws** used in model-to-ship correlation. This topic is foundational to the resistance and powering chapter that follows.

## 1.2 Physical Constants (Memorize for Exams)

| Property | Symbol | Fresh Water (20 °C) | Sea Water (20 °C) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Density | $\rho$ | **998 kg/m³** | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s | 1.08 × 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | **1.00 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | **1.20 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the parameter that enters the Reynolds number, which is the single most important dimensionless group in viscous flow analysis.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are walking briskly through a **crowded marketplace** (think of a market in Kochi on a Saturday). Your body has to push past people — that "push-back" you feel is the **viscous resistance** of the crowd.

- If you walk **slowly and smoothly**, people flow around you like a **laminar stream** — you feel a steady, gentle drag.
- If you sprint, you leave a **chaotic wake** behind you, and a few people bump into your back and sides harder — that is **turbulent viscous resistance** plus **pressure drag** from the disturbed crowd.
- A **sleek, streamlined** shopper (long coat, no bags) passes through the crowd with much less disturbance than a **box-shaped** shopper carrying a wide suitcase.

A ship behaves exactly the same way in water. The **wetted hull surface** is the "body," the **seawater** is the "crowd," and the **viscosity** is the friction between the crowd members. The faster the ship moves and the larger the hull, the more the crowd "complains" — and the more horsepower the propeller must supply to overcome this resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's mnemonic:** "Viscous drag = Surface area + Speed² + Viscosity." Larger, faster, dirtier-floated hulls pay a higher viscous penalty.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boundary Layer — Where Viscosity "Lives"

Viscosity is not a property of the freestream; it acts only in a **thin layer adjacent to the hull** called the **boundary layer** (Prandtl, 1904). Outside this layer the flow is essentially inviscid.

Structure of the boundary layer on a flat plate / ship hull:

| Zone | Thickness | Flow Character | Velocity Profile |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Leading edge → $x_{tr}$ | Growing | **Laminar** | Smooth, parabolic |
| $x_{tr}$ → Trailing edge | Growing rapidly | **Turbulent** | Fuller, with steep gradient near wall |
| At wall (within turbulent BL) | $\sim 10^{-5}$ m | **Viscous sublayer** | Linear, $u/U \propto y$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ship-sized Reynolds numbers ($R_n = 10^8$ to $10^9$), the boundary layer on most of the hull is **turbulent from the bow**. A small laminar zone exists only on the forward 1–2 % of the length in well-designed hulls.

## 2.2 Components of Total Resistance on a Floating Body

When a ship advances at velocity $V$, the total resistance is conventionally decomposed (Hughes, 1972; ITTC 1978):

$$R_T = R_F \;+\; R_{VP} \;+\; R_W \;+\; R_{AA} \;+\; R_{APP}$$

| Symbol | Component | Physical Cause | Dimensionless Group |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $R_F$ | Frictional resistance | Tangential viscous shear | Reynolds number $R_n$ |
| $R_{VP}$ | Viscous pressure resistance | Asymmetric normal pressure from BL / wake | $R_n$ |
| $R_W$ | Wave-making resistance | Gravity-wave generation | Froude number $F_n$ |
| $R_{AA}$ | Air resistance | Wind drag on superstructure | — |
| $R_{APP}$ | Appendage resistance | Rudder, bilge keel, brackets, shaft struts | $R_n$ |

The **viscous portion** that concerns this topic is:

$$R_V = R_F + R_{VP} + R_{APP}$$

## 2.3 Frictional Resistance — The Core Viscous Quantity

The shear stress at any point on the wetted surface is integrated over the **wetted surface area** $S$ to yield frictional resistance:

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V^2\, S\, C_F$$

A **frictional resistance coefficient** $C_F$ is then defined such that the formula above holds:

$$C_F \;=\; \frac{R_F}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S}$$

$C_F$ depends on the **Reynolds number**:

$$R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

where:
- $V$ = ship speed (m/s)
- $L$ = length between perpendiculars, $L_{PP}$ (m) — or length of run for model
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of water (m²/s)

## 2.4 Friction-Line Formulas (Empirical)

Several internationally accepted formulas relate $C_F$ to $R_n$. The KTU syllabus and ITTC 1978 recommended procedure use the **ITTC-57 correlation**:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

Two other standard formulas examiners may quote:

**Schoenherr (1932):**

$$\frac{0.242}{\sqrt{C_F}} = \log_{10}(R_n \cdot C_F)$$

**Prandtl–Schlichting (turbulent BL on flat plate):**

$$C_F = \frac{0.455}{(\log_{10} R_n)^{2.58}} \;-\; \frac{A}{R_n}$$

where $A \approx 1700$ for fully turbulent flow (the subtracted term accounts for the small laminar leading-edge portion).

## 2.5 Form Factor and the Viscous Pressure Component

The **total viscous resistance** is larger than the frictional resistance by a factor $1+k$:

$$R_V = (1+k)\,R_F$$

$$C_V = (1+k)\,C_F$$

The non-dimensional **form factor** $k$ captures the contribution of **viscous pressure drag** arising from three-dimensional hull shape, stern flow separation, and wake unsteadiness. Typical merchant-ship values:

| Hull Type | Form Factor $k$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Fine container ship | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| Tanker / bulk carrier (full form) | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Tug / trawler (bluff stern) | 0.30 – 0.50 |

> [!NOTE]
> The form factor **does not change with speed** (for a given hull) — it is a pure shape property. This is the basis of the **Prohaska method** used in KTU-typical model-test analysis.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Viscosity Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| Ship resistance prediction | Inputs to EHP / BHP calculations |
| Hull-form optimisation | Minimising $C_V$ via fair stern lines |
| Model → full-scale correlation | Reynolds-scaling friction line |
| Anti-fouling coating selection | Affects turbulent BL and roughness allowance |
| Wake fraction ($w$) and thrust deduction ($t$) | Determines propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ |
| IMO EEDI / EEXI compliance | Lower $R_V$ ⇒ lower emissions |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / When Used |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_n = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number for ship or model |
| 2 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$ | Froude number (wave-making regime) |
| 3 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-57 friction-line coefficient |
| 4 | $\dfrac{0.242}{\sqrt{C_F}} = \log_{10}(R_n C_F)$ | Schoenherr transcendental formula |
| 5 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.455}{(\log_{10} R_n)^{2.58}} - \dfrac{A}{R_n}$ | Prandtl–Schlichting (flat plate) |
| 6 | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F$ | Frictional resistance (N) |
| 7 | $C_V = (1+k)\,C_F$ | Total viscous coefficient |
| 8 | $R_V = (1+k)\,R_F$ | Total viscous resistance (N) |
| 9 | $R_T = R_F + R_{VP} + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{APP}$ | Decomposition of total resistance |
| 10 | $\Delta C_F = \left[\dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} - \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_{nS} - 2)^2}\right]$ | Roughness / correlation allowance |
| 11 | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | Effective power (W) |
| 12 | $P_B = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_H \eta_R \eta_S \eta_G}$ | Brake power via propulsive coefficients |

> [!TIP]
> All formulas above are **exam-eligible** under KTU 2024 Module 1 outcomes CO1 and CO2. Use them directly in the **derivation / numerical** parts of Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number for a Floating Body

We start from the **definition of Reynolds number** — the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a flow.

**Step 1 — Identify the characteristic scales.**

For a floating body of length $L$ moving at velocity $V$ through a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

- Inertial force per unit volume $\sim \rho V^2 / L$
- Viscous force per unit volume $\sim \mu V / L^2$

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{\text{Inertial force}}{\text{Viscous force}} \\
    &= \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} \\
    &= \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L} \\
    &= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert dynamic to kinematic viscosity.**

Using $\nu = \mu / \rho$, the result simplifies to the form universally used in naval architecture:

$$\boxed{\;R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

- $R_n \to \text{small}$ ( $< 5 \times 10^5$): laminar BL, low drag.
- $R_n \to \text{large}$ ( $> 10^7$): turbulent BL, drag scales as $V^{1.72}$ to $V^2$.
- For a model of scale ratio $\lambda = L_S / L_M$ at Froude-similar speed $V_M = V_S / \sqrt{\lambda}$, the model Reynolds number is lower by a factor $\sim \sqrt{\lambda}$, hence **friction is not automatically scaled** — this is the **Reynolds-scale problem** central to model testing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Frictional Resistance from the Boundary-Layer Integral

Consider a 2-D flat plate of length $L$ and unit span, with a turbulent boundary layer of thickness $\delta(x)$.

**Step 1 — Wall shear stress (Blasius-type local coefficient).**

At a position $x$ from the leading edge, the local skin-friction coefficient is:

$$c_f(x) = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2} = \frac{0.0592}{R_{n,x}^{1/5}}, \quad R_{n,x} = \frac{Vx}{\nu}$$

**Step 2 — Total drag per unit span by integration.**

$$R_F' = b \int_0^L \tau_w\,dx = \int_0^L \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 c_f(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_F' &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \int_0^L \frac{0.0592}{(Vx/\nu)^{1/5}} \, dx \\
     &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot 0.0592 \left(\frac{\nu}{V}\right)^{0.2} \int_0^L x^{-0.2}\,dx \\
     &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot 0.0592 \left(\frac{\nu}{V}\right)^{0.2} \left[\frac{x^{0.8}}{0.8}\right]_0^L \\
     &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot 0.072\, L \left(\frac{\nu}{V L}\right)^{0.2} \\
     &= 0.072 \,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 L \cdot R_n^{-0.2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express as the standard frictional resistance formula.**

Dividing by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S$ (with $S = bL$, $b=1$ here):

$$C_F = \frac{0.072}{R_n^{0.2}}$$

**Step 5 — Match the ITTC-57 line.**

The ITTC-57 correlation $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$ is fitted to the same physical curve over the range $10^6 < R_n < 10^9$, valid for ship-sized applications.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **theoretical backbone** KTU examiners expect when a "derivation" sub-part appears. Memorise the five steps and the final boxed form.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Frictional Resistance of a Tanker

> **Problem:** A VLCC of length $L_{PP} = 320$ m, wetted surface $S = 28\,000$ m², moves at service speed $V = 15.5$ knots in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). Estimate the frictional resistance using ITTC-57.

**Step 1 — Convert knots to m/s.**

$$V = 15.5 \times 0.5144 = 7.973 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{V L_{PP}}{\nu} = \frac{7.973 \times 320}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} \\
    &= \frac{2551.4}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.126 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_F$ using ITTC-57.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_n &= \log_{10}(2.126 \times 10^{9}) = 9.3274 \\
\log_{10} R_n - 2 &= 7.3274 \\
(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2 &= 53.69 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{53.69} = 1.397 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute frictional resistance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.973)^2 \times 28\,000 \times 1.397 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 63.57 \times 28\,000 \times 1.397 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 63.57 = 32\,580 \\
    &= 32\,580 \times 28\,000 = 9.122 \times 10^{8} \\
    &= 9.122 \times 10^{8} \times 1.397 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 1.274 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \\
    &= 1274 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — State the answer with the right units.**

$$\boxed{R_F \;\approx\; 1.27 \text{ MN} \;\;(\text{or } 1274 \text{ kN})}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Friction Formulas at a Glance

| $R_n$ | ITTC-57 $C_F$ | Schoenherr $C_F$ | Prandtl–Schlichting $C_F$ | Remarks |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $10^6$ | $4.13 \times 10^{-3}$ | $4.10 \times 10^{-3}$ | $4.10 \times 10^{-3}$ | All three converge |
| $10^7$ | $2.78 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.78 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.80 \times 10^{-3}$ | Divergence < 1 % |
| $10^8$ | $1.82 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.84 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.83 \times 10^{-3}$ | Within 2 % |
| $10^9$ | $1.21 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.22 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ | ITTC fits ship data best |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $R_n < 10^6$, all formulas need a laminar-flow correction. For $R_n > 10^9$, ITTC-57 is the **standard ITTC-recommended** line, used for ship-scale friction extrapolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Resistance Decomposition & Viscosity Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> D1[Flow Separation at Stern]
    D --> D2[Wake Asymmetry]
    D --> D3[Captured by Form Factor k]

    F --> F1[Wind Drag on Superstructure]
```
