<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Reynolds number as applied to a floating body and state its physical significance.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds number $R_n$ is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces acting on a floating body. For a ship or model of length $L$ moving at speed $V$ in a fluid of kinematic viscosity $\nu$:

$$R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

**Physical significance:**
- It characterises the **flow regime** in the boundary layer — small $R_n$ implies laminar flow, large $R_n$ implies turbulent flow.
- It is the **scaling parameter for frictional resistance** — viscous drag depends almost exclusively on $R_n$ for a given shape.
- It governs the **Reynolds-similarity law** in model testing: two geometrically similar hulls are dynamically similar in viscous flow only when their $R_n$ are equal.
- For a ship, $R_n$ is typically of order **$10^8$ to $10^9$**, placing the boundary layer firmly in the turbulent regime.

> [!NOTE]
> 3-mark answers should pair the **formula** with **two crisp points of significance**. Avoid lengthy derivations at this level.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between frictional resistance and wave-making resistance of a ship.
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Frictional Resistance $R_F$ | Wave-Making Resistance $R_W$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cause** | Viscous shear in the boundary layer | Gravity-wave generation at the free surface |
| **Governing group** | Reynolds number $R_n = VL/\nu$ | Froude number $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ |
| **Speed scaling** | $\propto V^{1.72}$ to $V^2$ | $\propto V^6$ (high-speed limit) |
| **Position on hull** | Distributed over entire wetted surface | Concentrated near bow and stern shoulders |
| **Effect of length** | Falls slowly as $L$ increases | Falls rapidly as $L$ increases |
| **Effect of form** | Depends mainly on wetted area | Strongly sensitive to hull shape and block coefficient |
| **Eliminable?** | No (always present) | No, but reducible by hull-form optimisation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners expect the answer in **tabular form** with at least 4–5 contrasting points. Bullet-only answers lose marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **one full question** (both sub-parts OR the full alternative).

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) — Components of Resistance on a Floating Body
**[7 Marks]**

**Statement:** *With neat block diagrams, explain the various components of total resistance acting on a ship moving through calm water. How are these components grouped under viscous and inviscid origins?*

**Model Solution:**

The total resistance $R_T$ encountered by a ship advancing at speed $V$ in calm water is the sum of several physically distinct components. Each is traced to a different fluid-mechanism, and each is governed by a different dimensionless group.

**1. Frictional Resistance $R_F$ (viscous, tangential):**

The tangential shear stresses integrated over the wetted surface $S$ give:

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_F, \quad C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

**2. Viscous Pressure Resistance $R_{VP}$ (viscous, normal):**

The asymmetric pressure field around a 3-D hull, exacerbated by stern flow separation, contributes a normal-pressure drag:

$$R_{VP} = k \cdot R_F, \quad k = \text{form factor}$$

**3. Wave-Making Resistance $R_W$ (inviscid, gravity):**

Energy radiated as surface gravity waves from the hull. Governed by Froude number $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$. Depends strongly on $F_n$, $L$, $B/T$, and $C_B$.

**4. Air Resistance $R_{AA}$ (inviscid, aerodynamic):**

Wind drag on the above-water superstructure. Typically 2–4 % of $R_T$ for merchant ships. Estimated using aerodynamic coefficients on frontal areas.

**5. Appendage Resistance $R_{APP}$ (viscous + form):**

Drag of rudder, bilge keels, shaft struts, sonar domes, etc. Computed by separate model tests or empirical allowances (≈ 3–8 % of $R_T$).

**Grouping under fluid-physical origins:**

| Group | Components | Governing Number |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Viscous** | $R_F,\; R_{VP},\; R_{APP}$ | $R_n$ |
| **Inviscid-wave** | $R_W$ | $F_n$ |
| **Inviscid-air** | $R_{AA}$ | $R_{n,\text{air}}$ |

**Final synthesis equation:**

$$\boxed{R_T = (1+k)\,R_F + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{APP}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Awarding 7 marks: ~2 marks for naming + defining components, ~2 marks for grouping, ~2 marks for governing numbers, ~1 mark for the final equation and a labelled diagram.

---

#### Q.A (b) — Numerical: Frictional Resistance of a Model
**[7 Marks]**

**Statement:** *A 1:25 scale model of a ship is towed in a towing tank at a speed such that Froude similarity is preserved. The prototype (ship) has $L_{PP} = 200$ m and wetted surface $S_S = 12\,000$ m². The model is tested in fresh water at 20 °C. Calculate:*
*(i) the model speed corresponding to ship speed of 18 knots, and*
*(ii) the frictional resistance of the model at that speed, given that the form factor is $k = 0.20$.*
*Use ITTC-57 correlation. Take $\nu_M = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\rho_M = 1000$ kg/m³.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric scale and prototype speed.**

Scale ratio $\lambda = 25$, so $L_M = 200/25 = 8$ m.
$V_S = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.260$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Froude-similar model speed.**

For Froude similarity, $F_{nS} = F_{nM}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_S}{\sqrt{g L_S}} &= \frac{V_M}{\sqrt{g L_M}} \\
V_M &= V_S \sqrt{\frac{L_M}{L_S}} = V_S \cdot \lambda^{-0.5} \\
V_M &= 9.260 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{9.260}{5} = 1.852 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Model Reynolds number.**

$$R_{nM} = \frac{V_M L_M}{\nu_M} = \frac{1.852 \times 8}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.482 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 4 — Model $C_F$ using ITTC-57.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_{nM} &= \log_{10}(1.482 \times 10^7) = 7.1708 \\
\log_{10} R_{nM} - 2 &= 5.1708 \\
(\log_{10} R_{nM} - 2)^2 &= 26.74 \\
C_{F,M} &= \frac{0.075}{26.74} = 2.805 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Model wetted surface (Cauchy-style scaling).**

Assuming $S \propto L^2$:

$$S_M = \frac{S_S}{\lambda^2} = \frac{12\,000}{625} = 19.20 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Total viscous resistance of the model.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{V,M} &= (1+k) C_{F,M} = 1.20 \times 2.805 \times 10^{-3} = 3.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
R_{V,M} &= 0.5 \times 1000 \times (1.852)^2 \times 19.20 \times 3.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
        &= 500 \times 3.430 \times 19.20 \times 3.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
        &= 500 \times 3.430 = 1715 \\
        &= 1715 \times 19.20 = 32\,928 \\
        &= 32\,928 \times 3.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
        &= 110.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_M = 1.852 \text{ m/s}, \quad R_{V,M} \approx 110.8 \text{ N}}$$

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q.B (a) — Derivation of Reynolds Number
**[7 Marks]**

**Statement:** *Derive the expression for the Reynolds number for a floating body. Explain why Reynolds similarity is not automatically satisfied in Froude-scale model testing of ships, and state the practical consequence on the extracted frictional resistance.*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the ratio of inertial to viscous forces per unit volume in a flow past a body of length $L$ at speed $V$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Inertial force} &\sim \rho \frac{V^2}{L} \\
\text{Viscous force} &\sim \mu \frac{V}{L^2} \\
R_n &= \frac{\text{Inertial}}{\text{Viscous}} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} \\
\therefore R_n &= \frac{V L}{\nu}, \quad \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why Reynolds similarity is not auto-satisfied in Froude testing:**

For a geometric scale ratio $\lambda = L_S / L_M$, Froude similarity gives:

$$V_M = \frac{V_S}{\sqrt{\lambda}} \;\Rightarrow\; R_{nM} = \frac{V_S L_S}{\nu_M \sqrt{\lambda}\, \lambda} = \frac{R_{nS}}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

Thus $R_{nM} \ll R_{nS}$ by a factor $\sqrt{\lambda}$ (typically 5 for $\lambda = 25$). The model's boundary layer is **laminar over a larger fraction** of its length, and $C_{F,M}$ is therefore **higher** than the friction-line value extrapolated to ship scale.

**Practical consequence:**

To compare model and ship, the model $C_{F,M}$ is replaced by the **ITTC-57 ship-scale value** $C_{F,S}$ at the same $R_{nS}$, and the difference $\Delta C_F = C_{F,M} - C_{F,S}$ is added as a **correlation allowance**:

$$C_{T,S} = (1+k)\,C_{F,S} + \Delta C_F + C_{R}$$

where $C_R$ is the **residuary resistance coefficient** (essentially wave-making) extracted from the model test.

> [!TIP]
> This is the heart of the **ITTC 1978 prediction method** — examiners love this 7-mark derivation.

---

#### Q.B (b) — Numerical: Froude-Scale Frictional Resistance of a Ship
**[7 Marks]**

**Statement:** *A ship of length $L_{PP} = 300$ m, wetted surface $S = 24\,000$ m², is to operate at 20 knots in seawater. Estimate the frictional resistance and the effective power due to friction alone.*
*Data: $\rho_S = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_S = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Use ITTC-57. (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert speed.**

$$V_S = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Ship Reynolds number.**

$$R_{nS} = \frac{V_S L_{PP}}{\nu_S} = \frac{10.288 \times 300}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3086.4}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.572 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 3 — $C_{F,S}$ using ITTC-57.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} R_{nS} &= \log_{10}(2.572 \times 10^9) = 9.4102 \\
\log_{10} R_{nS} - 2 &= 7.4102 \\
(\log_{10} R_{nS} - 2)^2 &= 54.91 \\
C_{F,S} &= \frac{0.075}{54.91} = 1.366 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Frictional resistance.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_F &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times (10.288)^2 \times 24\,000 \times 1.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 512.5 \times 105.84 \times 24\,000 \times 1.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 512.5 \times 105.84 = 54\,243 \\
    &= 54\,243 \times 24\,000 = 1.302 \times 10^{9} \\
    &= 1.302 \times 10^{9} \times 1.366 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 1.778 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \\
    &= 1.778 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Frictional Effective Power.**

$$P_{E,F} = R_F \times V_S = 1.778 \times 10^{6} \times 10.288 = 1.829 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} = 18.29 \text{ MW}$$

$$\boxed{R_F \approx 1.78 \text{ MN}, \quad P_{E,F} \approx 18.3 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students **forget to convert knots to m/s** before substituting in $R_n = VL/\nu$. This single error costs **2–3 marks** instantly. Always write "1 knot = 0.5144 m/s" at the top of any ship-speed calculation. A second common error is **using freshwater viscosity** instead of seawater — pick the value stated in the problem and **circle it** in your answer script.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Effect of Viscosity on Floating Bodies):**

- 🔹 **Viscosity is the source of frictional resistance and viscous pressure resistance**, not wave-making resistance.
- 🔹 **Reynolds number** $R_n = VL/\nu$ is the **only** dimensionless group governing viscous drag; Froude number governs wave drag.
- 🔹 For ships, $R_n$ is **$10^8$ to $10^9$** — boundary layer is **fully turbulent** over most of the hull.
- 🔹 **ITTC-57 formula** is the **default** friction line in KTU papers: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$.
- 🔹 **Total viscous resistance** is **$(1+k)$ times frictional** resistance; $k$ = form factor, typically 0.1–0.5.
- 🔹 **Froude-scaled model tests** do NOT preserve $R_n$ — this is the famous **Reynolds-scale effect**, fixed by the **ITTC-78 correlation procedure** (replace $C_{F,M}$ by $C_{F,S}$ + add $\Delta C_F$).
- 🔹 **Schoenherr** and **Prandtl–Schlichting** formulas are alternatives; all three agree within ±2 % in the ship-$R_n$ range.
- 🔹 **Wetted surface** $S$ scales as $L^2$ — keep the **scale ratio $\lambda$** straight when converting model to ship.
- 🔹 **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — *memorise this conversion*; it appears in almost every numerical.
- 🔹 **Seawater** (1025 kg/m³, 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) and **fresh water** (998 kg/m³, 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) have different $\rho$ and $\nu$ — read the problem carefully.
- 🔹 **Total resistance** $R_T = (1+k) R_F + R_W + R_{AA} + R_{APP}$ — never write $R_T = R_F + R_W$ in isolation.
- 🔹 **Effective power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ — converts resistance to the power that the propeller must deliver.
- 🔹 Examiners **love the block diagram** of resistance components — draw it once, reuse it in any related answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Tension and Its Effect on Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Tension and Its Effect on Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Tension (σ):** Surface tension is defined as the *tensile force per unit length* acting in the surface plane of a liquid, tending to contract the surface to the smallest possible area. Mathematically, it is expressed as the work (energy) required to create a unit area of new surface at constant temperature and pressure.

$$ \sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad \left[ \text{Units: N/m or J/m}^2 \right] $$

In the context of naval architecture, surface tension refers specifically to the **interfacial tension at the air–water interface** surrounding a ship's hull, which becomes critically important at small scales (model testing, small craft, high-speed planing vessels).

### 1.2 Physical Origin — Molecular Cohesion

At the bulk of a liquid, a molecule is pulled equally in **all directions** by neighboring molecules (cohesive forces). However, at the free surface, a molecule experiences a **net inward pull** because there are no liquid molecules above it. This imbalance causes the surface to behave like a stretched elastic membrane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cohesion:** Intermolecular attraction between *like* molecules (water–water).
> **Adhesion:** Intermolecular attraction between *unlike* molecules (water–ship hull).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a thin **rubber sheet** stretched across a circular frame. If you press a small ball gently onto it, the sheet deforms but tries to snap back — the surface acts like a stretched membrane. The water's surface behaves similarly: it is a *tensioned elastic skin* that resists being broken or stretched.

For a **ship**, this "skin" is the air–water interface. When the hull moves:
- Small disturbances must first "break" this skin to form waves and bubbles.
- This requires energy → manifests as **wave-making resistance** and **spray resistance**.
- For very small boats (e.g., model ships in towing tanks), surface tension dominates and the Froude scaling laws break down.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Values (at 20 °C)

| Liquid Pair | Surface Tension σ (N/m) |
|---|---|
| Water – Air | **0.0728 N/m** |
| Mercury – Air | **0.486 N/m** |
| Sea Water – Air | **0.0735 N/m** |
| Oil – Water | ~ 0.025 – 0.035 N/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Effect:** Surface tension *decreases* with rising temperature and becomes zero at the **critical temperature**.

### 1.5 Why Surface Tension Matters in Naval Architecture

- Determines the **minimum wave size** that can exist on the water surface (Kelvin's criterion).
- Governs **bubble and droplet formation** near propellers, cavitating flows, and breaking waves.
- Sets the **scale effects** in model testing (Weber number scaling).
- Influences **spray generation** at the bow and stern of high-speed craft.
- Critical in **capillary wave** analysis and **Marangoni effects** near the free surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Molecular (Microscopic) Picture

The surface tension phenomenon arises from the imbalance of intermolecular forces at the free surface:

1. **Bulk molecules:** Symmetric force field → net force = 0 → molecule is in equilibrium.
2. **Surface molecules:** Asymmetric force field (no molecules above to pull outward) → net force is **directed into the liquid**.
3. **Result:** The surface contracts to minimize area → *minimum surface energy configuration*.
4. **Energy interpretation:** Creating new surface area requires work against the cohesive pull, so the surface stores potential energy per unit area equal to σ.

### 2.2 Pressure Difference Across a Curved Interface (Young–Laplace Equation)

For a curved liquid interface, surface tension produces a **pressure jump** between the inside and outside of the surface.

For a **spherical bubble** of radius R:

$$ \Delta p = p_{\text{inside}} - p_{\text{outside}} = \frac{2\sigma}{R} $$

For a **soap bubble** (two surfaces — inner and outer):

$$ \Delta p = \frac{4\sigma}{R} $$

For a **general curved surface** with two principal radii of curvature R₁ and R₂:

$$ \Delta p = \sigma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> This pressure difference is what causes small bubbles to collapse quickly and large bubbles to be more stable. The same principle explains why **capillary waves** have shorter wavelengths for higher frequencies.

### 2.3 Capillarity — Capillary Rise and Fall

When a thin tube (capillary) of radius *r* is dipped into a liquid:

- **Wetting liquid (water):** Liquid **rises** in the tube.
- **Non-wetting liquid (mercury):** Liquid **falls** in the tube.

**Capillary rise formula:**

$$ h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} $$

Where:
- σ = surface tension
- θ = contact angle between liquid and tube wall
- ρ = density of liquid
- g = acceleration due to gravity
- r = radius of capillary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For pure water in a clean glass tube, θ ≈ 0°, so cos θ ≈ 1.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/L$ | Definition of surface tension | N/m |
| 2 | $W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$ | Work to create new surface area | J |
| 3 | $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$ | Pressure inside a liquid drop (one surface) | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta p = 4\sigma/R$ | Pressure inside a soap bubble (two surfaces) | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta p = \sigma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$ | Young–Laplace for general curved surface | Pa |
| 6 | $h = 2\sigma\cos\theta \,/\, \rho g r$ | Capillary rise / fall | m |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\min} = 2\pi\sqrt{\sigma/(\rho g)}$ | Minimum capillary wave length | m |
| 8 | $We = \rho V^2 L / \sigma$ | Weber number (inertia vs surface tension) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{\text{cap}} = \sigma \cdot L_{\text{wetted}}$ | Capillary force on a vertical plate | N |
| 10 | $p_{\text{inside}} = p_{\text{outside}} + 2\sigma/R$ | Pressure inside a small bubble | Pa |
| 11 | $\ln(p/p_0) = 2\sigma M / (\rho_l R R_u T)$ | Kelvin equation for vapor pressure over curved surface | – |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Model testing in towing tanks:** The dimensionless group **Weber number (We)** compares inertial forces to surface tension forces. Similarity requires matching We (and Fr) between model and prototype.
- **Propeller cavitation:** Surface tension determines the **critical bubble radius** below which a cavity collapses spontaneously (Rayleigh–Plesset analysis).
- **Bow spray formation:** Spray droplets are formed when the surface tension "skin" is broken; smaller droplets require more energy per unit volume.
- **Liquid cargo transport (LNG, oil tankers):** Surface tension affects sloshing dynamics and wave breaking in partially filled tanks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Difference Across a Spherical Bubble

**Objective:** Show that the excess pressure inside a spherical bubble of radius R is $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$.

**Setup:** Consider a spherical bubble of liquid of radius R, with surface tension σ acting tangentially along the curved surface. The bubble is in equilibrium with the external fluid.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram:** Cut the bubble in half. Consider the forces acting on one hemisphere:

- Outward force due to internal pressure: $F_p = p_i \cdot \pi R^2$ (projected area).
- Inward force due to external pressure: $F_{p_{\text{ext}}} = p_o \cdot \pi R^2$.
- Inward force due to surface tension around the rim: $F_\sigma = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$ (circumference × tension).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of the hemisphere:**

The hemisphere is in static equilibrium, so:

$$ F_p - F_{p_{\text{ext}}} = F_\sigma $$

**Step 3 — Substitute expressions:**

$$ p_i \cdot \pi R^2 - p_o \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R $$

**Step 4 — Factor out common terms:**

$$ (p_i - p_o) \cdot \pi R^2 = 2\pi R \sigma $$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\pi R$ from both sides:**

$$ (p_i - p_o) \cdot R = 2\sigma $$

**Step 6 — Solve for the pressure difference:**

$$ \Delta p = p_i - p_o = \frac{2\sigma}{R} $$

This is the **Young–Laplace equation** for a liquid drop or gas bubble with a single interface.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **soap bubble** (with an inner and outer liquid film), there are **two surfaces** producing surface tension force. Therefore the same derivation gives $\Delta p = 4\sigma/R$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Rise in a Vertical Tube

**Objective:** Derive the expression for the height to which a liquid rises in a capillary tube of radius r.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:** A vertical tube of inner radius *r* is dipped into a liquid of density ρ and surface tension σ. The contact angle between the liquid and tube is θ.

**Step 2 — Identify the forces:**

- **Upward force** due to surface tension along the contact line: $F_\sigma = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta$ (vertical component of surface tension pulling the liquid up).

- **Downward force** due to the weight of the liquid column raised: $W = \rho g h \cdot \pi r^2$ (volume of column × density × gravity).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition:**

At equilibrium, upward force = downward force:

$$ \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cos\theta = \rho g h \cdot \pi r^2 $$

**Step 4 — Simplify by dividing both sides by $\pi r$:**

$$ 2\sigma \cos\theta = \rho g h \cdot r $$

**Step 5 — Solve for the height h:**

$$ h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} $$

This shows that:
- The **rise is inversely proportional** to the tube radius.
- The rise is **directly proportional** to the surface tension.
- For θ > 90° (e.g., mercury on glass), cos θ is negative, and *h* is negative → **capillary depression**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Wavelength of a Capillary–Gravity Wave

**Objective:** Find the minimum wavelength $\lambda_{\min}$ that can exist on the surface of a liquid.

**Step 1 — Dispersion relation for capillary–gravity waves:**

For a wave of wavenumber *k* on a liquid surface, the phase speed is:

$$ c^2 = \frac{g}{k} + \frac{\sigma k}{\rho} $$

**Step 2 — Group velocity and minimum phase speed:**

The phase speed *c* is minimum where $dc/dk = 0$. Differentiating:

$$ \frac{d(c^2)}{dk} = -\frac{g}{k^2} + \frac{\sigma}{\rho} = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical wavenumber $k_c$:**

$$ \frac{g}{k_c^2} = \frac{\sigma}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_c = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g}{\sigma}} $$

**Step 4 — Find the minimum phase speed:**

Substituting back:

$$ c_{\min}^2 = \frac{g}{k_c} + \frac{\sigma k_c}{\rho} = 2\sqrt{\frac{g\sigma}{\rho}} $$

$$ c_{\min} = \left( \frac{4g\sigma}{\rho} \right)^{1/4} $$

**Step 5 — Minimum wavelength:**

$$ \lambda_{\min} = \frac{2\pi}{k_c} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho g}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For water ($\sigma = 0.0728$ N/m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):
> $$\lambda_{\min} \approx 1.73 \text{ cm}$$
> This means waves shorter than ~1.7 cm on water are *damped* by surface tension effects — a key result in ship wave patterns and ripple tanks.

### 3.4 Effect on Ships: Scaling Analysis

**Why small model ships have problems with surface tension:**

The ratio of surface tension forces to gravity forces is given by the **inverse Weber-based Bond number**, also called the **Eötvös number (Eo):**

$$ Eo = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\sigma} $$

- For **large ships** (L ≈ 100 m): $Eo \approx 10^9$ → surface tension is negligible.
- For **small models** (L ≈ 1 m): $Eo \approx 10^5$ → surface tension starts to matter.
- For **micro-robots** (L ≈ 1 cm): $Eo \approx 10^2$ → surface tension dominates.

In towing tank tests, to maintain dynamic similarity, both the **Froude number (Fr)** and the **Weber number (We)** must be matched:

$$ Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}, \qquad We = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma} $$

Since *Fr* and *We* cannot both be matched simultaneously for different scales, **Froude scaling** is used, and the resulting Weber number discrepancy is reported as a **scale effect** correction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Molecular Picture of Surface Tension

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Bulk Liquid Molecule] --> A2[Equal cohesive forces in all directions]
    A2 --> A3[Net force = 0, molecule is stable]
    B1[Surface Molecule] --> B2[No molecules above]
    B2 --> B3[Net inward cohesive pull]
    B3 --> B4[Surface contracts to minimum area]
    B4 --> B5[Result: Surface Tension Force]
    A3 --> C1[Result: No net force in bulk]
    B5 --> D1[Engineering Consequences]
    D1 --> D2[Capillary Rise]
    D1 --> D3[Pressure Jump in Bubbles]
    D1 --> D4[Minimum Wavelength Limit]
    D1 --> D5[Spray and Droplet Formation]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Effects of Surface Tension on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Hull moving through water] --> S1[Disturbs air water interface]
    S1 --> S2{Bubble Formation}
    S1 --> S3{Spray Generation at Bow}
    S1 --> S4{Capillary Wave Generation}
    S2 --> S2a[Propeller Cavitation]
    S2 --> S2b[Bilge Bubble Collapse Noise]
    S2 --> S2c[Young Laplace Pressure Jump]
    S3 --> S3a[Droplet Size governed by Weber Number]
    S3 --> S3b[Spray Resistance Component]
    S4 --> S4a[Minimum Wavelength ~1.7 cm for water]
    S4 --> S4b[High frequency ripples damped]
    S2c --> O1[Surface Energy Effects]
    S3b --> O1
    S4b --> O1
    O1 --> O2[Total Ship Resistance]
    O2 --> O3[Scale Effect Correction in Model Tests]
    O2 --> O4[Design of High Speed Planing Hulls]
    O2 --> O5[Cavitation Analysis of Propellers]
```

### 4.3 Free Body Diagram: Hemisphere of a Bubble (Young-Laplace Derivation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Cross section of spherical bubble radius R] --> L1[Internal pressure pi acts on projected area pi R squared]
    L0 --> L2[External pressure po acts on projected area pi R squared]
    L0 --> L3[Surface tension sigma acts along circumference 2 pi R]
    L1 --> L4[Net outward force Fp equals pi R squared times pi minus po]
    L2 --> L4
    L3 --> L5[Inward force F sigma equals 2 pi R sigma]
    L4 --> L6[Equilibrium Fp equals F sigma]
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7[Solve for pressure jump]
    L7 --> L8[Result delta p equals 2 sigma over R]
```

### 4.4 Capillary Rise Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Thin glass tube of radius r dipped in water] --> C1[Water wets glass contact angle theta small]
    C1 --> C2[Surface tension acts along contact line]
    C2 --> C3[Vertical component sigma cos theta pulls water up]
    C3 --> C4[Liquid column rises to height h]
    C4 --> C5[Weight of column W equals rho g h pi r squared]
    C5 --> C6[Equilibrium sigma 2 pi r cos theta equals rho g h pi r squared]
    C6 --> C7[Solve h equals 2 sigma cos theta over rho g r]
```

### 4.5 Weber Number Similarity in Ship Model Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Prototype Ship Length Lp Velocity Vp] --> P1[Calculate Froude Fr p and Weber We p]
    M0[Model Ship Length Lm] --> M1[Scale velocity using Fr similarity Vm equals Vp sqrt Lm over Lp]
    M1 --> M2[Calculate We m with Vm and Lm]
    P1 --> C1{We m equals We p check}
    M2 --> C1
    C1 --> C1a[No mismatch: Surface tension effects correctly scaled]
    C1 --> C1b[We m less than We p: Surface tension under represented in model]
    C1b --> C1c[Apply scale effect correction or use pressurized towing tank]
    C1a --> D1[Reliable resistance prediction]
    C1c --> D1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1.** Define surface tension. What are its SI units? Explain in one sentence why a liquid surface behaves like a stretched membrane.

**Model Answer:**
Surface tension is the tensile force per unit length acting along a liquid surface tending to contract it to the minimum area, with **SI units of N/m (or equivalently J/m²)**. A liquid surface behaves like a stretched membrane because surface molecules experience a net inward cohesive pull (no molecules above them), placing the surface in a state of tension analogous to an elastic film.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the capillary rise? Write the formula for capillary rise in a tube of radius *r* and explain why mercury shows a *depression* instead of a rise in a glass tube.

**Model Answer:**
Capillary rise is the phenomenon in which a liquid rises inside a narrow tube due to surface tension and adhesion. The formula is:

$$ h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} $$

Mercury does **not wet glass** — the contact angle θ between mercury and glass is greater than 90°, making cos θ negative. This makes *h* negative, i.e., mercury is **depressed** below the surrounding free surface inside the tube.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Question Pattern with Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Young–Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a spherical liquid bubble of radius R. Show clearly the free body diagram and force balance.

**Model Solution:**

Consider a spherical bubble of liquid with internal pressure $p_i$, external pressure $p_o$, radius R, and surface tension σ. Cut the bubble with a diametral plane to isolate one hemisphere.

**Forces acting on the hemisphere:**

- *Outward force due to internal pressure* acting on the projected (circular) area:
$$ F_p = p_i \cdot \pi R^2 $$

- *Inward force due to external pressure* acting on the same projected area:
$$ F_{p_{\text{ext}}} = p_o \cdot \pi R^2 $$

- *Inward force due to surface tension* acting along the circular rim of length $2\pi R$:
$$ F_\sigma = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R $$

**Equilibrium of the hemisphere:**

$$ p_i \cdot \pi R^2 - p_o \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R $$

$$ (p_i - p_o)\pi R^2 = 2\pi R \sigma $$

Dividing both sides by $\pi R$:

$$ (p_i - p_o) R = 2\sigma $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta p = p_i - p_o = \frac{2\sigma}{R}} $$

For a **soap bubble** with two surfaces, the right-hand side is doubled, giving $\Delta p = 4\sigma/R$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A spherical air bubble of radius 0.5 mm is released at a depth of 5 m in seawater. Calculate (i) the excess pressure inside the bubble at that depth, and (ii) the work done by surface tension in increasing the bubble radius from 0.5 mm to 1.0 mm. Use σ = 0.0735 N/m, ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², and atmospheric pressure = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Excess pressure inside the bubble (due to surface tension):**

$$ \Delta p_{\text{surface}} = \frac{2\sigma}{R} = \frac{2 \times 0.0735}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 294 \text{ Pa} $$

**Total pressure inside the bubble** at depth 5 m:

$$ p_{\text{hydrostatic}} = p_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h = 1.013 \times 10^5 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 5 $$

$$ p_{\text{hydrostatic}} = 1.013 \times 10^5 + 5.028 \times 10^4 = 1.516 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} $$

Total internal pressure:

$$ p_i = 1.516 \times 10^5 + 294 \approx 1.519 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} $$

**(ii) Work done by surface tension in expanding the bubble:**

Surface energy of a bubble with two surfaces (air bubble has one air-water interface, so single surface):

$$ E = \sigma \cdot A = \sigma \cdot (4\pi R^2) $$

Initial energy (R₁ = 0.5 mm):

$$ E_1 = 0.0735 \times 4\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 0.0735 \times 4\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} $$

$$ E_1 = 2.31 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} $$

Final energy (R₂ = 1.0 mm):

$$ E_2 = 0.0735 \times 4\pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 9.24 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} $$

Work done by surface tension (equals the increase in surface energy, since the system does positive work against surface tension to expand):

$$ W = E_2 - E_1 = 9.24 \times 10^{-7} - 2.31 \times 10^{-7} = 6.93 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} $$

$$ \boxed{W \approx 6.93 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} } $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of capillarity. Derive an expression for the capillary rise of a liquid in a vertical tube of radius r. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Phenomenon:** Capillarity is the rise or fall of a liquid in a narrow tube (or porous medium) due to the combined effects of surface tension and the contact angle between the liquid and the tube material.

**Assumptions:**
1. The tube is vertical and of uniform circular cross-section.
2. The contact angle θ is constant along the contact line.
3. The meniscus is hemispherical (valid for small r).
4. The liquid wets the tube material (θ < 90°) for rise; non-wetting (θ > 90°) for depression.
5. Temperature is uniform; surface tension is constant.

**Derivation:**

Consider a tube of inner radius r dipped in a liquid of density ρ and surface tension σ. The contact angle is θ.

**Upward force** due to surface tension along the contact circumference $2\pi r$:

$$ F_\sigma = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta $$

**Downward force** due to the weight of the liquid column of height h:

$$ W = \rho g \cdot (\pi r^2 h) $$

**Equilibrium:**

$$ \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^2 h $$

Dividing both sides by $\pi r$:

$$ 2\sigma \cos\theta = \rho g h r $$

$$ \boxed{h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}} $$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail how surface tension affects (i) the wave pattern generated by a ship, (ii) the formation of bubbles near the hull and propellers, and (iii) the spray pattern at the bow. Use appropriate dimensionless numbers where relevant.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Effect on ship wave pattern:**

Surface tension sets a **minimum wavelength** for capillary–gravity waves. From the dispersion relation, the minimum wavelength on water is:

$$ \lambda_{\min} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho g}} \approx 1.73 \text{ cm} $$

- Waves shorter than $\lambda_{\min}$ cannot propagate; they are **damped** by surface tension.
- For a large ship (transverse wavelength ~ 10–100 m), surface tension effects are negligible.
- For **small models** in towing tanks, the wave system is contaminated by capillary ripples not present at full scale, leading to **scale effects**.

The relevant dimensionless number is the **Weber number**:

$$ We = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma} $$

When $We \gg 1$, inertia dominates; when $We \ll 1$, surface tension dominates.

**(ii) Effect on bubble formation near hull and propellers:**

- Surface tension creates a **pressure jump** across the bubble surface given by $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$.
- For a propeller blade operating near the surface, when local pressure falls below vapor pressure, a **cavity (bubble) forms**.
- The minimum radius a bubble can sustain is governed by the **Kelvin equation** for vapor pressure over a curved surface:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) = \frac{2\sigma M}{\rho_l R R_u T} $$

- Smaller bubbles have higher internal pressure → collapse violently → cause **cavitation erosion** and noise.

**(iii) Effect on spray pattern at the bow:**

When the bow impacts the water surface, the air–water interface is broken. The size of spray droplets is governed by the **Weber number**:

$$ We = \frac{\rho V^2 d}{\sigma} $$

where d is the droplet diameter. Droplets form when surface tension cannot contain the inertial forces. Smaller droplets → higher surface energy per unit volume → more energy is dissipated in spray formation → contributes to **spray resistance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Units confusion:** Writing σ in N/m² instead of N/m. Energy per unit area is N/m², force per unit length is N/m — they are numerically equal but dimensionally different.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for soap bubbles** vs single-surface drops. Marks lost in derivation when applying $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$ to a soap bubble.
> 3. **Capillary rise sign error:** Not checking whether θ < 90° (rise) or θ > 90° (depression). Cosine can be negative!
> 4. **Confusing Froude and Weber scaling:** Surface tension is governed by **Weber number**, not Froude. Froude governs gravity waves. In model testing, only one can be matched, so a *correction factor* must be reported.
> 5. **Skipping the free body diagram** in the Young–Laplace derivation. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the FBD.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Tension and Its Effect on Ships**

- **Definition:** σ = Force per unit length (N/m) = Energy per unit area (J/m²).
- **Origin:** Net inward cohesive pull on surface molecules → "stretched membrane" behavior.
- **Young–Laplace Equation:** $\Delta p = 2\sigma/R$ (drop) or $4\sigma/R$ (soap bubble).
- **Capillary Rise:** $h = 2\sigma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ — rise if θ < 90°, depression if θ > 90°.
- **Minimum Capillary Wavelength:** $\lambda_{\min} = 2\pi\sqrt{\sigma/(\rho g)} \approx 1.73$ cm for water.
- **Weber Number:** $We = \rho V^2 L / \sigma$ — measures ratio of inertial to surface tension forces.
- **Eötvös Number:** $Eo = \rho g L^2 / \sigma$ — measures ratio of gravity to surface tension forces.
- **Effect on Ships:**
  - Sets minimum wave size in model tests.
  - Governs bubble/cavity dynamics near hull and propellers (cavitation).
  - Controls spray droplet size and spray resistance.
  - Negligible for large ships (L > 50 m), significant for small craft and models.
- **Temperature Effect:** σ *decreases* with increasing T → spray and bubble dynamics change with sea temperature.
- **Salinity Effect:** Sea water has slightly higher σ than fresh water (~0.0735 vs 0.0728 N/m).
- **Pressure jump is inversely proportional to radius:** small bubbles have very high internal pressures.
- **Capillary force on a vertical plate:** $F = \sigma \cdot L$ (where L is the contact-line length).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Pressure and Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapour Pressure and Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vapour Pressure — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Pressure ($P_v$)** is the **absolute pressure** exerted by a vapour when it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its own liquid (or solid) phase at a given temperature. Below this pressure, the liquid begins to **boil** or **flash-evaporate** at that temperature, even in the complete absence of any heat input from an external source.

Mathematically, vapour pressure is a strong function of temperature only:

$$P_v = f(T)$$

For water, $P_v$ rises **exponentially** with temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron relation). At **20 °C**, $P_v$ of fresh water is approximately **2.34 kPa (absolute)**, and at **100 °C** it equals atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa**), which is the basis for normal boiling.

In naval architecture, the relevant quantity is almost always $P_v$ of **sea water at the local operating temperature**, because sea water has a slightly *lower* vapour pressure than fresh water due to dissolved salts (Raoult's law depression).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Salinity Correction:** Sea water (≈ 35 ‰ salinity) has a vapour pressure roughly **1.8 %–2.0 % lower** than pure water at the same temperature. Examiners frequently test this nuance.

## 1.2 Cavitation — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is the dynamic process of **formation, growth, and violent collapse (implosion)** of vapour- (and gas-) filled cavities (bubbles) within a liquid, caused when the **local static pressure** in the flowing liquid falls **at or below** the local vapour pressure of the liquid, and then subsequently rises back above $P_v$ downstream, causing the cavities to collapse.

The phenomenon is governed by the inequality:

$$P_{local} \le P_v \quad \text{→ cavitation inception}$$

and the **cavitation number** $\sigma$ (Section 2) governs its severity and extent.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

**The "Boiling in a Cold Pipe" Analogy**
Imagine you are driving down a hill on a bicycle. The hill represents a region of **high velocity**, and because of energy conservation (Bernoulli), your speed (velocity head) increases at the cost of **pressure head** dropping — exactly like how water flowing over the **suction side of a propeller blade** experiences a local pressure dip. If you push hard enough down the hill, you can feel your ears "pop" — this is local pressure falling. Now imagine a propeller blade that pushes the water so fast that the local pressure falls **below the boiling pressure of the water itself**. The cold sea water literally **boils at room temperature** because its molecules now have enough kinetic energy to escape into the vapour phase! This is cavitation inception.

**The "Implosion" Analogy**
When those vapour bubbles drift downstream into a region of higher pressure (e.g., back of the blade, or wake of a pump impeller), the surrounding liquid rushes in to fill the void. The collapse is so rapid (microseconds) that it generates a **micro-jet** and a **shock wave** with local pressures of the order of **hundreds of MPa**, and temperatures of several thousand Kelvin at the bubble wall. This is what erodes metal surfaces, causes propeller pitting, and produces the audible "crackling" noise of a cavitating propeller.

## 1.4 Why Naval Architects Must Study This

Cavitation is **THE single most important hydrodynamic performance-limiting phenomenon** in marine propulsion. It directly governs:

- **Maximum attainable propeller RPM** (and hence top ship speed)
- **Hull–propeller interaction efficiency** in non-uniform wake
- **Induced vibration and noise** (signature of submarines and naval vessels)
- **Material erosion** of bronze, manganese–aluminium, or nickel–aluminium–bronze (NAB) blades
- **Blade-rate pressure fluctuations** transmitted to the hull

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1 — *Apply the principles of fluid statics and kinematics to naval architecture problems***, with sub-focus on identifying cavitation inception conditions in ship flow systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Conditions for Cavitation

Cavitation requires **three** concurrent conditions:

1. **Pressure Condition:** Local static pressure $P_{local} \le P_v$ (vapour pressure of the liquid at the local temperature).
2. **Nucleation Sites:** The liquid must contain **cavitation nuclei** — microscopic undissolved gas bubbles, solid impurities, or surface micro-crevices that lower the energy barrier for bubble formation. Pure, degassed water is very hard to cavitate; ordinary sea water cavitates easily.
3. **Time/Duration Condition:** The low-pressure region must persist for a duration longer than the **critical time** required for the nucleus to grow to a macroscopic size (Rayleigh-Plesset time scale, typically $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ s).

## 2.2 The Cavitation Number (Thoma's Parameter)

The dimensionless parameter that characterises the *tendency* of a flow to cavitate is the **cavitation number**, originally proposed by Wilhelm Thoma (1924) for hydraulic machinery and extended to ship propellers by Burrill & Emerson (1963):

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_{\infty}^{\,2}}$$

where:

- $P_{\infty}$ = free-stream (or downstream / pump inlet) absolute static pressure
- $P_v$ = local vapour pressure (absolute)
- $\rho$ = density of the liquid
- $V_{\infty}$ = free-stream (or characteristic) velocity

> [!CAUTION]
> **Absolute vs Gauge Pressure Trap (KTU favourite!):** $P_{\infty}$ and $P_v$ **MUST both be in absolute units**. Mixing gauge and absolute values is the #1 cause of wrong answers in KTU examinations. Always convert: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.

## 2.3 Critical / Incipient Cavitation Number

For any given body shape (hydrofoil, propeller blade section, pump impeller), there exists a **critical cavitation number** $\sigma_c$ such that:

- $\sigma > \sigma_c$ → **No cavitation** (cavitation-free flow)
- $\sigma = \sigma_c$ → **Cavitation inception** (first appearance of bubbles)
- $\sigma < \sigma_c$ → **Developed (supercavitating) flow**; cavity extent grows as $\sigma$ decreases further

For marine propellers, $\sigma_c$ depends on the **blade-section profile**, **angle of attack**, **camber**, **thickness ratio**, and **surface roughness**.

## 2.4 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

For a pump, propeller in a wake, or any suction-side device, the **Net Positive Suction Head available** is:

$$\text{NPSH}_{available} = \frac{P_{inlet,\,abs}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{inlet}^{\,2}}{2g} - \Delta h_{friction}$$

And the **NPSH required** by the machine is an experimentally determined curve $\text{NPSH}_{req}(\dot{Q})$. Cavitation is avoided when:

$$\text{NPSH}_{available} \ge \text{NPSH}_{required} + \text{margin}$$

Typical KTU-problem margin: **0.5 m to 1.0 m of head** for marine pumps.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $P_v = f(T)$ | Vapour pressure vs temperature | **Pa (abs)** | Boiling criterion |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ | Cavitation number | dimensionless | Propeller, pump, hydrofoil |
| 3 | $\sigma_c = \sigma$ at inception | Critical cavitation number | dimensionless | Design limit |
| 4 | $P_{local} = P_{\infty} - \Delta P_{dynamic}$ | Local static pressure (Bernoulli) | **Pa (abs)** | Suction-side low pressure |
| 5 | $\text{NPSH}_{av} = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_{f}$ | Available NPSH | **m of head** | Pump installation |
| 6 | $\text{NPSH}_{av} \ge \text{NPSH}_{req} + \text{margin}$ | Cavitation-free design criterion | **m** | Pump design check |
| 7 | $C_p = \dfrac{P - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ | Pressure coefficient | dimensionless | Blade pressure distribution |
| 8 | $P_v^{sea} \approx 0.982\,P_v^{fresh}$ | Sea-water vapour pressure correction | ratio | Salinity effect |
| 9 | $P_v^{T} = 0.61078\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ kPa | Magnus-Tetens (fresh water, $T$ in °C) | **kPa** | Quick numerical estimation |
| 10 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\rho g}$ | Minimum submergence to avoid surface cavitation | **m** | Hull appendage design |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute pressures are taken with respect to a **perfect vacuum reference**. Always state this explicitly in your KTU answer scripts.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture

| Real-world Device | Cavitation Risk | Consequence | Design Mitigation |
|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Ship propeller | Very high | Erosion, vibration, loss of thrust | Increase submergence, reduce RPM, section choice |
| Rudder (behind propeller) | High | Cavitation erosion of trailing edge | Move rudder forward of propeller; thicker sections |
| Pump impeller (bilge, cooling) | Moderate | Head loss, noise, impeller pitting | Adequate NPSH, lower speed |
| Hydrofoil strut (hydrofoils) | Very high | Strut erosion, ride loss | Tip-venting, supercavitating section |
| Sonar dome | Critical | Acoustic scattering, noise | Streamlined dome, smooth flow |
| NACA intakes (submarines) | Moderate | Cavitation at masts | Sacrificial coatings |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Local Pressure on a Propeller Blade Section

**Starting Point — Steady, Inviscid, Incompressible Bernoulli Equation** (applied along a streamline from the free stream far upstream to a point on the suction side of the blade):

$$P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = P_{local} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{local}^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for the local static pressure $P_{local}$:**

Subtract $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{local}^{2}$ from both sides of the Bernoulli equation:

$$P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} - \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{local}^{2} = P_{local}$$

Rearrange to isolate $P_{local}$:

$$P_{local} = P_{\infty} + \frac{1}{2}\rho\!\left(V_{\infty}^{2} - V_{local}^{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the pressure coefficient $C_p$ (a normalised pressure):**

Divide the dynamic-pressure difference by the free-stream dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{local} - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{V_{local}}{V_{\infty}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $P_{local}$ in terms of $C_p$:**

$$P_{local} = P_{\infty} + C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the cavitation inception criterion $P_{local} = P_v$ at the point of minimum $C_p$:**

At the **cavitation inception point**, the local pressure equals the vapour pressure exactly:

$$P_v = P_{\infty} + C_{p,\,min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the minimum allowable $C_p$:**

Subtract $P_{\infty}$ from both sides:

$$P_v - P_{\infty} = C_{p,\,min} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$$

Divide both sides by $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$:

$$C_{p,\,min} = \frac{P_v - P_{\infty}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = -\sigma$$

**Final Result — The beautiful connection:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_c = -\,C_{p,\,min}\;}$$

This is the **fundamental link** between cavitation number and pressure coefficient: the critical cavitation number of a given section is simply the **negative of its minimum pressure coefficient**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cavitation Number in Terms of Submergence Depth (Surface Vessels)

For a propeller or appendage located at a depth $h_s$ **below the free surface**, the local static pressure (hydrostatic) is:

$$P_{\infty} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_s$$

> *(We neglect the dynamic pressure of the free stream at the propeller disk for this simplified depth-based treatment; the full treatment adds the Bernoulli stagnation-pressure correction.)*

**Step 1 — Substitute into the cavitation number definition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{(P_{atm} + \rho g h_s) - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Split the numerator into atmospheric and hydrostatic parts:**

$$\sigma = \frac{(P_{atm} - P_v) + \rho g h_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the *atmospheric* cavitation number $\sigma_{atm}$:**

$$\sigma_{atm} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Submergence contribution term:**

$$\sigma_{sub} = \frac{\rho g h_s}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = \frac{2 g h_s}{V_{\infty}^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \sigma_{atm} + \frac{2 g h_s}{V_{\infty}^{2}}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

- Greater submergence $h_s$ → larger $\sigma$ → less cavitation tendency.
- Higher ship speed $V_{\infty}$ → smaller $\sigma$ → more cavitation tendency. This is why **planing craft and surface-piercing propellers** suffer severe cavitation at high speeds.
- Lower atmospheric pressure (e.g., operating in the **Andaman Sea tropical heat** vs **Arctic waters**) → smaller $\sigma_{atm}$ → slightly more cavitation tendency.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Ship Speed for Cavitation Inception

Given a propeller of design cavitation number $\sigma_c$, the **critical ship speed** $V_{crit}$ beyond which cavitation begins is found by setting $\sigma = \sigma_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P_{atm} + \rho g h_s - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{crit}^{\,2}}$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{crit}^{\,2}$:**

$$\sigma_c \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{crit}^{\,2} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_s - P_v$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $V_{crit}^{\,2}$:**

$$V_{crit}^{\,2} = \frac{2\left(P_{atm} + \rho g h_s - P_v\right)}{\rho \sigma_c}$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{\;V_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{2\left(P_{atm} + \rho g h_s - P_v\right)}{\rho \sigma_c}}\;}$$

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question** — students are asked to compute the maximum allowable ship speed for a given propeller submergence and design cavitation number.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A surface vessel operates in tropical sea water at $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$. The propeller is located at a submergence $h_s = 3.5\,\text{m}$ below the waterline. Atmospheric pressure is $P_{atm} = 101.0\,\text{kPa}$. The ship speed is $V = 12$ knots. The propeller blade section has a critical pressure coefficient $C_{p,\,min} = -0.85$. Determine:
> (i) the vapour pressure of sea water at 30 °C;
> (ii) the cavitation number of the propeller;
> (iii) whether cavitation will occur.

**Given Data (re-stated for clarity):**

- $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$
- $h_s = 3.5\,\text{m}$
- $P_{atm} = 101.0\,\text{kPa} = 101{,}000\,\text{Pa}$
- $V = 12$ knots
- $C_{p,\,min} = -0.85$
- $\rho_{sea} = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ (standard KTU textbook value)
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$

**Step A — Convert ship speed from knots to m/s:**

$$V = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step B — Compute the vapour pressure of fresh water at 30 °C using Magnus-Tetens:**

$$P_v^{fresh} = 0.61078 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3}\right)\;\text{kPa}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3} = \frac{518.1}{267.3} = 1.9384$$

Compute the exponential:

$$\exp(1.9384) = 6.948$$

Therefore:

$$P_v^{fresh} = 0.61078 \times 6.948 = 4.244\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step C — Apply sea-water salinity correction (≈ 1.8 % reduction):**

$$P_v^{sea} = 0.982 \times 4.244 = 4.168\,\text{kPa} = 4168\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step D — Compute the local static pressure at the propeller depth (hydrostatic):**

$$P_{\infty} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_s$$

$$P_{\infty} = 101{,}000 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3.5$$

Compute the hydrostatic term:

$$1025 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 = 35{,}196\,\text{Pa} = 35.196\,\text{kPa}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\infty} = 101{,}000 + 35{,}196 = 136{,}196\,\text{Pa} \approx 136.2\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step E — Compute the cavitation number $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v^{sea}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}}$$

Compute the denominator (dynamic pressure):

$$\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (6.173)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.107 = 19{,}530\,\text{Pa}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$P_{\infty} - P_v^{sea} = 136{,}196 - 4{,}168 = 132{,}028\,\text{Pa}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma = \frac{132{,}028}{19{,}530} = 6.76$$

**Step F — Compute the critical cavitation number from the given $C_{p,\,min}$:**

$$\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min} = -(-0.85) = 0.85$$

**Step G — Compare $\sigma$ with $\sigma_c$:**

$$\sigma = 6.76 \quad \text{vs} \quad \sigma_c = 0.85$$

Since $\sigma \gg \sigma_c$ (i.e., $6.76 > 0.85$), the flow is **well above** the cavitation inception threshold.

**Conclusion — NO cavitation will occur under these operating conditions.** The propeller has a comfortable cavitation margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse-Engineering the Limit:** If a student is asked the *critical speed* at which cavitation *just begins*, set $\sigma = \sigma_c = 0.85$ and solve the equation in **Section 3.3** for $V_{crit}$. This will give a *very* high speed (≈ 22–24 knots) for this propeller depth — confirming the large margin.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Will Cavitation Occur?

| Computed $\sigma$ | Compared to $\sigma_c$ | Operating Regime | Engineering Action |
|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| $\sigma > 3\,\sigma_c$ | $\sigma > \sigma_c$ | **Comfortably cavitation-free** | Safe; no action required |
| $1.5\,\sigma_c < \sigma \le 3\,\sigma_c$ | $\sigma > \sigma_c$ | **Cavitation-free with low margin** | Monitor; consider redesign if speed may rise |
| $\sigma_c < \sigma \le 1.5\,\sigma_c$ | $\sigma > \sigma_c$ | **Near-inception (incipient risk)** | Redesign section; increase depth; reduce RPM |
| $\sigma = \sigma_c$ | Inception | **Cavitation inception** | Limit further speed increase |
| $\sigma < \sigma_c$ | Inception exceeded | **Developed cavitation** | Mandatory redesign: supercavitating section, depth change, gear reduction |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Cavitation Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram maps the **causal chain** of cavitation inception → growth → collapse → consequence, mirroring the structure of a typical KTU 14-mark "explain the phenomenon of cavitation" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Liquid flow with nucleation sites] --> A1[Local static pressure P_local measured]
    A1 --> A2{P_local versus P_v comparison}
    A2 -->|P_local greater than P_v| A3[No cavitation: stable liquid flow]
    A2 -->|P_local equal to P_v| A4[Cavitation inception: first bubble appears]
    A2 -->|P_local less than P_v| A5[Flash evaporation: cavity grows in low-P zone]
    A5 --> A6[Cavity convects downstream into higher-P region]
    A6 --> A7[Condensation of vapour: cavity collapses violently]
    A7 --> A8[Micro-jet and shockwave impact on solid surface]
    A8 --> A9[Consequence 1: Metal erosion and pitting]
    A8 --> A10[Consequence 2: Noise and vibration]
    A8 --> A11[Consequence 3: Thrust loss and efficiency drop]
    A8 --> A12[Consequence 4: Blade-rate hull pressure pulses]
    A4 --> A6
    A3 --> A13[Safe steady operation]
    A9 --> A14[Maintenance cost and downtime]
    A10 --> A15[Acoustic signature increase]
    A11 --> A16[Propulsion performance penalty]
    A12 --> A17[Crew comfort and structural fatigue]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Cavitation Number Decision Tree

A schematic showing how $\sigma$ is computed and used in the design decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Start: Ship and propeller geometry] --> B1[Collect: V_inf, h_s, T, P_atm, P_v]
    B1 --> B2[Compute P_infty as P_atm plus rho g h_s]
    B2 --> B3[Compute dynamic pressure as half rho V_inf squared]
    B3 --> B4[Compute sigma = numerator over denominator]
    B4 --> B5{Compare sigma with sigma_c}
    B5 -->|sigma greater than sigma_c| B6[Cavitation free operation]
    B5 -->|sigma equal to sigma_c| B7[Inception: critical condition]
    B5 -->|sigma less than sigma_c| B8[Developed cavitation: redesign required]
    B6 --> B9[Document margin and proceed]
    B7 --> B10[Flag for design review]
    B8 --> B11[Modify section, depth, or RPM]
    B11 --> B12[Re-iterate computation]
    B12 --> B4
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Pressure Variation Around a Propeller Blade Section

A block-level schematic of the **suction-side pressure dip** that causes cavitation. (Adapted as functional architecture per protocol.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Free stream at infinity] --> C1[Reference static pressure P_infty]
    C1 --> C2[Leading edge stagnation: P_max]
    C2 --> C3[Suction peak: minimum pressure C_p_min]
    C3 --> C4[Diffuser region: pressure recovery]
    C4 --> C5[Trailing edge base pressure]
    C5 --> C6[Wake mixing downstream]
    C3 --> C7{P_min versus P_v check}
    C7 -->|P_min greater than P_v| C8[Attached flow: no cavitation]
    C7 -->|P_min less than or equal to P_v| C9[Cavitation: bubble forms at suction peak]
    C9 --> C10[Cavity grows along chord]
    C10 --> C11[Cavity collapses in pressure recovery region]
    C11 --> C12[Sheet cloud cavitation pattern]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024, PCNST302, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a). Define vapour pressure of a liquid. Why is the vapour pressure of sea water slightly lower than that of fresh water at the same temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Vapour pressure $P_v$ is the absolute pressure exerted by the vapour of a liquid when it is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its own liquid phase at a given temperature. Below $P_v$, the liquid boils at that temperature irrespective of external heating. **(1 mark)**
>
> Sea water contains dissolved salts (≈ 35 ‰ salinity). The dissolved solute molecules **reduce the escaping tendency** of water molecules from the liquid surface into the vapour phase, because the salt ions occupy surface sites and bind water molecules more strongly. **(1 mark)**
>
> By Raoult's law, the vapour pressure of an ideal solution is the product of the pure solvent vapour pressure and the mole fraction of the solvent; since the mole fraction of water in sea water is less than 1, $P_v^{sea} < P_v^{fresh}$ (typically by about **1.8 % to 2.0 %** at room temperature). **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023, PCNST302, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b). State the condition for cavitation inception in a flowing liquid and define the cavitation number.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Cavitation inception occurs when the **local static pressure** in the liquid falls to (or below) the **local vapour pressure** of the liquid at the ambient temperature, i.e., $P_{local} \le P_v$. **(1 mark)**
>
> The **cavitation number** $\sigma$ is a dimensionless parameter defined as the ratio of the pressure margin above vapour pressure to the free-stream dynamic pressure:
> $$\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$$ **(1 mark)**
>
> Cavitation inception for a given body shape occurs at a **critical cavitation number** $\sigma_c$, which is the negative of the minimum pressure coefficient: $\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min}$. Flow is cavitation-free when $\sigma > \sigma_c$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024, PCNST302, Q2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q2(a). [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the phenomenon of cavitation in a marine propeller. List the four main types of cavitation observed on ship propellers.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition and Mechanism (3 marks):**
Cavitation on a marine propeller is the formation of vapour-filled cavities on the **suction (low-pressure) side** of the blades when the local static pressure, due to high local velocity (Bernoulli effect), falls below the vapour pressure $P_v$ of the sea water. As the cavity is convected into a higher-pressure region downstream of the blade, it collapses violently, generating high local pressures, micro-jets, and shock waves. These collapse events erode the blade material (commonly manganese–aluminium bronze, NAB, or Ni–Al–bronze) and produce a characteristic crackling noise, vibration, and efficiency loss.

**Sketch description (2 marks):**
A propeller blade section showing the chord line, suction surface (low pressure peak), pressure surface (high pressure), the vapour cavity attached to the suction side, and the collapse region near the trailing edge. (Refer to Diagram 3 in Section 4 for the schematic.)

**Four Main Types of Propeller Cavitation (2 marks):**
1. **Sheet (or Bubble) Cavitation** — A thin vapour sheet attached to the suction side at moderate $\sigma$ values; most common type.
2. **Vortex (or Vortex) Cavitation** — Cavities form in the **tip and root vortices** shed from blade ends; occurs at slightly higher $\sigma$ than sheet cavitation.
3. **Cloud (or Wake) Cavitation** — Large, unsteady vapour clouds that form in the non-uniform wake of the ship hull, breaking off in a periodic manner.
4. **Supercavitating Cavitation** — The cavity extends well beyond the trailing edge, enclosing the entire section; occurs at very low $\sigma$; used deliberately in supercavitating torpedoes and high-speed propellers.

---

**Q2(b). [7 marks]** A surface ship operates in sea water at $T = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ with a propeller at a mean submergence of $h_s = 4.0$ m. Atmospheric pressure is $101.3$ kPa, ship speed $V = 15$ knots, and the propeller blade section has a critical pressure coefficient $C_{p,\,min} = -1.20$. Density of sea water = $1025$ kg/m³. Determine:
(i) Vapour pressure of sea water at 25 °C.
(ii) Cavitation number of the propeller.
(iii) Whether cavitation will occur.

**Given:** $T = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, $h_s = 4.0$ m, $P_{atm} = 101{,}300$ Pa, $V = 15$ knots, $C_{p,\,min} = -1.20$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert ship speed (0.5 mark):**

$$V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Vapour pressure of fresh water at 25 °C via Magnus-Tetens (1 mark):**

$$P_v^{fresh} = 0.61078 \times \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3}\right) = 0.61078 \times \exp(1.6496) = 0.61078 \times 5.205 = 3.179\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Sea-water correction (0.5 mark):**

$$P_v^{sea} = 0.982 \times 3.179 = 3.122\,\text{kPa} = 3122\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Local static pressure at propeller depth (1 mark):**

$$P_{\infty} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_s = 101{,}300 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.0$$

$$\rho g h_s = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 = 40{,}221\,\text{Pa}$$

$$P_{\infty} = 101{,}300 + 40{,}221 = 141{,}521\,\text{Pa} \approx 141.5\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Dynamic pressure (0.5 mark):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.716)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 = 30{,}514\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Cavitation number (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \frac{141{,}521 - 3{,}122}{30{,}514} = \frac{138{,}399}{30{,}514} = 4.535$$

**Step 7 — Critical cavitation number (0.5 mark):**

$$\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min} = -(-1.20) = 1.20$$

**Step 8 — Decision (1 mark):**

Since $\sigma = 4.535 > \sigma_c = 1.20$, the flow is **comfortably cavitation-free** at the given operating condition. The margin ratio is $4.535/1.20 \approx 3.78$, which is a very safe margin.

**Conclusion:** No cavitation will occur. **(0.5 mark for stating the conclusion clearly)**

**Step 9 — Bonus: Critical speed for inception (0.5 mark extra credit if asked):**

$$V_{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{atm} + \rho g h_s - P_v)}{\rho \sigma_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 138{,}399}{1025 \times 1.20}} = \sqrt{224.94} \approx 15.0\,\text{m/s} \approx 29.2\,\text{knots}$$

So the propeller can theoretically reach ≈ 29 knots before cavitation inception — a very robust design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023, PCNST302, Q2 Alternate]**

**Q2(a) alt. [7 marks]** Derive, from first principles using Bernoulli's equation, an expression for the cavitation number of a propeller blade section. Show that the critical cavitation number equals the negative of the minimum pressure coefficient of the section.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation along a streamline from the free stream to a point on the blade (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} = P_{local} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{local}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_{local}$ (1 mark):**

$$P_{local} = P_{\infty} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_{\infty}^{2} - V_{local}^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Define the pressure coefficient $C_p$ (1 mark):**

$$C_p = \frac{P_{local} - P_{\infty}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{V_{local}}{V_{\infty}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — State the cavitation inception condition: minimum local pressure equals $P_v$ (1 mark):**

At the point of lowest pressure (highest suction), $C_p$ attains its minimum $C_{p,\,min}$, and:

$$P_{local,\,min} = P_v$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the definition of $\sigma$ (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_{local,\,min}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} = -\,C_{p,\,min}$$

**Step 6 — State the final result and physical interpretation (1.5 marks):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min}}$$

The critical cavitation number of a given blade profile is uniquely determined by its minimum pressure coefficient (which is itself a function of angle of attack, camber, and thickness ratio). Profiles with **high camber** and **large thickness** have more negative $C_{p,\,min}$ and therefore require **lower $\sigma$** (i.e., more cavitation-prone) to operate without cavitation. This is why highly loaded, thick-section propeller blades (typical of low-speed, high-thrust applications) are more prone to cavitation than thin, lightly cambered sections.

---

**Q2(b) alt. [7 marks]** Explain the concept of Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) for a centrifugal pump. A centrifugal pump draws sea water at $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($P_v = 4.17$ kPa absolute). The pump suction line is at a depth of $1.5$ m below the sea water surface. The pipe diameter is $0.10$ m, the volume flow rate is $0.020$ m³/s, and the friction head loss in the suction line is $0.30$ m of water. Atmospheric pressure is $101.0$ kPa. Determine the NPSH available and comment on whether cavitation is likely if the pump's required NPSH is $2.5$ m.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept of NPSH (1.5 marks):**

> NPSH is the **total head available at the pump suction flange above the vapour pressure head**, expressed in metres of liquid. It is the margin by which the suction-side absolute pressure exceeds the vapour pressure, accounting for elevation, velocity head, and friction losses. NPSH exists in two forms: **NPSH available** (system-side, computed from installation geometry) and **NPSH required** (machine-side, given by the manufacturer as a function of flow rate). Cavitation is avoided when:
> $$\text{NPSH}_{av} \ge \text{NPSH}_{req} + \text{safety margin (typically 0.5 m to 1.0 m)}$$

**Given Data:** $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$, $P_v = 4.17\,\text{kPa} = 4170\,\text{Pa}$, depth $z = 1.5$ m below surface, $D = 0.10$ m, $Q = 0.020$ m³/s, $h_f = 0.30$ m, $P_{atm} = 101{,}000$ Pa, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\text{NPSH}_{req} = 2.5$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute the pipe cross-sectional area and velocity (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.020}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity head (0.5 mark):**

$$\frac{V^{2}}{2g} = \frac{(2.546)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.482}{19.62} = 0.3304\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Express the NPSH available equation (0.5 mark):**

$$\text{NPSH}_{av} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + z - h_f - \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

> Note: The pump is located at depth $z = 1.5$ m **below** the free surface, so the static submergence **adds** to the available head (positive contribution). The velocity head is **subtracted** because high velocity at the suction flange reduces static pressure (Bernoulli). Friction loss is also subtracted.

**Step 4 — Compute the pressure-head term (1 mark):**

$$\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{101{,}000 - 4{,}170}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{96{,}830}{10{,}055} = 9.629\,\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Sum all contributions (1 mark):**

$$\text{NPSH}_{av} = 9.629 + 1.5 - 0.30 - 0.3304 = 10.499\,\text{m}$$

$$\text{NPSH}_{av} \approx 10.50\,\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Decision (1 mark):**

$$\text{NPSH}_{av} = 10.50\,\text{m} \gg \text{NPSH}_{req} = 2.5\,\text{m}$$

**Margin:** $10.50 - 2.50 = 8.00$ m — a very large margin. **Cavitation is highly unlikely.** The pump installation is robustly designed against cavitation, with substantial reserve to accommodate higher temperatures (higher $P_v$) or future flow-rate increases.

**Step 7 — Critical submergence check (0.5 mark, bonus):**

The minimum submergence $z_{min}$ for incipient cavitation (i.e., $\text{NPSH}_{av} = \text{NPSH}_{req}$) would be:

$$z_{min} = \text{NPSH}_{req} - \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + h_f + \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

$$z_{min} = 2.5 - 9.629 + 0.30 + 0.3304 = -6.499\,\text{m}$$

A **negative** value means even with the pump at the free surface (or above it!) cavitation would not occur under these conditions. The pump is grossly oversized for this installation from a cavitation standpoint.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Mixing absolute and gauge pressure (Most common error, 1 to 2 marks lost):** Always convert gauge pressure to absolute before computing $\sigma$. KTU answer scripts frequently compute $P_{atm}$ as 0 instead of 101.3 kPa, giving wildly wrong answers.
> 2. **Wrong vapour pressure for sea water:** Students often quote $P_v$ of fresh water and forget the salinity correction. In 14-mark numerical questions, this loses 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in NPSH:** NPSH is in **metres of head**, not in Pascals. Converting incorrectly is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting velocity head in NPSH:** The $V^{2}/(2g)$ term is often dropped by students. It contributes 0.3–0.5 m in typical problems and is essential.
> 5. **Sign convention in $C_p$:** $C_{p,\,min}$ is **negative** on the suction side. Writing $\sigma_c = +C_{p,\,min}$ instead of $\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min}$ yields a wrong critical value.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert knots to m/s:** Ship speed is almost always given in knots in KTU problems; 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. This conversion is a 0.5-mark step but is frequently omitted.
> 7. **Reporting $\sigma$ without units is correct** (it is dimensionless), but reporting pressure in bar or m of water without stating the reference causes mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme, PCNST302, Module 1**

- ☐ **Vapour pressure $P_v$** is the equilibrium pressure of vapour above its liquid at a given temperature. It depends **only on temperature** (and weakly on salinity/solutes).
- ☐ **Cavitation** = local pressure $\le P_v$ → bubble forms → bubble collapses in higher-pressure region → erosion, noise, vibration, thrust loss.
- ☐ **Cavitation number** $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{\infty} - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ — **both pressures must be absolute**.
- ☐ **Critical cavitation number** $\sigma_c = -C_{p,\,min}$ — depends only on the body profile and angle of attack.
- ☐ Flow regime: $\sigma > \sigma_c$ → cavitation-free; $\sigma = \sigma_c$ → inception; $\sigma < \sigma_c$ → developed cavitation.
- ☐ **Salinity effect:** $P_v^{sea} \approx 0.982\,P_v^{fresh}$.
- ☐ **Magnus-Tetens formula:** $P_v^{fresh} = 0.61078\,\exp\!\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ kPa, with $T$ in °C.
- ☐ **NPSH available** = $\frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} + z_{suction} - h_f - \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$; must exceed **NPSH required** by ≈ 0.5–1.0 m.
- ☐ **Four types** of propeller cavitation: **sheet, vortex, cloud (wake), supercavitating**.
- ☐ **Effects of cavitation:** erosion, noise, vibration, thrust loss, blade-rate pressure pulses, efficiency drop.
- ☐ **Mitigation:** increase submergence, reduce RPM, choose thinner/cambered section, gear reduction, supercavitating section, tip-venting, surface coatings (NAB).
- ☐ **Speed conversion:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 m/s ≈ 1.944 knots.
- ☐ **Pressure conversion:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.332 m of fresh water = 10.08 m of sea water.
- ☐ **Always state assumptions** clearly in KTU 14-mark answers: steady flow, incompressible, inviscid, absolute pressure reference, isothermal (single-temperature) flow, hydrostatic free-stream pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure at a Point in a Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure at a Point in a Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Definition of Fluid Pressure:** Fluid pressure at a point is defined as the **normal compressive force per unit area** exerted by a fluid on an infinitesimal surface element surrounding that point. Mathematically,
> $$P = \lim_{\delta A \to 0} \frac{\delta F}{\delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$
> where $P$ is the pressure in $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pascal, Pa), $\delta F$ is the normal force component acting on the elemental area $\delta A$. 

In a static fluid, the resultant force exerted by the fluid on any surface is always **normal to that surface** because a fluid at rest cannot sustain shear stress. The pressure at a point in a static fluid is **isotropic** — meaning it has the same magnitude in every direction through that point, irrespective of the orientation of the imaginary surface element chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Isotropy of Pressure (Pascal's Legacy):** At any point within a static fluid, the pressure is identical in all directions. This is one of the most fundamental postulates of fluid statics and is proven rigorously using the equilibrium of a small triangular fluid element. It forms the basis for the design of hydraulic presses, ship stabilizers, and depth-control systems on submarines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge and Honey" Intuition

Imagine you place a sealed plastic bag (the "imaginary surface element") inside a tank of thick honey. No matter how you rotate that bag — upright, sideways, tilted at an angle — the honey pushes on every face of the bag with **exactly the same force per unit area**. That is precisely what isotropy of pressure means.

A simpler analogy: think of standing in a swimming pool. You feel **water pressure on your eardrums** increase as you go deeper, but you do **not** feel the pressure differently on your right ear versus your left ear — it pushes uniformly from all sides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Naval Architects:** When a submarine descends, the **hull experiences the same pressure** from every direction at any given depth. This is why submarines are built as **cylindrical pressure vessels** — the shape efficiently distributes uniform radial pressure. Surface ships, however, experience a *gradient* of pressure with depth, which produces the **buoyancy force** essential for floating.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold as Required)

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard value used in KTU numericals)
- **Density of fresh water at 4°C:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of sea water (standard):** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of fresh water:** $\gamma_{fw} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of sea water:** $\gamma_{sw} = 10055 \text{ N/m}^3$ (≈ $10.05 \text{ kN/m}^3$)
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** In numerical problems, unless otherwise specified, the examiner expects $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and the working fluid to be **fresh water** ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). For naval architecture problems involving the sea, use $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
