> 2. **Sign Confusion in Manometer Equations:** Mixing up $P_A - P_B$ vs. $P_B - P_A$. Always draw the U-tube clearly and indicate which side is **higher/lower** before writing the equation. (Loss: 2–3 marks per question.)
>
> 3. **Using $P_{abs}$ Instead of $P_{gauge}$ for Force Calculations:** The differential force on a submerged body uses $P_{gauge}$ (or the differential pressure across the surface), NOT the absolute pressure. The atmosphere acts on the other side. (Loss: 2–4 marks per question.)
>
> 4. **Wrong Choice of Density in Manometer Equations:** Using $\rho_m$ where $\rho_f$ should be (or vice versa). Remember: $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ in the differential manometer equation. (Loss: 1–3 marks per question.)
>
> 5. **Incomplete Sketch in Theory Questions:** Bourdon tube, inclined manometer, and dead-weight tester MUST have labelled sketches. An unlabelled diagram earns 0 marks for the figure component. (Loss: 2–4 marks per question.)

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pressure is a scalar** (no direction), measured in **Pa = N/m²**.
- **Pascal's Law** is the foundation: pressure transmits undiminished in a confined static fluid.
- **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = \rho g h$ — independent of container shape, only depends on **vertical depth**.
- **Absolute pressure identity:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
- **Differential manometer core equation:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$.
- **Inclined manometer magnification:** Effective sensitivity increases by factor $1/\sin\theta$.
- **Sea water density** (standard): $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ (always use this for marine problems unless stated otherwise).
- **Quick rule for marine draft-to-gauge-pressure:** $P_{gauge} \text{ (bar)} \approx 0.1 \times d \text{ (m)}$ in seawater.
- **Bourdon tube** is the most common marine mechanical gauge; **diaphragm gauges** for low pressures; **dead-weight tester** for calibration.
- **Hydrostatic force on a vertical rectangular plate from top edge:** $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho g b H^2$ acting at depth $\frac{2H}{3}$ from the top.
- **Atmospheric pressure standard:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mmHg} = 10.33 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}$.
- **For net thrust on a submerged body, use gauge pressure**, not absolute.
- **Centre of pressure** is always **below the centroid** for a submerged surface with the free surface above.
- **Ship applications** of pressure measurement: draft reading, depth sounding, boiler monitoring, bilge detection, tank sounding, and weather forecasting (barometer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic Forces on Submerged Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrostatic Forces on Submerged Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In naval hydrostatics, a **submerged body** is any solid object fully immersed in a static fluid (or partially immersed, such that hydrostatic pressure acts on its wetted surface). **Hydrostatic forces on submerged bodies** refer to the system of pressure-induced forces exerted by the fluid on the wetted surface of the body. These forces are characterised by:

- A **resultant hydrostatic force** $F_R$ (in Newtons, **N**), equal to the integral of gauge pressure over the wetted area.
- A **point of application** called the **Center of Pressure (CP)**, which generally lies *below* the geometric centroid for a horizontal fluid surface.
- A **buoyant force** $F_B$ equal to the weight of fluid displaced, acting vertically upward through the **Center of Buoyancy (CB)**.

For naval architects, this is the foundational analysis behind **hull pressure loading**, **gate and hatch design**, **bilge keel loads**, and **ship intact stability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly treats hydrostatic force on *plane horizontal, vertical, and inclined surfaces*, *curved surfaces*, and *submerged bodies in multiple fluids*. Mastery of $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ and force-moment decomposition is mandatory for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge in the Bathtub"

Imagine pushing a rectangular wooden board underwater in a bathtub, holding it at an angle. You immediately feel a much stronger push on the lower edge than the upper edge. Why? Because the water column sitting *above* the lower edge is taller and therefore *heavier*. The total push the water exerts on the board is the **resultant hydrostatic force**, and where that push effectively "concentrates" is the **center of pressure**.

Now, picture a sealed plastic toy duck floating in the same bathtub. The water pushes *up* on the bottom of the duck and *down* on its top — but since the bottom is deeper, the upward push is *greater*. The net upward push (the **buoyant force**) equals exactly the weight of water the duck displaces. This is **Archimedes' Principle**, the cornerstone of naval architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Specific weight of fresh water: $\gamma_{fw} = \mathbf{9810\ N/m^3}$
> - Specific weight of sea water: $\gamma_{sw} = \mathbf{10050\ N/m^3}$ to $\mathbf{10250\ N/m^3}$ (typically **$\mathbf{10050\ N/m^3}$** for KTU numericals)
> - Mass density of fresh water: $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$
> - Mass density of sea water: $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$

### 1.3 The Two Pillars of Submerged-Body Hydrostatics

| Pillar | Definition | Naval Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrostatic Pressure** | Scalar pressure acting normal to every wetted surface element | Hull plate scantling, bulkhead design |
| **Buoyancy (Archimedes)** | Net upward force = weight of displaced fluid | Draft, displacement tonnage, reserve buoyancy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pressure Prism Concept

A **pressure prism** is a visual aid used by naval architects to compute the resultant force and its line of action. For a plane surface of width $b$ immersed vertically in a fluid:

- The pressure at depth $h$ is $P = \rho g h$.
- The pressure distribution forms a **trapezoidal prism** whose volume equals the resultant force.

The volume of the prism = (Area of pressure diagram) × (width of surface).

### 2.2 Force on a Plane Surface — The Master Equation

For a plane surface of area $A$ inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal:

$$
F_R = \rho \, g \, \bar{h} \, A = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A
$$

where $\bar{h}$ is the **vertical depth of the centroid** of the surface below the free surface.

The **center of pressure depth** measured along the inclined plane from the free surface intersection is:

$$
h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{h} \, A} \sin^{2}\theta
$$

where $I_{xc}$ is the **second moment of area** of the surface about its own centroidal axis (parallel to the free surface).

### 2.3 Force on a Curved Surface

A curved surface is decomposed into:

- **Horizontal component** $F_H$ = pressure force on the vertical projection of the surface
- **Vertical component** $F_V$ = weight of fluid directly above (or virtual fluid column) the surface

$$
F_R = \sqrt{F_H^{2} + F_V^{2}}
$$

The direction $\alpha$ from horizontal is:

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H}
$$

### 2.4 Buoyancy and Archimedes' Principle

For a fully submerged body of volume $V$:

$$
F_B = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{displaced}
$$

For a **floating body** (partially submerged):

$$
F_B = W_{body} = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{submerged}
$$

### 2.5 Stability — Metacentric Height

For a floating body tilted by a small angle $\phi$:

- **Center of Gravity (G)** — fixed point inside body
- **Center of Buoyancy (CB)** — moves to a new position **B'**
- **Metacenter (M)** — intersection of vertical through new B' and original vertical through B
- **Metacentric height** $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$

**Stability Criterion:** $\overline{GM} > 0 \Rightarrow$ Stable; $\overline{GM} = 0 \Rightarrow$ Neutral; $\overline{GM} < 0 \Rightarrow$ Unstable.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM QUICK-REFERENCE TABLE** — All formulas below are high-yield for KTU ESE Module 1.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa ($N/m^2$) | $P_0$ = atmospheric/ambient |
| 2 | Gauge pressure | $P_g = \rho g h$ | Pa | Used in most KTU numericals |
| 3 | Force on plane surface | $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | N | $\bar{h}$ = centroid depth |
| 4 | Center of pressure (inclined) | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{\bar{h} A}\sin^{2}\theta$ | m | Below free surface along incline |
| 5 | Force on vertical surface | $F_R = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | N | $\bar{h} = h/2$ for rectangle from top |
| 6 | Horizontal force on curved surface | $F_H = \gamma h_c A_{proj}$ | N | $h_c$ = centroid depth of vertical projection |
| 7 | Vertical force on curved surface | $F_V = \gamma V_{fluid\ above}$ | N | Real or imaginary fluid weight |
| 8 | Resultant on curved surface | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^{2} + F_V^{2}}$ | N | Pythagorean combination |
| 9 | Buoyant force | $F_B = \gamma V_{displaced}$ | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 10 | Apparent weight | $W_{app} = W - F_B$ | N | For submerged body in fluid |
| 11 | Metacentric radius | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{disp}}$ | m | $I_T$ = transverse waterplane area 2nd moment |
| 12 | Metacentric height | $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ | m | Stability criterion: $> 0$ stable |
| 13 | Second moment — rectangle (about base) | $I_{xc} = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | $m^{4}$ | $b$ = width, $h$ = height (along slope) |
| 14 | Second moment — rectangle (about base axis) | $I_{base} = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{3}$ | $m^{4}$ | Used for top-edge reference |
| 15 | Second moment — circle (diameter $D$) | $I_{xc} = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$ | $m^{4}$ | Centroidal axis |
| 16 | Submerged body in two fluids | $F_B = \gamma_1 V_1 + \gamma_2 V_2$ | N | Layered fluids (e.g., oil over water) |
| 17 | Pressure prism volume | $V_{prism} = F_R$ | N | Force = volume of pressure diagram |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **naval architecture context**, hydrostatic forces govern:

- **Hull plate thickness** selection — a deeper-draft vessel experiences greater pressure at the keel, requiring thicker plating.
- **Bulkhead and tank design** — internal watertight bulkheads must resist the lateral pressure head of any fluid they contain.
- **Docking and grounding reactions** — when a ship rests on the seabed, hydrostatic pressure on the hull must be balanced by the seabed reaction.
- **Submarine integrity** — collapse depth calculations rely directly on hydrostatic pressure $P = \rho g h$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Force on a Vertical Rectangular Surface

**Problem Setup:** A vertical rectangular gate of width $b$ and height $h$ has its top edge at the free surface of a liquid of specific weight $\gamma$. Derive the resultant force and the depth of the center of pressure.

**Step 1 — Pressure variation with depth.**

At a depth $y$ below the free surface, the hydrostatic pressure is:

$$
P(y) = \gamma \, y
$$

**Step 2 — Differential force on an infinitesimal strip.**

Consider a horizontal strip at depth $y$ of thickness $dy$ and width $b$. Its area is $dA = b \, dy$. The differential force on this strip is:

$$
dF = P(y) \, dA = \gamma \, y \, b \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the full depth.**

Total resultant force:

$$
F_R = \int_{0}^{h} \gamma \, y \, b \, dy = \gamma \, b \left[ \frac{y^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{h} = \gamma \, b \, \frac{h^{2}}{2}
$$

Simplify using $A = b h$ (area of rectangle):

$$
F_R = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = \gamma \cdot \frac{h}{2} \cdot b h
$$

This confirms the master equation, with $\bar{h} = h/2$ being the centroid depth.

**Step 4 — First moment of the force about the free surface (numerator for CP).**

The depth of the center of pressure is found from moment equivalence:

$$
F_R \cdot h_{cp} = \int_{0}^{h} y \, dF = \int_{0}^{h} y \, (\gamma \, y \, b) \, dy = \gamma \, b \int_{0}^{h} y^{2} \, dy
$$

$$
F_R \cdot h_{cp} = \gamma \, b \, \left[ \frac{y^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{h} = \gamma \, b \, \frac{h^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_{cp}$.**

$$
h_{cp} = \frac{\gamma \, b \, h^{3} / 3}{\gamma \, b \, h^{2} / 2} = \frac{h^{3}/3}{h^{2}/2} = \frac{2h}{3}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
F_R = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \gamma \, b \, h^{2} \quad \text{and} \quad h_{cp} = \tfrac{2h}{3}
$$

This shows that for a rectangle with top edge at the free surface, the center of pressure lies **one-third of the height above the bottom** — a classic KTU result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Center of Pressure on an Inclined Plane Surface

**Problem Setup:** A plane surface inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal has its top edge at depth $h_1$ and its centroid at depth $\bar{h}$. Derive the general CP formula.

**Step 1 — Geometry along the inclined plane.**

Let $y$ be the slant distance measured from the intersection of the plane with the free surface. The vertical depth corresponding to slant distance $y$ is:

$$
h = y \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at slant position $y$.**

$$
P(y) = \gamma \, h = \gamma \, y \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Force on a strip of width $b$ and slant length $dy$.**

$$
dF = \gamma \, y \sin\theta \cdot b \, dy
$$

**Step 4 — Total force.**

$$
F_R = \int_{0}^{L} \gamma \, y \sin\theta \, b \, dy = \gamma \sin\theta \, b \, \frac{L^{2}}{2}
$$

Recognising $A = b L$ and $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$:

$$
F_R = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A
$$

This is the general master equation, valid for any orientation.

**Step 5 — Center of pressure slant distance.**

Take moments about the free-surface intersection (top of plane):

$$
F_R \, y_{cp} = \int_{0}^{L} y \, dF = \gamma \sin\theta \int_{0}^{L} y^{2} \, b \, dy = \gamma \sin\theta \, b \, \frac{L^{3}}{3}
$$

Using $I_{xc} = b L^{3} / 12$ and $A = b L$:

$$
F_R \, y_{cp} = \frac{\gamma \sin\theta \, b \, L^{3}}{3} = \gamma \sin\theta \left( \frac{b L^{3}}{12} \cdot 4 \right) = 4 \gamma \sin\theta \, I_{xc}
$$

Dividing by $F_R = \gamma \bar{y} \sin\theta \, A$:

$$
y_{cp} = \frac{4 I_{xc}}{\bar{y} A} = \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{y} A / 4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,y_{cp} = \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{y} A} \cdot \frac{\text{(shifted from centroid)}}{\text{geometry}}\,}
$$

After rigorous algebra (using parallel-axis theorem to express in terms of $\bar{y}$):

$$
y_{cp} - \bar{y} = \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{y} A}
$$

Therefore the **slant distance of CP from the centroid** is:

$$
y_{cp} = \bar{y} + \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{y} A}
$$

Converting to vertical depth using $h_{cp} = y_{cp} \sin\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc} \sin^{2}\theta}{\bar{h} A}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the center of pressure on an inclined plane surface.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Buoyancy on a Submerged Body in Layered Fluids

**Problem Setup:** A solid of volume $V$ is fully submerged in a two-layer fluid — a top layer of density $\rho_1$ (depth $H_1$) overlying a bottom layer of density $\rho_2$. The body occupies volume $V_1$ in the upper fluid and $V_2$ in the lower fluid. Find the net buoyant force.

**Step 1 — Buoyancy from upper fluid.**

$$
F_{B1} = \rho_1 \, g \, V_1
$$

**Step 2 — Buoyancy from lower fluid.**

$$
F_{B2} = \rho_2 \, g \, V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Net vertical buoyancy (both upward).**

$$
F_B = F_{B1} + F_{B2} = \rho_1 g V_1 + \rho_2 g V_2
$$

In compact form using specific weights $\gamma_i = \rho_i g$:

$$
\boxed{\,F_B = \gamma_1 V_1 + \gamma_2 V_2\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent density.** If the body were immersed in a single fluid, the equivalent density $\rho_{eq}$ is:

$$
\rho_{eq} = \frac{F_B}{g V} = \frac{\rho_1 V_1 + \rho_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Force Formula by Geometry

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix during the KTU ESE to choose the correct formula based on surface geometry — a common source of lost marks.

| Geometry | Orientation | Force Formula | CP Formula | Typical Naval Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plane rectangle | Vertical, top at surface | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \gamma b h^{2}$ | $h_{cp} = \tfrac{2h}{3}$ | Sluice gate, dock entrance |
| Plane rectangle | Vertical, top submerged | $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc}}{\bar{h} A}$ | Submerged hatch |
| Plane rectangle | Inclined | $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^{2}\theta}{\bar{h} A}$ | Ship hull plate section |
| Plane circle | Vertical | $F = \gamma \bar{h} A$ | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{\pi D^{4}/64}{\bar{h} \cdot \pi D^{2}/4} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{D^{2}}{16 \bar{h}}$ | Porthole, manhole cover |
| Curved surface | General | $F_H, F_V$ via projections; $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^{2}+F_V^{2}}$ | Line of action through fluid centroid | Ship hull bottom |
| Submerged body | Any | $F_B = \gamma V_{disp}$ | Acts through displaced-fluid centroid | Buoyancy of submarine |
| Floating body | At equilibrium | $F_B = W$ | CB at centroid of submerged volume | Ship displacement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrostatic Force Decomposition Architecture (Block Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision logic** an examiner expects a student to follow when classifying a hydrostatic force problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Wetted Surface Geometry] --> B{Surface Type?}
    B -- Plane --> C[Determine Orientation]
    C --> D{Vertical / Inclined / Horizontal}
    D -- Vertical --> E[Use F = γ h̄ A; h̄ = centroid depth]
    D -- Inclined --> F[Use F = γ h̄ A and CP shift formula]
    D -- Horizontal --> G[F = γ h × A; uniform pressure]
    B -- Curved --> H[Resolve into F_H and F_V]
    H --> I[F_H = γ h_c × A_proj]
    H --> J[F_V = Weight of fluid above / virtual column]
    I --> K[F_R = sqrt of F_H² + F_V²]
    J --> K
    B -- Submerged Body --> L[Apply Archimedes: F_B = γ V_disp]
    L --> M{Stability Required?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Compute GM via KB + BM − KG]
    M -- No --> O[Resultant = F_B upward through CB]
    F --> P[Report F_R and Center of Pressure location]
    E --> P
    G --> P
    K --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Pressure Prism Schematic — Vertical Gate (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:surface
        A["Gate Top Edge"] 
        B["Gate Mid-section"]
        C["Gate Bottom Edge"]
    end
    block:pressure
        D["Pressure = 0"]
        E["Pressure = ρg h/2"]
        F["Pressure = ρg h (Max)"]
    end
    block:arrow
        G["↑ Low Force"]
        H["↑ Moderate Force"]
        I["↑ Maximum Force"]
    end
    A --> D --> G
    B --> E --> H
    C --> F --> I
```

### 4.3 Curved Surface Force Decomposition (Free-Body Logic Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Curved Wetted Surface] --> B[Vertical Projection]
    A --> C[Horizontal Projection]
    B --> D[F_H Horizontal Force on Projection]
    C --> E[F_V Vertical Force equals Fluid Column Weight]
    D --> F[Combine Vectorially]
    E --> F
    F --> G[F_R = sqrt of F_H² + F_V²]
    G --> H[Direction: tan alpha = F_V / F_H]
```

### 4.4 Floating Body Stability — Metacentric Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Body at Equilibrium] --> B[Identify G: Center of Gravity]
    A --> C[Identify B: Center of Buoyancy at displaced volume centroid]
    A --> D[Identify K: Keel reference point]
    B --> E[Body Heels by small angle phi]
    E --> F[Buoyancy shifts: B moves to B']
    F --> G[Vertical through new B' intersects original vertical at M]
    G --> H{Metacentric Height GM}
    H --> I[GM = KB + BM − KG]
    I --> J{GM sign?}
    J -- Positive --> K[STABLE — restoring moment exists]
    J -- Zero --> L[NEUTRAL — no restoring]
    J -- Negative --> M[UNSTABLE — capsizing tendency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block diagram represents a *logical decision pathway* that mirrors how a naval architect decomposes a real hydrostatic problem. The arrows correspond to engineering decisions, not physical forces — this preserves clarity while remaining technically faithful to the KTU 2024 expected solution structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Center of Pressure" for a submerged plane surface. Why does it not coincide with the centroid of the area in general?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Center of Pressure (CP)** is the point on a submerged plane surface where the resultant hydrostatic force is considered to act. In general, it does **not** coincide with the geometric centroid because the hydrostatic pressure distribution is **not uniform** — it varies linearly with depth (governed by $P = \rho g h$). Since the pressure at the lower portion of the surface is greater than at the upper portion, the resultant force effectively shifts **downward** (deeper) relative to the centroid. The shift magnitude is given by $I_{xc} \sin^{2}\theta / (\bar{h} A)$.

*(Word count target for KTU: ~80 words — concise definition with reasoning.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State Archimedes' Principle. For a body of mass 500 kg fully submerged in sea water ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$), compute the buoyant force.**

**Model Answer:**
**Archimedes' Principle:** *Any body fully or partially immersed in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.*

Given: $m = 500\ kg$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$.

$$
F_B = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{body}
$$

We require $V_{body}$. Assuming a body density is not provided in this standard form, the *buoyant force formula requires volume*. For a standard KTU 3-mark problem, the body density is typically given or computed from a simple geometry. Assuming a solid cube of side $0.5\ m$ (volume $V = 0.125\ m^{3}$):

$$
F_B = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.125 = 1256.9\ N
$$

*(Examiner note: If body density $\rho_b$ is given, then $V = m/\rho_b$, and $F_B = (\rho_{sw}/\rho_b) \cdot m \cdot g$.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 1 questions are typically Part B with internal choice. Each carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. Full working must be shown.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** A vertical rectangular gate **3 m wide** and **4 m deep** has its top edge located **2 m below** the free surface of sea water ($\gamma = 10050\ N/m^{3}$). Determine:
1. The magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force on the gate. **(4 marks)**
2. The depth of the center of pressure below the free surface. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** An **inclined circular hatch** of diameter **1.5 m** makes an angle of **60°** with the horizontal. Its centroid is located **5 m** below the free surface of sea water. Calculate the magnitude and location (along the slant from the centroid) of the resultant hydrostatic force. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Centroid depth of gate.**

Top of gate is at $h_{top} = 2\ m$. Gate height $h = 4\ m$.

$$
\bar{h} = h_{top} + \frac{h}{2} = 2 + \frac{4}{2} = 4\ m
$$

**Step 2 — Area of gate.**

$$
A = b \times h = 3 \times 4 = 12\ m^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant force.**

$$
F_R = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = 10050 \times 4 \times 12 = 482400\ N = 482.4\ kN
$$

**Step 4 — Second moment of area about centroidal axis.**

$$
I_{xc} = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{3 \times 4^{3}}{12} = \frac{3 \times 64}{12} = 16\ m^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Depth of center of pressure.**

For a vertical surface, $\theta = 90°$ so $\sin^{2}\theta = 1$:

$$
h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{h} \, A} = 4 + \frac{16}{4 \times 12} = 4 + \frac{16}{48} = 4 + 0.333 = 4.333\ m
$$

**Final Answers (a):** $F_R = 482.4\ kN$; $h_{cp} = 4.333\ m$ below the free surface.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** Circular hatch, $D = 1.5\ m$, $\theta = 60°$, $\bar{h} = 5\ m$, $\gamma = 10050\ N/m^{3}$.

**Step 1 — Area.**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^{2}}{4} = \frac{2.25\pi}{4} = 1.767\ m^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Resultant force.**

$$
F_R = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = 10050 \times 5 \times 1.767 = 88792.5\ N \approx 88.79\ kN
$$

**Step 3 — Second moment of area of circle about centroidal axis.**

$$
I_{xc} = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625}{64} = 0.2485\ m^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Center of pressure shift along the slant.**

$$
\Delta y_{cp} = \frac{I_{xc}}{\bar{y} \, A}, \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\bar{h}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{5}{\sin 60°} = \frac{5}{0.866} = 5.774\ m
$$

$$
\Delta y_{cp} = \frac{0.2485}{5.774 \times 1.767} = \frac{0.2485}{10.203} = 0.02436\ m \approx 24.4\ mm
$$

**Step 5 — Direction of force.**

Since the surface is plane, $F_R$ acts **normal** to the surface at the CP.

**Final Answers (b):** $F_R \approx 88.79\ kN$ acting normal to the hatch, with the CP shifted approximately **24.4 mm** down the slant from the centroid.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a)** A **curved surface** in the form of a quarter-cylinder of radius **2 m** and length (into page) **3 m** is submerged with its straight edges vertical. The top edge is at the water surface and the curved surface bulges outward (away from the water). Determine the magnitude, direction, and line of action of the resultant hydrostatic force per metre length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **floating body** has a displacement volume of **$500\ m^{3}$** in sea water ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$). Its center of gravity $G$ is **1.2 m** above the keel, and the center of buoyancy $B$ at equilibrium is **0.8 m** above the keel. The transverse second moment of the waterplane area is $I_T = 8000\ m^{4}$. Determine the metacentric height $\overline{GM}$ and comment on the stability of the body. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Horizontal force component.**

The vertical projection of the quarter-cylinder is a rectangle of width $2\ m$ (radius) and height $2\ m$ (radius). Centroid depth of this rectangle:

$$
\bar{h}_c = \frac{2}{2} = 1\ m
$$

Area of vertical projection:

$$
A_{proj} = 2 \times 2 = 4\ m^{2} \quad (\text{per metre length})
$$

Wait — for per metre length, the projection area is $2\ m \times 1\ m = 2\ m^{2}$.

$$
F_H = \gamma \, \bar{h}_c \, A_{proj} = 9810 \times 1 \times 2 = 19620\ N \quad \text{(per metre length, fresh water)}
$$

*For sea water:* $F_H = 10050 \times 1 \times 2 = 20100\ N/m$.

**Step 2 — Vertical force component.**

The vertical force equals the weight of the fluid that *would* fill the quarter-cylinder volume (virtual column above the curved surface since the curve bulges outward). Per metre length:

$$
V_{virtual} = \frac{\pi r^{2}}{4} \times 1 = \frac{\pi \times 4}{4} = \pi \approx 3.1416\ m^{3}/m
$$

$$
F_V = \gamma \, V_{virtual} = 10050 \times 3.1416 = 31573\ N/m
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant magnitude.**

$$
F_R = \sqrt{F_H^{2} + F_V^{2}} = \sqrt{20100^{2} + 31573^{2}} = \sqrt{4.040 \times 10^{8} + 9.969 \times 10^{8}}
$$

$$
F_R = \sqrt{1.401 \times 10^{9}} \approx 37430\ N/m \approx 37.43\ kN/m
$$

**Step 4 — Direction.**

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{F_V}{F_H} = \frac{31573}{20100} = 1.571 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 57.5°
$$

measured from the horizontal, pointing into the curved surface (since the surface bulges away from the water).

**Step 5 — Line of action.**

The resultant passes through the **centroid of the virtual fluid volume**, located at the centroid of the quarter-circle area at distance $\bar{y} = 4r / (3\pi) = 4 \times 2 / (3\pi) = 0.8488\ m$ from the vertical centreline of the cylinder.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Metacentric radius $\overline{BM}$.**

$$
\overline{BM} = \frac{I_T}{V_{disp}} = \frac{8000}{500} = 16\ m
$$

**Step 2 — Distance $\overline{KB}$.**

$B$ is **0.8 m** above the keel, so $\overline{KB} = 0.8\ m$.

**Step 3 — Distance $\overline{KG}$.**

$G$ is **1.2 m** above the keel, so $\overline{KG} = 1.2\ m$.

**Step 4 — Metacentric height $\overline{GM}$.**

$$
\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} = 0.8 + 16 - 1.2 = 15.6\ m
$$

**Step 5 — Stability comment.**

Since $\overline{GM} = 15.6\ m > 0$, the floating body is **STABLE**. The large positive metacentric height indicates a strong restoring moment for any small heel angle, typical of a well-designed ship-form or large pontoon.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversions** — Marks are routinely deducted when students mix $\gamma$ (specific weight in $N/m^3$) with $\rho g$ (product) inconsistently. Always write the unit at every step.
> 2. **Vertical vs. slant depth** — A common 1–2 mark loss on inclined surface problems is forgetting that $\bar{h}$ (centroid depth) is the *vertical* depth, while CP shift uses the *slant* distance $\bar{y} = \bar{h}/\sin\theta$.
> 3. **Curved surface direction** — Students frequently state the resultant direction incorrectly. Always verify by sketching whether the fluid sits *above* or *below* the curved surface, which determines the sign of $F_V$.
> 4. **Center of pressure formula sign** — CP is **always** below the centroid for surfaces with free fluid above. A negative shift is a clear sign of formula misuse.
> 5. **Stability numericals** — Don't confuse $K$ (keel) with $G$ (gravity). The formula $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ is unforgiving if you swap $G$ and $B$ distances.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: Hydrostatic Forces on Submerged Bodies**

- **Master equation:** $F_R = \gamma \bar{h} A$ — applicable to any plane surface of any orientation. Always identify $\bar{h}$ as the **vertical centroid depth**.
- **Pressure grows linearly with depth:** $P = \rho g h$ — zero at the surface, maximum at the deepest point.
- **Center of pressure shift:** Always lies **deeper** than the centroid on a plane surface with the free surface above. Shift magnitude = $I_{xc} \sin^{2}\theta / (\bar{h} A)$.
- **Vertical rectangle (top at free surface):** $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma b h^{2}$ and $h_{cp} = \tfrac{2h}{3}$ — these are the two most-tested numerical results in KTU.
- **Curved surface rule:** Decompose into $F_H$ (horizontal projection) and $F_V$ (fluid column weight). Combine vectorially.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \gamma V_{disp}$, acting upward through the centroid of the displaced fluid volume (CB).
- **Floating body equilibrium:** $F_B = W$ at rest; CB and G lie on the same vertical at small heel angles.
- **Metacentric formula:** $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ with $\overline{BM} = I_T / V_{disp}$.
- **Stability criterion:** $\overline{GM} > 0$ stable, $= 0$ neutral, $< 0$ unstable.
- **Layered fluids:** Buoyancy is the **sum** of contributions from each layer — do not average densities prematurely.
- **Standard values to memorise:** $\gamma_{sw} = 10050\ N/m^{3}$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^{2}$.
- **Second moments to memorise:** Rectangle (centroidal) = $bh^{3}/12$; Circle (centroidal) = $\pi D^{4}/64$.
- **Pressure prism volume = Resultant force** — useful shortcut for verification in numericals.
- **Apparent weight** of a submerged body = $W - F_B$.
- **KTU exam tactic:** Always draw a free-body diagram, label all depths/areas/angles, and state the formula *before* substitution — examiners reward systematic working.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metacentre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metacentre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentre (M)** is a theoretical point in a floating body (such as a ship) at which a vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy (B') — when the body is tilted by a small angle — intersects the original vertical axis passing through the initial centre of gravity (G) and the initial centre of buoyancy (B). It is the instantaneous centre of rotation of the body for small angular displacements.

In naval architecture, the metacentre is *not* a fixed point on the ship. Its position depends on the **angle of heel (φ)**. For small angles (φ ≤ 10° to 15°), the metacentre can be approximated as a **fixed point** and used for stability analysis.

The **metacentric height (GM)** is defined as the vertical distance between the centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M). It is the single most important geometric parameter governing the **initial transverse stability** of a floating vessel.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balancing Pencil" Concept

Imagine holding a **pencil vertically on your fingertip**. The moment it tilts even slightly, it falls. Now, if you stick a small weight at the top of the pencil (above the pivot point), the pencil becomes highly unstable — it topples easily. However, if you attach a small weight at the *bottom* of the pencil, the pencil resists tilting — it is stable.

A **floating ship** behaves similarly. When it tilts:

- The **buoyancy force** shifts to the new centre of buoyancy (B').
- The line of action of buoyancy and the line of action of weight (gravity) become separated horizontally.
- This separation creates a **restoring couple** (if G is below M) or an **overturning couple** (if G is above M).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Picture a cruise ship tilted slightly by a wave. The further the metacentre (M) is *above* the centre of gravity (G), the more the ship "snaps back" to upright like a self-righting pendulum. If M falls *below* G, the ship behaves like a capsizing brick — it keeps rolling over. This is the famous **"deadship condition"** where GM is negative.

## 1.3 Types of Metacentre

| Type | Symbol | Plane of Tilt | Used For |
|------|--------|---------------|----------|
| **Transverse Metacentre** | **MT** | Tilt about the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis | Rolling stability (most critical) |
| **Longitudinal Metacentre** | **ML** | Tilt about the transverse (port-starboard) axis | Pitching stability (less critical) |

For a typical ship, **ML is much higher than MT** because the waterplane second moment of area is very large in the longitudinal direction (the ship is long and slender fore-aft).

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Notation)

- **B** — Centre of Buoyancy (centroid of displaced volume)
- **G** — Centre of Gravity (centroid of weight distribution)
- **M** — Metacentre
- **KB** — Height of centre of buoyancy above keel
- **KG** — Height of centre of gravity above keel
- **BM** — Metacentric radius (distance B to M)
- **GM** — Metacentric height (distance G to M)
- **I** — Second moment of area of waterplane about the axis of rotation
- **∇** — Displaced volume of the body
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does a Tilted Ship Develop a Restoring Moment?

When a floating body heels (tilts) by a small angle **φ**:

1. The **centre of gravity (G)** does NOT move (mass distribution is fixed to the ship).
2. The **wedge of immersion** on one side and the **wedge of emersion** on the other side cause the **centre of buoyancy (B)** to shift laterally to a new position **B'**.
3. The buoyancy force **Δ × g** (where **Δ** is the displaced mass and **g** is gravitational acceleration) now acts vertically upward through **B'**.
4. The weight **W = mg** acts vertically downward through **G**.
5. The horizontal offset between **B'** and **G** creates a **couple** = W × (horizontal distance).
6. If G lies *below* M, this couple acts to **restore** the ship upright — this is the **restoring couple (GR)**.
7. If G lies *above* M, the couple acts to **overturn** the ship — the ship is unstable.

## 2.2 The Metacentric Radius (BM) — The Core Formula

The metacentric radius for tilt about any axis is given by:

$$\boxed{BM = \dfrac{I}{\forall}}$$

Where:
- **BM** = Metacentric radius (metres)
- **I** = Second moment of area of the waterplane about the axis of rotation (m⁴)
- **∇** (∀) = Volume of displaced water = displaced volume of the floating body (m³)

This is a direct application of the **parallel axis theorem** combined with the **principle of virtual work** (or moment of buoyancy shift = moment of weight of the wedge).

## 2.3 Metacentric Height (GM) — Stability Number

$$\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}$$

Alternatively (the most exam-useful form):

$$\boxed{GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{\forall} - BG}$$

## 2.4 Transverse vs Longitudinal Metacentric Height

For a rectangular waterplane of length **L** and breadth **B**:

| Axis of Tilt | Waterplane Second Moment (I) | Metacentric Radius |
|--------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Transverse (rolling) | $\dfrac{LB^3}{12}$ | $BM_T = \dfrac{LB^3}{12 \forall}$ |
| Longitudinal (pitching) | $\dfrac{BL^3}{12}$ | $BM_L = \dfrac{BL^3}{12 \forall}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **L >> B** for a ship, **BM_L >> BM_T**. Longitudinal stability is almost always satisfied; **transverse stability is the critical design concern**.

## 2.5 Stability Conditions

| Condition | Meaning | Ship Behaviour |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **GM > 0** (positive) | M is above G | **Stable** — restoring moment exists |
| **GM = 0** (neutral) | M coincides with G | **Neutral** — no restoring moment, ship lists indefinitely |
| **GM < 0** (negative) | M is below G | **Unstable** — capsizing couple, ship will roll over |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|-------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $W = \rho g \forall$ | Weight = displaced weight (Archimedes) | Finding displacement |
| 2 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{\forall}$ | Metacentric radius | Core stability formula |
| 3 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | Stability check |
| 4 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Alt form of GM | When BG is given directly |
| 5 | $GR = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$ | Restoring moment at heel angle φ | Righting lever calculation |
| 6 | $I_T = \dfrac{LB^3}{12}$ | Transverse I for rectangle | Rectangular pontoon/float |
| 7 | $I_L = \dfrac{BL^3}{12}$ | Longitudinal I for rectangle | Pitching stability |
| 8 | $I_T = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | Transverse I for circular section | Cylindrical float |
| 9 | $KB = \dfrac{h}{2}$ (rectangle) | For uniform rectangular body | Centroid of half-submerged block |
| 10 | $KB = \dfrac{\pi D}{8}$ (circle) | For half-submerged cylinder | Centroid of semicircle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Ship designers use the metacentric height to determine the ship's **roll period** (using the metacentric formula $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$), passenger comfort, cargo shifting safety, and survivability in damaged condition. Naval architects target a GM of roughly **0.05 to 0.20 m** for ocean-going cargo vessels and **0.5 to 1.5 m** for passenger ships.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metacentric Radius Formula (BM = I / ∇)

### Setup
Consider a floating body heeled by a small angle **φ** about a longitudinal axis. The original waterplane is **WL**. The new waterplane is **W'L'**, inclined at angle φ.

```
        Original WL                    Tilted W'L'
    ┌───────────────┐              ┌───────────────┐ ←── M
    │               │              │       ╲        │      ╱
    │   ●B          │              │        ╲       │     ╱
    │   │           │              │         ╲ B'  │    ╱
    │   │           │              │          ╲    │   ╱
    │   ●G          │              │           ●G  │
    └───────────────┘              └───────────────┘
```

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Geometry of the Wedge**
When the body tilts by angle φ, a small wedge of volume **δV₁** is immersed on one side, and a small wedge of equal volume **δV₂** is emersed on the other side. By the principle of constant displacement (Archimedes), **δV₁ = δV₂ = δV**.

**Step 2: Shift in Centre of Buoyancy**
The horizontal shift **BB'** of the centre of buoyancy can be found by taking moments of the wedge volume about the original centreline axis (O):

Taking the moment of the immersed wedge about the original axis O:

$$BB' = \dfrac{\text{Moment of wedge about axis } O}{\text{Total displaced volume } \forall}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Moment of the Wedge**
Consider an elemental strip of the waterplane at distance **y** from the axis of rotation. Its length (perpendicular to tilt axis) is **dy** and its area is **y · dφ · dy** (since the height of the wedge at distance y is **y · tan φ ≈ y · φ** for small angles).

The volume of this elemental strip is:

$$d(\delta V) = y \tan\phi \cdot dy$$

For small angles, $\tan\phi \approx \phi$:

$$d(\delta V) = y \phi \cdot dy$$

**Step 4: Moment of the Wedge about the Centreline Axis**
The first moment of this elemental area about the axis O is **y · d(δV) = y² · φ · dy**.

Total moment of the wedge = integral over the waterplane area (A):

$$\text{Moment} = \int_A y^2 \phi \, dy = \phi \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area (I)** of the waterplane about the axis of rotation. So:

$$\text{Moment} = \phi \cdot I$$

**Step 5: Horizontal Shift of Centre of Buoyancy**

$$BB' = \dfrac{\phi \cdot I}{\forall}$$

**Step 6: Relating BB' to BM**
From the geometry of the metacentre (M lies on the original vertical through B, and B' lies on the new vertical through M, with M being the intersection):

$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{BB'}{BM}$$

For small angles, $\tan\phi \approx \phi$:

$$\phi = \dfrac{BB'}{BM}$$

Substituting the expression for BB':

$$\phi = \dfrac{\phi \cdot I / \forall}{BM}$$

Solving for BM:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi \cdot BM &= \dfrac{\phi \cdot I}{\forall} \\[6pt]
BM \cdot \forall &= I \\[6pt]
\boxed{BM &= \dfrac{I}{\forall}}
\end{aligned}$$

**This completes the proof.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Restoring Moment (GR = W · GM · sin φ)

### Setup
With the body tilted by angle φ:

```
         M
         │
         │╲ 
         │ ╲  GM
         │  ╲ 
    G ───┤   ╲ 
         │  φ ╲
         │     ╲
         │      •B'
         │
         │  GZ = GM·sinφ
         │
```

The horizontal distance between G and the line of action of buoyancy through B' is:

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$$

This distance **GZ** is called the **righting lever (or righting arm)**.

The restoring moment (couple) is then:

$$\boxed{GR = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi}$$

**Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** From the geometry, triangle GMB' is such that:

$$GZ = GB' \cdot \sin(\angle GB'M)$$

**Step 2:** For small angles, $\angle GB'M \approx \phi$ and $GB' \approx GM$ (M is far above B' for small heel).

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi$$

**Step 4:** The restoring couple = weight × righting lever:

$$GR = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Rectangular Barge

**Problem:** A rectangular barge of length **L = 50 m**, breadth **B = 10 m**, draft **T = 3 m** floats in seawater (**ρ = 1025 kg/m³**). The centre of gravity is **KG = 2.2 m** above the keel. Calculate the metacentric height GM and state whether the barge is stable.

### Step 1: Calculate the Displaced Volume

$$\forall = L \times B \times T = 50 \times 10 \times 3 = 1500 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Weight (Displacement)

$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \forall = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1500 = 15{,}084{,}375 \text{ N} \approx 15.08 \text{ MN}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Second Moment of Area (Transverse)

$$I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^3}{12} = \dfrac{50000}{12} = 4166.67 \text{ m}^4$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Metacentric Radius BM

$$BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\forall} = \dfrac{4166.67}{1500} = 2.778 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5: Calculate KB (Height of Centre of Buoyancy)

For a rectangular body floating at the surface with even keel:

$$KB = \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6: Calculate BG

$$BG = KG - KB = 2.2 - 1.5 = 0.7 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7: Calculate GM

$$GM = BM - BG = 2.778 - 0.7 = 2.078 \text{ m}$$

### Step 8: Stability Verdict

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **GM = +2.078 m > 0**, the metacentre lies **above** the centre of gravity. The barge is **positively stable** with a strong restoring moment. A typical cargo ship requires **0.15 to 0.50 m** of GM; this value is high, indicating a **stiff ship** with rapid roll (potentially uncomfortable).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Metacentre Concept Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Floating Body at Rest"] --> B["Heel by small angle phi"]
    B --> C["Wedge Immersion and Wedge Emersion"]
    C --> D["Centre of Buoyancy Shifts to B prime"]
    D --> E["Vertical line through B prime meets Original Vertical at M"]
    E --> F["Metacentre M identified"]
    F --> G["Compute BM using I divided by forall"]
    G --> H["Compute BG using KG minus KB"]
    H --> I["Compute GM = BM minus BG"]
    I --> J{GM positive or negative or zero}
    J -->|GM greater than 0| K["Stable — Restoring couple"]
    J -->|GM equals 0| L["Neutral — No restoring couple"]
    J -->|GM less than 0| M["Unstable — Capsizing couple"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: KG, KB, I, forall"] --> B["Compute BM = I divided by forall"]
    B --> C["Compute BG = KG minus KB"]
    C --> D["Compute GM = BM minus BG"]
    D --> E{Compare GM with threshold}
    E -->|GM between 0.05 and 0.5| F["Acceptable for cargo ship"]
    E -->|GM between 0.5 and 1.5| G["Acceptable for passenger ship"]
    E -->|GM less than 0.05| H["Too soft — slow roll, cargo shift risk"]
    E -->|GM greater than 1.5| I["Too stiff — rapid roll, passenger discomfort"]
    E -->|GM less than 0| J["UNSTABLE — capsize risk"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Forces and Moments on a Heeled Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPR["Forces on Heeled Ship"]
        W["Weight W acts downward at G"]
        FB["Buoyancy Force delta times g acts upward at B prime"]
    end
    subgraph MOM["Moment Arm"]
        LVR["Righting Lever GZ = GM times sin phi"]
    end
    subgraph RES["Resulting Couple"]
        RM["Restoring Moment GR = W times GZ"]
    end
    W --> LVR
    FB --> LVR
    LVR --> RM
    RM --> OUT{"If GM positive: Ship Rights Itself | If GM negative: Ship Capsizes"}
```

flowchart TD

        YAX["Y axis: Transverse — Port to Starboard direction"]
        ZAX["Z axis: Vertical — Keel Upwards direction"]
    end

        R1["Rotation about X axis"]
    end
