topics.title: Continuity Equation and Its Derivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation and Its Derivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental conservation principle in fluid mechanics that mathematically expresses the **law of conservation of mass** for a flowing fluid. It states that the rate of change of mass within a control volume plus the net rate of mass efflux through the control surface must equal zero, provided there are no sources or sinks of mass within the volume.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme fluid dynamics, the continuity equation is expressed in its most general integral form as:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

For a **steady, incompressible flow** through a stream tube, it reduces to the simple, high-yield form:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation is the *first governing equation* every naval architect and fluid mechanist studies. In ship hydrodynamics, it governs water flow around the hull, propeller disc loading, and the discharge of ballast tanks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **flexible garden hose** with water flowing through it. If you press your thumb partially over the nozzle, the cross-sectional area through which water escapes decreases, and the water squirts out **faster**. If you release your thumb, the area increases, and water flows out **slower**.

What is happening? The *amount* of water entering the hose per second (mass flow rate) is identical to the amount leaving per second — **no water is created or destroyed inside the hose**. So if the pipe narrows, the velocity must increase to "push" the same quantity of water through the smaller opening per unit time.

> **Real-world naval analogy:** As seawater rushes along the curved hull of a moving ship, the hull's curvature narrows the flow channel near the bow shoulder and broad-stern. By continuity, the water must accelerate through these narrow regions, producing a **pressure drop** (Bernoulli coupling) and sometimes visible **wave crests**. This is why the **continuity equation is the bedrock of ship wave-making resistance analysis**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | **kg/m³** |
| $V$ (or $\vec{V}$) | Flow velocity | **m/s** |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area | **m²** |
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | **m³/s** |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass flow rate | **kg/s** |
| $t$ | Time | **s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** *The continuity equation is the mathematical expression of the conservation of mass. It links the velocity field, density field, and geometry of the flow domain. In naval architecture, it is the first equation you write for any flow problem — from pipe flow in bilge systems to open-channel flow in flooded dock gates.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

The continuity equation is not a single formula; it is a **family of equivalent forms** derived from one underlying principle: **mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a closed system**. Let us break down the logical chain:

### Logical Step 1: Establish the Conservation Principle
For any chosen control volume (CV), the net change in mass inside the CV must equal the net mass that crosses the control surface (CS). If mass flows in faster than it flows out, the mass stored inside the CV grows — and vice versa.

### Logical Step 2: Write the Mass Balance Statement
$$
\text{Rate of mass accumulation} = \text{Mass in} - \text{Mass out}
$$

In the limit of zero accumulation (steady flow), mass in = mass out.

### Logical Step 3: Quantify the Mass Flow Through a Cross-Section
The mass crossing an elemental area $dA$ in time $dt$ is $\rho V \, dA \, dt$. Integrating over the entire cross-section yields the total mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.

### Logical Step 4: Apply to a Stream Tube
A **stream tube** is an imaginary bundle of streamlines bounded by a stream surface (no flow crosses it). For any two sections of this stream tube, the mass flow rate must be identical for a steady flow.

### Logical Step 5: Handle the Incompressible Special Case
For liquids (and low-speed gases), $\rho$ = constant. The density cancels out, leaving the elegant form $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.

### Logical Step 6: Generalize to a 3D Differential Form
By taking an **infinitesimal control volume** (a tiny cube of size $dx \, dy \, dz$) and applying the mass balance, we obtain the partial differential equation that holds at *every point* in the flow field.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** The continuity equation is the *first* of the Navier–Stokes family. Without it, you cannot solve *any* flow problem — not pump selection, not propeller design, not wave-pattern prediction.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Applies When | Special Notes |
|---|---------------|-------------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | Steady, 1D stream tube | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Compressible or incompressible, 1D | Mass flow rate is constant |
| 2 | Incompressible, 1D | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\rho$ = constant | Most-used form in naval arch. |
| 3 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = A V$ | Any 1D flow | Units: m³/s |
| 4 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Any 1D flow | Units: kg/s |
| 5 | General differential form | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Any flow | Vector/tensor form |
| 6 | Cartesian expansion | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | Cartesian coordinates | $u, v, w$ are velocity components |
| 7 | Incompressible, differential | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | $\rho$ = constant | Divergence-free velocity field |
| 8 | Cylindrical expansion | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | Incompressible, cylindrical | Used in propeller/rudder analysis |
| 9 | Integral (Reynolds) form | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V}_r \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$ | Unsteady, moving control volume | $\vec{V}_r$ is relative velocity |
| 10 | Stream function (2D) | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | 2D incompressible | Continuity is automatically satisfied |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reading Hint for KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently swap the word *control volume* with *control mass*, and they may ask for the *Reynolds Transport Theorem* form. Memorize both the integral and differential forms — at least one will appear in the ESE.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How Continuity Is Used |
|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Ship hull design** | Predicts velocity acceleration around bilges, affecting pressure and resistance |
| **Propeller disc analysis** | Inflow/outflow through actuator disc uses continuity to relate induction factors |
| **Bilge and ballast piping** | Sizing pumps by computing required $Q$ for emptying tanks in time $T$ |
| **Submarine hydrodynamics** | Cross-flow around control surfaces obeys 2D continuity |
| **Cavitation prediction** | Local pressure drop (from continuity + Bernoulli) yields vapor pressure regions |
| **Wave-structure interaction** | Velocity potential $\phi$ satisfies Laplace equation derived from continuity |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written explicitly. No intermediate results are skipped. Each block of math is followed by a one-line verbal explanation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1D Continuity Equation for a Stream Tube (Integral Form)

**Step 1 — Choose two sections of a stream tube.**
Consider a stream tube with two cross-sections, labelled $1$ and $2$, having areas $A_1$ and $A_2$. Velocities are uniform across each section with magnitudes $V_1$ and $V_2$, and densities are $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mass entering Section 1 in time $dt$.**
In a small time $dt$, the fluid column that crosses Section 1 has length $V_1 \, dt$ and cross-sectional area $A_1$. Its mass is:

$$
dm_1 = \rho_1 \cdot (A_1) \cdot (V_1 \, dt) = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt
$$

*(Explanation: Volume of the column = $A_1 \times V_1 dt$, and mass = density $\times$ volume.)*

**Step 3 — Compute the mass leaving Section 2 in time $dt$.**
By identical reasoning:

$$
dm_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Apply conservation of mass for steady flow.**
Under steady conditions, the mass inside the stream tube between sections 1 and 2 does *not* change with time. Therefore the mass entering at 1 must equal the mass leaving at 2:

$$
dm_1 = dm_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \, dt = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $dt$ and rearrange.**

$$
\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2
$$

This is the **1D continuity equation for compressible, steady flow** along a stream tube. The product $\rho A V$ is the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$, which is constant along the stream tube.

**Step 6 — Reduce to the incompressible case.**
If the fluid is **incompressible**, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho = \text{constant}$, so the density cancels:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \; (\text{volumetric flow rate})
$$

This is the **classical 1D continuity result**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Derive the continuity equation," most students write only Steps 4 and 6. To score full marks in KTU ESE, you must show all six steps explicitly, including the cancellation logic.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 3D Differential Form (Cartesian Coordinates)

**Step 1 — Choose an infinitesimal rectangular control volume.**
Take a tiny cube aligned with the Cartesian axes, of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$, located at point $(x, y, z)$. The fluid density is $\rho(x, y, z, t)$ and velocity is $\vec{V} = u \hat{i} + v \hat{j} + w \hat{k}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of mass accumulation inside the cube.**
Mass inside the cube at time $t$ is $\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$. The rate of change of this mass is:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz)
$$

Since the cube is fixed, $dx \, dy \, dz$ is constant, so:

$$
\text{Rate of accumulation} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the net mass flux through the $x$-faces.**
The cube has two faces perpendicular to the $x$-axis, at $x$ and $x + dx$, each of area $dy \, dz$.

*Mass flux entering at $x$:* The mass flow rate per unit area is $\rho u$. So mass entering per unit time through the left face is $\rho u \, dy \, dz$.

*Mass flux leaving at $x + dx$:* By Taylor expansion of $\rho u$ about $x$:

$$
(\rho u)\bigg|_{x+dx} = (\rho u)\bigg|_x + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx
$$

So mass leaving through the right face is:

$$
\left[ \rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz
$$

*Net $x$-flux out* = (mass out) $-$ (mass in):

$$
\text{Net } x\text{-flux} = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

*(Explanation: The first $\rho u$ terms cancel, leaving the differential contribution.)*

**Step 4 — Repeat for the $y$-faces and $z$-faces.**
By identical reasoning:

$$
\text{Net } y\text{-flux} = \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

$$
\text{Net } z\text{-flux} = \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 5 — Total net mass efflux through the control surface.**

$$
\text{Total efflux} = \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 6 — Apply conservation of mass.**
Mass accumulation + net efflux out = 0 (mass is conserved):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx \, dy \, dz = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Divide through by the cube volume $dx \, dy \, dz$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Rewrite in vector notation.**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

This is the **general 3D continuity equation in differential form**.

**Step 9 — Specialize to incompressible flow.**
If $\rho$ = constant, then $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$, and the density factors out of the divergence:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Many board questions ask: *"Show that the continuity equation for incompressible flow reduces to the divergence-free condition on the velocity field."* Memorize Step 9 — it appears almost every semester.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Continuity Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates

**Step 1 — Choose a cylindrical elemental volume.**
Consider a small cylindrical control volume of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, azimuthal angle extent $d\theta$, and axial length $dz$. The volume is:

$$
dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity components in cylindrical coordinates.**
Let $\vec{V} = v_r \hat{e}_r + v_\theta \hat{e}_\theta + v_z \hat{e}_z$.

**Step 3 — Mass flux through the radial faces.**
The two radial faces are at $r$ and $r + dr$, each of area $r \, d\theta \, dz$.

*Mass in at $r$:* $\rho v_r \cdot r \, d\theta \, dz$

*Mass out at $r + dr$:* Using Taylor expansion:

$$
\rho v_r \cdot (r + dr) \, d\theta \, dz \approx \left[ \rho v_r \, r + \frac{\partial (\rho v_r \, r)}{\partial r} dr \right] d\theta \, dz
$$

*Net radial efflux:*

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_r \, r)}{\partial r} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz = \frac{\partial (\rho v_r \, r)}{\partial r} \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Mass flux through the azimuthal faces.**
The two faces have area $dr \, dz$ and length $r \, d\theta$. The mass flux is $\rho v_\theta$. Net azimuthal efflux:

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flux through the axial faces.**
The two axial faces have area $r \, dr \, d\theta$. Net axial efflux:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 6 — Apply conservation of mass and divide by $dV$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Incompressible specialization.**

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cylindrical form matters in naval architecture:** Propeller slipstream, rudder flow, and bilge-vortex flow are all naturally cylindrical. Examiners love this form because it links directly to propeller-rudder interaction problems.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Continuity Equation Using the Stream Function (2D Incompressible)

**Step 1 — Define a stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$ for 2D incompressible flow.**
By construction:

$$
u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the 2D incompressible continuity equation.**

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

Compute the partials:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y}, \qquad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum them.**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Hence, **continuity is identically satisfied** for any $\psi$. The stream function method is a powerful trick that converts a partial differential equation (continuity) into a definition.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Note:** This is a frequent 7-mark question. Always show the substitution *and* the cancellation explicitly.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Which Form To Use

| Problem Type | Recommended Form | Reason |
|--------------|------------------|--------|
| Pipe flow (constant area sections) | 1D integral: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Simplest, fastest |
| Flow around a ship hull (cartesian mesh) | 3D differential Cartesian | Matches CFD grid |
| Propeller / vortex flow | Cylindrical differential | Geometry is naturally cylindrical |
| Compressible nozzle flow | 1D compressible: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Density varies |
| 2D potential flow past a hull section | Stream function $\psi$ | Continuity auto-satisfied |
| Transient filling of ballast tank | Unsteady integral form | Density/level changes with time |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Logical Flow of Continuity Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Law of Conservation of Mass"] --> B["Choose Control Volume or Stream Tube"]
    B --> C1["Path 1: Stream Tube Approach"]
    B --> C2["Path 2: Infinitesimal Cube Approach"]
    C1 --> D1["Compute mass flux at Section 1"]
    D1 --> E1["Compute mass flux at Section 2"]
    E1 --> F1["Equate mass fluxes for steady flow"]
    F1 --> G1["Result: rho1 A1 V1 equals rho2 A2 V2"]
    G1 --> H1["Incompressible: A1 V1 equals A2 V2"]
    C2 --> D2["Compute accumulation drho by dt times dV"]
    D2 --> E2["Compute net flux in x direction"]
    E2 --> F2["Compute net flux in y direction"]
    F2 --> G2["Compute net flux in z direction"]
    G2 --> H2["Sum and equate to zero"]
    H2 --> I2["Result: drho by dt plus divergence of rhoV equals 0"]
    I2 --> J2["Incompressible: divergence of V equals 0"]
    H1 --> K["Final Continuity Equation Family"]
    J2 --> K["Final Continuity Equation Family"]
    K --> L["Apply to Naval Architecture Problems"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Continuity in CFD Solvers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Input: Velocity Field u v w and Density rho"] --> Step1["Step 1: Compute Spatial Derivatives"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Evaluate Divergence of rhoV"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Compute Time Derivative of rho"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Step 4: Sum the Two Terms"]
    Step4 --> Check{"Equals Zero?"}
    Check -- Yes --> Pass["Continuity Satisfied: Proceed to Momentum Equation"]
    Check -- No --> Correct["Apply Pressure Correction or Density Update"]
    Correct --> Step1
    Pass --> Output["Output: Velocity and Pressure Fields"]
```

```mermaid
    Tube --> Out["Section 2: Area A2, Velocity V2, Density rho2"]

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Application Pipeline in Naval Hydrodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Hull["Ship Hull Geometry"] --> Mesh["CFD Mesh Generation"]
    Mesh --> BC["Boundary Conditions: Inlet U, Outlet P"]
    BC --> Solver["Iterative Solver: Navier-Stokes + Continuity"]
    Solver --> ContinuityCheck["Continuity Residual Check"]
    ContinuityCheck -- Not Converged --> PressureCorrection["Pressure Poisson Equation from Continuity"]
    PressureCorrection --> Solver
    ContinuityCheck -- Converged --> Result["Velocity and Pressure Fields"]
    Result --> Resistance["Wave and Viscous Resistance"]

    Result --> Seakeeping["Seakeeping Analysis"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid:** Each block above corresponds to a *real computation* done in naval architectural software (e.g., STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM, FINE/Marine). Continuity is the *coupling constraint* that ties velocity and pressure together.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024 Model Question, Module 2]**

### Question 1: Define the continuity equation. Write its form for a steady, incompressible 1D flow. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The continuity equation is the mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** applied to a fluid in motion. It states that the net mass efflux from a control volume plus the time-rate of change of mass inside the volume must equal zero, assuming no mass sources or sinks.

For a **steady, incompressible 1D flow** through a stream tube:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

where $A_1, A_2$ are the cross-sectional areas and $V_1, V_2$ are the corresponding flow velocities at two sections of the stream tube.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 2]**

### Question 2: What is the physical significance of the continuity equation? State the divergence form for incompressible flow. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
**Physical Significance:** The continuity equation guarantees that mass is neither created nor destroyed during fluid motion. It links the velocity field, the density field, and the geometry of the flow domain. Any computed velocity field that does not satisfy continuity is physically inadmissible.

For an **incompressible flow**, the continuity equation reduces to a **divergence-free condition** on the velocity field:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

This states that the volume of fluid entering any infinitesimal cube per unit time equals the volume leaving per unit time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question for ESE Module 2]**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### Part (a): Derive the continuity equation in its general differential form for a 3D compressible flow. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped (control volume) of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ fixed in space. The fluid density at the centre is $\rho(x, y, z, t)$ and velocity components are $u, v, w$.

**Step 2:** Mass contained in the element at time $t$:

$$
\delta m = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 3:** Time rate of change of mass inside the element:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz) = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 4:** Net mass flux out of the element in the $x$-direction. Mass entering at $x$: $\rho u \, dy \, dz$. Mass leaving at $x + dx$: $\left[ \rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz$.

Net $x$-flux out:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 5:** Similarly, net $y$-flux out:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 6:** Net $z$-flux out:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz
$$

**Step 7:** Conservation of mass — total rate of mass accumulation = $-$ (net efflux out):

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy \, dz + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Divide by $dx \, dy \, dz$:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0 \,}
$$

**Step 9 (Bonus, +1 mark if mentioned):** In vector form:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

#### Part (b): For an incompressible fluid, simplify the result. Hence, derive the stream function for 2D incompressible flow. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** For incompressible flow, $\rho$ = constant. The general continuity equation:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

becomes, after cancelling $\rho$:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{(divergence-free condition)}
$$

**Step 2:** For 2D flow ($w = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Define the stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$ such that:

$$
u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into continuity:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left( -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \right) = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

Hence, **continuity is automatically satisfied** for any scalar $\psi$.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a): Derive the continuity equation in cylindrical coordinates for an incompressible fluid. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Take a cylindrical elemental volume at radius $r$, of dimensions $dr, r \, d\theta, dz$. Volume: $dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz$.

**Step 2:** Velocity components: $v_r, v_\theta, v_z$.

**Step 3:** Mass flux through the two radial faces (at $r$ and $r + dr$):

*At $r$:* $\rho v_r \cdot r \, d\theta \, dz$ entering.

*At $r + dr$:* $\left[ \rho v_r \cdot r + \frac{\partial (\rho v_r \, r)}{\partial r} dr \right] d\theta \, dz$ leaving.

*Net radial efflux:*

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho v_r \, r)}{\partial r} \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 4:** Mass flux through the two azimuthal faces (at $\theta$ and $\theta + d\theta$), each of area $dr \, dz$:

*Net azimuthal efflux:*

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 5:** Mass flux through the two axial faces (at $z$ and $z + dz$), each of area $r \, dr \, d\theta$:

*Net axial efflux:*

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

**Step 6:** Conservation of mass:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz + \text{(net radial)} + \text{(net azimuthal)} + \text{(net axial)} = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Dividing by $dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz$:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\rho v_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0 \,}
$$

**Step 8 (Incompressible specialization):** With $\rho$ = constant:

$$
\boxed{\, \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0 \,}
$$

#### Part (b): A 50 mm diameter pipe contracts to a 25 mm diameter throat. Water (incompressible, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through it at 0.005 m³/s. Calculate the velocities at the two sections and the mass flow rate. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter at section 1: $D_1 = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Diameter at section 2: $D_2 = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 0.005$ m³/s
- Density: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Compute cross-sectional areas.**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute velocity at Section 1.**

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.005}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute velocity at Section 2 using continuity.**

For incompressible flow, $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$. Therefore:

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.005}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} = 10.186 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Verify by ratio method.**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \cdot \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = 2.546 \times \left(\frac{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = 2.546 \times 4 = 10.186 \; \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Compute mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.005 = 5.0 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Final Results:**
- $V_1 = 2.546$ m/s
- $V_2 = 10.186$ m/s (4× faster, as expected for half the diameter)
- $\dot{m} = 5.0$ kg/s

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in $Q$, $A$, $V$ — convert mm → m *before* computing (lose 1 mark per wrong unit).
> 2. **Skipping the verification step** using the area ratio. Examiners award 1 mark for independent re-check.
> 3. **Using $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ with mixed units** (e.g., $A$ in mm² and $V$ in m/s) — catastrophic error worth −2 marks.
> 4. **Writing continuity as $A_1 V_1 + A_2 V_2 = 0$** — this is the *wrong* sign convention; the correct form is $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ for *inflow* and *outflow* magnitudes.
> 5. **Confusing 1D and 3D forms** — examiners deduct 2 marks if you write the divergence form for a pipe-flow problem.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 2 — Continuity Equation**

- [x] The continuity equation is the **mathematical statement of conservation of mass**.
- [x] For a steady 1D flow: $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ (compressible) or $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (incompressible).
- [x] The general differential form is $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
- [x] Cartesian expansion has **four** partial derivative terms — memorize all four.
- [x] Incompressible form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (divergence-free).
- [x] Cylindrical form has the $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r}$ term — do not drop the $r$ inside the derivative.
- [x] The **stream function** $\psi$ exists only for 2D incompressible flow and *automatically* satisfies continuity.
- [x] The continuity equation is the **first of the Navier–Stokes triad** (continuity, momentum, energy).
- [x] In **naval architecture**: continuity governs propeller slipstream, hull boundary layer feeding, bilge-pump flow, and wave-induced velocity fields.
- [x] **KTU trap questions** often give density variations (compressed air, exhaust gases) — always check the compressible vs incompressible assumption first.
- [x] The integral form (Reynolds Transport Theorem form) is the most general — applicable to unsteady, moving control volumes.
- [x] Remember the unit chain: $\rho$ (kg/m³) × $A$ (m²) × $V$ (m/s) = $\dot{m}$ (kg/s).
- [x] The continuity equation is a **scalar** equation, even in 3D — one equation per coordinate set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word for KTU Aspirants:** Continuity is *the* foundational equation. Master all its forms — integral, differential, Cartesian, cylindrical, and stream function. Once you can write the appropriate form in less than 30 seconds, the rest of fluid mechanics becomes a structured exercise in applying momentum (Euler/Navier–Stokes) and energy (Bernoulli/Euler) on top of this foundation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circulation and Vorticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circulation and Vorticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Circulation

**Circulation ($\Gamma$)** is a scalar integral quantity that measures the macroscopic rotational tendency of a fluid along a closed curve. Mathematically, it is the line integral of the tangential component of the velocity vector around a closed contour $C$:

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l} = \oint_{C} (u\,dx + v\,dy + w\,dz)$$

where $\vec{V}$ is the velocity vector and $d\vec{l}$ is an infinitesimal tangential line element along the closed curve $C$. The orientation of the contour is conventionally taken as counter-clockwise when viewed from above, consistent with the right-hand rule.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition**
> Circulation is a **scalar (with sign)** quantity, **NOT a vector**. Its sign tells you the sense of rotation: positive $\Gamma$ corresponds to counter-clockwise circulation, negative $\Gamma$ corresponds to clockwise circulation. Its SI unit is **m²/s**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Vorticity

**Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$)** is a vector field defined as the **curl of the velocity vector field**. It quantifies the local, *infinitesimal* rotational (spin) behavior of a fluid element:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

In Cartesian coordinates, expanding the curl operator:

$$\vec{\omega} = \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)\hat{j} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)\hat{k}$$

For two-dimensional flow in the $x$–$y$ plane where $w = 0$ and velocities depend only on $x$ and $y$, vorticity reduces to a vector pointing out of the plane:

$$\omega_{z} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition**
> Vorticity has SI units of **s⁻¹** (per second). It represents angular velocity in **radians per second**, but is exactly **twice** the rigid-body angular velocity of a fluid element.

## 1.3 The Crucial Distinction: Circulation vs. Vorticity

This is the **single most important conceptual distinction** in this module, and examiners test it relentlessly:

| Feature | Circulation ($\Gamma$) | Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical type | Scalar (line integral) | Vector (curl of velocity) |
| Domain | A finite closed contour | An infinitesimal point |
| Represents | Global rotation of fluid around a loop | Local spin of a fluid element |
| Units | m²/s | s⁻¹ |
| Vanishes when | Net tangential flow around loop is zero | No local angular deformation (irrotational flow) |

## 1.4 Real-World Conceptual Analogy — The "Whirlpool vs. Spinning Coin" Picture

Imagine you are standing on the deck of a ship in two different scenarios:

- **Scenario A — The Whirlpool**: A bucket of water on deck has a small whirlpool spinning. If you trace a large circle around the whirlpool and add up the tangential fluid velocities along the way, you get a large, nonzero **circulation**. Yet, deep inside the bulk fluid far from the center, the fluid is essentially stationary and not locally spinning — its **vorticity is near zero**. Here, $\Gamma$ is large but $\omega \approx 0$. This is the **free vortex** (irrotational vortex).

- **Scenario B — The Spinning Coin**: A solid disc of fluid (like honey on a rotating turntable) spins as a rigid body. Every tiny fluid element rotates at the local angular velocity. The **vorticity is large everywhere** inside the disc and uniform. However, if you draw a circle inside the disc and integrate the tangential velocity, the **circulation depends on the size of the loop**. This is the **forced vortex** (rigid-body rotation).

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Lock-in**
> Think of **Vorticity = local spin of a tiny fluid particle** (microscopic property). Think of **Circulation = sum total of rotation around a finite loop** (macroscopic property). They are linked by **Stokes' Theorem**, which we will derive in Section 3.

## 1.5 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

The concept of vorticity partitions all fluid flows into two grand categories:

- **Irrotational Flow** (also called **potential flow**): $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ everywhere in the flow field (except possibly at isolated singularities). Fluid elements undergo pure translation and deformation but **no net rotation**. Examples: flow far from a wing, free vortex outside the core.

- **Rotational Flow**: $\vec{\omega} \neq 0$ at least somewhere in the flow. Fluid elements spin as they move. Examples: boundary layers, wakes, forced vortex, fluid inside a stirred cup.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Misconception**
> A flow with curved streamlines is **NOT necessarily rotational**. A free vortex has perfectly circular streamlines, yet it is **irrotational** (vorticity is zero except at the singular center). Rotationality is defined by vorticity, not by the curvature of streamlines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Vorticity Equals Twice the Angular Velocity — Physical Derivation

Consider a tiny square fluid element of side $dx$ and $dy$ at a point in a 2-D flow field. The fluid element has translational velocity $(u, v)$ at its center, but the velocity field varies across it. The corners experience different velocities:

- The corner at the center moves with $(u, v)$.
- The corner at $(x+dx, y)$ moves with $(u+\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}dx,\, v+\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}dx)$.
- The corner at $(x, y+dy)$ moves with $(u+\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}dy,\, v+\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}dy)$.

If we measure the angular rate of change of the line element originally aligned with the $x$-axis, the angular velocity component is:

$$\Omega_{z} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\omega_{z}}{2}$$

This is why **vorticity is twice the angular velocity** — a fact students frequently forget on the exam. Memorize this as $\omega = 2\Omega$.

## 2.2 Stokes' Circulation Theorem — The Master Link

Stokes' theorem, applied to the velocity field, provides the **fundamental bridge** between circulation and vorticity:

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C} \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = \iint_{A} (\nabla \times \vec{V})\cdot \hat{n}\, dA = \iint_{A} \vec{\omega}\cdot \hat{n}\, dA$$

where $A$ is any surface bounded by the closed contour $C$, and $\hat{n}$ is the unit normal to $A$ by the right-hand rule. This theorem is **the reason circulation and vorticity are not independent concepts**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam Power-Tip**
> If you ever forget whether a flow is rotational, just compute the circulation around *any* small closed loop encircling the point. If $\Gamma = 0$ for that loop, the flow is irrotational at that point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorize the table — it covers roughly 80% of numerical questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Circulation (general) | $\Gamma = \oint_{C}\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l}$ | m²/s |
| 2 | Circulation (2-D Cartesian) | $\Gamma = \oint_{C}(u\,dx + v\,dy)$ | m²/s |
| 3 | Vorticity (vector) | $\vec{\omega}=\nabla\times\vec{V}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | Vorticity (2-D, z-component) | $\omega_{z}=\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}-\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | Vorticity (cylindrical, z-component) | $\omega_{z}=\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial (r v_{\theta})}{\partial r}-\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_{r}}{\partial \theta}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | Relation to angular velocity | $\omega = 2\Omega$ | scalar/vector |
| 7 | Stokes' theorem | $\Gamma = \iint_{A}\vec{\omega}\cdot\hat{n}\,dA$ | bridge formula |
| 8 | Free vortex tangential velocity | $v_{\theta}=\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Free vortex stream function | $\psi=\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\ln r$ | m²/s |
| 10 | Free vortex velocity potential | $\phi=\dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\theta$ | m²/s |
| 11 | Forced vortex tangential velocity | $v_{\theta}=\Omega r$ | m/s |
| 12 | Forced vortex vorticity | $\omega_{z}=2\Omega$ | constant |
| 13 | Forced vortex pressure | $p = p_{0} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}r^{2}$ | Pa |
| 14 | Kutta–Joukowski lift | $L = \rho V_{\infty}\Gamma$ | N/m (per unit span) |
| 15 | Kelvin's theorem | $\dfrac{D\Gamma}{Dt}=0$ for inviscid, barotropic flow with conservative body forces | statement |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture / Ship Hydrodynamics**: Circulation theory underpins **lifting-line theory** for ship propeller blades and foil sections. The Kutta–Joukowski theorem (item 14) is the cornerstone for computing the side force on a rudder or the lift on a hydrofoil.
- **Aerodynamics & Sailing Yachts**: Bound circulation around a sail generates aerodynamic lift at low wind speeds.
- **Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV)**: Cross-flow on cylindrical offshore structures (mooring lines, risers, sonar domes on submarines) sheds alternating vortices (Kármán vortex street), whose strength is governed by circulation. VIV design codes (e.g., DNV-RP-F105) are written directly in terms of $\Gamma$.
- **Tornado & Cyclone Modelling**: Atmospheric vorticity is the predictive variable in numerical weather prediction; tropical cyclone intensity scales with integrated circulation.
- **Mixing & Stirred Reactors**: Chemical and bilge-water treatment tanks on ships use forced-vortex impellers; their design relies on the $\omega = 2\Omega$ relationship.

## 2.5 The Two Canonical Vortices — A Side-by-Side Logical Breakdown

### A. Free Vortex (Irrotational Vortex)

**Operational logic (the "Why")**: Tangential velocity must decrease exactly as $1/r$ so that the angular velocity $\Omega = v_\theta / r \propto 1/r^2$ vanishes as $r \to \infty$. This satisfies the irrotationality condition $\omega_z = 0$ everywhere except at the singular axis $r = 0$.

**How to recognize**: Streamlines are concentric circles; velocity falls off with distance; vorticity is zero everywhere (except at the origin).

### B. Forced Vortex (Solid-Body Rotation)

**Operational logic (the "Why")**: The fluid rotates *en masse* exactly like a solid disc. Angular velocity is constant everywhere. Tangential velocity must therefore grow linearly with $r$ to keep $\Omega$ uniform.

**How to recognize**: Streamlines are concentric circles; velocity grows linearly with $r$; vorticity is uniform and equal to $2\Omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Identification Hack for Exams**
> Given tangential velocity $v_\theta = C r^n$:
> - If $n = 1$: **Forced vortex** (solid-body rotation, $\omega \neq 0$).
> - If $n = -1$: **Free vortex** (irrotational, $\omega = 0$ for $r > 0$).
> - If $n \neq \pm 1$: General rotational vortex; vorticity is non-zero and radially varying.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT] **Derivation Mandate**
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. The reader should be able to reproduce each derivation from scratch in an examination setting.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vorticity From Rigid-Body Rotation

**Starting Premise**: A fluid rotates as a rigid body about the $z$-axis with constant angular velocity $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega\hat{k}$. We seek the vorticity field $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$.

### Step 1 — Express the velocity field of rigid-body rotation.

In cylindrical coordinates $(r,\theta,z)$, the velocity of a point at radius $r$ in pure rigid-body rotation is purely tangential:

$$\vec{V} = v_{\theta}\hat{\theta}, \quad \text{with} \quad v_{\theta} = \Omega r$$

### Step 2 — Write vorticity in cylindrical coordinates.

The general expression for the curl of a vector in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial v_{\theta}}{\partial z}\right]\hat{r} + \left[\frac{\partial v_{r}}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial r}\right]\hat{\theta} + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r v_{\theta})}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial v_{r}}{\partial \theta}\right]\hat{k}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the rigid-body velocity field.

Since the motion is purely tangential and depends only on $r$, we have $v_{r} = 0$, $v_{z} = 0$, and $v_{\theta} = \Omega r$ with no $z$- or $\theta$-dependence. Therefore all partial derivatives except $\frac{\partial(r v_\theta)}{\partial r}$ vanish. Substituting:

$$\vec{\omega} = \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r \cdot \Omega r)}{\partial r}\right]\hat{k}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the partial derivative.

$$\frac{\partial(r \cdot \Omega r)}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial(\Omega r^{2})}{\partial r} = 2\Omega r$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the final vorticity.

$$\vec{\omega} = \frac{1}{r}(2\Omega r)\hat{k} = 2\Omega\hat{k}$$

**Conclusion**: For solid-body rotation at angular velocity $\Omega$, the vorticity vector is constant and equals $\vec{\omega} = 2\Omega \hat{k}$, confirming the fundamental relationship $\omega = 2\Omega$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Circulation of a Free Vortex Using a Circular Contour

**Starting Premise**: In a free vortex, the tangential velocity at radius $r$ is $v_{\theta}(r) = \dfrac{C}{r}$ for some constant $C$. Compute the circulation around a circle of radius $r_{0}$ centered on the vortex axis.

### Step 1 — Parameterise the contour.

Use the standard polar parameterisation of a circle of radius $r_{0}$:

$$x = r_{0}\cos\theta, \quad y = r_{0}\sin\theta, \quad \theta \in [0,\, 2\pi]$$

The differential line element is tangent to the circle:

$$d\vec{l} = r_{0}\, d\theta\, \hat{\theta}$$

where $\hat{\theta}$ is the unit tangent vector (counter-clockwise direction).

### Step 2 — Evaluate $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{l}$.

The velocity vector is purely tangential: $\vec{V} = v_{\theta}(r_{0})\hat{\theta} = \dfrac{C}{r_{0}}\hat{\theta}$. Therefore:

$$\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = \frac{C}{r_{0}}\hat{\theta}\cdot(r_{0}\,d\theta\,\hat{\theta}) = \frac{C}{r_{0}}\cdot r_{0}\,d\theta = C\, d\theta$$

### Step 3 — Integrate around the closed loop.

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C}\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}C\,d\theta = C \cdot 2\pi$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the constant $C$.

$$C = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}$$

### Step 5 — Write the final velocity profile.

$$\boxed{\;v_{\theta}(r) = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\;}$$

**Conclusion**: For a free vortex, the velocity field and the single scalar circulation constant $\Gamma$ are completely equivalent; specifying $\Gamma$ uniquely determines the entire irrotational vortex field outside the singular axis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Circulation From Vorticity Using Stokes' Theorem (Verification)

**Starting Premise**: For a forced vortex with $v_\theta = \Omega r$, the vorticity is $\omega_z = 2\Omega$. Verify that Stokes' theorem holds for a circle of radius $r_0$.

### Step 1 — Compute circulation directly from the velocity.

Parameterising the circle of radius $r_0$:

$$\Gamma = \oint_{C}\vec{V}\cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}(\Omega r_0)\hat{\theta}\cdot(r_0\,d\theta\,\hat{\theta}) = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\Omega r_0^{2}\,d\theta = 2\pi \Omega r_0^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the same circulation via Stokes' theorem.

The surface $A$ enclosed by the circle is the disk of radius $r_0$. The unit normal is $\hat{n} = \hat{k}$. Therefore:

$$\Gamma = \iint_{A}\vec{\omega}\cdot\hat{n}\,dA = \iint_{A}(2\Omega)\,dA = 2\Omega \cdot (\pi r_0^{2}) = 2\pi \Omega r_0^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Confirm equivalence.

Both methods yield $\Gamma = 2\pi \Omega r_0^{2}$. This consistency check is a **favorite KTU verification question**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Distribution in a Forced Vortex

**Starting Premise**: In the rotating reference frame of the fluid, a fluid element at radius $r$ experiences a centrifugal body force per unit mass of magnitude $\Omega^{2}r$ directed radially outward. Find the pressure distribution $p(r)$ assuming $p = p_0$ at $r = 0$.

### Step 1 — Apply Euler's equation in the radial direction.

For steady, axisymmetric flow with no radial acceleration (rigid-body rotation is steady), the radial momentum equation in the inertial frame reduces to a balance between the centrifugal force and the pressure gradient:

$$\frac{dp}{dr} = \rho\,\Omega^{2}r$$

### Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.

$$dp = \rho\,\Omega^{2}r\,dr$$

Integrating from $r = 0$ (where $p = p_0$) to an arbitrary radius $r$:

$$\int_{p_0}^{p(r)}dp = \rho\,\Omega^{2}\int_{0}^{r}r'\,dr'$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.

$$p(r) - p_0 = \rho\,\Omega^{2}\cdot\frac{r^{2}}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Write the final pressure distribution.

$$\boxed{\;p(r) = p_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}r^{2}\;}$$

**Engineering Insight**: For a closed cylindrical tank of radius $R$ filled with liquid rotating at $\Omega$, the pressure at the wall is $p(R) = p_0 + \frac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}R^{2}$. This is used to size the girth of rotating bilge tanks and centrifugal separator drums on ships.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Showing That the Free Vortex Is Irrotational

**Starting Premise**: Given $v_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}$ and $v_r = 0$, show that $\omega_z = 0$ for $r > 0$.

### Step 1 — Write the vorticity formula in cylindrical coordinates.

$$\omega_{z} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_{\theta})}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_{r}}{\partial \theta}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the free-vortex velocity field.

Since $v_{r} = 0$, the second term vanishes. The first term becomes:

$$\omega_{z} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \cdot \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\right) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the derivative.

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\right) = 0$$

because $\Gamma$ is a constant for the free vortex.

### Step 4 — Conclude.

$$\omega_{z} = \frac{1}{r}(0) = 0$$

**Conclusion**: The free vortex is irrotational everywhere except on the singular axis $r = 0$, where a concentrated point vortex of strength $\Gamma$ resides (modelled as a Dirac delta in distribution theory).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Kutta–Joukowski Lift Theorem (Statement Derivation)

**Starting Premise**: An infinite cylinder of unit span moves through an inviscid, incompressible fluid with free-stream velocity $V_\infty$. The flow produces a bound circulation $\Gamma$ around the cylinder. Show that the resulting lift per unit span is $L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma$.

### Step 1 — Set up the problem.

Place the cylinder in a uniform flow. The net effect of viscosity (released from the cylinder surface by the Kutta condition) is mathematically equivalent to superposing a free vortex of strength $\Gamma$ onto the uniform flow.

### Step 2 — Compute the velocity field via superposition.

By potential-flow superposition, the complex potential is:

$$W(z) = V_\infty z + \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi i}\ln z$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Blasius–Chaplygin formula for force.

The complex force per unit span on a 2-D body in an inviscid flow is:

$$\bar{F} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \oint_{C}\left(\frac{dW}{dz}\right)^{2}dz$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the contour integral.

Using residue calculus at the singular point $z = 0$ (the location of the bound vortex):

$$\bar{F} = \frac{1}{2}\rho(2\pi i)\cdot 2V_\infty\cdot\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi i} = i\rho V_\infty \Gamma$$

### Step 5 — Extract the lift force.

The real part of $\bar{F}$ corresponds to drag (here zero, d'Alembert's paradox), and the imaginary part corresponds to lift. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;L' = \rho V_\infty \Gamma\;}$$

**Naval Architecture Application**: This is the foundation for hydrofoil lift, sail force, and the side force on a ship rudder. The rudder of a ship generates a transverse force $F_y = \rho V^2 A\, C_L$, where the lift coefficient $C_L$ can be back-computed from the circulation using this theorem.
