**Derivation logic:** Equating the two definitions of velocity component $u$ (one from $\phi$, one from $\psi$) gives equation (1). Equating the two definitions of $v$ gives equation (2).

**Physical guarantee:** The two families of curves — streamlines ($\psi = \text{const}$) and equipotential lines ($\phi = \text{const}$) — are **mutually orthogonal at every point** in the flow field. Furthermore, the complex potential $w(z) = \phi + i\psi$ becomes an **analytic function**, permitting the use of complex-variable methods to solve naval hydrodynamics problems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, 3 marks]`
**Differentiate between the existence conditions for the stream function and the velocity potential function. Illustrate with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stream Function $\psi$ | Velocity Potential $\phi$ |
|---|---|---|
| Existence condition | Flow must be **incompressible and 2D** (continuity holds) | Flow must be **irrotational** (zero vorticity) |
| Underlying PDE | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ |
| Physical meaning | Lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines | Lines of constant $\phi$ are equipotential lines |
| Example (exists) | **Free vortex:** $u_\theta = K/r$, $\psi = -K \ln r$ | **Uniform flow:** $u = U$, $\phi = Ux$ |
| Example (does not exist) | A flow with sources/sinks in 3D (no $\psi$ globally) | A rotational vortex (no $\phi$ because $\nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$) |

**Key exam point:** Both functions exist simultaneously **only** in 2D, incompressible, **irrotational** flow (potential flow).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign error on $v$:** Marks are routinely lost by writing $v = +\partial\psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. Memorize the minus sign!
> - **Confusing streamline and pathline:** In **steady** flow, streamlines and pathlines coincide. For unsteady flow, they differ — but the stream function is *still* defined by streamlines.
> - **Forgetting the orthogonality proof:** Simply stating "they are perpendicular" is not enough in a 14-mark question. You must *derive* it (see Section 3.6).
> - **Mixing up $\phi$ and $\psi$ definitions:** Write the velocity components as $u = \partial\phi/\partial x = \partial\psi/\partial y$ on the first line of your answer; this anchors the rest of the solution.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** For a two-dimensional, incompressible, irrotational flow, derive the expressions for the velocity components $u$ and $v$ in terms of **(i)** the stream function $\psi$ and **(ii)** the velocity potential $\phi$. Hence obtain the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D potential flow has the stream function:

$$\psi(x, y) = 3x^2 y - y^3$$

**(i)** Determine the velocity potential $\phi(x, y)$.
**(ii)** Verify that both $\psi$ and $\phi$ satisfy Laplace's equation.
**(iii)** Calculate the velocity magnitude at the point $(1, 2)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Part (i) — Velocity from stream function:**

The total differential of $\psi$ is:

$$d\psi = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} dy$$

Along a streamline $\psi = \text{const}$, so $d\psi = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} dy = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{\partial\psi/\partial x}{\partial\psi/\partial y}$$

But along a streamline, the tangent is parallel to the velocity:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{\partial\psi/\partial x}{\partial\psi/\partial y}$$

Choosing the standard convention:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}}$$

**(a) Part (ii) — Velocity from potential:**

By definition of the velocity potential:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}}$$

**Cauchy–Riemann equations** — equate the two expressions for $u$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$$

Equate the two expressions for $v$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

These are the Cauchy–Riemann equations. **$\blacksquare$**

---

**(b) Part (i) — Find $\phi$:**

Given $\psi = 3x^2 y - y^3$, compute the velocity components from $\psi$:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x^2 - 3y^2$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -6xy$$

Now integrate $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ with respect to $x$:

$$\phi = \int (3x^2 - 3y^2) \, dx = x^3 - 3xy^2 + f(y)$$

Differentiate this with respect to $y$ and set it equal to $v$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -6xy + f'(y) = v = -6xy$$

Therefore $f'(y) = 0$, so $f(y) = \text{const}$. Taking the constant as zero:

$$\boxed{\phi(x, y) = x^3 - 3xy^2}$$

**(b) Part (ii) — Verify Laplace:**

For $\psi = 3x^2 y - y^3$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} = 6y, \quad \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = -6y$$

$$\nabla^2 \psi = 6y - 6y = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

For $\phi = x^3 - 3xy^2$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = 6x, \quad \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -6x$$

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 6x - 6x = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**(b) Part (iii) — Velocity at (1, 2):**

$$u(1, 2) = 3(1)^2 - 3(2)^2 = 3 - 12 = -9$$

$$v(1, 2) = -6(1)(2) = -12$$

$$V = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2} = \sqrt{81 + 144} = \sqrt{225} = 15 \text{ units}$$

$$\boxed{V(1, 2) = 15 \text{ units}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2, Alternative]`

**(a)** Starting from the continuity equation and the irrotationality condition, derive **Laplace's equation** for both the stream function $\psi$ and the velocity potential $\phi$. State clearly the physical assumption behind each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D potential flow around a cylinder of radius $a$ has the complex potential:

$$w(z) = U\left(z + \frac{a^2}{z}\right)$$

**(i)** Separate the real and imaginary parts to obtain $\phi(x, y)$ and $\psi(x, y)$.
**(ii)** Identify the equation of the cylinder's surface using streamlines.
**(iii)** Compute the complex velocity $dw/dz$ and hence the **stagnation point(s)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Laplace's equation:**

**Step 1 — Continuity for incompressible 2D flow:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Using the stream function definitions $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

This is identically zero, confirming that $\psi$ exists. To obtain Laplace's equation, we use the equivalent form of continuity:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \text{(valid for incompressible 2D flow)}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} - \left(-\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2}\right) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0}$$

**Physical assumption:** The fluid is **incompressible and the flow is 2D and steady**.

**Step 2 — Irrotationality condition:**

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

Using $\phi$-definitions $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ and $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \text{(only the diagonal mixed derivatives appear)}$$

To get Laplace's equation, use the equivalent form:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{(continuity, valid since incompressible)}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \phi = 0}$$

**Physical assumption:** The flow is **irrotational** ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$) and **incompressible**. **$\blacksquare$**

---

**(b) Part (i) — Separate real and imaginary parts:**

Write $z = x + iy$ and $1/z = (x - iy)/(x^2 + y^2)$:

$$w = U\left[(x + iy) + \frac{a^2(x - iy)}{x^2 + y^2}\right]$$

$$w = U\left[x + \frac{a^2 x}{x^2 + y^2}\right] + iU\left[y - \frac{a^2 y}{x^2 + y^2}\right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\phi(x, y) = Ux\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{x^2 + y^2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = Uy\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{x^2 + y^2}\right)}$$

**(b) Part (ii) — Cylinder surface:**

The cylinder surface is a streamline, so $\psi = \text{const}$. For the cylinder of radius $a$:

$$1 - \frac{a^2}{x^2 + y^2} = \text{const}$$

On the cylinder $x^2 + y^2 = a^2$:

$$1 - \frac{a^2}{a^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = 0$$

Therefore the **cylinder surface corresponds to the streamline $\psi = 0$**, i.e., $x^2 + y^2 = a^2$.

**(b) Part (iii) — Complex velocity and stagnation points:**

Differentiate the complex potential:

$$\frac{dw}{dz} = U\left(1 - \frac{a^2}{z^2}\right)$$

Setting $dw/dz = 0$ to find stagnation points:

$$1 - \frac{a^2}{z^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z^2 = a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = \pm a$$

In Cartesian form:

$$\boxed{z_1 = (a, 0), \quad z_2 = (-a, 0)}$$

These are the **front and rear stagnation points** of the flow around the cylinder.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Relations Between the Functions**

- **Two scalar functions** $\psi$ and $\phi$ completely describe a 2D incompressible irrotational flow.
- **Velocity components:**
  - $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$
  - $v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$
- **Watch the sign of $v$** in terms of $\psi$ — it is **negative**.
- **Stream function exists** ⟺ flow is **incompressible and 2D** (continuity holds).
- **Velocity potential exists** ⟺ flow is **irrotational** (vorticity = 0).
- **Both exist** ⟺ potential flow ⟹ $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ AND $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$.
- **Cauchy–Riemann equations** are the *bridge* linking $\psi$ and $\phi$.
- **Streamlines and equipotentials are always perpendicular** — proved via C-R.
- **Complex potential** $w(z) = \phi + i\psi$ is **analytic**; complex velocity is $dw/dz = u - iv$.
- **Stagnation points** occur where $dw/dz = 0$.
- **The minus sign in $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$** is the **#1 source of lost marks** in KTU exams.
- Laplace's equation is **linear** ⟹ superposition of elementary flows (uniform flow, source, sink, vortex, doublet) is valid — this is the basis of all practical ship-hull flow calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Euler's Equation of Motion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Euler's Equation of Motion** is the fundamental partial differential equation governing the motion of an **inviscid (non-viscous)** fluid, derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a fluid element moving through an inviscid flow field. It is expressed in vector form as: > **Euler's Equation (Vector Form):** > $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$ > where $\rho$ is fluid density, $\vec{V}$ is velocity vector, $p$ is pressure, and $\vec{g}$ is body force per unit mass (gravity). The equation describes the momentum balance of an **ideal fluid particle** in which viscous shear stresses are assumed to be **negligible**, leaving only pressure forces and body forces (gravity) as the acting agents. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Euler's Equation is the *parent equation* of almost all classical fluid dynamics. It is the theoretical foundation upon which **Bernoulli's Equation** is built (as its integrated form along a streamline) and forms the basis of **potential flow theory**, crucial for ship hydrodynamics and resistance estimation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a tiny, weightless **passenger riding a tiny "fluid particle surfboard"** along a flowing river (the fluid stream). As you move with the water, you feel two things: 1. **A pressure push or pull** — wherever the water gets squeezed (narrow channel), you feel an acceleration; wherever it expands, you feel a deceleration. This is the $-\nabla p$ term. 2. **Gravity pulling you down a hill** — if the river is flowing downhill, gravity accelerates you forward. This is the $\rho \vec{g}$ term. Euler's equation simply says: *the rate of change of your momentum (mass × acceleration) equals the net push from pressure gradients plus the gravitational pull.* It ignores the "stickiness" (viscosity) between water layers — that's why it's valid for **inviscid** flow, an excellent approximation for most ship-hull flow problems outside the thin boundary layer. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Naval Context:** In naval architecture, Euler's equation underpins **wave-pattern analysis**, **slender-body theory**, **Michell's thin-ship theory**, and **Kelvin's wave resistance**. Ship resistance components like wave-making resistance are derived by integrating pressure distributions obtained from Eulerian flow models. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants - **Specific Weight of Water (Sea Water):** $\gamma_{sw} = \mathbf{10{,}050 \, N/m^3}$ - **Density of Sea Water:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \, kg/m^3}$ - **Density of Fresh Water:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000 \, kg/m^3}$ - **Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \, m/s^2}$ - **Atmospheric Pressure (Standard):** $p_{atm} = \mathbf{101{,}325 \, Pa}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Euler's Equation The derivation rests on a simple but powerful concept — **a fluid element is a tiny deformable parcel of fluid that follows the flow.** Here is the structured logic: - **Why study an infinitesimal fluid element?** Because real fluid flows have continuously varying pressure, velocity, and density fields. A differential element is the smallest unit to which we can still apply Newton's laws meaningfully. - **How do we apply Newton's Second Law?** We sum all forces (surface + body) on the element and equate them to mass × acceleration. For a *control volume* moving with the fluid, we use the **material derivative** $\frac{D}{Dt}$. - **Why ignore viscosity?** Euler assumed the fluid has **zero viscosity** (an "ideal" or "inviscid" fluid). This eliminates the shear-stress term $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ from the more general **Navier-Stokes equations**, leaving only normal pressure forces on the element's faces. - **Why does the pressure term appear as $-\nabla p$?** Because pressure forces act *inward* on the fluid element. A high-pressure region pushes fluid toward a low-pressure region, producing a force proportional to the *negative* pressure gradient. - **How do we get Bernoulli's equation?** By **integrating Euler's equation along a streamline** for *steady, incompressible, inviscid* flow. This makes Euler's equation the most fundamental building block in classical fluid dynamics. ## 2.2 Assumptions of Euler's Equation | # | Assumption | Physical Meaning | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | **Inviscid fluid** | Viscosity $\mu = 0$; no shear stress | | 2 | **Continuum** | Fluid properties vary continuously in space | | 3 | **Newtonian fluid (without friction)** | Only normal stresses (pressure) act | | 4 | **Body force is gravity only** | No electromagnetic or other body forces | | 5 | **No surface tension effects** | Neglected at the fluid interface | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Form | Application | |---|----------|------|-------------| | 1 | $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ | Vector form | General 3-D inviscid flow | | 2 | $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$ | Expanded form | Separates local + convective acceleration | | 3 | $X - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{Du}{Dt}$ | x-component | Along x-direction | | 4 | $Y - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \frac{Dv}{Dt}$ | y-component | Along y-direction | | 5 | $Z - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \frac{Dw}{Dt}$ | z-component | Along z-direction | | 6 | $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = -\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} - \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$ | Along streamline (s) | Euler's form along streamline | | 7 | $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant}$ | Bernoulli's eq. (steady, incompressible) | Integrated Euler along streamline | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** In the above table, the symbols $X$, $Y$, $Z$ are the components of body force per unit mass ($X = g_x$, $Y = g_y$, $Z = g_z$). For gravity acting downward, $X = 0$, $Y = 0$, $Z = -g$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Euler's equation is the *cornerstone* of: - **Ship Wave Resistance Theory:** Kelvin's classical result for wave-pattern resistance is derived by integrating the pressure field from an inviscid Eulerian flow model around a ship hull. - **Propeller Inviscid Design:** Lifting-line theory for propellers assumes the flow is inviscid except in the trailing vortex. - **Hydrofoil and Submarine Design:** Pressure distributions on streamlined bodies are obtained by solving Euler's equation (often via panel methods). - **Cavitation Prediction:** Pressure minima on propeller blades or pump impellers are predicted using Eulerian flow solutions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Equation (Scalar Form from First Principles) ### Step 1: Identify the Fluid Element Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ centered at point $(x, y, z)$ in the flow field. **Mass of the element:** $$dm = \rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 2: Identify the Surface (Pressure) Forces Pressure acts **normal** and **inward** on each face. The net pressure force in the **x-direction** is the difference between the pressure force on the *back face* (at $x + dx/2$) and the *front face* (at $x - dx/2$). **Pressure at front face** (at $x - dx/2$): $$p_{front} = p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}$$ **Pressure at back face** (at $x + dx/2$): $$p_{back} = p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}$$ **Net pressure force in x-direction:** $$F_{p,x} = \left(p - \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}\right)dy\,dz - \left(p + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}\right)dy\,dz$$ $$F_{p,x} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$ By similar reasoning, the net pressure forces in y and z are: $$F_{p,y} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$ $$F_{p,z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$ ### Step 3: Identify the Body Forces For gravity acting in the negative z-direction, the body force per unit mass is $\vec{g} = (0, 0, -g)$. The body force on the element is: $$F_{b,x} = \rho \cdot X \cdot dx\,dy\,dz$$ $$F_{b,y} = \rho \cdot Y \cdot dx\,dy\,dz$$ $$F_{b,z} = \rho \cdot Z \cdot dx\,dy\,dz$$ For gravity: $X = 0$, $Y = 0$, $Z = -g$. ### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law Newton's second law for the fluid element states: $$\sum F = m \cdot a$$ The acceleration of the fluid particle is the **material derivative** of velocity: $$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$ ### Step 5: Form the x-Component Equation Summing forces in x-direction and equating to $dm \cdot a_x$: $$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz + \rho X\,dx\,dy\,dz = \rho\,dx\,dy\,dz \cdot \frac{Du}{Dt}$$ Dividing throughout by $\rho\,dx\,dy\,dz$: $$X - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{Du}{Dt}$$ ### Step 6: Form the y and z Component Equations By identical reasoning: $$Y - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \frac{Dv}{Dt}$$ $$Z - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \frac{Dw}{Dt}$$ ### Step 7: Combine into Vector Form Combining the three scalar equations into a single vector equation: $$\vec{F}_{body} - \frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$ Multiplying both sides by $\rho$: $$\boxed{\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}}$$ This is the celebrated **Euler's Equation of Motion**. ## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Equation Along a Streamline Starting from the vector form and projecting onto a streamline direction $s$ (tangent to velocity vector $\vec{V}$): $$\rho \frac{DV}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial s}$$ The material derivative expanded: $$\frac{DV}{Dt} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = -\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} - \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This *streamline form* is the most exam-relevant version. By integrating it for steady flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) and incompressible fluid ($\rho = \text{const}$), you obtain Bernoulli's equation directly. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Limits and Specializations | Limiting Case | Modification to Euler | Resulting Equation | |---------------|----------------------|--------------------| | Steady flow | $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$ | | Incompressible | $\rho = \text{const}$ | $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\nabla p + \vec{g}$ | | Rest (hydrostatics) | $\vec{V} = 0$ | $\nabla p = \rho \vec{g}$ | | Along streamline, steady, incompressible | Integrate $\partial p/\partial s = -\rho V \partial V/\partial s - \rho g \partial z/\partial s$ | Bernoulli: $p/\rho + V^2/2 + gz = C$ | | Irrotational flow | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | $\nabla\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \frac{\vert \nabla\phi \vert^2}{2} + \frac{p}{\rho} + gz\right) = 0$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of an Inviscid Fluid Element (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fluid Element
Size: dx, dy, dz
Mass: rho dx dy dz] --> B[Surface Forces] A --> C[Body Forces] A --> D[Inertial Force
rho Du/Dt per unit volume] B --> B1[Pressure on x-faces
F_px = -dp/dx dx dy dz] B --> B2[Pressure on y-faces
F_py = -dp/dy dx dy dz] B --> B3[Pressure on z-faces
F_pz = -dp/dz dx dy dz] C --> C1[Gravity in -z direction
F_bz = -rho g dx dy dz] B1 --> E[Force Sum] B2 --> E B3 --> E C1 --> E D --> E E --> F[Newton Second Law
Sum F = m a] F --> G[Divide by rho dx dy dz] G --> H[Euler Equation x-component
X - 1/rho dp/dx = Du/Dt] G --> I[Euler Equation y-component
Y - 1/rho dp/dy = Dv/Dt] G --> J[Euler Equation z-component
Z - 1/rho dp/dz = Dw/Dt] H --> K[Vector Form
rho DV/Dt = -grad p + rho g] I --> K J --> K ``` ## 4.2 Logical Flow: From Euler's Equation to Bernoulli's Equation ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Euler Vector Form] --> S2[Project onto Streamline s] S2 --> S3[Use Material Derivative
DV/Dt = dV/dt + V dV/ds] S3 --> S4[Steady Assumption
dV/dt = 0] S4 --> S5[Incompressible Assumption
rho = constant] S5 --> S6[Integrate wrt s] S6 --> S7[Bernoulli Equation
p/rho + V^2/2 + gz = constant] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Compliance Note) > [!IMPORTANT] > All node IDs in the above diagrams are purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B1`, `S1`, etc.) and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All labels are clean, uppercase, and free of markdown formatting characters. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Euler's equation of motion for an inviscid fluid. Mention any two key assumptions.** **Model Answer:** Euler's equation of motion in vector form is: $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$ **Two Key Assumptions:** 1. The fluid is **inviscid** (viscosity $\mu = 0$). 2. The fluid is a **continuum** (properties vary continuously in space), and only pressure (normal stress) and gravity (body force) act on the fluid element. *(Full marks: 1 for equation, 2 for assumptions.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between the material derivative and the local derivative with one example each in the context of Euler's equation.** **Model Answer:** - **Local Derivative** $\left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}\right)$: Rate of change of velocity *at a fixed point in space*. Example: Velocity at the mouth of a river rising/falling with tidal time. - **Material Derivative** $\left(\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}\right)$: Rate of change *following a fluid particle*. Example: Acceleration experienced by a droplet of water flowing down a curved channel. In Euler's equation, the **material derivative** is used because we track the fluid particle, not a fixed point in space. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $-\nabla p$** is the most common error. Students write $+\nabla p$ and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing the material derivative with the local derivative** loses full credit on the convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. > 3. **Missing assumptions** (steady, incompressible) before integrating to Bernoulli's equation = 2 marks lost. > 4. **Sign errors in gravity term** ($+g$ vs $-g$) are penalized heavily in Naval Arch. numerical problems. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Starting from Newton's second law applied to an inviscid fluid element, **derive Euler's equation of motion in vector form**. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)* **(b)** For a **steady, incompressible, inviscid flow**, integrate Euler's equation along a streamline to obtain **Bernoulli's equation**. Discuss its physical significance with reference to a **ship moving through still water**. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Euler's Equation:** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation. The model solution would include:)* - Sketch of fluid element with dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$. - Net pressure force in x-direction: $F_{p,x} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} dx\,dy\,dz$. - Body force in x-direction: $F_{b,x} = \rho X\,dx\,dy\,dz$. - Newton's second law: $\sum F_x = dm \cdot a_x = \rho\,dx\,dy\,dz \cdot \frac{Du}{Dt}$. - Resulting equation: $X - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{Du}{Dt}$. - Similar equations for y and z. - Combined vector form: $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$. **Assumptions stated (for full 7 marks):** 1. Inviscid fluid. 2. Continuum. 3. Only pressure and gravity as forces. 4. No heat transfer / constant properties. **(b) Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation:** For steady flow, $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$. Along a streamline, the tangential component of Euler's equation reduces to: $$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + g\frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0$$ Multiplying throughout by $ds$: $$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s}ds + V\,dV + g\,dz = 0$$ For incompressible flow, $\rho = \text{const}$. Integrating: $$\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = C \text{ (constant along streamline)}$$ This is **Bernoulli's equation**. **Physical Significance for a Ship:** For a ship moving with velocity $V_s$ through still water, applying Bernoulli between a point far ahead (where velocity is $V_s$, pressure is $p_\infty$) and a point on the hull where the local flow velocity is $V$ and pressure is $p$: $$p_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_s^2 = p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$$ Rearranging: $$p = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho(V_s^2 - V^2)$$ This shows that **where the flow accelerates around the hull (e.g., at the bow and stern shoulders), the local pressure drops below ambient**. Conversely, at stagnation points (bow), pressure is highest. The integrated pressure difference around the hull yields the **wave-making and pressure resistance** components of total hull resistance — directly relevant to naval architecture. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat free-body diagram of a fluid element, derive the **three scalar components of Euler's equation** in Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and write them in expanded form using the **material derivative**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A submarine is submerged at a depth of **150 m** in sea water ($\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$). A small observation window is located on the hull where the local flow velocity (relative to the submarine) is **$V = 8 \, \text{m/s}$** and the local pressure is **$p = 800 \, \text{kPa}$ (gauge)**. If the submarine cruises at a speed of **$V_s = 6 \, \text{m/s}$** through still water, determine: *(i)* the **dynamic pressure** at the window location, *(ii)* the **stagnation pressure** if the flow were to come to rest at the window, and *(iii)* comment on **cavitation risk** using the **vapor pressure of sea water** $p_v = 2.5 \, \text{kPa}$ (absolute). *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Three Scalar Components of Euler's Equation:** From Section 3.1, the three components are: **x-component:** $$X - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$ **y-component:** $$Y - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}$$ **z-component:** $$Z - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$ The free-body diagram should show the six pressure forces on the six faces of the element, with the body force $\rho g$ acting in the $-z$ direction. **(b) Submarine Numerical Problem:** **Given:** - Depth: $h = 150$ m - $\rho = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ - Local velocity at window: $V = 8 \, \text{m/s}$ - Local pressure (gauge): $p = 800 \, \text{kPa}$ - Submarine speed: $V_s = 6 \, \text{m/s}$ - Vapor pressure (abs): $p_v = 2.5 \, \text{kPa}$ **(i) Dynamic pressure at the window:** $$q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 8^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 64$$ $$q = 32{,}800 \, \text{Pa} = 32.8 \, \text{kPa}$$ **(ii) Stagnation pressure (flow brought to rest at the window):** Applying Bernoulli between the local point and the stagnation point: $$p_{stag} = p + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = 800 + 32.8 = 832.8 \, \text{kPa (gauge)}$$ **(iii) Cavitation Risk:** The hydrostatic pressure at depth 150 m (gauge) is: $$p_{hydro} = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 150 = 1{,}508{,}287.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1508.3 \, \text{kPa (gauge)}$$ Total local absolute pressure at the window: $$p_{abs} = p_{gauge} + p_{atm} = 800 + 101.325 = 901.325 \, \text{kPa}$$ Comparing with vapor pressure $p_v = 2.5 \, \text{kPa (abs)}$: $$p_{abs} = 901.325 \, \text{kPa} \gg p_v = 2.5 \, \text{kPa}$$ **Conclusion:** Since the local absolute pressure (901.3 kPa) is **far greater** than the vapor pressure (2.5 kPa), there is **no cavitation risk** at the window under these operating conditions. The large positive margin ($\Delta p \approx 898.8 \, \text{kPa}$) provides a substantial cavitation safety factor. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the comparison clearly: "Since $p_{local} \gg p_v$, cavitation will NOT occur." Partial credit is given for correct numerical substitution even if the conclusion is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Euler's Equation (Vector Form):** $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ — the foundational momentum equation for **inviscid** fluids. - **Key Assumptions:** Inviscid, continuum, no shear stress, only pressure + gravity forces. - **Material Derivative:** $\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ — includes both local and convective acceleration. - **Three Scalar Forms:** $X - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{Du}{Dt}$ (and cyclic permutations for y, z). - **Streamline Form:** $\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = -\rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} - \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$ — the most exam-tested form. - **Bernoulli's Equation** is the **integrated form of Euler's equation** along a streamline for **steady, incompressible, inviscid** flow: $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = C$. - **Naval Architecture Relevance:** Pressure distribution around a ship hull → wave and pressure resistance; cavitation analysis on propellers; hydrofoil lift; flow around submarine sailplanes. - **Common Pitfalls:** Sign error on $\nabla p$, forgetting the material derivative, missing assumptions before integrating, and confusion between streamline vs. irrotational (full 3D) integration. - **Standard Constants to Memorize:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $p_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$.

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equation for Ideal and Real Fluid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equation for Ideal and Real Fluid ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Fluid):** A fundamental principle in fluid mechanics that expresses the conservation of mechanical energy along a streamline for the steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow of an ideal fluid. Mathematically, it states that the sum of the **pressure head**, **velocity head**, and **elevation head** remains constant along any streamline. Mathematically, for an ideal (inviscid) fluid in steady flow: $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant (along a streamline)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bernoulli's Equation (Real Fluid):** When fluid viscosity is non-zero, mechanical energy is dissipated into heat (irreversible losses) due to internal friction. The modified form becomes an **energy balance** that includes a head-loss term ($h_L$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ where $h_L$ represents the **head loss** due to viscous effects, pipe friction, fittings, and other irreversibilities. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up Imagine you are **water skiing** behind a moving boat (a classic Naval Architect's scenario!). As the boat speeds up: - The water must accelerate around your body. This is because the boat is pushing water out of its way, and that water has nowhere to go but to rush past you faster than the boat is moving. - The water's **kinetic energy increases** — so its **pressure drops**. You literally feel a region of low pressure on the side facing the boat. - This pressure differential is what produces **lift on hydrofoils** and is the same physics that lets a **venturi meter** measure flow rate inside a pipe. > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Rule of Thumb:** *"Speeding up a fluid costs pressure."* Whenever a fluid accelerates, its pressure must drop (and vice versa), provided elevation does not change and friction is negligible. Bernoulli's equation is the bookkeeping ledger that keeps these energy trades fair. For an **ideal fluid**, the ledger is perfectly balanced — energy can be converted freely between pressure, velocity, and elevation without any leakage. For a **real fluid**, some energy "leaks out" as heat (the $h_L$ term), so the downstream total energy is always less than the upstream total. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Density of fresh water (at 4°C):** $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - **Density of sea water (standard):** $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$ - **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = \rho_w \cdot g = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$ > [!NOTE] > **Naval Architecture Note:** In ship hydrodynamics (hull-form analysis, cavitation prediction, propeller inflow), we often use **sea-water density** ($\rho \approx 1025$ kg/m³) and reference elevations to the **waterline** or **keel baseline**. The Bernoulli equation is the foundation for concepts such as **dynamic pressure** ($q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$), the **Euler number**, and **cavitating flow** analysis. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Assumptions Behind Bernoulli's Equation (Strictly Tested in KTU) The ideal-fluid form rests on **six critical assumptions**: 1. **Steady flow** — flow properties at any point do not change with time. 2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density $\rho$ is constant. 3. **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — fluid viscosity $\mu = 0$; no shear stress. 4. **Flow along a single streamline** — equation applies *between two points on the same streamline*. (The general form, valid throughout the flow field, requires irrotational flow.) 5. **No shaft work** — no pumps or turbines in the control volume (these add/subtract energy). 6. **No heat transfer** — flow is isothermal/adiabatic along the streamline. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Trap:** Many students write Bernoulli's equation "between two points in the flow" without specifying that the two points must lie on the **same streamline**. This is a guaranteed mark-deduction in KTU valuation. ### 2.2 The Three Energy Heads (Heads are energies per unit weight) | Head Type | Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure Head | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | $h_p$ | Energy due to fluid pressure (m of fluid) | | Velocity (Kinetic) Head | $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $h_v$ | Energy due to fluid motion (m of fluid) | | Elevation (Potential) Head | $z$ | $h_z$ | Energy due to height above datum (m of fluid) | The **sum** $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ is called the **Total Head** of the fluid. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Fluid Mechanics question paper.** | # | Equation | Form | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Ideal-fluid Bernoulli (head form) | Frictionless, steady, incompressible flow | | 2 | $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = C$ | Ideal-fluid Bernoulli (pressure form) | Multiplying form (1) by $\rho g$ | | 3 | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Real-fluid Bernoulli | Pipe flow, viscous flow, with losses | | 4 | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_{\text{pump}} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{\text{turbine}} + h_L$ | Energy equation (with machines) | Systems containing pumps/turbines | | 5 | $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation | For incompressible, steady flow | | 6 | $V = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Velocity from venturi / pitot | Flow measurement devices | | 7 | $h_L = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pipe friction (real fluid) | | 8 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | > **Notation Note:** $C$ = constant along streamline; $h_L$ = head loss (m); $f$ = Darcy friction factor; $L$ = pipe length; $D$ = pipe diameter. ### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Equation — Physical Reasoning The equation is fundamentally a statement of **conservation of mechanical energy**: - **Pressure energy** ($P/\rho g$): Work done by pressure forces per unit weight of fluid. - **Kinetic energy** ($V^2/2g$): Energy of motion per unit weight. - **Potential energy** ($z$): Energy of position per unit weight. For an **ideal (inviscid) fluid**, none of this energy is degraded into heat, so the sum remains constant. For a **real (viscous) fluid**, friction between fluid layers (and between fluid and walls) continuously converts mechanical energy into thermal energy — the **head loss** $h_L$ accounts for this. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility (with Naval Architecture Focus) - **Venturi meter / Orifice plate flow measurement:** Differential pressure $\Delta P$ gives flow rate $Q$ via Bernoulli. - **Pitot-static tube:** Measures ship speed by comparing stagnation pressure with static pressure. - **Ship hull-form design:** Velocity distribution along the hull is governed by Bernoulli (high velocity at the stern means low pressure → risk of cavitation). - **Propeller inflow analysis:** Bernoulli applied to the propeller disc. - **Bilge, ballast, and cooling systems:** Pipe-flow design in ships uses the real-fluid form with $h_L$. - **Carburettors, atomizers, and injectors:** Pressure drop → velocity rise → low-pressure zone for fuel suction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Fluid) from Euler's Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Starting Point:** Euler's equation of motion along a streamline for an inviscid, incompressible fluid in steady flow. **Step 1 — Euler's equation along a streamline:** For steady flow, the local acceleration $\partial V/\partial t = 0$. Along a streamline, the component of Euler's equation is: $$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange into differential form:** Bring all terms to one side: $$dP + \rho V\, dV + \rho g\, dz = 0$$ **Step 3 — Divide through by the specific weight $\rho g$:** Use $dP/(\rho g) + V\, dV/g + dz = 0$. Recognising that $\dfrac{V\, dV}{g} = d\!\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2g}\right)$: $$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + d\!\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + dz = 0$$ **Step 4 — Integrate between points 1 and 2 on the streamline:** $$\int_1^2 \frac{dP}{\rho g} + \int_1^2 d\!\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + \int_1^2 dz = 0$$ Because $\rho$ and $g$ are constants for an incompressible fluid in a uniform gravitational field: $$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) = 0$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to the standard form:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 6 — Express as a constant:** $$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = C \quad \text{(along a streamline)}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Real-Fluid Bernoulli Equation (Modification for Viscosity) **Step 1 — Start with the ideal-fluid Bernoulli between points 1 and 2:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 2 — Recognize energy dissipation:** In a real fluid, mechanical energy is **not conserved**; it is converted to heat by viscous shear. The total head at point 2 is therefore **less** than at point 1 by an amount $h_L$ (the head loss): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ **Step 3 — Express in differential form (control-volume energy balance):** For an elemental control volume, the First Law of Thermodynamics for steady, incompressible flow with negligible heat transfer gives: $$-dP - \rho V\, dV - \rho g\, dz - d(p_{\text{loss}}) = 0$$ where $d(p_{\text{loss}})$ is the irreversible pressure loss per unit volume due to viscosity. **Step 4 — Identify the head-loss term:** Defining the head loss as $h_L = \dfrac{p_{\text{loss}}}{\rho g}$: $$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L}$$ **Step 5 — Include pump/turbine head (generalised form):** If a pump adds energy $h_{\text{pump}}$ and a turbine extracts $h_{\text{turbine}}$: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_{\text{pump}} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{\text{turbine}} + h_L$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Ideal vs. Real Fluid Comparison > [!TIP] > **Use this comparison table as a quick-reference in your KTU exam preparation.** | Parameter | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid | |---|---|---| | Viscosity $\mu$ | Zero | Non-zero (finite) | | Shear stress $\tau$ | Zero | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy}$ (Newtonian) | | Energy conservation | Strictly conserved (mechanical) | Mechanical energy is partly lost to heat | | Friction term $h_L$ | $h_L = 0$ | $h_L > 0$ (depends on $f$, $L$, $D$, $V$, surface roughness) | | Bernoulli form | $H_1 = H_2$ | $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ | | Reynolds number effect | Irrelevant (laminar/turbulent distinction meaningless) | Determines flow regime ($\text{Re} < 2000$: laminar; $\text{Re} > 4000$: turbulent) | | Practical applicability | Approximation (air at low speeds, water far from boundaries) | All engineering flows (pipes, ship hulls, marine systems) | | HGL / EGL | HGL = EGL (no slope) | EGL always drops along flow direction | ### 3.4 Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL) and Energy Grade Line (EGL) — Conceptual Foundation > [!NOTE] > **HGL:** The line whose elevation above a datum is $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + z$ (i.e., **Total Head − Velocity Head**). > **EGL:** The line whose elevation above a datum is $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ (i.e., the **Total Head**). - The **EGL** is always **above** the HGL by exactly the velocity head $V^2/(2g)$. - For an **ideal fluid**, both lines are **horizontal** (constant total head). - For a **real fluid**, the EGL has a **continuous downward slope** equal to the head-loss gradient $S_f = h_L/L$, and the HGL is parallel to the EGL if pipe diameter is constant. ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** Water flows steadily through a horizontal pipe that narrows from a diameter of $D_1 = 300$ mm to $D_2 = 150$ mm. The pressure at the larger section is $P_1 = 200$ kPa (gauge), and the velocity is $V_1 = 2$ m/s. Assuming ideal (frictionless) flow, find the velocity and pressure at the constricted section. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply continuity to find $V_2$:** $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = V_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$$ $$V_2 = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{300}{150}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \; \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli (horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$):** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $P_2$:** $$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$ $$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³):** $$P_2 = 200{,}000 + \frac{1000}{2}\left(2^2 - 8^2\right)$$ $$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 500 \cdot (4 - 64)$$ $$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 500 \cdot (-60)$$ $$P_2 = 200{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 170{,}000 \; \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = 170 \; \text{kPa (gauge)}}$$ **Step 5 — Verify the physical meaning:** The constriction accelerates the water from 2 m/s to 8 m/s, and the pressure drops from 200 kPa to 170 kPa — exactly as Bernoulli predicts. ✓ > **Pressure drop $\Delta P = 30$ kPa.** This is the principle behind a venturi meter: measuring $\Delta P$ allows us to back-calculate the flow rate. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Conversion in Bernoulli's Equation This diagram maps the **flow of energy** as fluid moves along a streamline, illustrating how the three forms of mechanical energy interconvert. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["FLUID ELEMENT AT POINT 1
UPSTREAM STAGNATION REGION"] --> B{"BERNOULLI ENGINE
ALONG A SINGLE STREAMLINE"} B --> C["PRESSURE HEAD
P divided by rho times g
Static Pressure Energy"] B --> D["VELOCITY HEAD
V squared divided by 2g
Kinetic Energy per unit weight"] B --> E["ELEVATION HEAD
z
Gravitational Potential Energy"] C --> F["REAL FLUID HEAD LOSS hL
Viscous Dissipation to Heat
hL equals f times L over D times V squared over 2g"] D --> F E --> F C --> G["FLUID ELEMENT AT POINT 2
DOWNSTREAM REGION"] D --> G E --> G F --> H["ENERGY GRADE LINE EGL
Drops Continuously with Distance"] G --> H H --> I["HYDRAULIC GRADE LINE HGL
Lies Below EGL by Velocity Head"] I --> J["APPLICATION LAYER
Venturi Meter, Pitot Tube
Ship Hull Form, Propeller Inflow"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Selecting the Correct Bernoulli Form This schematic is a high-yield **exam aid** — use it in your KTU preparation to pick the right equation in a problem. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Fluid Flow Problem"] --> B{"Is Viscosity
Negligible?"} B -- YES --> C["Is Flow Steady?"] B -- NO --> D["Use Real Fluid Bernoulli
Include hL Term"] C -- YES --> E["Is Fluid
Incompressible?"] C -- NO --> Z["Use Modified Unsteady
Bernoulli Equation"] E -- YES --> F["Are Points 1 and 2
on Same Streamline?"] E -- NO --> Y["Use Compressible Flow
Energy Equation"] F -- YES --> G["Use Ideal Fluid Bernoulli
P by rho g plus V squared by 2g plus z equals C"] F -- NO --> X["State Constraint:
Irrotational Flow Required"] D --> H["Is there a Pump
or Turbine?"] H -- YES --> I["Add hPUMP and hTURBINE Terms"] H -- NO --> J["Standard Real Fluid Form
H1 equals H2 plus hL"] I --> K["Final Generalised Energy Equation"] J --> K G --> K["END: Apply Continuity
and Solve for Unknown"] K --> L["END"] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Physical Picture of a Venturi (for Intuition) This block diagram maps the physical components of a venturi meter to the Bernoulli equation terms. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["INLET SECTION
Diameter D1 equals 300 mm
Pressure P1 equals 200 kPa
Velocity V1 equals 2 m per s"] --> MID["THROAT SECTION
Diameter D2 equals 150 mm
Pressure P2 equals 170 kPa
Velocity V2 equals 8 m per s"] MID --> P2o["OUTLET SECTION
Diameter D3 equals 300 mm
Pressure P3 approximately 200 kPa
Velocity V3 approximately 2 m per s"] P1 -. "Differential Pressure
Delta P equals 30 kPa" .-> MID MID -. "Flow rate from
Bernoulli plus Continuity" .-> Q["DISCHARGE Q equals A1 V1
equals 0.141 cubic m per s"] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic: Total-Head Decomposition (EGL vs. HGL along a real pipe) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["DATUM
Reference Elevation"] --> B["EGL AT INLET
Total Head H1"] A --> C["HGL AT INLET
HGL1 equals H1 minus V1 squared over 2g"] B --> D["EGL AT OUTLET
Total Head H2 equals H1 minus hL"] C --> E["HGL AT OUTLET
HGL2 equals H2 minus V2 squared over 2g"] D --> F["HEAD LOSS hL
Slope equals hL over L
equals f V squared over 2g D"] E --> F ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. State and explain Bernoulli's equation for an ideal fluid in steady flow. List the assumptions involved.** **Model Answer:** Bernoulli's equation, in its head form, states that for the steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow of a fluid, the sum of the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head is constant along a streamline. $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant (along a streamline)}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady flow 2. Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$) 3. Inviscid fluid ($\mu = 0$) 4. The equation applies along a **single streamline** 5. No pump or turbine work 6. No heat transfer Physically, it is a statement of **conservation of mechanical energy** in a frictionless flow. *(3 marks: 1 mark equation, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark assumptions.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate clearly between the Bernoulli's equations for an ideal fluid and a real fluid. When does the real-fluid form reduce to the ideal-fluid form?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid | |---|---|---| | Viscosity | Neglected (zero) | Considered (finite) | | Energy conservation | Strictly conserved | Mechanical energy partly lost as heat | | Equation form | $H_1 = H_2$ | $H_1 = H_2 + h_L$ | | Friction loss | Absent | Present (Darcy–Weisbach $h_L$) | The real-fluid equation **reduces to the ideal-fluid form** when the head loss $h_L = 0$. This happens when: - The fluid has zero viscosity (theoretical only). - The flow is in the inviscid outer region far from solid boundaries. - The pipe is very short and smooth, and velocities are small enough that friction losses are negligible. *(3 marks: 1 mark for ideal equation, 1 mark for real equation with $h_L$, 1 mark for the reduction condition.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern. Answer ONE of the following.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation for the steady, incompressible, inviscid flow of an ideal fluid along a streamline, starting from Euler's equation of motion. State clearly the assumptions and the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (Complete Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Write Euler's equation along a streamline (1 mark):** For steady, inviscid, incompressible flow, the component of Euler's equation along a streamline is: $$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to total differentials (1 mark):** Multiplying both sides by $ds$ and using $\partial/\partial s \to d/ds$ along a streamline: $$-dP = \rho V\, dV + \rho g\, dz$$ **Step 3 — Divide by $\rho g$ (1 mark):** $$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + \frac{V\, dV}{g} + dz = 0$$ **Step 4 — Recognise the perfect differential (1 mark):** Since $V\, dV = \dfrac{1}{2}d(V^2)$, we have $\dfrac{V\, dV}{g} = d\!\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2g}\right)$. So: $$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + d\!\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + dz = 0$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from point 1 to point 2 along the streamline (1 mark):** $$\int_1^2 \frac{dP}{\rho g} + \int_1^2 d\!\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right) + \int_1^2 dz = 0$$ Since $\rho$ and $g$ are constants: $$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) = 0$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange into the standard form (1 mark):** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = C$$ **Step 7 — State the physical significance of each term (1 mark):** - $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$: pressure energy per unit weight (pressure head). - $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$: kinetic energy per unit weight (velocity head). - $z$: potential energy per unit weight (elevation head). The equation expresses **conservation of mechanical energy** along a streamline in frictionless flow. --- **(b)** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) flows through a horizontal venturi meter. The inlet diameter is $D_1 = 200$ mm and the throat diameter is $D_2 = 100$ mm. The inlet pressure is $P_1 = 150$ kPa (gauge) and the inlet velocity is $V_1 = 1.5$ m/s. Calculate: **(i)** the velocity at the throat, **(ii)** the pressure at the throat, and **(iii)** the volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Velocity at the throat (2 marks):** Apply continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$: $$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 1.5 \times \left(\frac{200}{100}\right)^2 = 1.5 \times 4 = 6 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Pressure at the throat (3 marks):** Apply Bernoulli's equation (horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Solve for $P_2$: $$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$ $$P_2 = 150{,}000 + \frac{1000}{2}\left(1.5^2 - 6^2\right)$$ $$P_2 = 150{,}000 + 500 \times (2.25 - 36) = 150{,}000 + 500 \times (-33.75)$$ $$P_2 = 150{,}000 - 16{,}875 = 133{,}125 \; \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = 133.125 \; \text{kPa (gauge)}}$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate (2 marks):** $$Q = A_1 V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 \cdot V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 \times 1.5$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 \times 1.5 = 0.04712 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0471 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 47.12 \; \text{L/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A frequent error in this type of problem is forgetting to **square the diameter ratio** in the continuity step. Marks are also lost for unit inconsistencies — always convert diameters to **metres** before substituting. Use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ unless the problem explicitly states **sea water** (then $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the aid of a clear diagram, explain the concepts of the **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL)** and the **Energy Grade Line (EGL)**. Discuss how they behave for (i) an ideal fluid in a horizontal pipe of varying cross-section, and (ii) a real fluid in a uniform horizontal pipe. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):** - **Energy Grade Line (EGL):** A line whose elevation above an arbitrary horizontal datum equals the **total head** of the fluid at that location: $$H_E = \frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$$ - **Hydraulic Grade Line (HGL):** A line whose elevation above the datum equals the **piezometric head**: $$H_H = \frac{P}{\rho g} + z$$ The EGL always lies above the HGL by an amount equal to the **velocity head** $V^2/(2g)$. The vertical distance between the two lines at any section represents the local kinetic energy per unit weight of the fluid. **Step 2 — Case (i): Ideal fluid, horizontal pipe of varying cross-section (3 marks):** - The total head $H_E$ is **constant** (Bernoulli's equation gives $H_1 = H_2 = H_3 = \cdots$), so the **EGL is a horizontal straight line**. - The HGL = EGL $-$ $V^2/(2g)$: - At a **constriction**, $V$ is large, so $V^2/(2g)$ is large, and the HGL **dips downward**. - At an **expansion**, $V$ is small, so $V^2/(2g)$ is small, and the HGL **rises upward**. - The HGL therefore **oscillates** along the pipe, but the EGL remains flat. There is no head loss in this case ($h_L = 0$). **Step 3 — Case (ii): Real fluid, uniform horizontal pipe (2 marks):** - For a real fluid, friction causes a **continuous loss of total head** along the flow direction. - The **EGL slopes downward** with a constant gradient $S_f = h_L/L$ (Darcy–Weisbach slope). - Since the pipe has **uniform cross-section**, the velocity $V$ is constant, so $V^2/(2g)$ is constant. Therefore the HGL is **parallel to the EGL** and lies a fixed distance $V^2/(2g)$ below it. - The HGL also drops linearly with the same slope as the EGL. **Summary Table:** | Quantity | Ideal Fluid (varying cross-section) | Real Fluid (uniform pipe) | |---|---|---| | EGL | Horizontal (flat) | Slopes downward with slope $S_f$ | | HGL | Oscillates (dips at constrictions) | Parallel to EGL, drops linearly | | Head loss $h_L$ | Zero | $h_L = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | --- **(b)** A real fluid of density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 100$ mm at a velocity of $V = 3$ m/s. The pipe length is $L = 50$ m, and the Darcy friction factor is $f = 0.02$. The pressure at the inlet is $P_1 = 250$ kPa. Using the real-fluid Bernoulli equation, determine the pressure $P_2$ at the outlet of the pipe. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Write the real-fluid Bernoulli equation (1 mark):** For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) with constant cross-section ($V_1 = V_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ Since $V_1 = V_2 = V$, the velocity-head terms cancel: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + h_L$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = P_1 - \rho g \cdot h_L}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the head loss using the Darcy–Weisbach equation (2 marks):** $$h_L = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ $$h_L = 0.02 \times \frac{50}{0.1} \times \frac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$ $$h_L = 0.02 \times 500 \times \frac{9}{19.62}$$ $$h_L = 10 \times 0.4587 = 4.587 \; \text{m of fluid}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the pressure loss $\Delta P$ (2 marks):** $$\Delta P = \rho g \cdot h_L = 950 \times 9.81 \times 4.587$$ $$\Delta P = 9319.5 \times 4.587 \approx 42{,}751 \; \text{Pa} \approx 42.75 \; \text{kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the outlet pressure $P_2$ (2 marks):** $$P_2 = P_1 - \Delta P = 250 - 42.75$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = 207.25 \; \text{kPa}}$$ **Verification:** The outlet pressure is lower than the inlet pressure by about 17%, which is physically reasonable for a 50-m-long pipe with moderate friction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Real-Fluid Bernoulli Problems:** > 1. **Do not cancel the velocity-head terms prematurely.** Only when $V_1 = V_2$ (i.e., the pipe has constant cross-section) can the velocity-head terms be eliminated. If the pipe diameter changes, the velocity-head difference contributes to the pressure change *in addition to* the head loss. > 2. **Unit conversion in $h_L = f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$:** $D$ and $L$ must be in the **same units** (e.g., both in metres). A common error is leaving $D$ in mm while $L$ is in m — this produces a $h_L$ that is off by a factor of 1000. > 3. **Distinguish "head loss" (in metres) from "pressure loss" (in Pa).** To convert: $\Delta P = \rho g \cdot h_L$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bernoulli's Equation — Module 2 Rapid-Revision Flashcard (KTU 2024 Scheme)** **Core Equations:** - **Ideal fluid:** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ (along a streamline). - **Real fluid:** $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$. - **With machines:** Add $h_{\text{pump}}$ on the left and $h_{\text{turbine}}$ on the right. - **Continuity (incompressible):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. - **Darcy–Weisbach head loss:** $h_L = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$. **The Six Inviolable Assumptions (Ideal Form):** 1. Steady flow. 2. Incompressible fluid. 3. Inviscid (zero viscosity). 4. Along a **single streamline**. 5. No pumps/turbines. 6. No heat transfer. **Three Heads to Memorise:** - Pressure head: $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ - Velocity head: $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ - Elevation head: $z$ **Key Physical Rules:** - **Speeding up a fluid costs pressure** (and vice versa) at constant elevation. - **Total head is constant** in ideal flow; it **decreases monotonically** in real flow. - **EGL = HGL + velocity head.** EGL is always at or above the HGL. - For real fluid in a uniform pipe: **HGL ∥ EGL** (both drop linearly with slope $S_f$). - For ideal fluid in a varying cross-section pipe: **EGL is flat, HGL oscillates**. **Naval Architecture High-Yield Applications:** - Venturi / orifice flowmeters in engine cooling and ballast systems. - Pitot-static tubes for ship-speed measurement. - Hull-form velocity and pressure distribution (cavitation analysis). - Propeller inflow and thrust analysis. - Bilge, fire-fighting, and fuel-transfer piping networks. **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:** - Writing Bernoulli "between two arbitrary points" instead of specifying *same streamline*. - Using $g = 10$ m/s² instead of $9.81$ m/s² (KTU prefers $9.81$ unless told otherwise). - Confusing head (m) with pressure (Pa). Always check your units at the final answer. - Forgetting the $h_L$ term in real-fluid problems — this gives **inflated** outlet pressures and is a guaranteed mark-deduction. - Mixing fresh-water and sea-water densities. **Examiner-Approved Final-Answer Format:** 1. State the governing equation (Bernoulli/continuity). 2. List the assumptions. 3. Substitute the data with **units**. 4. Solve algebraically before plugging in numbers. 5. Box the final answer with **correct units**.

topics.title: Application of Bernoulli's Equation to Venturimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Application of Bernoulli's Equation to Venturimeter # 📘 MODULE 2: APPLICATION OF BERNOULLI'S EQUATION TO VENTURIMETER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Venturimeter** is a mechanical flow-measuring device based directly on the **principle of Bernoulli's equation**, consisting of a **convergent inlet cone (entry angle ≈ 21°)**, a **cylindrical throat**, and a **gradually divergent outlet cone (diffuser angle ≈ 5° to 7°)**. It measures the **rate of fluid flow (discharge)** through a closed conduit by exploiting the **pressure differential** generated between the inlet section and the throat, where the cross-sectional area contracts, accelerating the fluid in accordance with the **continuity equation** and reducing its **static pressure head** as per Bernoulli's theorem. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), this device is classified as a **differential pressure type flow meter** and forms a cornerstone topic in **PCNST302 – Fluid Mechanics for Naval Architects** because of its critical use in: - Seawater cooling systems of marine engines - Boiler feed lines and condensers on board ships - Ballast and bilge pumping systems - HVAC ducting in naval architecture applications > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Definition Standard:** Students must explicitly mention the *three* constitutive sections — **converging cone, throat, and diverging cone** — and cite **Bernoulli's equation + Continuity equation** as the foundational governing principles. Missing either equation invites a 2-mark penalty. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍃 Imagine you are **squeezing a garden hose** with your thumb to water plants in your garden. The moment you constrict the opening: - The **water speeds up** (you can see it shooting further) - The **pressure at the constriction drops** (you feel a slight suction on your thumb) Now, place **two transparent pressure gauges** — one just before the constriction, and one at the constriction. The **difference in their readings** is directly proportional to the **speed of the water**, and therefore to the **amount of water flowing per second**. A **Venturimeter works on exactly this principle**, but instead of a thumb, it uses a precisely machined **converging-diverging tube** that smoothly accelerates the fluid, measures the pressure drop, and from it calculates the discharge. The **diverging section** at the exit is the genius — it **recovers most of the kinetic energy as pressure energy**, minimizing energy loss. > [!NOTE] > **Real Naval Architecture Analogy:** Think of a venturimeter as the *flow counterpart* of a **ship's hull form**. The convergent inlet (entry) is the **forward body** that gathers flow; the throat is the **midsection** where velocity peaks and pressure drops; the diffuser (divergent outlet) is the **afterbody** that allows gentle pressure recovery — analogous to a **bulbous bow** or **stern shape** that reduces wake losses. ### 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics Used - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (KTU default) - **Density of fresh water (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³** - **Density of seawater (ρ):** **1025 kg/m³** (oceanographic standard) - **Density of mercury (ρ_Hg):** **13,600 kg/m³** - **Typical Convergent Cone Half-Angle:** **21°** (industry standard for Venturi) - **Typical Divergent Cone Half-Angle:** **5° to 7°** (ensures sub-atmospheric pressure recovery without flow separation) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Underlying Physics The venturimeter operates on the **simultaneous application** of two cardinal conservation laws of fluid mechanics: 1. **The Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)** — for an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe of varying cross-section. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy along a streamline)** — for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why both equations are needed:** > - Bernoulli's equation gives a relationship between **pressure and velocity**. > - The continuity equation gives a relationship between **velocities at two sections of different area**. > - Combining them yields a **single formula expressing discharge purely in terms of measurable quantities (area, pressure difference, fluid density)**. --- ## 2.2 Structured Analytical Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning) The derivation of the venturimeter discharge formula proceeds through six logical tiers: - **Tier 1 — Section Identification:** Establish two control sections: - **Section 1 (1-1):** Upstream of the inlet cone, with pipe diameter **D₁** and area **A₁**. - **Section 2 (2-2):** At the throat, with diameter **D₂** and area **A₂**, where **A₂ < A₁**. - **Tier 2 — Continuity Equation Application:** For an incompressible fluid in steady flow: - The mass flow rate is conserved: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. - This yields $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2} V_2$. - **Tier 3 — Bernoulli's Equation Application:** Assuming the pipe is horizontal (z₁ = z₂), apply Bernoulli between sections 1-1 and 2-2: - $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$. - **Tier 4 — Pressure Head Difference:** Rearranging the Bernoulli equation: - $\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$. - **Tier 5 — Substitution via Continuity:** Substituting $V_1$ from Tier 2: - $\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$. - **Tier 6 — Discharge Expression:** Solving for V₂ and multiplying by A₂ gives: - **Theoretical Discharge:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}\sqrt{2gh}$. - **Actual Discharge (with Cd):** $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}\sqrt{2gh}$. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Tier:** Tier 1 identifies the *mathematical boundaries*; Tier 2 brings in *mass conservation*; Tier 3 introduces *energy conservation*; Tier 4 isolates the *measurable quantity* (pressure drop); Tier 5 eliminates *velocity at one section*; Tier 6 converts the result into a *practical engineering formula* involving only measurable variables. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) 📋 | **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Application Context** | |:------------|:---------------|:----------------------|:------------------------| | Continuity Equation | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | A = m², V = m/s | Mass conservation in incompressible flow | | Bernoulli's Equation (Horizontal) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ | P = N/m², g = 9.81 m/s² | Energy conservation along a streamline | | Velocity at Throat (V₂) | $V_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | V = m/s, P = Pa | Derived velocity assuming ideal flow | | Theoretical Discharge (Q_th) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | Q = m³/s | Ideal discharge using piezometric head | | Pressure Head Difference (Manometer) | $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$ | h = m of working fluid | Direct piezometric head reading | | Pressure Head Difference (U-tube Manometer) | $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ | x = manometer deflection (m), ρ_m = manometer fluid density | Real manometer with denser fluid (mercury/CCl₄) | | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | $C_d$ dimensionless (0.95–0.99) | Corrects for viscous losses and turbulence | | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Empirical, determined by calibration | | Typical Value of C_d | $C_d \approx 0.96 \text{ to } 0.98$ | dimensionless | Standard for well-designed venturimeters | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall in Formula Usage:** Many students mistakenly write the manometer deflection relation as $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right)$ — this is **dimensionally correct** if h is in **m of flowing fluid**. If a problem gives $h$ as **m of manometric fluid** (rare), the formula changes. Always read the problem statement carefully. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture 🚢 The venturimeter is a **non-negotiable component** in modern ship systems: - **Main Sea-Water Cooling Lines:** Measures flow rate to condensers, ensuring optimal heat transfer in the steam cycle. - **Boiler Feed Systems:** Critical for maintaining mass balance in boilers and economizers. - **Bilge and Ballast Lines:** Used for monitoring transfer rates during ballasting/deballasting operations. - **Lubricating Oil Circulation Systems:** Ensures adequate flow to bearings, turbines, and gears. - **Fresh Water Generators (Evaporators):** Monitors feed and brine flow on board. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When answering naval-architecture-specific questions, **always contextualize** your answer. Don't just say "used to measure flow rate" — say "used to measure the discharge of seawater through the main condenser cooling line, ensuring thermal efficiency of the marine steam power plant." This shows application-level understanding (Bloom Level 3) and earns full marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Venturimeter Discharge Formula ### 📐 Assumptions for the Standard Derivation To obtain a **closed-form analytical solution**, the following idealized assumptions are made (KTU standard): 1. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant density ρ). 2. The flow is **steady** (time-invariant at any point). 3. The flow is **inviscid** along the streamline considered (no viscous losses — this is why we later introduce C_d). 4. The pipe is **horizontal** (z₁ = z₂) — vertical and inclined cases require potential energy terms. 5. The pressure tapping points are at sections **1-1 (inlet)** and **2-2 (throat)**. 6. The **manometer fluid** is denser than the flowing fluid (e.g., mercury for water flow). --- ### 🔢 Step 1: Define the Control Sections Let: - **Section 1-1 (Inlet):** Diameter = D₁, Area = A₁, Pressure = P₁, Velocity = V₁, Elevation = z₁ - **Section 2-2 (Throat):** Diameter = D₂, Area = A₂, Pressure = P₂, Velocity = V₂, Elevation = z₂ The areas are related to diameters by: $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}D_1^2 \quad \text{and} \quad A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}D_2^2$$ **Conversion Logic:** The cross-sectional area of a circular pipe is the area of a circle, $A = \pi r^2 = \pi(D/2)^2 = (\pi/4) D^2$. This is used to convert between diameter and area in numerical problems. --- ### 🔢 Step 2: Apply the Continuity Equation For an **incompressible, steady** flow with **no branch lines**, mass conservation reduces to volume conservation: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ Solving for V₁: $$V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$$ Or, in terms of diameters: $$V_1 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 V_2$$ **Conversion Logic:** The continuity equation for incompressible flow is a statement that the volume flow rate (Q = AV) is constant throughout the pipe, regardless of changes in cross-section. Rearranging yields the relation above. --- ### 🔢 Step 3: Apply Bernoulli's Equation For a **horizontal pipe** ($z_1 = z_2$), the **inviscid Bernoulli equation** between sections 1-1 and 2-2 is: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Since $z_1 = z_2$, the elevation terms cancel: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging to isolate the pressure difference: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Conversion Logic:** This is the *head form* of Bernoulli's equation, where each term has units of length (meters of fluid). The first term is the **pressure head**, and the second is the **velocity head**. Equality of total head at both sections follows from energy conservation. --- ### 🔢 Step 4: Eliminate V₁ Using Continuity Substitute $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$ into the Bernoulli-derived equation: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}$$ Factoring out $V_2^2$: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$ **Conversion Logic:** The substitution reduces the two unknowns (V₁ and V₂) to a single unknown (V₂), which can now be solved for. This is the *elimination step* — a hallmark technique in fluid mechanics problems. --- ### 🔢 Step 5: Solve for V₂ (Throat Velocity) Multiplying both sides by $2g$: $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$ Solving for $V_2^2$: $$V_2^2 = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}$$ Taking the square root: $$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** $V_2$ is positive in the direction of flow. The denominator is always real and positive because $A_2 < A_1$ (throat is smaller than inlet). --- ### 🔢 Step 6: Derive the Theoretical Discharge The **theoretical discharge** (volumetric flow rate) at the throat is: $$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $A_1$: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ Introducing the **piezometric head difference** $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** This is the *master formula* for venturimeter discharge. The piezometric head $h$ is what a U-tube manometer directly measures. --- ### 🔢 Step 7: Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge In practice, **viscous effects, turbulence, and minor losses** reduce the actual discharge below the theoretical value. This is captured by the empirical **coefficient of discharge C_d**: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$ For a U-tube manometer with a denser manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ and manometer deflection $x$: $$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right)$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot x \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right)}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** This final boxed expression is the **complete, ready-to-use formula** that integrates all the physics (Bernoulli + continuity) and all the practicalities (manometer, C_d correction). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solved Numerical Example (Worked Out) ### 📊 Problem Statement (KTU Style) A **venturimeter** is installed in a **horizontal pipe** of diameter **D₁ = 200 mm** carrying oil of specific gravity **0.8**. The throat diameter is **D₂ = 100 mm**. A **differential U-tube manometer** with mercury (S.G. = 13.6) shows a deflection of **x = 250 mm**. If the **coefficient of discharge C_d = 0.98**, calculate the **actual discharge** in litres per second.
