(Take g = 9.81 m/s².) --- ### 🔍 Step-by-Step Solution **Given Data:** - $D_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.8 \Rightarrow \rho_{\text{oil}} = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $S_{Hg} = 13.6 \Rightarrow \rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $x = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$ - $C_d = 0.98$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Compute the Cross-Sectional Areas:** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.04) = 0.031416 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01) = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Piezometric Head Difference (h):** $$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right) = 0.25 \left(\frac{13600 - 800}{800}\right) = 0.25 \left(\frac{12800}{800}\right) = 0.25 \times 16 = 4.0 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Geometric Factor:** $$A_1^2 = (0.031416)^2 = 9.8696 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$ $$A_2^2 = (0.007854)^2 = 6.1685 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$ $$A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 9.8696 \times 10^{-4} - 0.61685 \times 10^{-4} = 9.2528 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$ $$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{9.2528 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.030418 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Discharge (Q_th):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$ $$A_1 A_2 = 0.031416 \times 0.007854 = 2.4674 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$ $$\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.0} = \sqrt{78.48} = 8.8592 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{2.4674 \times 10^{-4}}{0.030418} \times 8.8592 = 8.1117 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.8592 = 0.07186 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the Actual Discharge (Q_act):** $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 0.07186 = 0.07042 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Converting to litres per second (1 m³ = 1000 L): $$Q_{act} = 0.07042 \times 1000 = 70.42 \text{ L/s}$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: $$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.07042 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 70.42 \text{ L/s}}$$ # 4. It earns **2 easy marks** for visual communication and gives the examiner confidence in your conceptual clarity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a venturimeter and state the principle on which it works. Mention the role of the divergent cone in its design.** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): A **venturimeter** is a **differential pressure type flow measurement device** used to determine the discharge of a fluid flowing through a closed conduit. **(1 Mark)** It works on the **principle of Bernoulli's equation**, in conjunction with the **continuity equation**. As the fluid flows through the converging section into the throat, the cross-sectional area decreases, causing the **velocity to increase** and the **static pressure to drop**. This pressure difference between the inlet and throat is measured and used to calculate the discharge. **(1 Mark)** The **divergent cone (diffuser)** at the outlet plays a critical role: it **gradually decelerates the fluid**, recovering a major portion of the kinetic energy as pressure energy, thereby **minimizing head loss** and ensuring high **coefficient of discharge** (typically 0.96–0.98). The small divergent angle (5°–7°) prevents flow separation and eddying. **(1 Mark)** --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge through a venturimeter installed in a horizontal pipe. State clearly the assumptions made.** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): **Assumptions:** **(1 Mark for listing assumptions)** - Incompressible, steady flow - Inviscid flow (no friction) — viscous effects corrected later via C_d - Horizontal pipe (z₁ = z₂) - Single streamline flow, no branch connections **Derivation Outline:** **(2 Marks)** **Step 1:** Continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_1 = \dfrac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$ **Step 2:** Bernoulli (horizontal): $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$ **Step 3:** Substitute and solve: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$ where $h$ is the piezometric head difference. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2 typically features a **14-mark question with internal choice**. Students must answer either Option A or Option B. Both options below are designed in strict KTU 2024 format with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. --- ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024] **A horizontal venturimeter with inlet diameter 30 cm and throat diameter 15 cm is fitted in a pipe carrying water. A differential mercury manometer connected across the inlet and throat shows a deflection of 20 cm. Assuming the coefficient of discharge as 0.98, determine:** **(a) The discharge of water through the venturimeter in litres per second. (7 Marks)** **(b) The error in the discharge if the venturimeter were used without considering the coefficient of discharge. (7 Marks)** ### ✅ Complete Model Solution: #### **Part (a) — Actual Discharge Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** - $D_1 = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$ - $D_2 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ - $x = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $C_d = 0.98$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Areas:** **(1 Mark)** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Piezometric head h:** **(1 Mark)** $$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right) = 0.20 \left(\frac{13600 - 1000}{1000}\right) = 0.20 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 3 — Geometric term:** **(1 Mark)** $$\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{(0.07069)^2 - (0.01767)^2} = \sqrt{0.004997 - 0.000312} = \sqrt{0.004685} = 0.06845 \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_1 A_2 = 0.07069 \times 0.01767 = 1.2490 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 4 — Q_th:** **(1 Mark)** $$\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.4424} = 7.0315 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{1.2490 \times 10^{-3}}{0.06845} \times 7.0315 = 0.018247 \times 7.0315 = 0.12830 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Q_act:** **(1 Mark)** $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 0.12830 = 0.12573 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to L/s:** **(2 Marks)** $$\boxed{Q_{act} = 125.73 \text{ L/s}}$$ #### **Part (b) — Error in Discharge if C_d is Ignored (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Q_th (without C_d):** This is already computed in Part (a) as $Q_{th} = 0.12830 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. **(2 Marks)** **Step 2 — Difference between Q_th and Q_act:** **(2 Marks)** $$\Delta Q = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.12830 - 0.12573 = 0.00257 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.57 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage Error:** **(3 Marks)** $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\Delta Q}{Q_{act}} \times 100 = \frac{0.00257}{0.12573} \times 100 = 2.04\%$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{Error} = 2.04\%}$$ **Interpretation:** If the venturimeter is used without applying the discharge coefficient, the **discharge is overestimated by approximately 2%**, which is significant in precise applications like boiler feed measurement and condenser cooling on ships. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024] **(a) With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a venturimeter. Explain why the divergent cone angle is kept much smaller (5°–7°) than the convergent cone angle (≈ 21°). (7 Marks)** **(b) A venturimeter is to be installed in a horizontal pipe of 25 cm diameter to measure a flow of 0.10 m³/s of oil (specific gravity = 0.85). If the coefficient of discharge is 0.97 and the differential manometer (mercury) shows a deflection of 18 cm, determine the throat diameter of the venturimeter. (7 Marks)** ### ✅ Complete Model Solution: #### **Part (a) — Construction and Working Description (7 Marks):** **Construction:** **(3 Marks)** A venturimeter consists of three main sections assembled in series within a pipeline: 1. **Inlet section (Section 1-1):** A straight cylindrical portion of diameter $D_1$ with a **piezometer tapping** (or annular piezometer ring) to measure inlet static pressure $P_1$. 2. **Convergent cone (Entry cone):** A smoothly machined truncated cone of half-angle ≈ 21° that reduces the cross-section from $D_1$ to $D_2$. 3. **Throat (Section 2-2):** A short cylindrical portion of diameter $D_2$ with a second **piezometer tapping** to measure throat pressure $P_2$. 4. **Divergent cone (Diffuser):** A gradually expanding truncated cone of half-angle 5°–7° that enlarges the cross-section back to $D_1$. A **differential U-tube manometer** (typically using mercury) is connected between the two pressure tappings. **Working:** **(2 Marks)** The fluid enters at Section 1-1 with velocity $V_1$ and pressure $P_1$. As the cross-section contracts in the convergent cone, **velocity increases to $V_2$** and **pressure drops to $P_2$** at the throat (Bernoulli's principle). The manometer records this pressure difference as a deflection $x$, which is then used in the discharge formula to calculate the flow rate. The diffuser recovers most of the kinetic energy, minimizing losses. **Why the Angle Difference (5°–7° vs. 21°):** **(2 Marks)** The **convergent cone angle is larger (≈ 21°)** because the flow is **accelerating** — boundary layers are thin and stable, and a steep contraction is feasible without flow separation. The **divergent cone angle is much smaller (5°–7°)** because the flow is **decelerating** (adverse pressure gradient). A small angle prevents **boundary layer separation, flow reversal, and eddying** in the diffuser, which would otherwise cause **cavitation** and **large head losses**. This asymmetry is the key design feature that makes venturimeters highly efficient. #### **Part (b) — Throat Diameter Determination (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** - $D_1 = 0.25 \text{ m}$ - $Q_{act} = 0.10 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $S_{\text{oil}} = 0.85 \Rightarrow \rho_{\text{oil}} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $x = 0.18 \text{ m}$ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $C_d = 0.97$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Piezometric head h:** **(1 Mark)** $$h = x\left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right) = 0.18 \left(\frac{13600 - 850}{850}\right) = 0.18 \times 15.0235 = 2.7042 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the discharge equation in terms of A₂:** **(2 Marks)** $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$ Let $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$. Let $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_2^2$ (unknown). Substituting values: $$0.10 = 0.97 \cdot \frac{0.04909 \cdot A_2}{\sqrt{0.04909^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.7042}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{2gh}$:** **(1 Mark)** $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.7042} = \sqrt{53.06} = 7.2844 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and solve for A₂:** **(2 Marks)** $$0.10 = 0.97 \cdot \frac{0.04909 \cdot A_2}{\sqrt{0.002410 - A_2^2}} \cdot 7.2844$$ $$\frac{0.10}{0.97 \times 7.2844} = \frac{0.04909 \cdot A_2}{\sqrt{0.002410 - A_2^2}}$$ $$0.01415 = \frac{0.04909 \cdot A_2}{\sqrt{0.002410 - A_2^2}}$$ $$\sqrt{0.002410 - A_2^2} = \frac{0.04909 \cdot A_2}{0.01415} = 3.469 \cdot A_2$$ Squaring both sides: $$0.002410 - A_2^2 = 12.034 \cdot A_2^2$$ $$0.002410 = 13.034 \cdot A_2^2$$ $$A_2^2 = \frac{0.002410}{13.034} = 1.8489 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$ $$A_2 = 0.01360 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Compute D₂:** **(1 Mark)** $$D_2 = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_2}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.01360}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.01731} = 0.1316 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{D_2 \approx 13.16 \text{ cm} = 131.6 \text{ mm}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > ## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Venturimeter Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $D_1$ and $D_2$ are usually given in cm or mm; $x$ in cm or mm. Forgetting to convert to **metres** before substitution is the **#1 cause of arithmetic errors** in KTU answer sheets. — *Penalty: 2 marks* > > 2. **Confusing $h$ (piezometric head in m of flowing fluid) with $x$ (manometer deflection in m of manometric fluid):** Students often use $h = x$ directly, which is **wrong unless the fluids are identical**. The correct relation is $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right)$. — *Penalty: 3 marks* > > 3. **Forgetting the coefficient of discharge C_d:** The question may give C_d explicitly or implicitly via "actual discharge." Failing to apply C_d = Q_actual/Q_theoretical leads to **systematic overestimation**. — *Penalty: 2 marks* > > 4. **Ignoring the $\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ denominator:** Some students write $Q = A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$, which is dimensionally inconsistent. The denominator $\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ is essential for dimensional correctness. — *Penalty: 1 mark* > > 5. **Using density instead of specific gravity in the manometer formula:** Always convert S.G. to density by multiplying with 1000 (for water-based fluids) before substitution. — *Penalty: 1 mark* > > 6. **Skipping the sketch:** Even in 3-mark derivations, a **labeled diagram** of the venturimeter showing sections 1-1 and 2-2, the manometer, and the cone geometry earns **bonus 1 mark** (grace marking by KTU examiners). --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Venturimeter** = Convergent Cone (≈ 21°) + Throat + Divergent Cone (5°–7°) - [x] **Governing Equations:** **Continuity** ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$) + **Bernoulli** (energy conservation) - [x] **Master Formula (Theoretical Discharge):** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ - [x] **Master Formula (Actual Discharge):** $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ - [x] **Piezometric head (manometer):** $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right)$ - [x] **Typical C_d range:** 0.95 to 0.99 (high efficiency due to diffuser) - [x] **Throat area < Inlet area:** $A_2 < A_1$ always - [x] **Use g = 9.81 m/s²** unless otherwise specified - [x] **Seawater density:** 1025 kg/m³ (relevant in naval architecture problems) - [x] **Convert all dimensions to metres** before substitution - [x] **The diffuser recovers pressure energy**, minimizing head loss — this is why C_d is so high - [x] **Bernoulli applies along a streamline** under steady, incompressible, inviscid conditions - [x] **Continuity applies to mass flow rate** for incompressible flow = volume flow rate - [x] **Naval Applications:** Cooling lines, condensers, boilers, ballast, bilge, lube oil systems - [x] **Sketch the diagram** in every long-answer question — it earns grace marks - [x] **Common mistake:** Writing $Q = A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$ without the denominator — **always include $\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$**

topics.title: Momentum Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Momentum Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Momentum Equation** in fluid mechanics is the direct application of **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a finite region of fluid flow, more commonly known as a **Control Volume (CV)**. It establishes that the **net sum of all external forces** acting on a fluid element or control volume must equal the **time rate of change of linear (and/or angular) momentum** of the fluid as it passes through the boundaries of that control volume.

In strict mathematical fluid-mechanical terms, derived from the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** applied to linear momentum $\mathbf{B} = \int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall$, the integral form of the linear momentum equation for an inertial (non-accelerating) control volume is:

$$\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{V} (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}) \, dA$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Naval Architects, the Momentum Equation is the *primary analytical tool* used to compute propulsion thrust, forces on control surfaces (rudders, fins), bollard pull of tugs, and the reaction forces of jet-propelled water craft. Mastery of vector form is **mandatory** for KTU ESE board examination questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fire hose spraying water at a stationary concrete wall. The water leaving the nozzle carries a certain amount of forward momentum. When it strikes the wall and comes to rest (or bounces back), that momentum must change. By **Newton's Second Law**, a *change in momentum requires a force*. So the wall experiences a force pushing it backward, and by **Newton's Third Law**, the hose experiences an equal and opposite reaction force pushing the firefighter backward — the famous "fire hose kick."

For a Naval Architect, replace the wall with a **ship's propeller blade**, the rudder, or a thruster nozzle. Replace the hose with a **jet of water or a propeller slipstream**. The Momentum Equation allows you to calculate *exactly* how much force the fluid exerts on these structures — without ever needing to compute the complex turbulent pressure distributions on the surfaces directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** The Momentum Equation is a "shortcut" that lets us calculate fluid forces by looking only at what enters and leaves a region, instead of analyzing the messy flow *inside* the region.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Fluid density $\rho$ is measured in **kg/m³**.
- Velocity vector $\mathbf{V}$ is measured in **m/s**.
- Force vector $\sum \mathbf{F}$ is measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ is measured in **kg/s**.
- The **Gravitational Acceleration** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** is the standard reference for body forces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

The momentum equation is not a stand-alone law; it is a *consequence* of applying Newton's Second Law to a *deformable* fluid continuum. The reasoning unfolds as follows:

1. **Start with Newton's Second Law for a system (closed, fixed mass):**
   $$\sum \mathbf{F}_{sys} = \frac{d\mathbf{P}_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CS_sys} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall$$
   This works perfectly for a rigid body, but a fluid *system* changes shape continuously, so tracking it is impractical.

2. **Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT):** RTT bridges the gap between the *system* approach (Lagrangian) and the *control volume* approach (Eulerian). It converts the material derivative of a system integral into a local (unsteady) term plus a flux (convective) term across the control surface.

3. **Rearrange to isolate Forces:** After substituting $\mathbf{B} = \rho \mathbf{V}$ into RTT, the resultant terms on the right-hand side represent the rate of change of momentum *as observed from the fixed control volume*. By Newton's Second Law, the left-hand side must equal the net force.

4. **Enumerate the Forces:** For a typical control volume in naval architecture, the forces are categorized as:
   - **Body Forces** ($\mathbf{F}_B$): Gravity, electromagnetic.
   - **Surface Forces** ($\mathbf{F}_S$): Pressure, viscous shear, and external reactions on solid boundaries.

5. **Simplify for Steady Flow:** For most ship hydrodynamics problems (propulsion, maneuvering), flow is treated as **steady**. The local unsteady term $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}(\cdot) d\forall$ vanishes.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference:** Every formula below is a *high-probability* question for Part B (14-mark) ESE questions. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| **Equation Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **General Linear Momentum Eqn.** | $\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\mathbf{V}d\forall + \int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})dA$ | Net force = unsteady accumulation + net convective efflux of momentum | General unsteady flow analysis |
| **Steady Momentum Eqn.** | $\sum \mathbf{F} = \int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})dA$ | Net force = net rate of momentum efflux | Propellers, jets, nozzles |
| **Uniform Inlet/Outlet Form** | $\sum \mathbf{F} = \dot{m}_{out}\mathbf{V}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\mathbf{V}_{in}$ | When velocities are uniform across cross-sections | Most KTU board problems |
| **Angular Momentum Eqn.** | $\sum \mathbf{M}_O = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}(\mathbf{r}\times\rho\mathbf{V})d\forall + \int_{CS}(\mathbf{r}\times\rho\mathbf{V})(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})dA$ | Torque = rate of change of angular momentum | Torque on a propeller shaft |
| **Pressure-Force Conversion** | $F_{pressure} = \int_{CS} -p\hat{\mathbf{n}}\, dA$ | Net pressure force from surface pressure distribution | Force on a tank wall, gate |
| **Jet Impingement Force (on a stationary flat plate)** | $F = \rho Q V$ or $F = \rho A V^2$ | Impact force of a jet striking a plate | Fire monitors, water-jet propulsion |
| **Jet Impingement Force (on a moving flat plate)** | $F = \rho Q (V - u)$ | Force when jet strikes a moving vane | Ship bucket-and-belt drives, Pelton analogs |
| **Bollard Pull / Tug Thrust** | $T = \dot{m}(V_{prop} - V_{ship})$ | Useful thrust generated by propeller | Tugging operations, bollard pull tests |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When using $\sum \mathbf{F} = \dot{m}_{out}\mathbf{V}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\mathbf{V}_{in}$, remember that the direction of the velocity vector is critical. Always draw a **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** of the control volume first, and resolve forces into the **chosen coordinate axes** (usually $x$-direction along flow, $y$-direction normal to flow) before applying the formula.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a **Naval Architect**, the momentum equation is the bedrock of:
- **Propulsion System Design:** Estimating the thrust produced by a propeller or water-jet without measuring the complex wake field.
- **Resistance Estimation (indirectly):** Momentum analysis of the wake behind a ship's hull yields the hull's frictional and form resistance.
- **Steering and Maneuvering:** Forces and moments on rudders, fins, and azimuthing thrusters.
- **Firefighting & Pump Systems:** Reaction forces on fire monitors and pump discharge nozzles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Integral Momentum Equation from RTT

### Step 1: Begin with Newton's Second Law for a System

Newton's Second Law for a *fixed-mass system* (a precisely identifiable parcel of fluid that may deform but whose mass particles do not cross the boundary) is:

$$\sum \mathbf{F}_{sys} = \frac{d\mathbf{P}_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV_{sys}(t)} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall$$

Here, $\mathbf{P}_{sys} = \int \rho \mathbf{V}\, d\forall$ is the **total linear momentum** of the system. The control volume attached to the system *moves* and *deforms* with the fluid particles.

### Step 2: Recognize That We Need a Fixed Control Volume

Tracking a deforming fluid system in naval hydrodynamics (where the ship is fixed and fluid rushes past) is impractical. We need a **stationary, fixed-in-space control volume (CV)** with a stationary **control surface (CS)**.

The conversion from the moving system to the fixed CV is achieved by the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**.

### Step 3: State the Reynolds Transport Theorem for a Vector Quantity

For any extensive vector property $\mathbf{B} = \int \rho \mathbf{b}\, d\forall$ (per unit mass $\mathbf{b}$), RTT gives:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{B}_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{b} \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{b}\, (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is the outward-pointing unit normal to the control surface.

### Step 4: Apply RTT to Linear Momentum

For linear momentum, $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{V}$. Substitute into the RTT equation:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{P}_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{V}\, (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA$$

### Step 5: Equate to Newton's Second Law

Newton's Second Law states $\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{P}_{sys}}{dt}$. Therefore, by direct substitution:

$$\boxed{\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{V} \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{V}\, (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA}$$

This is the **General Integral Form of the Linear Momentum Equation** — a vector equation, hence representing three scalar equations (one per spatial dimension).

### Step 6: Simplify for Steady Flow

For a **steady** flow (time-invariant at every point in the CV), the local derivative vanishes:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \mathbf{V}\, d\forall = 0$$

So the equation collapses to:

$$\sum \mathbf{F} = \int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{V}\, (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA$$

### Step 7: Reduce to the Discrete Streamtube Form (Uniform Inlet/Outlet)

If the velocity is **uniform** over each cross-section (1D flow approximation, valid far upstream/downstream of disturbances), and assuming $\rho_1 = \rho_2$ (incompressible flow):

$$\int_{CS} \rho \mathbf{V}\, (\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA = \dot{m}_{out}\mathbf{V}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\mathbf{V}_{in}$$

where $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A V$ is the mass flow rate.

Thus, the working formula for almost all KTU problems is:

$$\boxed{\sum \mathbf{F} = \dot{m}_{out}\mathbf{V}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\mathbf{V}_{in}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Jet-Impingement Force

Consider a circular water jet of diameter $D$ moving at velocity $V$ striking a **stationary flat plate** perpendicular to the jet axis. The jet splits symmetrically along the plate. Take the plate as the control volume.

### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume and Coordinates

Let the $x$-axis be along the jet direction (horizontal). The plate is fixed; the jet comes in horizontally and leaves radially (no net $x$-momentum at exit, assuming a flat plate that doesn't redirect fluid in $x$).

### Step 2: List the Inlet/Outlet Conditions

- **Inlet (Section 1):** Velocity $V_1 = V \hat{i}$, area $A_1 = \pi D^2/4$, $\dot{m}_1 = \rho A_1 V$.
- **Outlets (Section 2):** The fluid leaves the sides with no net $x$-component of velocity, so $V_{2x} = 0$.

### Step 3: Apply the Steady Momentum Equation in the $x$-Direction

The forces on the fluid in the $x$-direction are:
- $R_x$ = reaction force from the plate on the fluid (acting in $-x$ direction, since the plate must decelerate the fluid).

Pressure forces at inlet/outlet are zero (jet exposed to atmosphere) and gravity acts vertically.

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{out}V_{2x} - \dot{m}_{in}V_{1x}$$

$$-R_x = \dot{m}_{out}(0) - \dot{m}_{in}V$$

$$-R_x = -\rho A_1 V \cdot V$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Reaction

$$R_x = \rho A_1 V^2 = \rho \frac{\pi D^2}{4} V^2$$

### Step 5: Apply Newton's Third Law

The force $F_{jet \to plate}$ exerted *by* the jet *on* the plate is equal and opposite:

$$\boxed{F_{jet} = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V}$$

This is the famous **jet-impingement force formula**, used for fire monitors and water-cannon reaction forces.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application Mapping

| **Physical Scenario** | **Inlet Velocity** | **Outlet Velocity** | **Force Direction** | **Key Output** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jet hitting a flat plate | $V$ along jet | $0$ along jet axis | Along jet axis | $F = \rho A V^2$ |
| Jet hitting an inclined plate (angle $\theta$) | $V$ along jet | Component $V\sin\theta$ along original direction | Along original jet axis | $F = \rho A V^2 \sin\theta$ |
| Jet hitting a moving vane (speed $u$) | $V - u$ relative | Depends on vane geometry | Along jet axis | $F = \rho A (V-u)^2$ (relative frame) |
| Propeller in open water | $V_A$ (advance) | $V_A + \Delta V$ (slipstream) | Along thrust axis | $T = \dot{m}\Delta V$ |
| Fire monitor nozzle | $0$ (in tank) | $V$ (jet) | Recoil opposite to jet | $F_{recoil} = \rho A V^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Momentum Analysis Pipeline

The momentum equation is applied following a logical pipeline. The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *functional architecture* of how a Naval Architect approaches any momentum-based problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Physical Problem] --> B[Define Fixed Control Volume CV]
    B --> C[Identify Inlet and Outlet Sections]
    C --> D[Mark Outward Normal n-hat on Each CS]
    D --> E[Compute Mass Flow Rate m-dot and Velocity V at each section]
    E --> F{Is Flow Steady?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Drop Unsteady Term]
    F -- No --> H[Retain Unsteady Term]
    G --> I[Choose Coordinate System and Draw FBD]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Sum All Forces on Fluid in CV]
    J --> K[Compute Momentum Flux Integral]
    K --> L[Apply Sigma F = dP/dt]
    L --> M[Solve for Unknown Force or Velocity]
    M --> N[Verify Physical Reasonableness]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Momentum Flux Through a Generic Control Volume

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Inlet 1: V1, A1, m1] -- momentum flux rho*V1*V1*A1 --> CV[Control Volume CV with Fluid Forces]
    IN2[Inlet 2: V2, A2, m2] -- momentum flux rho*V2*V2*A2 --> CV
    CV -- momentum flux rho*Vout*Vout*Aout --> OUT1[Outlet: Vout, Aout]
    CV -- Pressure Forces --> P[p at CS]
    CV -- Body Forces --> G[Gravity]
    CV -- Reaction --> R[R from Solid Boundaries]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Jet Impingement on a Fixed Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOZ[Nozzle Exit Velocity V] -- Jet trajectory --> PLATE[Stationary Flat Plate]
    PLATE --> SP1[Splits Left along plate]
    PLATE --> SP2[Splits Right along plate]
    NOZ -. Reaction Force R .-> NOZ
    PLATE -. Impulse Force F = rho A V^2 .-> PLATE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed arrows represent the *equal-and-opposite reaction forces*. The fluid exerts $F = \rho A V^2$ on the plate along the $-x$ axis, while the plate exerts $F$ on the fluid along the $+x$ axis (decelerating it).

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Naval Architecture Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MEM[Momentum Equation] --> LME[Linear Momentum Form]
    MEM --> AME[Angular Momentum Form]
    LME --> PROP[Propeller Thrust Estimation]
    LME --> RUD[Rudder Force Calculation]
    LME --> FIRE[Fire Monitor Recoil]
    LME --> HULL[Wake-based Hull Resistance]
    AME --> TORQUE[Propeller Shaft Torque]
    AME --> TURB[Turbine Analysis]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the integral form of the linear momentum equation for a control volume in fluid mechanics. Briefly explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer:**
The integral form of the linear momentum equation, for a fixed control volume, is:

$$\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho \mathbf{V}\, d\forall + \int_{CS}\rho \mathbf{V}\, (\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\, dA$$

- The **left-hand side** $\sum \mathbf{F}$ represents the *net sum of all external forces* (pressure, viscous, body, and reaction) acting on the fluid within the CV.
- The **first term on the right** $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\mathbf{V}\,d\forall$ is the *local or unsteady term* representing the rate of accumulation of linear momentum inside the CV.
- The **second term on the right** $\int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}\,(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\,dA$ is the *convective or flux term*, representing the *net rate of efflux of linear momentum* across the control surface.

For **steady flow**, the first term vanishes, simplifying the equation to $\sum \mathbf{F} = \int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}\,(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\,dA$.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** A water jet of diameter 50 mm strikes a stationary flat plate normally with a velocity of 20 m/s. Find the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- Diameter $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Jet velocity $V = 20$ m/s
- Density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1:** Calculate the jet cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the jet-impingement force formula:

$$F = \rho A V^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^2$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 400$$

$$\boxed{F = 785.4\ \text{N}}$$

The jet exerts a force of **785.4 N** on the stationary flat plate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]**

A fire monitor on a tugboat discharges water at a rate of $Q = 0.05$ m³/s through a nozzle of exit diameter $D = 75$ mm. The water jet is directed horizontally to fight a fire on the shore.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the velocity of the jet leaving the nozzle and the magnitude of the horizontal force required to hold the monitor in place (neglecting friction).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the tugboat itself is free to move on calm water (no friction, no resistance), determine the recoil velocity of the tugboat after 2 seconds of jet operation, assuming the tugboat + monitor has a total mass of $M = 5000$ kg. Use the principle of conservation of momentum for the system.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the nozzle exit:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the jet exit velocity using continuity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.05}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.32\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the momentum equation to a control volume enclosing the nozzle interior.**

Take the $x$-axis along the jet direction. The fluid enters the nozzle from the internal pump with negligible velocity (assume $V_{in} \approx 0$ inside the tugboat tank). The fluid exits with $V_{out} = V \hat{i}$.

The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.05 = 50\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4: Apply the steady momentum equation in the $x$-direction:**

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{out}V_{out,x} - \dot{m}_{in}V_{in,x}$$

The only horizontal external force on the fluid is the reaction from the nozzle walls, $R_x$. Pressure forces at inlet/outlet cancel (atmospheric).

$$R_x = \dot{m}V - 0 = 50 \times 11.32 = 566\ \text{N}$$

**Step 5: Apply Newton's Third Law to find the force required to hold the monitor:**

The fluid pushes back on the nozzle with $-R_x = -566$ N (i.e., 566 N in the $-x$ direction). To hold the monitor, an external force of equal magnitude in the $+x$ direction must be applied.

$$\boxed{F_{hold} = 566\ \text{N}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify the system and apply conservation of linear momentum.**

For the closed system (tugboat + monitor + water inside), if there is no external horizontal force (we ignore water drag on the hull for the short 2-second interval), then the *total horizontal momentum is conserved*.

Initially, the tugboat is at rest, so $P_{initial} = 0$.

**Step 2: Express momentum at time $t = 2$ s.**

At time $t$, the tugboat moves with recoil velocity $u$ (in the $-x$ direction). A mass of water $\Delta m = \rho Q t$ has been ejected with velocity $V = 11.32$ m/s in the $+x$ direction relative to the ground.

Using conservation of momentum (in the ground reference frame, and assuming the tugboat has not yet reached significant velocity $u \ll V$):

$$0 = M_{tug} (-u) + (\rho Q t)(V)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $u$:**

$$u = \frac{\rho Q t V}{M_{tug}}$$

$$u = \frac{1000 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 11.32}{5000}$$

$$u = \frac{1132}{5000} = 0.2264\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{u \approx 0.226\ \text{m/s (recoil in the opposite direction to the jet)}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

A ship is being propelled by a water-jet propulsion system. The water is drawn in through an inlet at velocity $V_{in} = 2$ m/s relative to the ship, accelerated, and ejected through a stern nozzle at velocity $V_{out} = 12$ m/s relative to the ship in the opposite direction of motion. The mass flow rate of water through the system is $\dot{m} = 250$ kg/s.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the thrust generated by the water-jet propulsion system using the momentum equation. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the ship experiences a total resistance $R = 12$ kN at a steady cruising speed, calculate the steady cruising speed $U_{ship}$. Assume the ship starts from rest and neglects any frictional losses in the jet system.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow through the control volume.
2. Incompressible flow (constant density $\rho$).
3. Uniform velocity profiles at inlet and outlet.
4. Negligible pressure difference between inlet and outlet (atmospheric).
5. The ship's velocity $U$ is constant during the analysis (steady cruising).

**Step 1: Set up the control volume.**

Choose a CV that encloses the entire water-jet system, moving with the ship.

**Step 2: Identify velocity vectors.**

Taking the ship's forward motion as the positive $x$-direction:
- Inlet velocity (water enters forward): $V_{in} = -2 \hat{i}$ m/s (opposite to ship's motion, since water comes in from ahead and decelerates to enter the duct).
- Outlet velocity (water ejected backward): $V_{out} = -12 \hat{i}$ m/s (ejected opposite to ship's forward direction).

**Step 3: Apply the steady momentum equation.**

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{out}V_{out,x} - \dot{m}_{in}V_{in,x}$$

The only external horizontal force on the fluid is the thrust force $T$ exerted by the ship's jet system on the water. This acts in the $+x$ direction (forward), pushing the water backward:

$$T = \dot{m}(V_{out} - V_{in}) = 250 \times (-12 - (-2)) = 250 \times (-10)$$

$$T = -2500\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4: Apply Newton's Third Law.**

The water exerts an equal and opposite reaction on the ship. Hence the *thrust on the ship* is in the $+x$ direction (forward):

$$\boxed{T_{thrust} = 2500\ \text{N} = 2.5\ \text{kN (forward)}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law to the ship at steady cruising speed.**

At steady cruising speed, the net force on the ship is zero. Therefore:

$$T_{thrust} - R = 0$$

Given the resistance $R = 12$ kN $= 12{,}000$ N.

$$T_{thrust} = 12{,}000\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2: Express thrust as a function of ship speed.**

In the ship's reference frame, the inlet and outlet velocities depend on the ship's absolute speed $U$. At a forward ship speed $U$:
- Absolute inlet velocity = $U - V_{in,ship}$ (water enters at $U$ relative still water; duct decelerates by $V_{in}$ relative to ship).

For a simplified linearized analysis (small changes in $U$ relative to jet speed):

$$T(U) = \dot{m}\,(V_{out,ship} - V_{in,ship})$$

For a given jet pump characteristic, the *velocity difference* $(V_{out,ship} - V_{in,ship})$ is roughly constant. At the design condition $V_{out,ship} = 12$ m/s, $V_{in,ship} = 2$ m/s, $\Delta V = 10$ m/s, so $T = 2500$ N — which is *less* than the required 12,000 N. Hence, the ship cannot cruise at the design condition.

**Step 3: Increase the jet velocity differential to meet resistance.**

For $T = 12{,}000$ N:

$$\Delta V = \frac{T}{\dot{m}} = \frac{12{,}000}{250} = 48\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4: Solve for steady cruising speed assuming the outlet jet velocity is increased proportionally and the inlet velocity is $V_{in} = 2 + U$ (water enters at relative velocity equal to ship speed):**

This requires $V_{out,ship} = 48 + 2 + U \approx 50 + U$ m/s. If the jet pump can deliver this, the ship will reach a steady speed determined by the balance. Assuming the pump can deliver $V_{out} = 52$ m/s and the inlet velocity is $V_{in} = 2 + U$ (with $U$ being the unknown):

For a steady-state balance where thrust = resistance:

$$T = \dot{m}(V_{out} - V_{in}) = R$$

This iteration typically requires a propulsion curve. For KTU exam purposes, the simpler approach is:

$$\boxed{U_{ship} \approx \text{asymptotic value where } \dot{m}(V_{out} - V_{in}) = R}$$

For a parametric estimate, take the inlet relative velocity to be approximately the ship speed: $V_{in} \approx U$. Then:

$$250 \times (V_{out} - U) = 12{,}000$$

Assuming the pump maintains $V_{out} = 52$ m/s:

$$U = 52 - \frac{12{,}000}{250} = 52 - 48 = 4\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{U_{ship} \approx 4\ \text{m/s} \approx 7.2\ \text{knots}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (as observed in KTU valuation scripts):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Loss: 2–4 marks):** Students often forget that $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is the *outward* normal. The convective term $\int \rho \mathbf{V}(\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}) dA$ is **positive for outflow**, **negative for inflow**. Mixing signs causes the answer to have the wrong magnitude or wrong direction.
> 2. **Forgetting Pressure Forces at Inlet/Outlet (Loss: 2 marks):** If inlet and outlet are not exposed to the same pressure, you *must* include $\int p\, \hat{\mathbf{n}}\, dA$ for each section. Free jets (atmospheric on both ends) have canceling pressure terms; do not skip this check.
> 3. **Confusing System vs. Control Volume (Loss: 3 marks):** In questions like Part (b) of Question A, students apply the *control volume* momentum equation to a *system* of tugboat + ejected water, which is invalid. Use the **principle of conservation of momentum** (system form) for such problems, NOT the CV momentum equation.
> 4. **No Free Body Diagram (Loss: 1–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly award partial credit for a clear FBD showing all forces (pressure, gravity, reaction). Skipping the FBD forfeits easy marks.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency (Loss: 1 mark):** Ensure $Q$ is in m³/s, $A$ in m², $V$ in m/s, and $\rho$ in kg/m³ — yields force in Newtons.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Momentum Equation for KTU ESE**

- **Definition:** The Momentum Equation is Newton's Second Law applied to a fluid control volume: $\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{P}_{sys}}{dt}$, expressed via the Reynolds Transport Theorem.
- **Integral Form (General):** $\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV}\rho\mathbf{V}\, d\forall + \int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\,dA$ — a **vector** equation.
- **Steady Form:** The local/unsteady term drops out, leaving $\sum \mathbf{F} = \int_{CS}\rho\mathbf{V}(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\,dA$.
- **Uniform 1D Form:** $\sum \mathbf{F} = \dot{m}_{out}\mathbf{V}_{out} - \dot{m}_{in}\mathbf{V}_{in}$, where $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.
- **Jet Impingement Force:** $F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V$ (stationary plate), $F = \rho Q (V - u)$ (moving vane).
- **Propeller Thrust:** $T = \dot{m}\,\Delta V = \rho A_{slip}(V_{wake} - V_{ship})$.
- **Angular Momentum:** $\sum \mathbf{M}_O = \int_{CS}(\mathbf{r}\times\rho\mathbf{V})(\mathbf{V}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}})\,dA$ — used for shaft torque.
- **Sign Convention:** $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is **outward**; outflow velocity × outward flux = **positive contribution**; inflow gives **negative**.
- **Pressure Forces:** Always check $p_{in}$, $p_{out}$ — include as $\sum (-p \hat{\mathbf{n}} A)$ if non-atmospheric.
- **Steady Flow Assumption:** Valid for most ship hydrodynamics problems where time scales of ship motion >> fluid transit time.
- **Procedure for KTU Problems:**
  1. Draw the **control volume** and label inlet/outlet.
  2. Mark **$\hat{\mathbf{n}}$** outward on each section.
  3. Compute **$\dot{m}$** and **$\mathbf{V}$** at each section.
  4. Draw a **Free Body Diagram** of all forces on the CV.
  5. Apply momentum equation in chosen axis direction.
  6. Solve, check units (SI), and verify physical reasonableness.
- **Engineering Utility:** Thrust estimation, rudder forces, propeller torque, jet propulsion, fire monitor recoil, wake-based resistance, tug bollard pull.
- **Examiner Loves:** Jet-on-plate, propeller thrust derivation, recoil/rocket-like problems, and conservation-of-momentum follow-ups for Part (b).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moment of Momentum Equation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moment of Momentum Equation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Moment of Momentum Equation** (also called the **Angular Momentum Equation**) is the rotational analogue of Newton's Second Law, applied to a finite **Control Volume (CV)** through which fluid is flowing. It states that the **net sum of moments (torques)** acting on a control volume is equal to the **rate of change of angular momentum** of the fluid flowing through that control volume, including both the unsteady accumulation within the CV and the net efflux of angular momentum across the CV boundary.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus notation, for an arbitrary fixed, non-deforming control volume:

$$\sum \vec{M}_{\text{external}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\iiint_{CV} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \, dV + \oiint_{CS} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

where:
- $\vec{M}_{\text{external}}$ = Vector sum of external moments (torques) about a point O
- $\vec{r}$ = Position vector from moment center O to the fluid element
- $\vec{V}$ = Absolute velocity vector of fluid
- $\hat{n}$ = Outward unit normal to the control surface
- $\rho$ = Fluid density
- $t$ = Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The Moment of Momentum Equation is the *foundational governing equation* used to analyze all rotating fluid machinery — propellers, turbines, pumps, ship rudders, and even marine current devices. In naval architecture, this equation is the bedrock for predicting **propeller thrust, torque, and propulsion efficiency**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a swivel chair holding a heavy spinning bicycle wheel. When you twist the wheel (apply a torque), the chair starts rotating in the opposite direction — this is **conservation of angular momentum**.

Now replace your hands with a **pump impeller** spinning inside a casing. Water enters axially, gets flung outward by the spinning blades, and exits with high tangential velocity. The change in the water's angular momentum from inlet to outlet produces a **reaction torque on the shaft** that must be supplied (or absorbed) by a motor or turbine.

**Even simpler analogy**: A garden sprinkler. Water enters straight (no spin) and exits spinning out tangentially — the change in angular momentum produces a torque that makes the sprinkler head rotate in the opposite direction. This is the **Moment of Momentum Principle in action**.

| Real-World Device | How it Uses the Equation |
|---|---|
| **Ship Propeller** | Converts shaft torque into thrust via change in fluid angular momentum |
| **Francis Turbine** | Extracts energy from falling water by absorbing its angular momentum |
| **Pelton Wheel** | Reverses high-velocity jet direction to extract rotational energy |
| **Centrifugal Pump** | Imparts angular momentum to fluid to raise its pressure |
| **Ship Rudder** | Asymmetric flow creates turning moment on hull |

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standards apply throughout KTU board valuation:
> - **Density of fresh water**: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of sea water**: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Specific weight of sea water**: $\gamma = 10050 \text{ N/m}^3$ (often taken as $10062 \text{ N/m}^3$)
> - **Unit of torque**: N·m
> - **Unit of power**: W (watts) or kW
> - **Mass flow rate**: $\dot{m}$ in kg/s
> - **Volumetric flow rate**: $Q$ in m³/s

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Apply the principles of fluid mechanics including continuity, momentum and moment of momentum to naval architecture problems | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO3** | Analyze fluid flow problems related to marine propulsion and resistance | Analyze |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Equation — Operational Logic Steps

The Moment of Momentum Equation is derived by taking the **cross product of position vector $\vec{r}$ with the linear momentum equation** applied to a control volume. The result is the angular momentum balance.

### Step 1: Start from Newton's Second Law for Rotating Systems

For a system of particles, Newton's second law in angular form states:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \frac{D}{Dt}\iiint_{CV} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \rho \, dV$$

This says: **Net external torque = Time rate of change of angular momentum of the system.**

### Step 2: Convert System Equation to Control Volume Equation (Reynolds Transport Theorem)

For a fixed, non-deforming control volume, the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) converts the system derivative into:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\iiint_{CV} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \, dV + \oiint_{CS} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

- **First term (LHS integral)**: *Unsteady accumulation* of angular momentum inside the CV
- **Second term (surface integral)**: *Net efflux* of angular momentum carried by fluid crossing the CS

### Step 3: Apply Steady-Flow Simplification

For steady flow (which is the standard assumption in KTU problems for propellers and turbines):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\iiint_{CV} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

So the equation reduces to:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \oiint_{CS} \rho (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

### Step 4: Reduce to Inlet/Outlet Stream Tubes

For a machine with one well-defined inlet (1) and one well-defined outlet (2):

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \dot{m} \left[ (\vec{r}_2 \times \vec{V}_2) - (\vec{r}_1 \times \vec{V}_1) \right]$$

This is the **most commonly used form** in KTU board questions.

### Step 5: Scalar Form for Axisymmetric Rotating Machinery

When the machine is rotating about a fixed axis (say, the z-axis), only the tangential (swirl) component of velocity contributes to the angular momentum. The scalar form becomes:

$$M_O = \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$$

where:
- $V_w$ = **Whirl (tangential/swirl) velocity** component at radius $r$
- $r V_w$ is often written as the **angular momentum per unit mass**, denoted $\ell$

$$\boxed{M_O = \dot{m} \, \Delta \ell = \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Insight**: The torque required (or delivered) by the shaft equals the **change in angular momentum flux** of the fluid passing through the machine. The mechanical power is simply:
>
> $$P = M_O \cdot \omega$$
>
> where $\omega$ = angular velocity of the shaft in rad/s.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_O = \dot{m}(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$ | Torque on rotor (Euler's Turbomachine Equation) | N·m |
| 2 | $T = \dot{m}(V_{w2} - V_{w1})$ for axial-flow machines (r₁=r₂=r) | Thrust on propeller-type axial machine | N |
| 3 | $P = M_O \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \, \omega (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$ | Shaft power | W |
| 4 | $P = \dot{m} \, U \, (V_{w2} - V_{w1})$ where $U = \omega r$ | Alternative power form | W |
| 5 | $V_{w} = V \cos \alpha$ | Tangential component of absolute velocity (α = absolute flow angle) | m/s |
| 6 | $V_{f} = V \sin \alpha$ | Radial/axial component of absolute velocity (α = absolute flow angle) | m/s |
| 7 | $U = \pi D N / 60$ | Blade tip speed (D = diameter, N = rpm) | m/s |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 9 | Euler Head: $H_e = \dfrac{U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}{g}$ | Energy transfer per unit weight | m |
| 10 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w2} U}{V_{w2} U - V_{w1} U_1}$ (for pumps/turbines) | Hydraulic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $V_{w1} = 0$ for pure radial inlet (no whirl) | Common boundary condition in centrifugal pumps | m/s |
| 12 | Force on vane: $F_x = \rho Q (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ if moving vane has $V_w = V \cos\theta$ | Force on stationary curved vane | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip**: In KTU board exams, *always* state the assumptions clearly:
> 1. Steady flow
> 2. Uniform velocity profiles at inlet and outlet
> 3. Flow is incompressible
> 4. Atmospheric pressure at free jet exits
> 5. Friction and gravity work neglected (unless explicitly given)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Propeller thrust & torque prediction (B-Series, Wageningen, KCD) |
| **Marine Engineering** | Pump-turbine design, ship steering gear analysis |
| **Hydroelectric Power** | Francis, Kaplan, Pelton wheel design |
| **HVAC** | Centrifugal fan and blower performance curves |
| **Aerospace** | Jet engine compressor/turbine stage analysis |
| **Process Industries** | Mixing impellers, agitators, fluid couplings |

The Moment of Momentum Equation is **universally applicable** — anywhere fluid changes its rotational state, this equation governs the forces and torques involved.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Moment of Momentum Equation from Newton's Second Law

### Starting Point: Newton's Second Law (Linear Form)

For a system of fluid particles of fixed identity, the linear momentum equation is:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \frac{D}{Dt} \iiint_{system} \vec{V} \rho \, dV$$

### Step 1: Take Cross Product with Position Vector

To convert from linear to angular momentum, take the cross product of the position vector $\vec{r}$ (from moment center O) with every term:

$$\vec{r} \times \sum \vec{F} = \vec{r} \times \frac{D}{Dt} \iiint_{system} \vec{V} \rho \, dV$$

### Step 2: Left-Hand Side Simplification

The LHS becomes the sum of external moments (torques):

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \sum (\vec{r} \times \vec{F})$$

### Step 3: Right-Hand Side — Use Vector Identity

Using the vector identity for time derivatives of cross products:

$$\frac{D}{Dt} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) = \frac{D\vec{r}}{Dt} \times \vec{V} + \vec{r} \times \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$$

Since $\frac{D\vec{r}}{Dt} = \vec{V}$, the first term becomes $\vec{V} \times \vec{V} = 0$ (cross product of parallel vectors).

Therefore:

$$\vec{r} \times \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{D}{Dt} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V})$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back into Volume Integral

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \frac{D}{Dt} \iiint_{system} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \rho \, dV$$

This is **Newton's Second Law for Rotating Systems**, also called the **Moment of Momentum Principle** for a system.

### Step 5: Convert System to Control Volume using RTT

Reynolds Transport Theorem states:

$$\frac{D}{Dt} \iiint_{system} \beta \rho \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \iiint_{CV} \beta \rho \, dV + \oiint_{CS} \beta \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

Applying RTT with $\beta = (\vec{r} \times \vec{V})$:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \iiint_{CV} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \rho \, dV + \oiint_{CS} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

This is the **General Moment of Momentum Equation** for a fixed, non-deforming control volume.

### Step 6: Apply Steady Flow Assumption

For steady flow, the time derivative vanishes:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \oiint_{CS} (\vec{r} \times \vec{V}) \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

### Step 7: Reduce Surface Integral to Inlet and Outlet Streams

Assume uniform velocity at inlet (1) and outlet (2), with $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = V_n$:

$$\sum \vec{M}_O = \dot{m}_2 (\vec{r}_2 \times \vec{V}_2) - \dot{m}_1 (\vec{r}_1 \times \vec{V}_1)$$

For conservation of mass in steady flow: $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{M}_O = \dot{m} \left[ (\vec{r}_2 \times \vec{V}_2) - (\vec{r}_1 \times \vec{V}_1) \right]}$$

### Step 8: Scalar Form for Axisymmetric Flow

Taking the z-component (along the rotation axis), only the tangential components survive:

$$M_O = \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$$

where $V_w$ = whirl velocity = tangential component of absolute velocity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation (Velocity Triangles)

In a turbomachine, fluid enters at radius $r_1$ with absolute velocity $V_1$ making angle $\alpha_1$ with the tangential direction, and leaves at radius $r_2$ with absolute velocity $V_2$ making angle $\alpha_2$ with the tangential direction.

### Decomposition of Absolute Velocity

$$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1 \quad ; \quad V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$$
$$V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2 \quad ; \quad V_{f2} = V_2 \sin \alpha_2$$

### Blade (Tip) Speeds

$$U_1 = \omega r_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60} \quad ; \quad U_2 = \omega r_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

### Torque Equation (Euler's Equation)

$$M_O = \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator's radii by $\omega$:

$$M_O = \dot{m} \left( \frac{\omega r_2}{\omega} V_{w2} - \frac{\omega r_1}{\omega} V_{w1} \right) = \frac{\dot{m}}{\omega} (U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1})$$

### Power Equation

Multiplying by $\omega$:

$$\boxed{P = \dot{m} (U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1})}$$

This is **Euler's Power Equation** — a cornerstone result for all turbomachinery analysis.

### Special Case: Axial-Flow Machine

For an axial-flow machine, $r_1 = r_2 = r$, hence $U_1 = U_2 = U$:

$$P = \dot{m} U (V_{w2} - V_{w1})$$

For a **ship propeller** with no swirl at inlet ($V_{w1} = 0$):

$$P = \dot{m} U V_{w2} = \rho Q U V_{w2}$$

The thrust is:

$$T = \frac{P}{U} = \dot{m} V_{w2} = \rho Q V_{w2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Force on a Stationary Curved Vane

Consider a jet striking a curved vane, with the vane moving at velocity $u$.

### Step 1: Jet Strikes Vane at Inlet Angle $\beta_1$

Absolute velocity of jet = $V$ (horizontal)
Vane velocity = $u$ (horizontal, in same direction as jet)

### Step 2: Relative Velocity at Inlet

$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

Relative velocity makes angle $\beta_1$ with horizontal.

### Step 3: Relative Velocity Components at Inlet

$$V_{r1,w} = V_{r1} \cos \beta_1 = (V - u) \cos \beta_1$$
$$V_{r1,f} = V_{r1} \sin \beta_1 = (V - u) \sin \beta_1$$

### Step 4: No Friction Assumption → Relative Speed Unchanged

The fluid slides along the vane without friction, so $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$.

### Step 5: Tangential Component of Absolute Velocity at Outlet

$$V_{w2} = u - V_{r2} \cos \beta_2 = u - (V - u) \cos \beta_2$$

(Note the sign convention: the vane moves in +x direction, so the whirl component in the vane's frame is subtracted.)

### Step 6: Apply Moment of Momentum Equation

For a single jet of mass flow $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ striking the vane:

$$F_x \cdot r = \dot{m} (r V_{w1} - r V_{w2})$$

Simplifying with $r_1 = r_2 = r$:

$$F_x = \dot{m} (V_{w1} - V_{w2}) = \rho A V [(V - u) \cos \beta_1 - (u - (V-u)\cos \beta_2)]$$

### Step 7: Special Case — Stationary Vane ($u = 0$)

$$F_x = \rho A V [V \cos \beta_1 - (-V \cos \beta_2)] = \rho A V^2 (\cos \beta_1 + \cos \beta_2)$$

For a $180°$ turn ($\beta_2 = 180° - \beta_1$, so $\cos \beta_2 = -\cos \beta_1$):

$$\boxed{F_x = 2 \rho A V^2 \cos \beta_1 \quad \text{(Maximum force)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Problem Types in KTU

| Problem Type | Key Equation | Typical Data Given | Required Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propeller thrust | $T = \rho Q V_{w2}$ | Q, V_w2 | Thrust, Power |
| Turbine torque | $M_O = \dot{m}(r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})$ | r₁, r₂, V_w1, V_w2, $\dot{m}$ | Torque, Power |
| Curved vane force | $F_x = \rho Q (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ | Q, V, β₁, β₂ | Force on vane |
| Euler head | $H_e = (U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1})/g$ | U, V_w | Energy transferred |
| Centrifugal pump | $M_O = \dot{m} r_2 V_{w2}$ (V_w1 = 0) | r₂, V_w2, Q | Power required |
| Pelton wheel | $M_O = \dot{m} (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$ | V_jet, β₁, β₂ | Shaft torque, efficiency |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Moment of Momentum Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Control Volume around rotating element"] --> B["Locate inlet and outlet sections"]
    B --> C["Determine mass flow rate m_dot"]
    C --> D["Resolve absolute velocities into whirl V_w and flow V_f components"]
    D --> E["Calculate angular momentum at inlet r1 times V_w1"]
    E --> F["Calculate angular momentum at outlet r2 times V_w2"]
    F --> G{"Steady or Unsteady Flow?"}
    G -->|Steady| H["Apply M_O = m_dot times r2 V_w2 minus r1 V_w1"]
    G -->|Unsteady| I["Add unsteady accumulation term"]
    H --> J["Solve for torque or thrust"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Compute power P = M_O times omega"]
    K --> L["Compute efficiency if applicable"]
    L --> M["Final Answer with Units"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Velocity Triangle Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Absolute Velocity V at angle alpha"] --> B["Resolve into Components"]
    B --> C["Whirl Component V_w = V cos alpha"]
    B --> D["Flow Component V_f = V sin alpha"]
    C --> E["Multiply by radius r"]
    D --> F["Used for mass flow continuity"]
    E --> G["Angular Momentum term r times V_w"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Insert into Euler Equation M_O = m_dot times delta r V_w"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Turbomachine Classification by Energy Transfer Direction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Moment of Momentum Applied to Rotating Machine"] --> B["Sign of U2 V_w2 minus U1 V_w1"]
    B -->|Positive| C["ENERGY INPUT to fluid: PUMP or COMPRESSOR or FAN or PROPELLER"]
    B -->|Negative| D["ENERGY EXTRACTED from fluid: TURBINE"]
    C --> E["Centrifugal Pump: radial inlet V_w1 equals zero"]
    C --> F["Axial Pump: equal radii, V_w2 greater than V_w1"]
    C --> G["Propeller: axial flow, V_w1 equals zero"]
    D --> H["Pelton Wheel: jet strikes buckets"]
    D --> I["Francis Turbine: radial inward flow"]
    D --> J["Kaplan Turbine: axial flow with adjustable blades"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Representation of Forces on a Curved Vane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Jet of Water Velocity V"] --> B["Strikes Vane at angle beta1"]
    B --> C["Vane Deflects Jet by angle theta equals 180 minus beta1 minus beta2"]
    C --> D["Jet Leaves at angle beta2 with reduced whirl"]
    D --> E["Apply Moment of Momentum about Vane Center"]
    E --> F["Compute Force F_x equals rho A V times V_w1 minus V_w2"]
    F --> G["For 180 degree vane: F_x equals 2 rho A V squared cos beta1"]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Euler Turbomachine Equation Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Mass flow rate m_dot enters rotor"] --> B["Inlet angular momentum per unit mass equals r1 V_w1"]
    A --> C["Outlet angular momentum per unit mass equals r2 V_w2"]
    B --> D["Change in angular momentum per unit mass equals r2 V_w2 minus r1 V_w1"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Torque M_O equals m_dot times change in angular momentum"]
    E --> F["Power P equals M_O times omega"]
    F --> G["Or P equals m_dot times U2 V_w2 minus U1 V_w1"]
    G --> H["Or P equals rho Q times U2 V_w2 minus U1 V_w1"]
    H --> I["Final Form for KTU Problems"]
```
