# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 marks]`
**Define laminar flow and state the Reynolds number criterion for flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**
> Laminar flow is the regime of fluid motion in which the fluid moves in smooth, parallel layers (laminae) with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. Momentum transfer between layers occurs solely through viscous shear.
>
> For internal flow through a circular pipe, the flow is laminar when:
> $$\boxed{\, Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} < 2300 \,}$$
>
> Above $Re_D \approx 4000$ the flow is fully turbulent; between 2300 and 4000 lies the transitional regime.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 3 marks]`
**State the Hagen-Poiseuille equation and list the assumptions used in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**
> The Hagen-Poiseuille equation for volumetric flow rate through a circular pipe is:
> $$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta p}{128 \mu L}$$
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady flow.
> 2. Incompressible, Newtonian fluid.
> 3. Fully-developed, axisymmetric, laminar flow.
> 4. Horizontal pipe (gravity neglected in the flow direction) **or** constant axial pressure gradient.
> 5. No-slip condition at the wall.
> 6. Constant viscosity $\mu$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the velocity profile for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$ under pressure gradient $dp/dx$. State all boundary conditions explicitly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shipboard lubrication oil of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.15 \text{ Pa·s}$ and density $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 25 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 8 \text{ m}$. The pressure drop is $\Delta p = 60 \text{ kPa}$. Determine:
- (i) The mean velocity and maximum velocity.
- (ii) The wall shear stress.
- (iii) The Darcy friction factor and verify the flow is laminar.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Coordinate system and continuity:**
Use cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, x)$ with $x$ along the pipe axis. By axisymmetry $\partial/\partial\theta = 0$ and $v_\theta = 0$. Continuity:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$$

For fully-developed flow $\partial v_x / \partial x = 0$, hence $v_r = 0$ and $v_x = v_x(r)$ only.

**Step 2 — x-momentum equation:**

$$0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \, \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_x}{dr}\right)$$

**Step 3 — First integration:**

$$r \frac{dv_x}{dr} = \frac{r^2}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition 1 (symmetry at $r = 0$):**
$\dfrac{dv_x}{dr}\Big|_{r=0}$ must be finite $\Rightarrow C_1 = 0$.

**Step 5 — Second integration:**

$$v_x(r) = \frac{r^2}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Boundary condition 2 (no-slip at $r = R$):**
$v_x(R) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = -\dfrac{R^2}{4\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}$.

**Step 7 — Final profile:**

$$\boxed{\, v_x(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) \,}$$

This is parabolic, with $v_{max}$ at $r = 0$ and zero at the wall.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\mu = 0.15 \text{ Pa·s}$, $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $D = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $L = 8 \text{ m}$, $\Delta p = 60\,000 \text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Mean and maximum velocity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \frac{\Delta p \, D^2}{32 \mu L} = \frac{60\,000 \times (0.025)^2}{32 \times 0.15 \times 8} \\
&= \frac{60\,000 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{38.4} = \frac{37.5}{38.4} = 0.9766 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{max} &= 2 V_{avg} = 2 \times 0.9766 = 1.953 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Wall shear stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \frac{D \Delta p}{4 L} = \frac{0.025 \times 60\,000}{4 \times 8} = \frac{1500}{32} = 46.875 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Friction factor and laminar verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_D &= \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 0.9766 \times 0.025}{0.15} = \frac{21.48}{0.15} = 143.2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{64}{Re_D} = \frac{64}{143.2} = 0.447
\end{aligned}$$

**Check:** $Re_D = 143.2 < 2300$ $\checkmark$ — flow is laminar.

> **Final Answers:** $V_{avg} = 0.977 \text{ m/s}$, $v_{max} = 1.953 \text{ m/s}$, $\tau_w = 46.88 \text{ Pa}$, $f = 0.447$.

---

### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For steady laminar flow between two infinite parallel plates separated by distance $2h$, with the upper plate moving at velocity $U$ and a pressure gradient $dp/dx$ applied simultaneously, derive the general velocity profile $u(y)$. Identify the relative contributions of Couette and Poiseuille components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A submarine's periscope hydraulic line consists of two parallel plates 4 mm apart, with the upper plate moving at $0.5 \text{ m/s}$ relative to the lower. The oil has viscosity $\mu = 0.08 \text{ Pa·s}$ and there is a pressure drop of $\Delta p = 20 \text{ kPa}$ over a length of 2 m. Calculate:
- (i) The velocity at the mid-plane.
- (ii) The shear stress on the lower plate.
- (iii) The volumetric flow rate per unit width.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Governing ODE** (steady, fully-developed, $u = u(y)$, $v = w = 0$):

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Integrate twice:**

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Boundary conditions** (plates at $y = 0$ and $y = 2h$):
- $u(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.
- $u(2h) = U \Rightarrow U = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dx}(2h)^2 + 2h C_1$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \frac{U}{2h} - \frac{2h}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Final general profile:**

$$\boxed{\, u(y) = \underbrace{\frac{U y}{2h}}_{\text{Couette (shear-driven)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(y^2 - 2h y\right)}_{\text{Poiseuille (pressure-driven)}} \,}$$

The Couette component is **linear** in $y$, while the Poiseuille component is **parabolic**. Their superposition is the key strength of linear ODE theory — only valid because the Navier-Stokes equation is linear in $u$ for this fully-developed configuration.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $2h = 0.004 \text{ m} \Rightarrow h = 0.002 \text{ m}$, $U = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$, $\mu = 0.08 \text{ Pa·s}$, $\Delta p = 20\,000 \text{ Pa}$ over $L = 2 \text{ m}$.

Pressure gradient:
$$\frac{dp}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta p}{L} = -\frac{20\,000}{2} = -10\,000 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**(i) Velocity at mid-plane** ($y = h = 0.002 \text{ m}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
u(h) &= \frac{U h}{2h} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(h^2 - 2h^2) \\
&= \frac{U}{2} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(-h^2) \\
&= \frac{0.5}{2} + \frac{(-10\,000)}{2 \times 0.08}(-4 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 0.25 + \frac{0.04}{0.16} \times 10^{-6} \times 10\,000 \\
&= 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Detailed check:**
$$\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(-h^2) = \frac{-10\,000}{0.16} \times (-4 \times 10^{-6}) = \frac{0.04}{0.16} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\, u_{mid} = 0.5 \text{ m/s} \,}$$

**(ii) Shear stress on the lower plate** ($y = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \mu \frac{du}{dy}\Big|_{y=0} \\
\frac{du}{dy} &= \frac{U}{2h} + \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} (y - h) \\
\tau_w &= \mu \left[\frac{U}{2h} + \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(-h)\right] = \frac{\mu U}{2h} - h\frac{dp}{dx} \\
&= \frac{0.08 \times 0.5}{0.004} - 0.002 \times (-10\,000) \\
&= 10 + 20 = 30 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate per unit width:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \int_0^{2h} u(y) \, dy \\
&= \int_0^{2h} \left[\frac{U y}{2h} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}(y^2 - 2h y)\right] dy \\
&= \frac{U}{2h} \cdot \frac{(2h)^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left[\frac{y^3}{3} - h y^2\right]_0^{2h} \\
&= U h + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left[\frac{8h^3}{3} - 4h^3\right] \\
&= U h + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(-\frac{4h^3}{3}\right) \\
&= (0.5)(0.002) + \frac{(-10\,000)}{0.16}\left(-\frac{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-9}}{3}\right) \\
&= 0.001 + 6.67 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\, q = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \text{ per unit width} \,}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Examiners Award Zero or Partial Marks in Laminar Flow Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the no-slip condition.** A student who writes "the constant of integration is zero by symmetry" but does not write "$v_x(R) = 0$ at the wall" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong friction factor.** Mixing up Darcy ($f = 64/Re$) and Fanning ($C_f = 16/Re$) without justification.
> 3. **Failing to convert units.** Diameter given in mm, length in m, pressure in kPa — examiners check unit consistency. A single Pa-vs-kPa slip is a full-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the laminar verification.** Always compute $Re$ and explicitly state "Since $Re < 2300$, the laminar assumption is valid." A problem statement that says "laminar" still requires the student to confirm it.
> 5. **Mixing up Couette and Poiseuille profiles.** Couette is **linear**; Poiseuille is **parabolic**. Writing the wrong shape is a conceptual error worth losing 3–4 marks even if the algebra is right.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laminar Flow — 30-Second Recap:**
>
> - **Definition:** Smooth, parallel, layered flow; viscous shear dominant; $Re < 2300$ (pipe) or $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$ (flat plate BL).
> - **Governing equation:** Incompressible Navier-Stokes; convective term vanishes for fully-developed flow.
> - **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta p / (128 \mu L)$; $V_{avg} = v_{max}/2$.
> - **Friction factor (Darcy):** $f = 64 / Re_D$ — the universal laminar signature.
> - **Plane Couette:** Linear profile $u = Uy/h$; constant shear $\tau = \mu U / h$.
> - **Plane Poiseuille:** Parabolic profile; $V_{avg} = h^2 (-dp/dx) / (3\mu)$.
> - **Inclined film:** Gravity-driven; maximum at free surface, zero at wall.
> - **Boundary conditions to memorize:** no-slip at solid wall, symmetry at centerline, zero-shear at free surface.
> - **Naval relevance:** Hull skin-friction estimation (laminar fraction), pipe networks (bilge/ballast/lubrication), boundary-layer control.
> - **Pitfall:** $V_{avg} = v_{max}/2$ for **pipe**, but $V_{avg} = 2 v_{max}/3$ for **plane Poiseuille**. Know both!
> - **Always verify:** Calculate $Re$ first, then confirm laminar regime before applying $f = 64/Re$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Turbulent flow** is a regime of fluid motion characterized by **chaotic, random, and time-dependent fluctuations** in velocity and pressure fields. The fluid particles move in irregular, intermixing eddies of varying sizes superimposed on the main flow direction. Mathematically, the instantaneous velocity at any point is expressed as the superposition of a time-averaged (mean) component and a fluctuating component:

$$V(t) = \bar{V} + v'(t)$$

where $\bar{V}$ is the **time-averaged velocity** and $v'(t)$ is the **fluctuating component** with the property that its time-average $\overline{v'(t)} = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Turbulent flow is identified when the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** exceeds a critical threshold. For flow in pipes, transition typically occurs at $Re \approx 2300$ (lower critical) to $Re \approx 4000$ (upper critical), while for flow along flat plates (highly relevant to ship hulls), transition occurs around $Re_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **smooth, orderly queue of people walking down a narrow corridor** (this is laminar flow). Everyone moves at the same speed, in the same direction, with no crossing of paths. Now imagine a **crowded railway platform during peak hours** — people bump into each other, change directions unpredictably, swirl around in small groups, and yet the entire crowd still drifts from the entrance toward the exit. The bulk movement (drift) is still in one direction, but locally, everything is chaotic. **This is turbulent flow.**

For a **naval architect**, this is the dominant reality. Water flowing past a ship hull at typical cruising speeds (say 15–25 knots) is almost always turbulent. The boundary layer on the hull is turbulent, which directly determines the **frictional resistance** (skin friction) of the vessel — a major component of the total drag that limits a ship's speed and fuel efficiency.

### Key Distinguishing Characteristics of Turbulent Flow

| Property | Behavior in Turbulent Flow |
|----------|---------------------------|
| **Velocity field** | Fluctuating, random, 3D even in nominally 2D flow |
| **Mixing** | Vigorous; momentum/heat/mass transfer is greatly enhanced |
| **Energy dissipation** | Very high; turbulent eddies continuously dissipate kinetic energy into heat |
| **Shear stress** | Dominated by **Reynolds stresses**, not viscous stresses |
| **Pressure variation** | Fluctuating; smaller time-averaged pressure drop per unit length than laminar |
| **Velocity profile** | "Flatter" or more "plug-like" than the parabolic laminar profile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C: $\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**
> - **Density of seawater at 15 °C: $\rho \approx 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$**
> - **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow: $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$**
> - **Critical Reynolds Number for flat plate: $Re_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Turbulence

Turbulence arises when **inertial forces dominate viscous forces** in the flow. The dimensionless number that quantifies this ratio is the **Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

where $V$ is a characteristic velocity, $L$ is a characteristic length, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity, and $\rho$ is fluid density.

### 2.1.1 The Logical Chain — Why and How Turbulence Develops

1. **Why does transition occur?**
   Small disturbances (vibrations, surface roughness, inlet geometry imperfections) are always present in real flows. In laminar flow, viscous forces damp these disturbances quickly. In turbulent flow, **inertial amplification** of disturbances outpaces viscous damping, so they grow and eventually overwhelm the flow.

2. **How is turbulence sustained?**
   The **energy cascade**: Large eddies (of size comparable to the flow geometry) receive energy from the mean flow. They are unstable and **break down into progressively smaller eddies** (Richardson's cascade). At the smallest scales — the **Kolmogorov microscales** — viscous dissipation finally converts the kinetic energy into heat.

3. **Why does turbulent flow mix so efficiently?**
   Because eddies transport mass, momentum, and energy over distances far greater than molecular diffusion would allow. The effective "mixing" far exceeds molecular viscosity.

## 2.2 Reynolds Decomposition and Reynolds Stresses

Any instantaneous flow quantity is decomposed into a mean and a fluctuating part. For the x-component of velocity:

$$u(t) = \bar{u} + u'(t)$$

When this decomposition is substituted into the **Navier-Stokes equations** and time-averaged, the resulting equations are called the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations**. They contain an additional apparent stress term:

$$\tau_{turb} = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$$

This is the **Reynolds shear stress** (note the negative sign by convention). The total shear stress in a turbulent flow is:

$$\tau_{total} = \mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} + \tau_{turb} = (\mu + \mu_t)\frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

where $\mu_t = \rho \varepsilon$ is the **eddy viscosity** and $\varepsilon$ is the **eddy diffusivity of momentum** (Boussinesq hypothesis, 1877).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In fully turbulent flow, the Reynolds stress $\rho \overline{u'v'}$ dominates over the viscous stress $\mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$ except in the thin region very close to the wall (the **viscous sublayer**).

## 2.3 Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory (1925)

Prandtl introduced the **mixing length $l$** — the average distance a fluid parcel travels perpendicular to the flow before losing its identity (analogous to the "mean free path" in kinetic theory). The Reynolds stress becomes:

$$\tau = \rho l^2 \left\vert \frac{du}{dy} \right\vert \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is the foundation for deriving the **logarithmic velocity profile**.

## 2.4 Turbulent Flow in Pipes — Velocity Distributions

For turbulent flow in a smooth pipe, the measured velocity profile is much "fuller" than the laminar parabolic profile. Two empirical/analytical laws are universally used:

### 2.4.1 The Power Law (1/n-th Power Law)

$$\frac{u}{u_{max}} = \left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^{1/n}$$

where $y$ is the distance from the wall, $R$ is the pipe radius, and the exponent $n$ depends on Reynolds number:

| $Re$ | $n$ |
|------|-----|
| $4 \times 10^3$ | **6.0** |
| $1.1 \times 10^5$ | **7.0** |
| $2 \times 10^6$ | **10.0** |
| $3.2 \times 10^6$ | **very large** |

### 2.4.2 The Logarithmic Distribution (Universal Law of the Wall)

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{k_s}\right) + C$$

where:
- $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ is the **friction (shear) velocity**
- $\kappa \approx 0.41$ is the **von Kármán constant**
- $k_s$ is the equivalent sand-grain roughness
- $C$ is a constant (≈ 8.5 for hydraulically smooth walls)

This law is universal across many turbulent wall-bounded flows and is the basis for modern **CFD boundary layer modeling**.

## 2.5 Friction Factor in Turbulent Pipe Flow

### 2.5.1 Smooth Pipes — Blasius Equation (valid for $Re < 10^5$)

$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$

### 2.5.2 Smooth Pipes — Prandtl Universal Formula (all Re)

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.8$$

### 2.5.3 Rough Pipes — Colebrook-White Equation (industry standard)

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{k_s}{3.7D} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

This is the **implicit** equation plotted on the **Moody Chart** and is used worldwide for marine piping design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility for Naval Architects:** Friction factor $f$ is directly used to compute **head loss in marine piping systems** (cooling seawater, bilge, firefighting, ballast lines) and the **frictional resistance** of ship hulls.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Application** |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | Transition criterion |
| Darcy-Weisbach Equation | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Pipe head loss |
| Friction Factor (Laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $Re < 2300$ |
| Blasius (Smooth, Turb.) | $f = 0.316 Re^{-0.25}$ | $4000 < Re < 10^5$ |
| Colebrook (Universal) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{k_s}{3.7D} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | All turbulent $Re$, rough/smooth |
| Friction Velocity | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_w/\rho}$ | Boundary layer analysis |
| Power Law Profile | $\dfrac{u}{u_{max}} = \left(\dfrac{y}{R}\right)^{1/n}$ | Empirical velocity profile |
| Logarithmic Law | $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0$ | Smooth wall (viscous units) |
| Reynolds Stress | $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u' v'}$ | RANS closure |
| Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{f}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Skin friction on hull |
| Boundary Layer Thickness (Turb.) | $\delta \approx \dfrac{0.37 x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Flat plate (Blasius) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Velocity in Turbulent Pipe Flow from the Power Law

We want to relate the **mean (bulk) velocity** $V$ to the **centerline (maximum) velocity** $u_{max}$ using the empirical power-law profile.

**Step 1 — Write the assumed velocity profile.**

From the power-law distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(y) = u_{max}\left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $y$ is measured from the pipe wall inward (so $y = 0$ at the wall and $y = R$ at the centerline).

**Step 2 — Set up the integral for the volumetric flow rate $Q$.**

For a circular cross-section of radius $R$, we slice the pipe into thin concentric rings. At a distance $y$ from the wall, the local radius from the center is $r = R - y$. The elemental area is $dA = 2\pi r\,dr$.

**Step 3 — Convert the integration variable.**

Because $r = R - y$, we have $dr = -dy$. As $r$ goes from $0$ to $R$, $y$ goes from $R$ to $0$. Inverting the limits removes the minus sign. Also note $r = R - y$, so the power-law becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u = u_{max}\left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^{1/n} = u_{max}\left(1 - \frac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the flow rate $Q$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_0^R u \cdot 2\pi r\, dr \\
  &= 2\pi u_{max} \int_0^R \left(1 - \frac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n} r\, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the substitution $z = 1 - r/R$ so that $r = R(1-z)$ and $dr = -R\,dz$.**

When $r = 0 \Rightarrow z = 1$; when $r = R \Rightarrow z = 0$. Flipping limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 2\pi u_{max} \int_0^1 z^{1/n} \cdot R(1-z) \cdot R\, dz \\
  &= 2\pi u_{max} R^2 \int_0^1 z^{1/n}(1-z)\, dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Beta function form.**

The integral $\int_0^1 z^{a-1}(1-z)^{b-1}dz = B(a,b) = \dfrac{\Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)}{\Gamma(a+b)}$. With $a = 1/n + 1$ and $b = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^1 z^{1/n}(1-z)\,dz = \frac{\Gamma(1 + \tfrac{1}{n})\cdot \Gamma(2)}{\Gamma(3 + \tfrac{1}{n})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\Gamma(2)=1!=1$ and $\Gamma(3+1/n)=(2+1/n)\Gamma(2+1/n)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^1 z^{1/n}(1-z)\,dz = \frac{\Gamma(1+\tfrac{1}{n})}{(2+\tfrac{1}{n})\Gamma(2+\tfrac{1}{n})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Relate $\Gamma(1+1/n)$ and $\Gamma(2+1/n)$.**

Using $\Gamma(x+1) = x\,\Gamma(x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma\!\left(2 + \tfrac{1}{n}\right) = \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{n}\right)\Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the integral simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^1 z^{1/n}(1-z)\,dz = \frac{1}{(2 + \tfrac{1}{n})(1 + \tfrac{1}{n})} = \frac{n^2}{(2n+1)(n+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back into $Q$ and find mean velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 2\pi u_{max} R^2 \cdot \frac{n^2}{(2n+1)(n+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The mean velocity is $V = Q/A = Q/(\pi R^2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = 2u_{max} \cdot \frac{n^2}{(2n+1)(n+1)} = \frac{2n^2}{(n+1)(2n+1)}\, u_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{V}{u_{max}} = \dfrac{2n^2}{(n+1)(2n+1)}\;}
$$

**Verification for limiting cases:**
- $n = 1$ (linear profile): $V/u_{max} = 2/3 \approx 0.667$ ✓
- $n = 7$ (typical turbulent): $V/u_{max} = 98/120 \approx 0.817$
- $n \to \infty$ (flat/plug profile): $V/u_{max} \to 1$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Derivation of the Logarithmic Velocity Profile

This is one of the most famous derivations in fluid mechanics.

**Step 1 — Assume Reynolds stress dominates (away from the wall).**

In the fully turbulent region (outer layer), viscous stress is negligible, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau \approx \tau_t = \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(taking the absolute value and assuming positive $du/dy$).

**Step 2 — Assume the mixing length $l$ is proportional to the distance from the wall.**

Prandtl's hypothesis: $l = \kappa y$, where $\kappa \approx 0.41$ is the von Kármán constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau = \rho (\kappa y)^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 = \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume shear stress is approximately constant near the wall.**

For fully developed flow, $\tau \approx \tau_w = \rho u_*^2$, where $u_*$ is the friction velocity. This is the **constant stress layer** approximation, valid for $y \ll$ flow depth/diameter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho u_*^2 = \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Cancel $\rho$ and take the square root:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_* = \kappa y \frac{du}{dy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{du}{u_*} = \frac{dy}{\kappa y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating from a reference point $(y_0, u_0)$ to $(y, u)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{u_0}^{u} du = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \int_{y_0}^{y} \frac{dy}{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u - u_0 = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\frac{y}{y_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express $u_0$ in terms of a roughness length.**

For smooth walls, experiments show that $y_0 = \nu/(9 u_*)$ approximately, with $u_0 = 0$ at this sublayer edge. Substituting gives the smooth-wall log law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\frac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result (Universal Log Law of the Wall):**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Naval Architects:** This profile is used in ship model testing and CFD to extrapolate from measured near-wall velocities to the freestream — and is the basis for ITTC-78 hull form frictional resistance prediction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wall Shear Stress and Friction Velocity in a Pipe

**Step 1 — Force balance on a pipe element.**

For a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$, with pressure drop $\Delta p$ and wall shear stress $\tau_w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\tau_w$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D}{4 L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the Darcy-Weisbach relation.**

The Darcy-Weisbach equation states $h_f = \dfrac{V^2 L}{2 g D}\cdot \dfrac{f}{4}\cdot 4 = f\dfrac{L}{D}\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$. By definition $h_f = \Delta p/(\rho g)$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g} \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta p = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two $\Delta p$ expressions.**

From Step 2: $\Delta p = 4\tau_w L / D$. Setting equal to the Darcy result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 \tau_w \frac{L}{D} = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_w = \frac{f}{8}\,\rho V^2 = \frac{f}{4}\cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental connection** between the friction factor and wall shear stress — the cornerstone of hull frictional resistance estimation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Laminar vs. Turbulent Pipe Flow

| **Parameter** | **Laminar Flow** | **Turbulent Flow** |
|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic: $u = u_{max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$ | Power law / Log law (much flatter) |
| Mean/Max ratio $V/u_{max}$ | **0.5** | **0.75–0.90** (Re-dependent) |
| Friction factor $f$ | $64/Re$ | Blasius / Colebrook (≈constant at high Re) |
| $h_f$ dependence on $V$ | $h_f \propto V$ | $h_f \propto V^{1.75\text{–}2.0}$ |
| Head loss per unit length | High at given flow | Lower than laminar (efficient transport) |
| Kinetic energy correction $\alpha$ | **2.0** | **≈ 1.05–1.10** |
| Momentum correction $\beta$ | **4/3** | **≈ 1.01–1.04** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turbulent Flow Analysis — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Conditions: V_in, D, k_s, nu] --> B{Reynolds Number Re = VD/nu}
    B -->|Re less than 2300| C[Laminar Flow Branch: f = 64/Re]
    B -->|Re between 2300 to 4000| D[Transitional Zone]
    B -->|Re greater than 4000| E[Turbulent Flow Branch]
    C --> F[Friction Factor f]
    D --> F
    E --> G{Smooth or Rough Pipe?}
    G -->|Smooth, Re less than 1e5| H[Blasius: f = 0.316 Re^-0.25]
    G -->|Smooth, All Re| I[Prandtl: 1/sqrt f = 2 log10 Re sqrt f - 0.8]
    G -->|Rough or Mixed| J[COLEBROOK EQUATION: 1/sqrt f = -2 log10 of k_s/3.7D + 2.51 over Re sqrt f]
    H --> F
    I --> F
    J --> F
    F --> K[Compute Wall Shear: tau_w = f rho V squared over 8]
    F --> L[Compute Head Loss: h_f = f L V squared over D 2g]
    K --> M[Friction Velocity: u_star = sqrt tau_w over rho]
    M --> N[Log Law Velocity Profile: u over u_star = 1 over kappa ln of u_star y over nu + 5.0]
    L --> O[Naval Arch Output: Hull Skin Friction, Pipe Pressure Drop]
    N --> O
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The Reynolds number is the **single gatekeeper** that determines which branch of analysis applies.
- For turbulent flow, the next gatekeeper is the **relative roughness** $k_s/D$.
- The final outputs (`tau_w`, `h_f`, `u_*`, velocity profile) all feed into naval-architecture-relevant quantities: hull skin friction and piping-system pressure drops.

---

## 4.2 Turbulent Boundary Layer Structure on a Ship Hull

```
flowchart LR
    L1[Leading Edge of Hull: y = 0] --> L2[Laminar Sublayer: very thin, viscous dominates]
    L2 --> L3[Buffer Layer: viscous and turbulent comparable]
    L3 --> L4[Log Layer: turbulent dominates, log law applies]
    L4 --> L5[Outer Layer / Wake: free stream mixing]
    L5 --> L6[Freestream V_inf]
    L7[Vessel Speed V_s = 15 to 25 knots] --> L1
    L1 --> L8[Boundary Layer Grows: delta approx 0.37 x over Re_x^0.2]
    L8 --> L9[Wall Shear Stress tau_w integrated over Hull Wetted Area]
    L9 --> L10[Frictional Resistance R_f = C_f 1/2 rho V^2 S]
    L10 --> L11[Total Hull Drag: R_total = R_f + R_wave + R_form]
```

This block diagram shows the **logical sequence** of how the turbulent boundary layer, originating at the leading edge, propagates along the hull and ultimately determines the ship's frictional resistance — a primary concern in powering predictions and fuel-efficiency studies.

---

## 4.3 Velocity Profile Comparison (Laminar vs Turbulent)

```
flowchart TD
    P1[Pipe Radius R] --> P2{Laminar or Turbulent?}
    P2 -->|Laminar| P3[Parabolic: u over u_max = 1 - r^2 over R^2]
    P2 -->|Turbulent| P4[Power Law: u over u_max = y/R ^ 1/n]
    P3 --> P5[Mean to Max Ratio: V over u_max = 0.50]
    P4 --> P6[Mean to Max Ratio: V over u_max = 2n^2 over n+1 times 2n+1]
    P5 --> P7[Plot Profile: blunt parabola]
    P6 --> P8[Plot Profile: flat topped plug]
```
