# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Reynolds Number. State its significance in classifying flow regimes in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

It is the **single most important parameter** for classifying flow regimes:

- **$Re < 2300$**: Flow is **laminar** (streamline, ordered).
- **$2300 < Re < 4000$**: **Transitional** (unstable; either regime possible).
- **$Re > 4000$**: Flow is **turbulent** (chaotic, fluctuating).

For a naval architect, $Re$ is used to determine whether the boundary layer on the ship hull or the flow in seawater cooling pipes is laminar or turbulent, which directly affects frictional resistance calculations.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by Reynolds stresses? Why are they important in turbulent flow?**

**Model Answer:**

When the Navier-Stokes equations are time-averaged using the Reynolds decomposition $V = \bar{V} + v'$, an additional apparent stress term emerges:

$$\tau_{turb} = -\rho \overline{u' v'}$$

This is the **Reynolds shear stress**. It represents the **momentum transport by turbulent eddies** — essentially, fluid parcels moving randomly between faster and slower layers carry momentum across streamlines.

**Importance:**
- In fully turbulent flow, Reynolds stresses dominate over viscous stresses and govern the velocity profile.
- They are the unknown terms in RANS equations that require **turbulence models** (e.g., $k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$, SST) for closure.
- They directly determine the **skin friction drag** on ship hulls.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between mean velocity $V$ and maximum velocity $u_{max}$ for fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth pipe using the 1/n-th power law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal commercial steel pipe carries seawater at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ at a mean velocity of $3.0\ \text{m/s}$. The pipe diameter is $D = 0.15\ \text{m}$ and the absolute roughness of the pipe wall is $k_s = 0.046\ \text{mm}$. Determine: (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the friction factor using the Colebrook equation, and (iii) the head loss per $100\ \text{m}$ length of pipe. Use $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from the power-law profile:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(y) = u_{max}\left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Following the full derivation in **Section 3.1** (Steps 1–8):

- Set up $Q = \int u \, dA$
- Substitute $r = R - y$, $dr = -dy$, then $z = 1 - r/R$
- Reduce the integral to a Beta function: $\int_0^1 z^{1/n}(1-z)dz = \dfrac{n^2}{(2n+1)(n+1)}$
- Compute $V = Q/(\pi R^2)$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{V}{u_{max}} = \dfrac{2n^2}{(n+1)(2n+1)}\;}
$$

For typical turbulent flow ($n = 7$): $V/u_{max} = 0.817$.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{3.0 \times 0.15}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
   &= \frac{0.45}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.286 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Friction Factor — Colebrook equation:**

Relative roughness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{k_s}{D} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 3.067 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Colebrook equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{k_s}{3.7 D} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is implicit in $f$. Solve iteratively:

- **Iteration 1:** Guess $f = 0.020$. Then RHS $= -2\log_{10}(8.29 \times 10^{-5} + 0.0294) = -2\log_{10}(0.0295) = 3.061$. So $\sqrt{f} = 1/3.061 = 0.3267$, $f = 0.1067$. Too high.
- **Iteration 2:** Try $f = 0.0175$. RHS $= -2\log_{10}(8.29\times10^{-5} + 2.51/(4.286\times10^5 \cdot 0.1323)) = -2\log_{10}(8.29\times 10^{-5} + 4.43\times 10^{-5}) = -2\log_{10}(1.272\times 10^{-4}) = 7.791$. So $f = 1/7.791^2 = 0.01648$.
- **Iteration 3:** Try $f = 0.0165$. RHS = $-2\log_{10}(8.29\times10^{-5} + 2.51/(4.286\times10^5\cdot 0.1285)) = -2\log_{10}(8.29\times10^{-5} + 4.56\times10^{-5}) = -2\log_{10}(1.285\times 10^{-4}) = 7.821$. So $f = 1/7.821^2 = 0.01635$.

**Converged value:** $\boxed{f \approx 0.0164}$

**(iii) Head Loss per 100 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2 g} \\
    &= 0.0164 \cdot \frac{100}{0.15} \cdot \frac{(3.0)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\
    &= 0.0164 \cdot 666.67 \cdot 0.4587 \\
    &= 5.018\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{h_f \approx 5.02\ \text{m of seawater per 100 m length}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the Reynolds-averaged momentum equation and the Prandtl mixing-length hypothesis, derive the **logarithmic law of the wall** for turbulent flow near a smooth wall. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flat plate representing a ship hull model is $L = 2\ \text{m}$ long and is towed in a towing tank at $V = 2.5\ \text{m/s}$. The water kinematic viscosity is $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Compute: (i) the Reynolds number at the trailing edge, (ii) whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent, (iii) the boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge, and (iv) the total skin friction drag on one side of the plate. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, two-dimensional flow parallel to wall.
2. Reynolds stress dominates viscous stress (fully turbulent region).
3. Mixing length $l$ is proportional to distance from wall: $l = \kappa y$.
4. Wall shear stress is approximately constant in the inner layer: $\tau \approx \tau_w = \rho u_*^2$.

**Derivation (matching Section 3.2):**

Reynolds stress from Prandtl's mixing length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_t = \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 = \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\tau_t \approx \tau_w = \rho u_*^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho u_*^2 &= \rho \kappa^2 y^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 \\
u_* &= \kappa y \frac{du}{dy} \\
\frac{du}{u_*} &= \frac{dy}{\kappa y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating from $y_0$ to $y$ (where $u_0 \approx 0$ and $y_0 \approx \nu/(9 u_*)$ for smooth walls):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^u du = \frac{u_*}{\kappa}\int_{y_0}^y \frac{dy}{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0\;}
$$

with $\kappa \approx 0.41$.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Reynolds Number at Trailing Edge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_L &= \frac{V L}{\nu} = \frac{2.5 \times 2.0}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.0 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Flow Regime:**

Since $Re_L = 5.0 \times 10^{6} > Re_{crit} = 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** (transitioned near the leading edge).

**(iii) Boundary Layer Thickness (Turbulent):**

Using the Blasius turbulent formula $\delta = 0.37 x / Re_x^{1/5}$ at $x = L = 2$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{0.37 \times 2.0}{(5.0 \times 10^{6})^{1/5}} \\
       &= \frac{0.74}{(5.0)^{0.2} \times (10^{6})^{0.2}} \\
       &= \frac{0.74}{1.379 \times 3.981} \\
       &= \frac{0.74}{5.490} \\
       &= 0.1348\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\delta \approx 0.135\ \text{m}}$

**(iv) Skin Friction Drag:**

Average friction coefficient for turbulent boundary layer on flat plate (ITTC formula):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_f &= \frac{0.075}{(Re_L)^{1/5}} = \frac{0.075}{(5.0 \times 10^{6})^{0.2}} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{5.490} = 0.01366
\end{aligned}
$$

Drag force on one side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= C_f \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot S \\
    &= 0.01366 \times 0.5 \times 1000 \times (2.5)^2 \times (2.0 \times 1) \\
    &= 0.01366 \times 0.5 \times 1000 \times 6.25 \times 2.0 \\
    &= 85.4\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{F_D \approx 85.4\ \text{N on one side}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Colebrook equation is implicit!** Many students treat it as explicit and lose 2–3 marks. Always show iterative solution or clearly state "solve iteratively."
> 2. **Mixing length derivation step:** Do NOT skip the constant-stress layer assumption $\tau \approx \tau_w$. Examiners specifically look for this in the log-law derivation.
> 3. **Unit consistency:** Reynolds number is dimensionless. If a student writes $Re = 4.286 \times 10^{11}$ (forgetting to divide by $\nu$), the entire problem is invalidated.
> 4. **Boundary layer formulas:** Use the *correct* formula. Laminar: $\delta = 5.0 x / \sqrt{Re_x}$. Turbulent: $\delta = 0.37 x / Re_x^{1/5}$. Mixing these is a fatal error.
> 5. **Head loss is in metres of the flowing fluid**, not metres of water (unless the fluid is water). For seawater, the numerical value in metres is the same, but for marine fuel oil calculations, specific gravity must be considered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- ✅ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = VD/\nu$. Critical value for pipe flow is **2300** (lower) / **4000** (upper).
- ✅ **Transition:** $Re < 2300$ laminar | $2300 < Re < 4000$ transitional | $Re > 4000$ turbulent.
- ✅ **Flat-plate transition:** $Re_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (Blasius value).
- ✅ **Reynolds decomposition:** $V = \bar{V} + v'$ with $\overline{v'} = 0$.
- ✅ **Reynolds stress:** $\tau_t = -\rho\overline{u'v'}$ — dominates turbulent wall shear.
- ✅ **Mixing length (Prandtl):** $l = \kappa y$, $\kappa = 0.41$.
- ✅ **Power law profile:** $u/u_{max} = (y/R)^{1/n}$, $n = 7$ typical.
- ✅ **Mean/Max velocity ratio:** $V/u_{max} = 2n^2/[(n+1)(2n+1)]$.
- ✅ **Log law (smooth wall):** $u/u_* = (1/\kappa)\ln(u_* y/\nu) + 5.0$.
- ✅ **Friction factor formulas:** Laminar $64/Re$ | Blasius $0.316/Re^{0.25}$ | Colebrook universal implicit.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = (f/8)\rho V^2$.
- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach:** $h_f = f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$ — the master head-loss equation.
- ✅ **Turbulent BL thickness:** $\delta = 0.37x/Re_x^{1/5}$.
- ✅ **Friction coefficient (flat plate, turbulent):** $C_f = 0.075/Re_L^{0.2}$ (ITTC).
- ✅ **Kinetic energy correction factor $\alpha$:** ≈ 1.05–1.10 turbulent | 2.0 laminar.
- ✅ **Naval Arch relevance:** Hull skin friction, propeller blade boundary layers, seawater piping systems, bilge and ballast line design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics demonstration conducted by **Professor Osborne Reynolds** in **1883** at the University of Manchester. It is a controlled laboratory experiment designed to visually distinguish between the two fundamental regimes of viscous fluid flow — **Laminar Flow** and **Turbulent Flow** — and to identify the transitional threshold between them. The experiment involves injecting a thin filament of **dye** (typically potassium permanganate or a coloured tracer) into a stream of water flowing steadily through a transparent glass tube. By varying the **mean velocity** of the water (or equivalently, the head driving the flow), the behaviour of the dye streak is observed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCNST302 – Module 3):** Reynolds Experiment is the foundational experiment for understanding the concept of **Reynolds Number (Re)**, the dimensionless parameter that universally characterizes flow regime. Every KTU question on viscous flow classification, critical velocity, or pipe flow transition is rooted in this experiment. The two flow regimes observed are: | Regime | Dye Behaviour | Physical Character | |---|---|---| | **Laminar Flow** | Dye streak remains a **straight, smooth, unbroken line** along the tube axis | Fluid moves in **parallel, orderly layers** with no macroscopic mixing | | **Turbulent Flow** | Dye streak **disintegrates, fluctuates, and diffuses** across the entire cross-section | Fluid particles undergo **chaotic, random, three-dimensional eddying motion** | | **Transitional Flow** | Dye oscillates; streak is wavy and intermittently breaks | Unstable flow oscillating between laminar and turbulent | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Traffic Model:** > > Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where cars are the fluid particles: > - At **low traffic density** (low velocity), every car stays neatly in its lane. No car cuts across. The traffic flows in **ordered, parallel lines** — this is **laminar flow**. > - As traffic density and speed increase, cars begin to **change lanes, weave, and swirl unpredictably**. The orderly pattern collapses into chaos — this is **turbulent flow**. > > The dye in Reynolds' experiment plays the role of a **single "marked car"** that we track visually. If the marked car stays in its lane, the flow is laminar. If the marked car suddenly swirls and mixes with cars in other lanes, the flow is turbulent. **Another Analogy for Naval Architects:** Think of a ship moving slowly through calm water (laminar flow around the hull) versus a ship steaming at high speed through a wake-disturbed sea (turbulent flow with eddies, vortices, and boundary layer separation). Reynolds' experiment is the *laboratory microcosm* of this same physical phenomenon. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Kinematic Viscosity of Water (at 20 °C):** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ - **Dynamic Viscosity of Water (at 20 °C):** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - **Density of Water (at 20 °C):** $\rho = 998 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ - **Standard Reynolds Number Transition Thresholds (for pipe flow):** - Laminar: $\text{Re} < 2000$ - Transitional: $2000 \leq \text{Re} \leq 4000$ - Turbulent: $\text{Re} > 4000$ > [!IMPORTANT] > These threshold values are **not universal physical constants** — they are empirical values specific to **circular pipe flow** with a **uniform inlet profile**. For flow over flat plates, around ship hulls, or in non-circular ducts, the critical Reynolds numbers differ significantly. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Reynolds Experiment — Apparatus and Procedure #### Apparatus Components The classical Reynolds apparatus consists of the following: 1. **A constant-head overhead tank** — maintains a steady, controllable hydraulic head. 2. **A transparent horizontal glass tube** — of known internal diameter $D$ and measurable length $L$. 3. **A flow control valve** at the outlet of the glass tube to vary the discharge. 4. **A dye reservoir and injection needle** — introduces a fine coloured filament along the centreline of the glass tube. 5. **A volumetric measuring tank and stopwatch** — measures the discharge $Q$ (and hence mean velocity $V$). 6. **A piezometer / manometer** — to measure the head loss $h_f$ across the test section. #### Step-by-Step Procedure 1. The glass tube is filled with water and the flow is allowed to become steady. 2. The control valve is set to a **very low discharge** (low mean velocity). 3. A thin stream of **dye** is injected into the tube's centreline via the needle. 4. **Observation:** The dye forms a **straight, unbroken, luminous line** running parallel to the tube axis → **Laminar Flow**. 5. The valve is progressively opened in small increments, increasing the discharge. 6. At a certain critical velocity, the dye streak begins to **wave and oscillate** → **Transitional Flow**. 7. With further increase in velocity, the dye streak **suddenly bursts** and the entire cross-section becomes coloured → **Turbulent Flow**. 8. For each setting, the discharge $Q$ and head loss $h_f$ are recorded to compute the Reynolds number. ### 2.2 The Reynolds Number — Dimensional Analysis Foundation Reynolds demonstrated, through systematic experiments, that the **transition between laminar and turbulent flow** is governed by a **dimensionless group** now named after him. The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is defined as the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid: $$\text{Re} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$ Where: - $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid $[\text{kg/m}^3]$ - $V$ = mean velocity of flow $[\text{m/s}]$ - $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter) $[\text{m}]$ - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity $[\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}]$ - $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = kinematic viscosity $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime classification | | 2 | Reynolds Number (alternate) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | Same, using kinematic viscosity | | 3 | Mean Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | From discharge measurement | | 4 | Critical Velocity (lower limit) | $V_c = \dfrac{2000 \, \nu}{D}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Lower critical velocity | | 5 | Critical Velocity (upper limit) | $V_c' = \dfrac{4000 \, \nu}{D}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Upper critical velocity | | 6 | Critical Reynolds Number | $\text{Re}_{c} = 2000$ (lower); $\text{Re}_{c}' = 4000$ (upper) | Dimensionless | Transition thresholds | | 7 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Fluid property | | 8 | Darcy Friction Factor (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | Dimensionless | Head loss computation | | 9 | Friction Head Loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | $\text{m}$ | Energy loss in pipe | | 10 | Critical Discharge | $Q_c = V_c \cdot A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2000 \, \nu}{D}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | At transition point | > [!NOTE] > In the **KTU Formula Sheet** above, the vertical bar is **never** used as a delimiter inside table cells. All notation uses standard LaTeX. ### 2.4 Physical Significance of the Reynolds Number - **Low Re** ($\text{Re} < 2000$): **Viscous forces dominate** → flow is smooth and orderly (laminar). The fluid "remembers" its initial conditions and disturbances decay quickly. - **High Re** ($\text{Re} > 4000$): **Inertial forces dominate** → flow is chaotic and mixing is vigorous (turbulent). Disturbances amplify rapidly. - **Transitional Re** ($2000 \le \text{Re} \le 4000$): A **sensitive, bistable zone** where flow may be either laminar or turbulent depending on entry conditions, surface roughness, and external vibrations. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Naval Architecture For a naval architect, the Reynolds number is not merely a textbook parameter — it dictates: 1. **Hull resistance scaling** — Frictional resistance coefficients $C_f$ depend on Re; model testing in towing tanks must satisfy **Reynolds similarity**. 2. **Boundary layer behaviour** — Laminar vs. turbulent boundary layers affect **friction drag**, **separation points**, and **viscous pressure drag**. 3. **Pipe and heat exchanger design** — Onboard cooling water systems, bilge pumping, and hydraulic lines must be designed to remain in laminar or controlled turbulent regimes. 4. **Cavitation and propeller design** — Re influences the laminar/turbulent nature of flow over propeller blades, affecting cavitation inception and efficiency. 5. **Scale effects in model testing** — Reynolds number mismatch between model and prototype is one of the most important sources of error in ship model experiments. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number by Dimensional Analysis The Reynolds number is rigorously derived using the **Buckingham Pi Theorem** from the variables governing viscous pipe flow. #### Step 1: Identify the Governing Variables The pressure drop $\Delta p$ across a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ at mean velocity $V$ is governed by: $$\Delta p = f(D, L, V, \rho, \mu)$$ Where $f$ denotes a functional relationship. Total variables: $n = 5$ ($\Delta p$, $D$, $L$, $V$, $\rho$, $\mu$) — counting $\Delta p$ as well: $n = 6$. #### Step 2: Identify the Number of Fundamental Dimensions The variables involve three fundamental dimensions: **Mass [M]**, **Length [L]**, and **Time [T]**. Thus, $k = 3$. #### Step 3: Number of Pi Groups By the Buckingham Pi Theorem: $$\pi = n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$$ So there are **3 dimensionless Pi groups**. #### Step 4: Choose Repeating Variables Choose $D$, $V$, and $\rho$ as the repeating variables (they together span all three dimensions M, L, T). #### Step 5: Form the Pi Groups **Pi 1 — using $\Delta p$:** $$\pi_1 = \Delta p \cdot D^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}$$ Writing dimensions: $$[M^0 L^0 T^0] = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}] \cdot [L]^a \cdot [L T^{-1}]^b \cdot [M L^{-3}]^c$$ Equating exponents of M, L, T: - M: $0 = 1 + c \Rightarrow c = -1$ - T: $0 = -2 - b \Rightarrow b = -2$ - L: $0 = -1 + a + b - 3c \Rightarrow 0 = -1 + a - 2 + 3 \Rightarrow a = 0$ Thus: $$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} \quad \text{(Pressure coefficient)}$$ **Pi 2 — using $L$:** $$\pi_2 = L \cdot D^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}$$ $$[M^0 L^0 T^0] = [L] \cdot [L]^a \cdot [L T^{-1}]^b \cdot [M L^{-3}]^c$$ - M: $0 = c \Rightarrow c = 0$ - T: $0 = -b \Rightarrow b = 0$ - L: $0 = 1 + a \Rightarrow a = -1$ Thus: $$\pi_2 = \frac{L}{D} \quad \text{(Geometric ratio)}$$ **Pi 3 — using $\mu$:** $$\pi_3 = \mu \cdot D^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}$$ $$[M^0 L^0 T^0] = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}] \cdot [L]^a \cdot [L T^{-1}]^b \cdot [M L^{-3}]^c$$ - M: $0 = 1 + c \Rightarrow c = -1$ - T: $0 = -1 - b \Rightarrow b = -1$ - L: $0 = -1 + a + b - 3c \Rightarrow 0 = -1 + a - 1 + 3 \Rightarrow a = -1$ Thus: $$\pi_3 = \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}} \quad \text{(Inverse Reynolds number)}$$ #### Step 6: The Functional Relationship The Buckingham Pi theorem gives the dimensionless relationship: $$\frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right)$$ This is the **canonical form of the pipe flow equation**, and $\dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \text{Re}$ is the **Reynolds number**. > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **KTU 14-mark favourite**. The examiner expects you to explicitly state the theorem, list the variables, identify repeating variables, and solve for each exponent with full working. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Velocity Starting from the definition of Reynolds number: $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$ Solving for $V$: $$V = \frac{\text{Re} \cdot \nu}{D}$$ For the **lower critical Reynolds number** $\text{Re}_c = 2000$: $$V_c = \frac{2000 \, \nu}{D}$$ For the **upper critical Reynolds number** $\text{Re}_c' = 4000$: $$V_c' = \frac{4000 \, \nu}{D}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Critical Discharge The discharge at the critical condition is: $$Q_c = V_c \cdot A = \frac{2000 \, \nu}{D} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ Simplifying: $$Q_c = \frac{\pi \cdot 2000 \cdot \nu \cdot D}{4} = 500 \, \pi \, \nu \, D$$ > [!TIP] > Memorize $Q_c = 500 \pi \nu D$ — this is the form most KTU numerical answers expect. ### 3.4 Derivation of the Darcy Friction Factor in Laminar Flow For laminar flow in a circular pipe, the Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the pressure drop: $$\Delta p = \frac{32 \mu V L}{D^2}$$ The **Darcy–Weisbach equation** is: $$h_f = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$ Equating the two: $$\frac{32 \mu V L}{D^2} = \frac{f L V^2 \rho}{2 D}$$ Solving for $f$: $$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{\text{Re}}$$ This is the famous result: **Darcy friction factor for laminar flow** is inversely proportional to Re. ### 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Type **Problem:** Oil with kinematic viscosity $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ flows through a horizontal circular pipe of diameter $D = 50 \ \text{mm}$ at a mean velocity of $V = 0.6 \ \text{m/s}$. Determine: 1. The Reynolds number 2. The flow regime 3. The critical velocity at which transition begins 4. The Darcy friction factor (if laminar) **Solution:** **Part 1: Reynolds Number** $$\text{Re} = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.05}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.03}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\text{Re} = 666.67$$ **Part 2: Flow Regime** Since $\text{Re} = 666.67 < 2000$, the flow is **Laminar**. **Part 3: Critical Velocity (lower limit)** $$V_c = \frac{2000 \, \nu}{D} = \frac{2000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = \frac{0.09}{0.05} = 1.8 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Part 4: Darcy Friction Factor** $$f = \frac{64}{\text{Re}} = \frac{64}{666.67} = 0.096$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Reynolds Experiment Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[OVERHEAD TANK
Constant Head] --> B[FLOW CONTROL VALVE
Inlet Regulation] B --> C[INLET STILLING CHAMBER
Flow Straightener] C --> D[TRANSPARENT GLASS TUBE
Test Section Diameter D] E[DYE RESERVOIR
Coloured Tracer] --> F[DYE INJECTION NEEDLE
Centreline Filament] F --> D D --> G[OUTLET CONTROL VALVE
Discharge Regulation] D --> H[PIEZOMETER MANOMETER
Head Loss Measurement] G --> I[VOLUMETRIC TANK
Discharge Q Measurement] H --> J[DATA RECORDING
Q, h_f, V Calculation] I --> J J --> K[REYNOLDS NUMBER COMPUTATION
Re equals rho V D by mu] K --> L{FLOW REGIME
CLASSIFICATION} L -->|Re less than 2000| M[LAMINAR FLOW
Dye streak straight] L -->|2000 to 4000| N[TRANSITIONAL FLOW
Dye oscillates] L -->|Re greater than 4000| O[TURBULENT FLOW
Dye fully diffused] ``` ### 4.2 Reynolds Number Flow Regime Map — Decision Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P[START: Compute Re equals V D by nu] --> Q{Re less than 2000} Q -->|YES| R[LAMINAR REGIME
f equals 64 by Re
Parabolic Velocity Profile] Q -->|NO| S{Re between 2000 and 4000} S -->|YES| T[TRANSITIONAL REGIME
Empirical Correlations
Moody Chart Entry] S -->|NO| U{Re greater than 4000} U -->|YES| V[TURBULENT REGIME
f from Colebrook or Moody
Flat Velocity Core] R --> W[END: Regime Classified] T --> W V --> W ``` ### 4.3 Visualisation of Dye Behaviour vs. Reynolds Number ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW_RE[Re less than 2000 LAMINAR] A1[Glass Tube Cross Section] --> A2[Dye Streak
STRAIGHT LINE
Parallel Layers] end subgraph MID_RE[2000 less than or equal to Re less than or equal to 4000 TRANSITIONAL] B1[Glass Tube Cross Section] --> B2[Dye Streak
WAVY UNSTABLE
Intermittent Bursts] end subgraph HIGH_RE[Re greater than 4000 TURBULENT] C1[Glass Tube Cross Section] --> C2[Dye Field
FULLY DIFFUSED
Random Eddies] end ``` ### 4.4 Force Ratio Interpretation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[REYNOLDS NUMBER Re] --> B[Inertial Forces F sub i] A --> C[Viscous Forces F sub v] B --> D{Re less than 1
Viscous Dominant
Stokes Flow} C --> D B --> E{Re around 1
Transition Zone
Matched Forces] C --> E B --> F{Re greater than 1
Inertial Dominant
Turbulent Tendency} C --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Mermaid diagrams above** are Block-Level Functional Architectures representing the physical setup and conceptual flow of the Reynolds experiment. Each node uses purely alphanumeric identifiers and clean uppercase labels without markdown formatting, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the significance of the Reynolds number in fluid flow. What are its typical values for laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow in a circular pipe?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Reynolds number $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ is a **dimensionless parameter** that represents the ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces** in a flowing fluid. Its significance: 1. It is the **sole criterion** (for a given geometry) to determine whether a flow is laminar or turbulent. 2. It enables **dynamic similarity** between model and prototype in scale experiments. 3. It governs the **frictional resistance** in pipe flow and **boundary layer behaviour** over surfaces. Typical values for circular pipe flow: - **Laminar flow:** $\text{Re} < 2000$ - **Transitional flow:** $2000 \le \text{Re} \le 4000$ - **Turbulent flow:** $\text{Re} > 4000$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define the upper and lower critical velocities. Why is there a range rather than a single value at which transition occurs?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **lower critical velocity** $V_c$ is the mean velocity corresponding to $\text{Re}_c = 2000$: $V_c = \dfrac{2000 \nu}{D}$. - The **upper critical velocity** $V_c'$ corresponds to $\text{Re}_c' = 4000$: $V_c' = \dfrac{4000 \nu}{D}$. A range exists because **transition is not an instantaneous or purely deterministic phenomenon**. It depends on: 1. The **disturbance level** in the inlet flow (free-stream turbulence intensity). 2. **Wall roughness** and surface imperfections. 3. **External vibrations** and acoustic noise in the apparatus. 4. The **history of the flow** (e.g., whether the flow has always been laminar or has been previously disturbed). In practice, the **lower critical Reynolds number (2000)** is treated as the engineering transition point because flows above it can no longer be guaranteed to remain laminar. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Reynolds experiment** to demonstrate laminar and turbulent flow in a pipe. State the observations and conclusions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **Buckingham Pi theorem**, derive the expression for the Reynolds number as a dimensionless group governing flow through a circular pipe. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Description of Reynolds Experiment (7 marks)** **Apparatus** (2 marks): The apparatus consists of: 1. A **constant-head overhead tank** supplying water under steady head. 2. A **horizontal transparent glass tube** of known diameter $D$ and length $L$. 3. A **flow-regulating valve** at the downstream end of the glass tube. 4. A **dye reservoir with injection needle** aligned along the centreline of the tube. 5. A **measuring tank** and **stopwatch** for discharge $Q$ measurement. 6. **Piezometer taps** to measure head loss $h_f$ across the test section. ``` +---------------------------+ | OVERHEAD CONSTANT HEAD | | TANK | +---------------------------+ | v +---------------------------+ | GLASS TRANSPARENT TUBE | | ----- | | |DYE| →→→→→→ | | ----- | +---------------------------+ | v +---------------------------+ | VOLUMETRIC MEASURING TANK| +---------------------------+ ``` **Procedure** (2 marks): 1. The tube is filled with water and steady flow is established at very low discharge. 2. Dye is injected through the needle into the centreline. 3. **At low velocity** — the dye forms a **straight, thin, unbroken filament** parallel to the tube axis. This is **Laminar Flow**. 4. The valve is opened progressively, increasing the velocity. 5. **At a critical velocity** — the dye begins to **wave and oscillate**. This is **Transitional Flow**. 6. **At higher velocity** — the dye streak **suddenly bursts** and the entire cross-section becomes coloured. This is **Turbulent Flow**. 7. For each regime, $Q$, $V = Q/A$, and $h_f$ are recorded, and Re is computed. **Observations and Conclusions** (3 marks): | Re Range | Flow Regime | Dye Behaviour | Velocity Profile | |---|---|---|---| | $< 2000$ | Laminar | Straight line | Parabolic (Hagen–Poiseuille) | | $2000$–$4000$ | Transitional | Wavy, unstable | Undefined | | $> 4000$ | Turbulent | Fully diffused | Flat core, thin viscous sublayer | **Conclusions:** 1. The flow regime depends on the **dimensionless Reynolds number** $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$. 2. In laminar flow, viscous forces dominate; in turbulent flow, inertial forces dominate. 3. The transition is governed by a **critical Re**, not by velocity alone. --- **(b) Buckingham Pi Derivation of Reynolds Number (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Variables and Dimensions** (1 mark): The pressure drop $\Delta p$ in a pipe depends on: $$\Delta p = f(D, L, V, \rho, \mu)$$ | Variable | Symbol | Dimensions | |---|---|---| | Pressure drop | $\Delta p$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-2}$ | | Pipe diameter | $D$ | $L$ | | Pipe length | $L$ | $L$ | | Mean velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ | | Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ | | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ | Total variables $n = 6$; fundamental dimensions $k = 3$; so $\pi$ groups $= 6 - 3 = 3$. **Step 2 — Choose Repeating Variables** (1 mark): Choose $D$, $V$, $\rho$ (they span M, L, T and exclude $\Delta p$ and $\mu$). **Step 3 — Form Pi Groups** (4 marks): **Pi 1** — with $\Delta p$: $$\pi_1 = \Delta p \cdot D^{a_1} V^{b_1} \rho^{c_1}$$ $$M^0 L^0 T^0 = (M L^{-1} T^{-2})(L)^{a_1} (L T^{-1})^{b_1} (M L^{-3})^{c_1}$$ Solving: - M: $0 = 1 + c_1 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1$ - T: $0 = -2 - b_1 \Rightarrow b_1 = -2$ - L: $0 = -1 + a_1 - 2 + 3 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$ $$\boxed{\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2}}$$ **Pi 2** — with $L$: $$\pi_2 = L \cdot D^{a_2} V^{b_2} \rho^{c_2}$$ Solving: $$\boxed{\pi_2 = \frac{L}{D}}$$ **Pi 3** — with $\mu$: $$\pi_3 = \mu \cdot D^{a_3} V^{b_3} \rho^{c_3}$$ Solving: - M: $0 = 1 + c_3 \Rightarrow c_3 = -1$ - T: $0 = -1 - b_3 \Rightarrow b_3 = -1$ - L: $0 = -1 + a_3 - 1 + 3 \Rightarrow a_3 = -1$ $$\boxed{\pi_3 = \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}}}$$ **Step 4 — Functional Relation** (1 mark): $$\frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right) = \phi\!\left(\frac{L}{D}, \text{Re}\right)$$ Hence, the **Reynolds number** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ is rigorously established as the governing dimensionless group. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Define the **Reynolds number** and derive expressions for the **lower and upper critical velocities** in terms of the pipe diameter and kinematic viscosity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An oil of kinematic viscosity $\nu = 3 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ flows through a pipe of diameter $D = 100 \ \text{mm}$. Determine: (i) the **lower critical velocity**; (ii) the **upper critical velocity**; (iii) the **Reynolds number** when the actual mean velocity is $V = 2 \ \text{m/s}$; and (iv) state the **flow regime**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Reynolds Number and Critical Velocities (7 marks)** **Definition** (2 marks): The **Reynolds number** $\text{Re}$ is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid: $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$ Where $\rho$ is density, $V$ is mean velocity, $D$ is pipe diameter, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is kinematic viscosity. **Derivation of Critical Velocities** (5 marks): By definition: $$V = \frac{\text{Re} \cdot \nu}{D}$$ The **lower critical Reynolds number** is $\text{Re}_c = 2000$ (the threshold below which flow is guaranteed laminar). The **upper critical Reynolds number** is $\text{Re}_c' = 4000$ (above which flow is guaranteed turbulent). **Lower critical velocity:** $$V_c = \frac{\text{Re}_c \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{2000 \, \nu}{D}$$ **Upper critical velocity:** $$V_c' = \frac{\text{Re}_c' \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{4000 \, \nu}{D}$$ **Physical Meaning:** Below $V_c$, viscous forces are strong enough to suppress any disturbance → flow remains laminar. Above $V_c'$, inertial forces overwhelm viscous damping → flow is turbulent. Between $V_c$ and $V_c'$, the flow regime is **sensitive to disturbances** and may be either. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)** **Given:** - Kinematic viscosity $\nu = 3 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ - Pipe diameter $D = 100 \ \text{mm} = 0.1 \ \text{m}$ - Mean velocity $V = 2 \ \text{m/s}$ **(i) Lower critical velocity** (2 marks): $$V_c = \frac{2000 \times 3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = \frac{0.06}{0.1} = 0.6 \ \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Upper critical velocity** (2 marks): $$V_c' = \frac{4000 \times 3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = \frac{0.12}{0.1} = 1.2 \ \text{m/s}$$ **(iii) Reynolds number at $V = 2 \ \text{m/s}$** (2 marks): $$\text{Re} = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 6666.67$$ **(iv) Flow regime** (1 mark): Since $\text{Re} = 6666.67 > 4000$, the flow is **Turbulent**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Reynolds Experiment Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if $D$ is left in mm and $\nu$ in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ without conversion. Always convert to SI (m, m²/s) before substituting. > 2. **Confusing dynamic and kinematic viscosity** — A wrong factor of $\rho$ in the formula loses **2 marks** instantly. For $\text{Re}$, use $\nu$ directly: $\text{Re} = V D / \nu$. > 3. **Writing the critical velocity as a single value** — KTU expects both **lower** ($2000$) and **upper** ($4000$) critical Re. A single value loses **1 mark**. > 4. **In Buckingham Pi problems, omitting the dimension matrix** — The examiner wants to see the **systematic equating of exponents**. Skipping this step costs **2–3 marks**. > 5. **Not sketching the apparatus** — In Part B (a), even a **block diagram** of the apparatus is worth at least **1 mark**. Always include one. > 6. **Confusing the Darcy friction factor formula** — In laminar flow, $f = 64 / \text{Re}$ is the **Darcy** form. The **Fanning** form is $f_{\text{Fanning}} = 16 / \text{Re}$. KTU uses the **Darcy** convention. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reynolds Experiment (Module 3)** - The Reynolds experiment was performed by **Osborne Reynolds in 1883** to demonstrate laminar vs. turbulent flow. - **Apparatus:** constant-head tank, transparent glass tube, dye injection needle, control valve, measuring tank, piezometer. - **Dye behaviour:** straight line (laminar) → wavy (transitional) → fully diffused (turbulent). - **Reynolds Number definition:** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$. - **Significance:** Ratio of **inertial to viscous forces**; governs flow regime. - **Critical values for pipe flow:** $\text{Re}_c = 2000$ (lower), $\text{Re}_c' = 4000$ (upper). - **Lower critical velocity:** $V_c = 2000 \nu / D$. - **Upper critical velocity:** $V_c' = 4000 \nu / D$. - **Critical discharge:** $Q_c = 500 \pi \nu D$. - **Laminar friction factor (Darcy):** $f = 64 / \text{Re}$. - **Buckingham Pi derivation** yields 3 Pi groups: $\Delta p / (\rho V^2)$, $L / D$, and $\mu / (\rho V D)$. - **Naval Architecture relevance:** Hull resistance scaling, boundary layer classification, propeller design, ship model testing similarity. - **Always use SI units:** $D$ in m, $\nu$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $V$ in m/s. - **Darcy (not Fanning) convention** is the KTU standard for friction factor. ---

topics.title: Reynolds Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reynolds Number (Re)** is a dimensionless quantity in fluid mechanics that quantifies the relative dominance of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid flow. It serves as a fundamental similarity parameter used to predict the flow regime — whether the flow will be **laminar**, **transitional**, or **turbulent**.

Mathematically, Reynolds Number is defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
- $V$ = Characteristic velocity of the flow $[\text{m/s}]$
- $L$ = Characteristic length (geometry-dependent) $[\text{m}]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid $[\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}]$
- $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = Kinematic viscosity $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Reynolds Number is the cornerstone of dimensional analysis in fluid mechanics. For Naval Architects, it governs the scaling laws for model testing in towing tanks, cavitation analysis, and boundary layer classification over ship hulls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a flowing river. When water moves slowly and smoothly (think of a quiet stream in a forest), the fluid particles glide past each other in an orderly, layered fashion. Now imagine a rapid river cascading over rocks — the water becomes chaotic, with swirls, eddies, and unpredictable mixing.

**What changed?** The **balance between two competing forces**:
- **Inertial forces** (the fluid's natural tendency to keep moving and resist changes in motion, governed by $\rho V^2 L^2$)
- **Viscous forces** (the fluid's internal "friction" that tries to keep things orderly, governed by $\mu V L$)

When **inertial forces dominate** (high Re), the flow becomes chaotic and turbulent. When **viscous forces dominate** (low Re), the flow remains smooth and laminar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of Reynolds Number as a "chaos meter" for fluids. 
> - **Low Re** (< 2300 in pipes) = Calm, disciplined, parade-like flow (Laminar)
> - **High Re** (> 4000 in pipes) = Rushing crowd, chaotic, with eddies (Turbulent)
> - **In between** = The transition zone where things start getting messy

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Key Metrics

| Property | Standard Symbol | Typical SI Unit |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Density of seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of freshwater | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Critical Re (pipe flow) | $Re_{cr}$ | **2300** (laminar-turbulent transition) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Reynolds Number Exists

Reynolds Number emerges from **dimensional analysis** of the **Navier-Stokes equations**. When Osborne Reynolds (1883) conducted his famous dye-injection experiments, he discovered that the flow pattern in a pipe depends not on any single variable, but on a specific **dimensionless combination** of them.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Context from Navier-Stokes

The incompressible Navier-Stokes equation is:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

Non-dimensionalizing this equation using characteristic scales $V_0$, $L$, and $T = L/V_0$ yields a dimensionless group:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V_0 L}{\mu}$$

This dimensionless group is the **only** parameter (along with geometric ratios) that governs the dynamic similarity of viscous flows.

## 2.2 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

1. **Step 1 — Inertial Force Identification**: In a fluid element of volume $L^3$ moving at velocity $V$, the inertial force scales as $F_i \sim \rho V^2 L^2$.

2. **Step 2 — Viscous Force Identification**: The shear stress $\tau \sim \mu V/L$ acting over area $L^2$ gives viscous force $F_v \sim \mu V L$.

3. **Step 3 — Forming the Ratio**: 
$$Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

4. **Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**:
   - **Re << 1**: Viscous forces dominate → creeping flow (e.g., micro-scale flows, lubrication)
   - **Re ~ 1**: Both forces comparable → transitional microscale phenomena
   - **Re >> 1**: Inertial forces dominate → turbulent flow (e.g., ship hulls, aircraft)

5. **Step 5 — Application to Naval Architecture**: For a ship of length $L$ moving at speed $V$, the Reynolds number typically ranges from **10⁷ to 10⁹**, indicating fully turbulent flow around the hull.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Standard Reynolds Number | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number using kinematic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Re_{pipe} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | For pipe flow (D = diameter) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Re_{plate} = \dfrac{\rho V x}{\mu}$ | For flat plate (x = distance from leading edge) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re_{ship} = \dfrac{V L_{pp}}{\nu}$ | For ship model testing (L_pp = length between perpendiculars) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_m = V_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_m}{L_s}\right)^{1/2}$ | Froude scaling (gravity similarity) | m/s |
| 7 | $V_m = V_s \cdot \dfrac{\nu_m}{\nu_s} \cdot \dfrac{L_s}{L_m}$ | Reynolds scaling (viscous similarity) | m/s |
| 8 | $Re_{cr} = 2300$ | Critical Re for pipe flow (laminar-turbulent) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$ | Critical Re for flat plate (laminar-turbulent boundary layer) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_f = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log Re - 2)^2}$ | ITTC friction line for turbulent plate (1978) | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Naval Architects:** Ship model testing involves TWO similarity requirements:
> - **Froude Number similarity** (gravity/wave effects)
> - **Reynolds Number similarity** (viscous effects)
> 
> These cannot be simultaneously satisfied with the same fluid (e.g., water), leading to the **Reynolds Number Froude Number scaling conflict** — a fundamental challenge in ship hydrodynamics.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Towing Tank Testing**: Predicting full-scale ship resistance from model tests
- **Boundary Layer Analysis**: Determining whether the boundary layer over a hull is laminar, transitional, or turbulent
- **Pipe Flow Design**: Sizing piping systems on ships for cooling water, fuel, bilge systems
- **Cavitation Prediction**: Reynolds Number affects the boundary layer thickness, which influences pressure distribution and cavitation inception on propellers
- **Scale Modeling**: Establishing dynamic similarity between model and prototype
- **Dimensional Analysis**: Reducing the number of variables in complex flow problems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Reynolds Number from Navier-Stokes

We begin with the **incompressible Navier-Stokes equation** in vector form:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 1: Expand the Material Derivative

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

Therefore the momentum equation becomes:

$$\rho \left[ \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} \right] = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 2: Define Non-Dimensional Variables

To extract the Reynolds Number, we non-dimensionalize all variables using characteristic scales. Let:

$$\vec{V}^* = \frac{\vec{V}}{V_0}, \quad t^* = \frac{t \, V_0}{L}, \quad \nabla^* = L \nabla, \quad p^* = \frac{p}{\rho V_0^2}$$

Where $V_0$ is the characteristic velocity and $L$ is the characteristic length.

### Step 3: Substitute Non-Dimensional Variables

For the **convective (inertial) term** $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$:

$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \frac{V_0^2}{L} (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*)\vec{V}^*$$

For the **viscous term** $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$:

$$\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} = \frac{\mu V_0}{L^2} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^*$$

### Step 4: Assemble the Non-Dimensional Equation

Dividing the entire equation by $\rho V_0^2 / L$:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*)\vec{V}^* = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{\mu}{\rho V_0 L} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{\vec{g} L}{V_0^2}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Dimensionless Groups

We can now read off the two key dimensionless groups:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V_0 L}{\mu} = \frac{V_0 L}{\nu}}$$

$$Fr = \frac{V_0}{\sqrt{g L}}$$

### Step 6: Final Non-Dimensional Form

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}^*}{\partial t^*} + (\vec{V}^* \cdot \nabla^*)\vec{V}^* = -\nabla^* p^* + \frac{1}{Re} \nabla^{*2} \vec{V}^* + \frac{1}{Fr^2} \hat{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The Reynolds Number appears naturally as the inverse coefficient of the viscous diffusion term. When $Re$ is very large (inertia dominates), the viscous term becomes negligible — this is the basis for **inviscid flow theory** used in initial hull form design.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reynolds Number Across Different Geometries

| Geometry | Characteristic Length $L$ | Typical Re Range | Flow Behavior |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Pipe (circular) | Diameter $D$ | $< 2300$ | Laminar |
| Pipe (circular) | Diameter $D$ | $2300 < Re < 4000$ | Transitional |
| Pipe (circular) | Diameter $D$ | $> 4000$ | Turbulent |
| Flat plate | Distance $x$ from LE | $< 5 \times 10^5$ | Laminar BL |
| Flat plate | Distance $x$ from LE | $> 5 \times 10^5$ | Turbulent BL |
| Sphere | Diameter $D$ | $Re < 1$ | Stokes flow |
| Sphere | Diameter $D$ | $1 < Re < 10^5$ | Attached flow |
| Sphere | Diameter $D$ | $Re > 3 \times 10^5$ | Super-critical (drag crisis) |
| Ship hull | Length $L_{pp}$ | $10^7 - 10^9$ | Fully turbulent |
| Submarine | Length $L$ | $10^8 - 10^{10}$ | Fully turbulent |
| Fish swimming | Length $L$ | $10^4 - 10^6$ | Transitional-turbulent |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Reynolds Number for a Naval Vessel

**Problem:** A frigate of length $L_{pp} = 120$ m moves at $V = 15$ knots in seawater at 20°C. Calculate the Reynolds Number.

**Given:**
- $L_{pp} = 120$ m
- $V = 15$ knots
- Seawater at 20°C: $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units**

$$V = 15 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s per knot} = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Reynolds Number formula**

$$Re = \frac{V L_{pp}}{\nu} = \frac{7.716 \times 120}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$V L_{pp} = 7.716 \times 120 = 925.92 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Compute final result**

$$Re = \frac{925.92}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.818 \times 10^{8}$$

**Conclusion:** The flow around the frigate is **fully turbulent**, with $Re \approx 8.82 \times 10^8$. This is typical for full-scale naval vessels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Reynolds Number Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Flow Problem] --> B{What is the Geometry?}
    B --> C[Pipe Flow]
    B --> D[Flat Plate]
    B --> E[Ship Hull]
    B --> F[Submerged Body]
    
    C --> C1[Use L = Diameter D]
    D --> D1[Use L = x distance from LE]
    E --> E1[Use L = Length between Perpendiculars]
    F --> F1[Use L = Characteristic body length]
    
    C1 --> G[Identify Fluid Properties]
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    
    G --> G1[Density rho]
    G --> G2[Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    G --> G3[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    
    G1 --> H[Compute Reynolds Number]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    
    H --> H1[Re = rho V L divided by mu]
    H --> H2[Re = V L divided by nu]
    
    H1 --> I{Compare to Critical Re}
    H2 --> I
    
    I --> I1[Re less than 2300: LAMINAR]
    I --> I2[2300 less than Re less than 4000: TRANSITIONAL]
    I --> I3[Re greater than 4000: TURBULENT]
    
    I1 --> J[Apply Laminar Flow Equations]
    I2 --> K[Apply Empirical Correlations]
    I3 --> L[Apply Turbulent Flow Equations]
    
    J --> M[END: Flow Regime Identified]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

    end

    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
