<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Turbulent Flat Plate Boundary Layer — Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Turbulent Boundary Layer** is the thin region adjacent to a solid surface (here, a flat plate) where the flow is **fully turbulent**, characterized by random, three-dimensional, fluctuating eddies superimposed on the time-averaged mean flow. For a flat plate of zero pressure gradient (ZPG), transition from laminar to turbulent flow occurs at a critical Reynolds number based on distance from the leading edge:

$$\boxed{Re_{x,\,crit} = \dfrac{\rho\, U_\infty\, x_{crit}}{\mu} = \dfrac{U_\infty\, x_{crit}}{\nu} \;\approx\; 5\times 10^{5}}$$

Below this value, the boundary layer is **laminar**; above it, turbulence sustains itself.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The River Beside the River"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a wide, slow river (the free stream, $U_\infty$). Now place a long, smooth barge parallel to the flow (the flat plate). Right beside the barge's hull, water is "stuck" to it by viscosity — this is the **laminar sublayer**, where flow is calm and orderly. Just above it, the water forms tiny whirlpools (eddies) that tumble chaotically, mixing slow fluid near the hull with fast fluid away from it — this is the **turbulent region**. The transition between these regions is not sharp; there is a thin **buffer layer** in between where both viscous and turbulent effects are equally important.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\rho$ | Density of fluid (sea-water for Naval Arch.) | $\mathbf{1025\;\text{kg/m}^3}$ (standard seawater) |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | $\mathbf{1.08\times 10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}}$ |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\mathbf{1.05\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}}$ |
| $U_\infty$ | Free-stream velocity | $\mathbf{m/s}$ |
| $\delta_{99}$ | Boundary layer thickness (99% $U_\infty$) | $\mathbf{m}$ |
| $Re_x$ | Local Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| $\tau_w$ | Wall shear stress | $\mathbf{N/m^2}$ (Pa) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student must be able to (i) derive expressions for boundary layer thickness $\delta$, (ii) compute skin-friction coefficient $C_f$, (iii) plot velocity profiles, and (iv) compare turbulent vs. laminar boundary layers quantitatively. This question is **almost guaranteed** to appear in the ESE Module 3 section.

---

## 1.2 Characteristics of a Turbulent Flat-Plate Boundary Layer

Unlike its laminar counterpart, a turbulent boundary layer possesses the following distinguishing features:

1. **Enhanced Momentum Transfer:** Chaotic eddies transport momentum far more efficiently than molecular viscosity alone. The BL grows much faster ($\delta \propto x^{4/5}$ vs. $\delta \propto x^{1/2}$ for laminar).
2. **Time-Averaged Velocity Profile:** Even though instantaneous flow is unsteady, the time-averaged profile $\bar{u}(y)$ is smooth and reproducible. The "1/7th Power Law" (Prandtl, 1925) is the canonical empirical fit:

$$\dfrac{\bar{u}}{U_\infty} = \left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/n}, \qquad n = 7 \;\text{(commonly used)}$$

3. **Three Sub-regions (inner region):**
   * **Viscous sublayer** ($0 \le y^+ \le 5$): viscous forces dominate, $\bar{u}^+ = y^+$
   * **Buffer layer** ($5 < y^+ \le 30$): viscous and turbulent stresses comparable
   * **Log-law region** ($30 < y^+ \le 400$): $\bar{u}^+ = \dfrac{1}{\kappa}\ln y^+ + B$, $\kappa \approx 0.41$, $B \approx 5.0$
4. **Higher Wall Shear Stress:** Although flow mixing reduces the near-wall velocity gradient in some sense, the **integrated momentum deficit is greater**, giving higher $C_f$ than laminar (at same $Re_x$).
5. **Pressure Gradient Sensitivity:** Turbulent BL tolerates adverse pressure gradients better (delays separation) — a critical consideration in **submarine hull** and **ship hull** design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Naval Architects, the turbulent boundary layer directly governs **frictional resistance (viscous drag)** of a ship's hull. The 1947 **ITTC Standard Ship Model Correlation Line** for frigate/ship resistance is essentially a turbulent $C_f$–$Re$ curve of the form $C_f = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$, derived from turbulent flat-plate theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3: Turbulent Flat Plate Boundary Layer — Section 2

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Analysis

The full analytical treatment proceeds in **three coupled stages**:

**Stage 1 — Postulate a Velocity Profile**
The integral method does **not** solve the Navier–Stokes equations pointwise; instead, a *plausible shape* for the time-averaged profile is assumed. The **1/7th Power Law** is chosen because (a) it fits experimental data for $10^6 \le Re_x \le 10^7$ within ~5%, (b) it is differentiable, integrable, and physically well-behaved ($u=0$ at $y=0$, $u=U_\infty$ at $y=\delta$).

**Stage 2 — Compute Integral Thicknesses**
With $\eta = y/\delta$ and $\zeta = (u/U_\infty)$, the displacement thickness $\delta^*$ and momentum thickness $\theta$ are integrals of the profile. The ratio $\delta^*/\theta = H$ (shape factor) is a single number that determines how "full" the profile is.

**Stage 3 — Apply von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation**
This integral relation is the workhorse for BL analysis:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$$

It expresses momentum conservation in the BL and converts a 2D PDE problem into an ODE in $\delta(x)$. The wall shear is **closed** by an empirical law — the "law of the wall" or the Blasius-type equivalent for turbulent flow: $C_f/2 \propto Re_\delta^{-1/4}$ (for the 1/7 power law).

### KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas. Each appears in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks) of the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Valid Range |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dfrac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$ | 1/7th power-law velocity profile | $10^6 \le Re_x \le 10^7$ |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{7}{72}\,\delta$ | Momentum thickness (n = 7) | Turbulent ZPG |
| 3 | $\delta^{*} = \dfrac{1}{8}\,\delta$ | Displacement thickness (n = 7) | Turbulent ZPG |
| 4 | $H = \dfrac{\delta^{*}}{\theta} = \dfrac{9}{7} \approx 1.286$ | Shape factor (n = 7) | Turbulent ZPG |
| 5 | $\delta = \dfrac{0.37\, x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Boundary layer thickness | $5\times 10^5 \le Re_x \le 10^7$ |
| 6 | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0576}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Local skin-friction coefficient | Same as above |
| 7 | $\overline{C}_f = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}$ | Average skin-friction coefficient | $Re_L < 10^7$ |
| 8 | $\tau_w = 0.0288\;\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\;Re_x^{-1/5}$ | Local wall shear stress | Turbulent ZPG |
| 9 | $\delta = \dfrac{0.16\, x}{Re_x^{1/7}}$ | BL thickness (1/9 power law) | $Re_x > 10^7$ |
| 10 | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0592}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Local skin friction (1/9 power law) | $Re_x > 10^7$ |
| 11 | $Re_x = \dfrac{U_\infty\, x}{\nu}$ | Local Reynolds number | Universal |
| 12 | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}}$ | von Kármán momentum integral | Universal (any BL) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Reminder for Tables:** When embedding absolute-value bars inside any formula row, always write them as `\vert \cdot \vert` rather than the literal pipe `|`. This prevents the markdown table from breaking. (KTU answer sheets use clean LaTeX; do the same in your written work.)

### Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Ship Hull Resistance:** The ITTC-1957 / ITTC-1978 frictional-resistance formulas are direct descendants of $C_f = 0.075/(\log_{10}Re - 2)^2$, derived from flat-plate turbulent BL theory. A naval architect computes $R_f = C_f \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_{wet}$ where $S_{wet}$ is the wetted surface area.
* **Submarine Sonar Domes:** Turbulent BL noise radiates acoustic signals; minimising $\tau_w$ and boundary layer fluctuations reduces the **self-noise** that masks incoming sonar echoes.
* **Aircraft Wings, Turbine Blades, Heat Exchangers:** Turbulent BL enhances convective heat transfer (good) but increases drag (bad). The trade-off is central to naval propulsion engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3: Turbulent Flat Plate Boundary Layer — Section 3

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is explicit. No "…hence…" or "similarly…" shortcuts are permitted in KTU board answers. Reproduce the same level of detail in your exam scripts.

### DERIVATION 1 — Momentum and Displacement Thickness for the 1/7th Power-Law Profile

**Step 1 — Define the power-law profile.** Adopt Prandtl's empirical velocity profile with exponent $n=7$:

$$\dfrac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the displacement-thickness integral.** By definition:

$$\delta^{*} = \int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the profile and non-dimensionalise.** Let $\eta = y/\delta$, so $dy = \delta\, d\eta$. Then:

$$\delta^{*} = \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\left[1 - \eta^{1/7}\right] d\eta$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral term by term.**

$$\int_{0}^{1}\!1\,d\eta = \big[\eta\big]_{0}^{1} = 1$$

$$\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{1/7}\,d\eta = \left[\dfrac{\eta^{8/7}}{8/7}\right]_{0}^{1} = \dfrac{7}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$\delta^{*} = \delta\left(1 - \dfrac{7}{8}\right) = \dfrac{\delta}{8}$$

**Step 6 — Set up the momentum-thickness integral.** By definition:

$$\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}\!\dfrac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{1/7}\left(1 - \eta^{1/7}\right) d\eta$$

**Step 7 — Expand the integrand.**

$$\eta^{1/7}\left(1 - \eta^{1/7}\right) = \eta^{1/7} - \eta^{2/7}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate each term.**

$$\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{1/7}\,d\eta = \dfrac{7}{8}$$

$$\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{2/7}\,d\eta = \left[\dfrac{\eta^{9/7}}{9/7}\right]_{0}^{1} = \dfrac{7}{9}$$

**Step 9 — Combine.**

$$\theta = \delta\left(\dfrac{7}{8} - \dfrac{7}{9}\right) = 7\delta\left(\dfrac{9-8}{72}\right) = \dfrac{7\delta}{72}$$

**Step 10 — Compute the shape factor.**

$$H = \dfrac{\delta^{*}}{\theta} = \dfrac{\delta/8}{7\delta/72} = \dfrac{72}{8\times 7} = \dfrac{9}{7} \approx 1.286$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with the **Blasius laminar** shape factor: $H_{laminar} = \delta^*/\theta \approx 2.59$. A turbulent profile is *fuller* (less curvature) than a laminar one — direct consequence of stronger mixing.

---

### DERIVATION 2 — Boundary-Layer Thickness $\delta(x)$ for the 1/7th Power Law

**Step 1 — Apply von Kármán momentum integral equation.**

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\,U_\infty^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Use the closure relation for turbulent wall shear stress (empirical).** For the 1/7 power-law profile, the equivalent of the Blasius relation is:

$$\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\,U_\infty^{2}} = 0.0225\, Re_\delta^{-1/4} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{U_\infty\,\delta}{\nu}\right)^{-1/4}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\theta$ in terms of $\delta$ using Derivation 1.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{7}{72}\,\delta \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{7}{72}\,\dfrac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the momentum equation.**

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\,\dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{-1/4}\delta^{-1/4}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $\delta$ and $x$.** Multiply both sides by $\delta^{1/4}$:

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\,\delta^{1/4}\,\dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 6 — Recognise the LHS as an exact derivative.**

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\cdot\dfrac{4}{5}\,\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\delta^{5/4}\right) = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

$$\dfrac{7}{90}\,\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\delta^{5/4}\right) = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 7 — Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $x$.**

$$\dfrac{7}{90}\,\delta^{5/4} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}\,x$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $\delta^{5/4}$.**

$$\delta^{5/4} = \dfrac{0.0225 \times 90}{7}\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}x = 0.2893\,x\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 9 — Raise both sides to the power $4/5$.**

$$\delta = (0.2893)^{4/5}\, x^{4/5}\,\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate the numerical prefactor.** $(0.2893)^{4/5}$:

$$\ln(0.2893) = -1.2404 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -1.2404 \times 0.8 = -0.9923 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e^{-0.9923} = 0.3707$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta(x) = \dfrac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;}$$

**Step 11 — Rewrite in terms of $Re_x$.** Since $Re_x = U_\infty x / \nu$:

$$\dfrac{\delta}{x} = 0.37\,Re_x^{-1/5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Turbulent BL grows as $x^{4/5}$ — substantially faster than laminar ($x^{1/2}$). At $Re_x = 10^7$, $\delta/x \approx 0.37 \times (10^7)^{-1/5} \approx 0.037$ (3.7% of plate length).

---

### DERIVATION 3 — Local Skin-Friction Coefficient $C_{f,x}$

**Step 1 — Express $Re_\delta$ in terms of $Re_x$.** Using $\delta = 0.37 x / Re_x^{1/5}$:

$$Re_\delta = \dfrac{U_\infty\,\delta}{\nu} = \dfrac{U_\infty}{\nu}\cdot\dfrac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}} = 0.37\,\dfrac{Re_x}{Re_x^{1/5}} = 0.37\,Re_x^{4/5}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the empirical closure.**

$$\dfrac{C_f}{2} = 0.0225\,Re_\delta^{-1/4} = 0.0225\,(0.37)^{-1/4}\,Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerical factor.** $(0.37)^{-1/4}$:

$$\ln(0.37) = -0.9943 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (-0.9943)(-0.25) = 0.2486 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e^{0.2486} = 1.2822$$

$$\dfrac{C_f}{2} = 0.0225 \times 1.2822 \times Re_x^{-1/5} = 0.02885\,Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2 to obtain $C_f$.**

$$\boxed{\;C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0577}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;}$$

---

### DERIVATION 4 — Average Skin-Friction Coefficient $\overline{C_f}$ over a Plate of Length $L$

**Step 1 — Definition of average skin-friction coefficient.**

$$\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{1}{L}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!C_{f,x}\,dx = \dfrac{0.0577}{L}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!Re_x^{-1/5}\,dx$$

**Step 2 — Express $Re_x^{-1/5}$ in terms of $x$.**

$$Re_x^{-1/5} = \left(\dfrac{U_\infty\,x}{\nu}\right)^{-1/5} = \left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}\,x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate.**

$$\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{0.0577}{L}\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!x^{-1/5}\,dx = \dfrac{0.0577}{L}\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}\!\left[\dfrac{x^{4/5}}{4/5}\right]_{0}^{L}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits.**

$$\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{0.0577}{L}\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}\cdot\dfrac{5}{4}\cdot L^{4/5} = \dfrac{5}{4}\cdot 0.0577\cdot\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty\,L}\right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise $Re_L = U_\infty L/\nu$.**

$$\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{5}{4}\cdot 0.0577\cdot Re_L^{-1/5} = 0.0721\,Re_L^{-1/5}$$

$$\boxed{\;\overline{C_f} \approx \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}\;}$$

(The slight discrepancy from $0.0721$ to the textbook $0.074$ arises because we used the local constant $0.0577$; the canonical ITTC value is $0.074$.)

---

### DERIVATION 5 — Wall Shear Stress $\tau_w$ and Drag Force $D_f$

**Step 1 — Wall shear stress from skin friction.**

$$\tau_w(x) = \dfrac{C_{f,x}}{2}\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2} = \dfrac{0.0577}{2}\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\,Re_x^{-1/5} = 0.0288\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\,Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 2 — Drag on a plate of width $b$ and length $L$ (one side wetted):**

$$D_f = \int_{0}^{L}\!\tau_w(x)\cdot b\,dx = 0.0288\,b\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!Re_x^{-1/5}\,dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$D_f = 0.0288\,b\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\cdot\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5}\cdot\dfrac{5}{4}\cdot L^{4/5}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $\overline{C_f}$ and plate area $A = bL$.**

$$\boxed{\;D_f = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\cdot A\cdot\overline{C_f}\;}$$

This is the standard drag equation $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 C_D A$, with $C_D \to \overline{C_f}$ for a flat plate.

---

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Model)

> **Problem:** A flat plate of length $L = 5\;\text{m}$ and width $b = 1\;\text{m}$ is towed in seawater at $U_\infty = 5\;\text{m/s}$. Given $\rho = 1025\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Assume the BL is fully turbulent from the leading edge. Compute $\delta$, $C_f$, $\tau_w$, and $D_f$.

**Solution Outline:**

1. $Re_L = \dfrac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \dfrac{5 \times 5}{1.05\times 10^{-6}} = 2.38 \times 10^{7}$ — turbulent regime.
2. $\delta = \dfrac{0.37 L}{Re_L^{1/5}} = \dfrac{0.37 \times 5}{(2.38 \times 10^{7})^{0.2}}$

$$(2.38 \times 10^{7})^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(2.38\times 10^{7})} = e^{0.2 \times 17.286} = e^{3.457} = 31.7$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{1.85}{31.7} = 0.0584\;\text{m} = 58.4\;\text{mm}$$

3. $\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}} = \dfrac{0.074}{31.7} = 2.33 \times 10^{-3}$
4. $\tau_w(L) = 0.0288 \rho U_\infty^{2} Re_L^{-1/5} = 0.0288 \times 1025 \times 25 \times \dfrac{1}{31.7} = 23.3\;\text{Pa}$
5. $D_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} \cdot bL \cdot \overline{C_f} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 25 \times 5 \times 2.33\times 10^{-3} = 149\;\text{N}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Module 3: Turbulent Flat Plate Boundary Layer — Section 4

## 4.1 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A — Internal Structure of a Turbulent Boundary Layer (Wall-Normal Layering)

The figure below shows the wall-normal ($y^+$) subdivision of a turbulent BL, distinguishing the viscous sublayer, buffer layer, log-law region, and outer (wake) region. Each layer's characteristic velocity law is annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Freestream U_infty Region]:::outerLayer
    B["Outer Wake Layer (y / delta greater than 0.2): Velocity Deficit Law"]:::outerLayer
    C["Log Law Region (y plus 30 to 400): u plus equals 2.44 ln y plus plus 5.0"]:::logLayer
    D["Buffer Layer (y plus 5 to 30): Both Viscous and Turbulent Shear Active"]:::bufferLayer
    E["Viscous Sublayer (y plus 0 to 5): u plus equals y plus Linear Viscous"]:::viscousLayer
    F["Wall y equals 0, no slip boundary tau_w is maximum"]:::wall

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F

    subgraph ScaleAnnotations["Wall Units Definition"]
        G["y plus equals y u_tau over nu, where u_tau equals sqrt of tau_w over rho"]:::annot
    end

    classDef outerLayer fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b2545
    classDef logLayer fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#5d3a00
    classDef bufferLayer fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a1411
    classDef viscousLayer fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#0e3b1e
    classDef wall fill:#34495e,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef annot fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:1px,color:#5d4406
```

### Diagram B — Block-Level Flow Architecture for Turbulent BL Analysis Pipeline

This block diagram captures the **engineering workflow** a Naval Architect follows when computing the turbulent BL resistance of a hull form, from free-stream conditions to drag force.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Input: U_infinity, L, b, rho, nu, transition point x_crit"]:::input
    BLOCK1["Compute Re_L = U_infinity L over nu"]:::compute
    BLOCK2A["If Re_L below 5e5: Laminar Block"]:::laminar
    BLOCK2B["If Re_L above 5e5: Turbulent Block"]:::turbulent
    BLOCK3["Adopt 1/7 Power Law Profile: u over U_infinity = y over delta to the 1 over 7"]:::profile
    BLOCK4["Compute theta = 7 delta over 72 and delta star = delta over 8"]:::thickness
    BLOCK5["Apply von Karman Momentum Integral: d theta over dx = tau_w over rho U_infinity squared"]:::momentum
    BLOCK6["Close with Empirical Cf over 2 = 0.0225 Re_delta to minus 1 over 4"]:::closure
    BLOCK7["Integrate ODE to obtain delta equals 0.37 x over Re_x to 1 over 5"]:::integrate
    BLOCK8["Compute Local Cf_x = 0.0577 over Re_x to 1 over 5"]:::cfLocal
    BLOCK9["Integrate along L to obtain Cf_bar = 0.074 over Re_L to 1 over 5"]:::cfAvg
    BLOCK10["Compute Drag D_f = 1 over 2 rho U_infinity squared A Cf_bar"]:::drag
    OUTPUT["Output: delta, Cf_x, tau_w, D_f, Resistance Plot"]:::output

    INPUT --> BLOCK1
    BLOCK1 --> BLOCK2A
    BLOCK1 --> BLOCK2B
    BLOCK2B --> BLOCK3
    BLOCK3 --> BLOCK4
    BLOCK4 --> BLOCK5
    BLOCK5 --> BLOCK6
    BLOCK6 --> BLOCK7
    BLOCK7 --> BLOCK8
    BLOCK8 --> BLOCK9
    BLOCK9 --> BLOCK10
    BLOCK10 --> OUTPUT

    classDef input fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#9a7d0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a2f04
    classDef compute fill:#eaf2f8,stroke:#21618c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0a2638
    classDef laminar fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:1px,color:#0e3b1e
    classDef turbulent fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a1411
    classDef profile fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:1px,color:#2d0f3a
    classDef thickness fill:#e8f8f5,stroke:#117a65,stroke-width:1px,color:#063d33
    classDef momentum fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a,stroke-width:1px,color:#3a2f04
    classDef closure fill:#fdedec,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:1px,color:#4a1411
    classDef integrate fill:#ebf5fb,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:1px,color:#0b2545
    classDef cfLocal fill:#eafaf1,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:1px,color:#0e3b1e
    classDef cfAvg fill:#f5eef8,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:1px,color:#2d0f3a
    classDef drag fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#3d3003
    classDef output fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5278,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a2638
```

### Diagram C — Velocity Profile Comparison: Laminar vs. Turbulent

A schematic representation of the **relative fullness** of the two profiles at equal $Re_x$:

| Profile Type | Shape | Approximate Equation | $H = \delta^*/\theta$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Laminar (Blasius) | Concave, narrow near wall | $u/U_\infty \propto \sin(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\cdot y/\delta)$ | $\approx 2.59$ |
| Turbulent (1/7 power) | Fuller, broader wake | $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ | $\approx 1.29$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** A *smaller* $H$ means the profile is "fuller" (closer to top-hat). Turbulent mixing pulls fast fluid inward, reducing the velocity deficit near the wall. This fuller profile is exactly why turbulent BLs are **less susceptible to separation** than laminar BLs under adverse pressure gradients — a critical fact for designing ship appendages (rudders, struts) that operate at small angles of attack.

# Module 3: Turbulent Flat Plate Boundary Layer — Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose 2-3 marks by (i) confusing $Re_x$ (local) with $Re_L$ (global) — they have *different* exponents in different formulas, (ii) forgetting to multiply by 2 when going from $C_f/2$ to $C_f$, and (iii) writing $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ but then integrating as if the exponent were $1/2$ (Blasius!). Always re-derive your profile substitution on the answer script.

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Distinguish between laminar and turbulent boundary layers on a flat plate. Compare their velocity profiles and growth rates."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Laminar BL | Turbulent BL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic-like, $u \propto y$, narrow near wall | Fuller, $u \propto y^{1/7}$, broader wake |
| Thickness growth | $\delta \propto x^{1/2}$ | $\delta \propto x^{4/5}$ |
| Wall shear stress | Lower | Higher (at same $Re_x$) |
| Shape factor $H$ | $\approx 2.59$ | $\approx 1.29$ |
| Pressure gradient tolerance | Poor — separates early | Good — delays separation |
| Transition | $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$ | $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$ |

*Marking split (KTU):* 1 mark each for (i) growth-rate difference, (ii) shape-factor difference, (iii) one engineering implication.

---

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"For a turbulent flat-plate boundary layer with the 1/7th power-law profile, show that the displacement thickness is $\delta^* = \delta/8$."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\delta^{*} = \int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\left[1 - \eta^{1/7}\right] d\eta$$

$$= \delta\left[1 - \dfrac{7}{8}\right] = \boxed{\dfrac{\delta}{8}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

*Marking split:* 1 mark for setup, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for evaluation.

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> **KTU Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Internal choice** is provided between Question A and Question B. Both are independent problems.

---

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Choice 1]`

**A flat plate of length $L = 4\;\text{m}$ and width $b = 1.5\;\text{m}$ is subjected to a free-stream flow of seawater at $U_\infty = 6\;\text{m/s}$. Given $\rho = 1025\;\text{kg/m}^3$ and $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, assume the boundary layer is fully turbulent from the leading edge. Using the 1/7th power-law velocity profile:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the boundary layer thickness $\delta(x)$ and compute $\delta$ at $x = L$ and at $x = L/2$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the local skin-friction coefficient $C_{f,x}$ and compute the total frictional drag $D_f$ on one side of the plate.

---

##### Model Solution A

**Part (a) — Derivation of $\delta(x)$ and Computation:**

**Step 1.** Apply von Kármán momentum integral equation:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}}$$

**Step 2.** For the 1/7 power law:

$$\theta = \dfrac{7\delta}{72}, \qquad \delta^{*} = \dfrac{\delta}{8}$$

**Step 3.** Empirical closure relation (analogous to Blasius):

$$\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}} = 0.0225\,Re_\delta^{-1/4}$$

**Step 4.** Substituting and separating variables:

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\,\delta^{1/4}\,\dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\cdot\dfrac{4}{5}\,\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\delta^{5/4}\right) = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 5.** Integrate from $0$ to $x$:

$$\dfrac{7}{90}\,\delta^{5/4} = 0.0225\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}x$$

$$\delta^{5/4} = 0.2893\,x\left(\dfrac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 6.** Raise to power $4/5$:

$$\boxed{\;\delta(x) = \dfrac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;}$$

**Step 7.** Numerical evaluation.

$$Re_L = \dfrac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \dfrac{6 \times 4}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.286 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\delta(L) = \dfrac{0.37 \times 4}{(2.286\times 10^{7})^{0.2}} = \dfrac{1.48}{31.3} = 0.0473\;\text{m} = 47.3\;\text{mm}$$

$$Re_{L/2} = 1.143 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\delta(L/2) = \dfrac{0.37 \times 2}{(1.143 \times 10^{7})^{0.2}} = \dfrac{0.74}{25.7} = 0.0288\;\text{m} = 28.8\;\text{mm}$$

**Part (b) — $C_{f,x}$ and Total Drag:**

**Step 1.** Compute $Re_\delta$ from $\delta(L)$:

$$Re_\delta(L) = \dfrac{U_\infty \delta(L)}{\nu} = \dfrac{6 \times 0.0473}{1.05\times 10^{-6}} = 2.70 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2.** Local $C_f$ at $L$:

$$C_{f,x}(L) = \dfrac{0.0577}{Re_L^{1/5}} = \dfrac{0.0577}{31.3} = 1.84 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3.** Average $C_f$ over plate:

$$\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}} = \dfrac{0.074}{31.3} = 2.36 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4.** Total frictional drag (one side, area $A = bL = 6\;\text{m}^2$):

$$D_f = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, U_\infty^{2}\,A\,\overline{C_f} = \dfrac{1}{2}\times 1025 \times 36 \times 6 \times 2.36\times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{D_f \approx 261\;\text{N}}$$

*Marking split (Part a):* 3 marks derivation, 2 marks formula substitution, 2 marks numerical computation. *Marking split (Part b):* 3 marks derivation, 2 marks formula substitution, 2 marks numerical evaluation.

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Choice 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the von Kármán momentum integral equation, derive the relationship between the local skin-friction coefficient $C_{f,x}$ and the local Reynolds number $Re_x$ for a turbulent flat plate assuming the 1/7th power-law velocity profile. State the assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ship model of length $L = 6\;\text{m}$ is tested in a towing tank at $U_\infty = 3\;\text{m/s}$. The wetted surface area is $S_{wet} = 8\;\text{m}^2$. Using the ITTC-1957 frictional formula $\overline{C_f} = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re_L - 2)^2$, compute the frictional resistance and compare it with the value obtained from the simple $0.074/Re_L^{1/5}$ formula. Take $\rho = 998\;\text{kg/m}^3$ and $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

##### Model Solution B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions stated (1 mark):**
1. Zero pressure gradient (flat plate).
2. Steady, incompressible flow.
3. Fully turbulent boundary layer from the leading edge.
4. 1/7th power-law profile is valid ($10^6 \le Re_x \le 10^7$).
5. Constant fluid properties.

**Step 1.** von Kármán momentum integral equation:

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}}$$

**Step 2.** Substituting $\theta = (7/72)\delta$:

$$\dfrac{7}{72}\,\dfrac{d\delta}{dx} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}}$$

**Step 3.** Empirical relation from the 1/7 power law (matching the Blasius-type result):

$$\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}} = 0.0225\,Re_\delta^{-1/4}$$

**Step 4.** Substituting $\delta = 0.37\,x\,Re_x^{-1/5}$ (derived in part-a), one obtains:

$$Re_\delta = 0.37\,Re_x^{4/5}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho\, U_\infty^{2}} = 0.0225\,(0.37)^{-1/4}\,Re_x^{-1/5} = 0.0288\,Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 6.** Since $\tau_w = (C_f/2)\rho U_\infty^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\;C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0577}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;} \quad \text{(valid for } 5\times 10^5 \le Re_x \le 10^7\text{)}$$

**Part (b) — ITTC vs. 1/7 Power-Law Comparison:**

**Step 1.** Compute $Re_L$:

$$Re_L = \dfrac{3 \times 6}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.8 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 2.** ITTC-1957 formula:

$$\log_{10}(1.8\times 10^{7}) = 7.255$$

$$\overline{C_f}^{ITTC} = \dfrac{0.075}{(7.255 - 2)^2} = \dfrac{0.075}{27.63} = 2.715 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3.** 1/7 Power-law formula:

$$\overline{C_f}^{PL} = \dfrac{0.074}{(1.8\times 10^{7})^{0.2}} = \dfrac{0.074}{29.81} = 2.482 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4.** Frictional resistance (ITTC):

$$R_f^{ITTC} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} S_{wet} \overline{C_f}^{ITTC} = 0.5 \times 998 \times 9 \times 8 \times 2.715\times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_f^{ITTC} = 97.5\;\text{N}$$

**Step 5.** Frictional resistance (Power-law):

$$R_f^{PL} = 0.5 \times 998 \times 9 \times 8 \times 2.482 \times 10^{-3} = 89.1\;\text{N}$$

**Step 6.** Percentage difference:

$$\Delta\% = \dfrac{97.5 - 89.1}{89.1} \times 100\% \approx 9.4\%$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The ITTC formula predicts ~9% higher resistance because it is empirically fitted to turbulent ship-model and full-scale data, including surface roughness effects that the ideal 1/7 power law does not capture. Naval architects prefer the ITTC line for **predicting full-scale frictional resistance** because it implicitly accounts for the **form factor** and roughness.

*Marking split (Part a):* 1 mark assumptions, 3 marks derivation steps, 3 marks final relation. *Marking split (Part b):* 3 marks ITTC computation, 3 marks power-law computation, 1 mark physical comparison.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

* **Critical Reynolds Number:** $Re_{x,crit} = 5 \times 10^5$ for transition on a smooth flat plate. Below this: laminar Blasius. Above this: turbulent 1/7 power law (for $Re_x \le 10^7$).
* **1/7 Power Law:** $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$. Memorise the exponents; they govern every integral.
* **Memorise the Three "Magic Numbers":** $\theta/\delta = 7/72$, $\delta^*/\delta = 1/8$, $H = 9/7 \approx 1.286$.
* **Boundary Layer Growth:** $\delta/x = 0.37\,Re_x^{-1/5}$ (turbulent) vs. $\delta/x = 5.0\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ (laminar).
* **Local Skin Friction:** $C_{f,x} = 0.0577/Re_x^{1/5}$ (turbulent) vs. $C_{f,x} = 0.664/Re_x^{1/2}$ (laminar Blasius).
* **Average Skin Friction:** $\overline{C_f} = 0.074/Re_L^{1/5}$.
* **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w = (C_f/2)\rho U_\infty^2$. To get drag, integrate $\tau_w$ over wetted area: $D_f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 A \overline{C_f}$.
* **Higher $Re_x$ Range ($Re_x > 10^7$):** Use the **1/9 power-law** with $\delta/x = 0.16\,Re_x^{-1/7}$ and $C_{f,x} = 0.0592/Re_x^{1/5}$ — same $C_f$ exponent but different prefactor.
* **Engineering Wisdom:** Turbulent BL = higher drag but **delayed separation** → better for ship rudders, hydrofoils, and aircraft wings operating at moderate angles of attack.
* **Ship Application:** For full-scale frictional resistance predictions, use the **ITTC-1957 line**: $\overline{C_f} = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$. For academic KTU problems, the **1/7 power-law formula** is preferred.
* **Most Common Mistake:** Using $Re_L$ where $Re_x$ is required, or vice versa. Always read the problem statement for what "$x$" refers to.
* **Common Sign Error:** $\theta = 7\delta/72$, NOT $72\delta/7$. The smaller fraction goes in the denominator.
* **Unit Consistency:** $\nu$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $U$ in $\text{m/s}$, $x$ in $\text{m}$ → $Re$ is dimensionless. Always check.
* **Naval Architecture Connection:** The turbulent flat-plate theory underpins the **frictional resistance** component of a ship's total resistance ($R_T = R_f + R_w + R_{form} + R_{app}$). Without this, you cannot solve a single hull-resistance problem in naval architecture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
