> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Momentum Integral Technique for Boundary Layers on Flat Plate ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Momentum Integral Technique (von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation):** > An approximate, **integral (control-volume) approach** to solving the **2-D steady incompressible laminar boundary-layer equations** on a **flat plate with zero pressure gradient**, in which an *assumed* velocity-profile shape is substituted into the integrated momentum conservation law to obtain closed-form expressions for the **boundary-layer thickness δ(x)**, **wall shear stress τ_w(x)**, and the **local skin-friction coefficient C_f(x)** — without solving the full Prandtl differential equations point-by-point. The governing integrated equation for a flat plate (zero pressure gradient, dU/dx = 0) reduces to the elegant one-line form: $$ \frac{\tau_w}{\rho \, U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{d\theta}{dx} $$ where **θ** is the **momentum thickness** — defined rigorously as: $$ \theta \;\equiv\; \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{u}{U}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U}\right) dy $$ This momentum loss across the boundary layer is the *single scalar quantity* that the technique tracks, instead of the full velocity field u(x,y). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sled on Snow" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: The Braking Sled** > > Imagine a **sled sliding across a flat snow field at speed U**. The runners touch the snow at a thin strip of width δ — this is the *boundary layer*. The snow in contact with the runners is essentially stationary (no-slip), while snow farther up is moving freely. The sled decelerates because of the *momentum deficit* carried by the slowly-moving snow layer near the surface. > > The **momentum thickness θ** is, intuitively, *how thick an equivalent layer of completely-stilled fluid would have to be to represent the total loss of momentum*. A bigger θ means bigger "drag-equivalent loss". > > The **momentum integral technique** says: *we don't need to know the exact velocity at every snow particle; we only need a reasonable guess of the profile shape* (linear, parabolic, cubic, etc.), plug it in, and the integrated momentum balance gives us δ(x) in one integration step. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---| | Free-stream velocity | **U** | 1 – 50 m/s (subsonic naval flows) | | Kinematic viscosity (water, 20 °C) | **ν** | 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s | | Boundary-layer thickness | **δ** | 0.1 – 50 mm (laminar) | | Momentum thickness | **θ** | ≈ δ / 7 to δ / 5 | | Displacement thickness | **δ*** | ≈ δ / 3 | | Local skin-friction coefficient | **C_f** | 0.5 / √Re_x to 1.5 / √Re_x | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Reasoning Behind the von Kármán Equation - The Prandtl boundary-layer equations are exact but **partial differential equations** — solving them analytically (Blasius similarity solution) requires advanced mathematics. - For many engineering design questions (ship hulls, submarines, hydrofoils), we **only need integral quantities** like total drag. - von Kármán (1921) proposed: integrate the x-momentum equation across the boundary layer thickness δ in the y-direction. The result is one **ordinary differential equation** in θ(x). - We sacrifice pointwise accuracy of u(x,y) but **gain a tractable, closed-form engineering tool**. ### 2.2 Why Choose a Velocity-Profile Assumption? > [!IMPORTANT] > The accuracy of the momentum-integral solution depends *entirely* on how well the assumed profile approximates reality. A profile that satisfies the boundary conditions at y = 0 and y = δ, and ideally also τ_w and ∂²u/∂y² at the wall, gives better results. Boundary conditions that **must** be satisfied: 1. No-slip: u(0) = 0 2. Matching free-stream: u(δ) = U 3. Smooth merging: ∂u/∂y \mid_{y=δ} = 0 4. Wall shear stress (sometimes): ∂u/∂y \mid_{y=0} = τ_w/μ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.** | # | Equation | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}\dfrac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\dfrac{u}{U}\right)dy$ | Definition of **momentum thickness** | | 2 | $\delta^{*}=\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(1-\dfrac{u}{U}\right)dy$ | Definition of **displacement thickness** | | 3 | $H=\delta^{*}/\theta$ | **Shape factor** (≈ 2.0 – 2.6 laminar flat plate) | | 4 | $\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}}=\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ | **von Kármán equation** (ZPG flat plate) | | 5 | $C_f=\dfrac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}}$ | **Local skin-friction coefficient** | | 6 | $C_D=\dfrac{1}{L}\!\int_{0}^{L}\!C_f\,dx$ | **Average drag coefficient** (plate of length L) | | 7 | $Re_x=\dfrac{Ux}{\nu}$ | **Local Reynolds number** | | 8 | $\tau_w=\mu\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\Big\vert_{y=0}$ | **Newton's law of viscosity** at the wall | | 9 | $\delta=\dfrac{c\, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | **General BL thickness form** (c is profile-dependent constant) | | 10 | $C_f=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | **General local C_f form** (k is profile-dependent constant) | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture | Naval Application | Why this technique is used | |---|---| | **Ship-hull resistance estimation** | Quick estimate of frictional drag without CFD | | **Submarine sail / fin design** | Boundary-layer growth dictates stall onset | | **Turbomachinery blade cascades** | Rapid design iteration of blade profiles | | **Cavitation prediction** | δ near hull surface determines pressure-gradient influence | | **Biofouling & roughness** | δ sets the "active" layer for marine-growth drag penalties | The **momentum-thickness Reynolds number Re_θ** computed from this technique is the modern bridge to **transition prediction** (e.g., eⁿ method, Langtry–Menter SST correlation) used in ship CFD codes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation (ZPG Flat Plate) Consider a 2-D control volume bounded by: - Inlet at station x (height δ) - Outlet at station x + dx (height δ + dδ) - Lower wall y = 0 - Upper streamline y = δ (approximately, by definition) **Conservation of mass (continuity):** Mass flow in at x: $\dot m_{in}=\int_{0}^{\delta}\rho u\,dy$ Mass flow out at x+dx: $\dot m_{out}=\int_{0}^{\delta+d\delta}\rho u\,dy$ Net outflow per unit width: $\rho \, \dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\delta}u\,dy\right]dx$ **Conservation of x-momentum:** Momentum flux out − in = (pressure forces) + (wall shear forces) After careful bookkeeping (assuming uniform pressure p = p_∞ everywhere on top and bottom of CV — i.e., zero pressure gradient), one obtains: $$ \frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{d\theta}{dx} $$ where $\theta \equiv \int_{0}^{\delta}(u/U)(1-u/U)\,dy$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Zero Pressure Gradient"?** On a flat plate aligned with the flow, by Bernoulli's equation the free-stream velocity U is **constant**, hence dU/dx = 0. The general von Kármán equation for a *non-flat surface* is $\tau_w/(\rho U^2) = d\theta/dx + (\delta^*/U)(dU/dx)$; the second term vanishes here. --- ### 3.2 Case Study A — Linear Velocity Profile **Assume:** $u/U = y/\delta = \eta$ (a straight line) #### Step 1 — Compute θ $$ \theta \;=\; \int_{0}^{\delta}\eta(1-\eta)\,dy \;=\; \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}(\eta-\eta^{2})\,d\eta $$ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\left[\frac{\eta^{2}}{2}-\frac{\eta^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1} \;=\; \delta\!\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right) \;=\; \frac{\delta}{6} $$ #### Step 2 — Compute wall shear stress $$ \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0} \;=\; \frac{U}{\delta} $$ $$ \tau_w \;=\; \mu\,\frac{U}{\delta} $$ #### Step 3 — Substitute into von Kármán equation $$ \frac{\mu U}{\rho U^{2}\,\delta} \;=\; \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{6}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{6}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ $$ \frac{\nu}{U\delta} \;=\; \frac{1}{6}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ #### Step 4 — Integrate with B.C. δ = 0 at x = 0 $$ \delta\,d\delta \;=\; \frac{6\nu}{U}\,dx \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\delta^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{6\nu x}{U} $$ $$ \delta^{2} \;=\; \frac{12\nu x}{U} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\delta \;=\; 3.464\,x\,Re_x^{-1/2}\;} $$ #### Step 5 — Compute C_f $$ C_f \;=\; \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2\nu}{U\delta} \;=\; \frac{2}{3.464\,\sqrt{Re_x}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;C_f \;=\; \frac{0.578}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;} $$ --- ### 3.3 Case Study B — Parabolic Velocity Profile **Assume:** $u/U = 2\eta - \eta^{2}$ #### Step 1 — Compute θ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}(2\eta-\eta^{2})(1-2\eta+\eta^{2})\,d\eta $$ Expanding the integrand: $$ (2\eta-\eta^{2})(1-2\eta+\eta^{2}) \;=\; 2\eta - 5\eta^{2} + 4\eta^{3} - \eta^{4} $$ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\left[1 - \frac{5}{3} + 1 - \frac{1}{5}\right]\delta \quad\text{(after integration)} \;\;=\; \frac{2\delta}{15} $$ #### Step 2 — Compute τ_w $$ \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0} \;=\; \frac{U}{\delta}\!\left.\frac{d}{d\eta}(2\eta-\eta^{2})\right\vert_{\eta=0} \;=\; \frac{2U}{\delta} $$ $$ \tau_w \;=\; \frac{2\mu U}{\delta} $$ #### Step 3 — Apply von Kármán $$ \frac{2\mu U}{\rho U^{2}\delta} \;=\; \frac{2}{15}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ $$ \frac{2\nu}{U\delta} \;=\; \frac{2}{15}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ #### Step 4 — Integrate $$ \delta\,d\delta \;=\; \frac{15\nu}{U}\,dx \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta^{2} \;=\; \frac{30\nu x}{U} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\delta \;=\; 5.477\,x\,Re_x^{-1/2}\;} $$ #### Step 5 — Compute C_f $$ C_f \;=\; \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{4\nu}{U\delta} \;=\; \frac{4}{\sqrt{30}\,\sqrt{Re_x}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;C_f \;=\; \frac{0.730}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;} $$ --- ### 3.4 Case Study C — Pohlhausen Cubic Profile (Best Engineering Accuracy) **Assume:** $u/U = (3/2)\eta - (1/2)\eta^{3}$ This profile satisfies *all four* boundary conditions: u(0)=0, u(δ)=U, ∂u/∂y\mid_δ = 0, **and** matches the wall shear stress via τ_w. #### Step 1 — Expand (u/U)(1 − u/U) Let $A=(3/2)\eta,\; B=(1/2)\eta^{3}$. Then: $$ \frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right) \;=\; (A-B)(1-A+B) \;=\; A - A^{2} + 2AB - B - B^{2} $$ Substituting: $$ = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{9}{4}\eta^{2} + \frac{3}{2}\eta^{4} - \frac{1}{2}\eta^{3} - \frac{1}{4}\eta^{6} $$ #### Step 2 — Compute θ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\! \left[\frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{9}{4}\eta^{2} - \frac{1}{2}\eta^{3} + \frac{3}{2}\eta^{4} - \frac{1}{4}\eta^{6}\right]d\eta $$ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\left[\frac{3}{4} - \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{10} - \frac{1}{28}\right] $$ Using LCM = 280: $$ \theta \;=\; \delta \cdot \frac{105 - 210 - 35 + 84 - 10}{280} \;=\; \frac{39\delta}{280} $$ #### Step 3 — Compute τ_w $$ \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y=0} \;=\; \frac{U}{\delta}\!\left[\frac{3}{2} - \frac{3}{2}\eta^{2}\right]_{\eta=0} \;=\; \frac{3U}{2\delta} $$ $$ \tau_w \;=\; \frac{3\mu U}{2\delta} $$ #### Step 4 — Apply von Kármán $$ \frac{3\mu U}{2\rho U^{2}\delta} \;=\; \frac{39}{280}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ $$ \delta\,d\delta \;=\; \frac{280 \times 2}{3 \times 39}\frac{\nu}{U}\,dx \;=\; \frac{140}{13}\frac{\nu x}{U} $$ #### Step 5 — Solve $$ \delta^{2} \;=\; \frac{280\nu x}{13U} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\delta \;=\; 4.64\,x\,Re_x^{-1/2}\;} $$ #### Step 6 — Compute C_f $$ C_f \;=\; \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{3\nu}{U\delta} \;=\; \frac{3\sqrt{13}}{\sqrt{280}\,\sqrt{Re_x}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;C_f \;=\; \frac{0.646}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;} $$ --- ### 3.5 Case Study D — 1/7th Power Law (Turbulent BL, Empirical Input) **Assume:** $u/U = (y/\delta)^{1/7} = \eta^{1/7}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For *turbulent* boundary layers, the wall shear stress is **not** a Newtonian viscous quantity but is given by an empirical relation such as the **Blasius 1/7-power-law formula**: > $$\tau_w = 0.0225\,\rho U^{2}\!\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4}$$ #### Step 1 — Compute θ $$ \theta \;=\; \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{1/7}\!\left(1-\eta^{1/7}\right)d\eta \;=\; \delta\!\left[\frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9}\right] \;=\; \frac{7\delta}{72} $$ #### Step 2 — Apply von Kármán with empirical τ_w $$ 0.0225\,\rho U^{2}\!\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4} \;=\; \rho U^{2} \cdot \frac{7}{72}\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ #### Step 3 — Separate and integrate $$ \delta^{1/4}\,d\delta \;=\; 0.2314\,\nu^{1/4}U^{-1/4}\,dx $$ $$ \frac{4}{5}\delta^{5/4} \;=\; 0.2314\,\nu^{1/4}U^{-1/4}\,x $$ $$ \boxed{\;\delta \;=\; 0.376\,\dfrac{x}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;} $$ #### Step 4 — Compute C_f $$ \boxed{\;C_f \;=\; \frac{0.0576}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;} $$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU) | Velocity Profile | $\theta/\delta$ | $\tau_w\,\delta/\mu U$ | $\delta/x$ | $C_f\,\sqrt{Re_x}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Linear** $\eta$ | $1/6 = 0.1667$ | $1.000$ | $3.464\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | **0.578** | | **Parabolic** $2\eta-\eta^{2}$ | $2/15 = 0.1333$ | $2.000$ | $5.477\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | **0.730** | | **Pohlhausen Cubic** $\tfrac{3}{2}\eta-\tfrac{1}{2}\eta^{3}$ | $39/280 = 0.1393$ | $1.500$ | $4.640\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | **0.646** | | **Blasius Exact (reference)** | $0.664\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | $0.332\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | $5.000\,Re_x^{-1/2}$ | **0.664** | | **1/7 Power Law (turbulent)** | $7/72 = 0.0972$ | (empirical) | $0.376\,Re_x^{-1/5}$ | $0.0576\,Re_x^{-1/10}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The Pohlhausen cubic (0.646) sits closest to the *exact Blasius solution* (0.664) — within 3 % error — making it the **recommended** choice for KTU problems whenever an assumed profile is requested. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Solution Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Problem Given
U, ν, x, plate length L] --> B{Zero Pressure
Gradient?} B -- Yes --> C[Apply von Kármán
τw/ρU² = dθ/dx] B -- No --> C2[Use general form
τw/ρU² = dθ/dx + δ*/U·dU/dx] C --> D[Assume Velocity Profile
u/U = f y/δ] D --> D1[Linear: η] D --> D2[Parabolic: 2η − η²] D --> D3[Pohlhausen Cubic: 3η/2 − η³/2] D --> D4[Turbulent 1/7-Power: η^1/7] D1 --> E[Compute θ via integral
θ = ∫ u/U·1 − u/U dy] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E1[Use empirical τw
Blasius 1/7-law] E --> F[Compute τw from wall gradient
τw = μ·∂u/∂y at y = 0] E1 --> G[Substitute into von Kármán] F --> G[Substitute into von Kármán] G --> H[Integrate ODE for δx] H --> I[Apply B.C.: δ = 0 at x = 0] I --> J[Get δx and τwx] J --> K[Compute Cf = 2τw/ρU²] K --> L[Compute CD for plate length L] L --> M[END: Final Answer] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Momentum Integral Method ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] I1[Free-stream U] I2[Kinematic ν] I3[Position x or L] I4[Profile choice] end subgraph KernelLayer["Computational Kernel"] K1[Profile Function
u/U = fη] K2[θ Integrator
∫ u/U · 1 − u/U dy] K3[Wall Shear Module
τw = μ·∂u/∂y at y = 0] K4[von Kármán Solver
ODE: dθ/dx] K5[Cf Calculator] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"] O1[δ x] O2[θ x] O3[τw x] O4[Cf x] O5[CD for plate] end I1 --> K1 I2 --> K3 I3 --> K4 I4 --> K1 K1 --> K2 K2 --> K4 K4 --> O2 K3 --> K4 K4 --> O3 K3 --> O1 K4 --> O5 K1 --> K5 K5 --> O4 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Description of Control Volume > [!NOTE] > **Text Schematic — Visualize this control volume for any 14-mark derivation:** ``` y ↑ ╔═══════════════════════════════════╗ ← free-stream U │ ║ ─────────────────────────────► ║ │ ║ ρu·u at x ρu·u at x+dx ║ │ ║ ║ ║ ║ δ+ │ ║ ║ momentum- ║ ║ dδ │ ║ ▼ thick region ▼ ║ │ ║ ρ∫u dy ρ∫u dy ║ │ ║ ║ │ ║ ◄── τw ──► ║ │ ║ Wall (no-slip, y = 0) ║ │ ╚═══════════════════════════════════╝ └────────────────────────────────────────────► x x x + dx ``` The horizontal force balance on this slice gives the von Kármán integral equation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define momentum thickness (θ) and displacement thickness (δ*). For a flat-plate laminar boundary layer with parabolic velocity profile u/U = 2(y/δ) − (y/δ)², find the ratio δ*/θ.* **Model Answer:** > **Momentum thickness θ** is a measure of the loss of momentum flux due to the boundary layer, defined as: > $$\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}\frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right)dy$$ > > **Displacement thickness δ*** is the distance by which the external streamline is displaced outward due to the reduction in flow rate inside the BL: > $$\delta^{*}=\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right)dy$$ For $u/U = 2\eta-\eta^{2}$: $$ \delta^{*} = \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}(1-2\eta+\eta^{2})d\eta = \delta\!\left[1 - 1 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right] = \frac{\delta}{3} $$ $$ \theta = \frac{2\delta}{15} \quad(\text{from §3.3}) $$ $$ \boxed{\;\frac{\delta^{*}}{\theta} = \frac{\delta/3}{2\delta/15} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5\;} $$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State the von Kármán momentum integral equation for a flat plate with zero pressure gradient. Why is this approach preferred over solving the Prandtl boundary-layer equations directly?* **Model Answer:** For ZPG flat plate (dU/dx = 0): $$ \frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} $$ **Advantages over direct solution:** 1. Reduces a PDE to a single **ODE** — much easier to integrate. 2. Requires only an *assumed* velocity-profile shape — no detailed flow field needed. 3. Provides **closed-form** expressions for δ(x), τ_w(x), and C_f(x) — directly usable in design. 4. For naval architects, gives **rapid engineering estimates** of frictional drag without CFD. --- ### 📘 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *KTU 2024 Scheme follows a Module-level Internal Choice pattern. Students answer **one** of the two alternatives below.* --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **(a)** Derive the von Kármán momentum integral equation for a 2-D steady incompressible boundary-layer flow over a flat plate with zero external pressure gradient. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the momentum integral technique with the assumed velocity profile u/U = 2(y/δ) − (y/δ)², determine: - (i) The boundary-layer thickness δ as a function of x. **(3 marks)** - (ii) The local wall shear stress τ_w(x). **(2 marks)** - (iii) The local skin-friction coefficient C_f(x). **(2 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **(a) Derivation of von Kármán equation:** Apply conservation of mass and x-momentum to a 2-D control volume extending from x to x+dx and from y = 0 to y = δ(x) (above which flow is uniform at U). **Continuity:** Mass in = Mass out → $$ \frac{d}{dx}\!\int_{0}^{\delta}\rho u\,dy \;=\; -\rho\,\frac{d}{dx}(U\delta^{*}) \quad\text{(equivalent form)} $$ **x-Momentum:** Sum of forces = net momentum flux out. Pressure force on top face (at y = δ) cancels pressure on bottom face (at wall) for ZPG → net pressure force on CV = 0 in x-direction. Wall shear (resisting flow) acts in −x direction with magnitude τ_w dx. Writing x-momentum balance per unit width: $$ \tau_w\,dx \;=\; \rho\,U^{2}\!\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right)dy_{\,\text{out}} - \rho\,U^{2}\!\int_{0}^{\delta}\!\frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right)dy_{\,\text{in}} $$ Dividing by dx: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{d}{dx}\!\int_{0}^{\delta}\frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right)dy \;=\; \frac{d\theta}{dx}\;} $$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, 2-D, incompressible flow. 2. Boundary-layer approximation: $v \ll u$ and $\partial p/\partial y = 0$. 3. Zero external pressure gradient (dU/dx = 0). 4. Newtonian fluid with constant μ, ρ. 5. Velocity profile monotonic from 0 at wall to U at y = δ. --- **(b) Parabolic profile u/U = 2η − η²:** **(i) Boundary-layer thickness:** From §3.3, with θ = 2δ/15 and τ_w = 2μU/δ: $$ \delta\,d\delta = \frac{15\nu}{U}\,dx \;\Rightarrow\; \delta^{2} = \frac{30\nu x}{U} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\delta(x) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{30\nu x}{U}} \;=\; \frac{5.477\,x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;} $$ **(ii) Wall shear stress:** $$ \tau_w(x) \;=\; \frac{2\mu U}{\delta} \;=\; \frac{2\mu U}{\sqrt{30\nu x/U}} \;=\; \frac{2\rho U^{2}}{\sqrt{30\,Re_x}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\tau_w(x) \;=\; 0.365\,\rho U^{2}\,Re_x^{-1/2}\;} $$ **(iii) Local skin-friction coefficient:** $$ \boxed{\;C_f(x) \;=\; \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.730}{\sqrt{Re_x}}\;} $$ --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]* **(a)** For a flat-plate laminar boundary layer, using the velocity profile $u/U = (3/2)(y/\delta) - (1/2)(y/\delta)^3$ (Pohlhausen cubic), show step-by-step that the momentum thickness is $\theta = 39\delta/280$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a turbulent boundary layer on a flat plate, using the 1/7th-power velocity profile and the empirical wall shear stress relation $\tau_w = 0.0225\,\rho U^{2}(\nu/U\delta)^{1/4}$, derive expressions for: - (i) $\delta(x)$ **(5 marks)** - (ii) $C_f(x)$ **(2 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **(a) θ for Pohlhausen cubic:** Let $\eta = y/\delta$, so $u/U = (3/2)\eta - (1/2)\eta^{3}$. Compute $1 - u/U = 1 - (3/2)\eta + (1/2)\eta^{3}$. Product: $$ \frac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\frac{u}{U}\right) = \left[\frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{1}{2}\eta^{3}\right]\!\left[1 - \frac{3}{2}\eta + \frac{1}{2}\eta^{3}\right] $$ Multiply term by term (already done in §3.4): $$ = \frac{3}{2}\eta - \frac{9}{4}\eta^{2} - \frac{1}{2}\eta^{3} + \frac{3}{2}\eta^{4} - \frac{1}{4}\eta^{6} $$ Integrate from 0 to 1: $$ \int_{0}^{1} = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{9}{12} - \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{10} - \frac{1}{28} $$ Convert to common denominator 280: $$ = \frac{210}{280} - \frac{210}{280} - \frac{35}{280} + \frac{84}{280} - \frac{10}{280} $$ $$ = \frac{210 - 210 - 35 + 84 - 10}{280} = \frac{39}{280} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\theta = \delta \cdot \frac{39}{280} = \frac{39\delta}{280}\;} $$ --- **(b) Turbulent 1/7-power-law boundary layer:** **(i) Derivation of δ(x):** Given $u/U = \eta^{1/7}$, compute θ: $$ \theta = \delta\!\int_{0}^{1}\!\eta^{1/7}\!\left(1-\eta^{1/7}\right)d\eta = \delta\!\left[\frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9}\right] = \frac{7\delta}{72} $$ Apply von Kármán with empirical τ_w: $$ 0.0225\,\rho U^{2}\!\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4} = \rho U^{2}\cdot\frac{7}{72}\cdot\frac{d\delta}{dx} $$ Simplify: $$ \frac{d\delta}{dx} = 0.0225 \cdot \frac{72}{7}\cdot\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4} = 0.2314\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4} $$ Separate variables: $$ \delta^{1/4}\,d\delta = 0.2314\,\nu^{1/4}U^{-1/4}\,dx $$ Integrate with δ = 0 at x = 0: $$ \frac{4}{5}\,\delta^{5/4} = 0.2314\,\nu^{1/4}U^{-1/4}\,x $$ $$ \delta^{5/4} = 0.2893\,\nu^{1/4}U^{-1/4}\,x $$ Raise both sides to power 4/5: $$ \boxed{\;\delta(x) = 0.376\,\frac{x}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;} $$ **(ii) Local skin-friction coefficient:** $$ C_f = \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U^{2}} = 2(0.0225)\!\left(\frac{\nu}{U\delta}\right)^{1/4} = 0.045\!\left(\frac{\nu}{U\cdot 0.376x\,Re_x^{-1/5}}\right)^{1/4} $$ Simplify $\nu/(U\delta)$: $$ \frac{\nu}{U\delta} = \frac{\nu}{0.376\,Ux}\cdot Re_x^{1/5} = \frac{1}{0.376}\cdot\frac{1}{Re_x}\cdot Re_x^{1/5} = \frac{2.66}{Re_x^{4/5}} $$ Then: $$ C_f = 0.045\cdot(2.66)^{1/4}\cdot Re_x^{-1/5} = 0.045\cdot 1.278\cdot Re_x^{-1/5} $$ $$ \boxed{\;C_f(x) = \frac{0.0576}{Re_x^{1/5}}\;} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in τ_w:** Many students write $\tau_w = \mu(\partial u/\partial y)$ instead of the **Newtonian wall-stress** form (which IS the same, but be careful in turbulent case — it's empirical, not a derivative). > 2. **Wrong integration limits:** θ must integrate over **0 to δ**, with substitution $\eta = y/\delta$ → limits **0 to 1**. > 3. **Missing the (1 − u/U) term in θ:** Confusing θ with δ* is a classic 2-mark loss. > 4. **Wrong exponent for turbulent BL:** δ ∝ x^(4/5) and C_f ∝ Re_x^(−1/5) — students often write 1/2 by reflex. > 5. **Mixing units:** μ has units Pa·s, ν has m²/s. Always write final answers dimensionally consistent. > 6. **Numerical slip in parabolic:** $\theta = 2\delta/15$, **not** δ/15 — recheck expansion step. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Momentum integral equation** (ZPG): $\tau_w/(\rho U^2) = d\theta/dx$. - ✅ **θ definition**: $\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}(u/U)(1-u/U)\,dy$ — measures momentum loss. - ✅ **δ\* definition**: $\delta^* = \int_{0}^{\delta}(1-u/U)\,dy$ — measures streamline displacement. - ✅ **Shape factor** $H = \delta^*/\theta$ — separation indicator (H > ~2.6 → incipient separation). - ✅ **Solution recipe**: assume profile → compute θ → compute τ_w from wall gradient (laminar) or empirical law (turbulent) → substitute → integrate → get δ(x) and C_f. - ✅ **Linear profile** → C_f = 0.578/√Re_x (underestimates drag). - ✅ **Parabolic profile** → C_f = 0.730/√Re_x (overestimates drag). - ✅ **Pohlhausen cubic** → C_f = 0.646/√Re_x (best engineering accuracy; ~3 % from exact Blasius 0.664). - ✅ **1/7-Power (turbulent)** → C_f = 0.0576/Re_x^(1/5) — NOT proportional to Re^(−1/2). - ✅ **Average C_D** = (1/L)∫₀ᴸ C_f dx = 2×C_f(L) for ZPG flat plate (laminar). - ✅ **Naval relevance**: ship-hull frictional resistance, sail/foil drag estimation, transition prediction via Re_θ. - ✅ **Always quote B.C.**: δ(0) = 0, u(0) = 0, u(δ) = U, ∂u/∂y|_δ = 0. - ✅ **Typical KTU mark split**: θ computation 3 marks + τ_w 2 marks + integration 3 marks + C_f 2 marks (for a 10-mark derivation). - ✅ **Memorize** the result **δ = 5.0x/√Re_x** and **C_f = 0.664/√Re_x** as the **exact Blasius reference values**.

topics.title: Basics and Introduction to Solution of a Simple Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST302 - FLUID MECHANICS FOR NAVAL ARCHITECTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics and Introduction to Solution of a Simple Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Solving a Simple Fluid Mechanics Problem" Mean?

In the context of **Fluid Mechanics for Naval Architects**, Module 4 introduces the foundational problem-solving framework that governs how fluids (water and air) interact with marine structures, ship hulls, and offshore platforms. A *simple problem* typically involves determining the **pressure distribution**, **hydrostatic thrust force**, or **center of pressure** on a submerged or partially submerged planar surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *A simple fluid mechanics problem is one in which the fluid is assumed to be incompressible, in static equilibrium (or steady, inviscid flow), and the geometry of the immersed body is regular (rectangular, triangular, or circular). The solution requires direct application of the hydrostatic pressure equation or Bernoulli's equation along a streamline.*

## 1.2 Physical Significance for Naval Architects

For a naval architect, the *basics* of problem-solving in fluid mechanics are not academic exercises — they are the **first principles** used to:
- Calculate the **buoyancy force** acting on a ship hull.
- Determine the **bending moment** due to hydrostatic pressure on the hull plating.
- Size **sea valves**, **shell plating**, and **bulkhead stiffeners**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Pool"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Stacked Water Column**
> Imagine you are standing at the deep end of a swimming pool. The pressure you feel on your eardrum is not because of the water "pushing" sideways — it is because of the **weight of the entire column of water above you**. The deeper you go, the heavier the column, and the higher the pressure. Now imagine tilting a flat rectangular glass window into that pool at an angle. The pressure is not uniform on the glass — it is *zero* at the water surface and *maximum* at the bottom edge. This *non-uniform pressure distribution* is the heart of almost every simple fluid mechanics problem solved in naval architecture.

## 1.4 The Three Universal Fluid Properties Used in Every Simple Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every simple fluid mechanics problem begins with these three properties:**

| Property | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea Water) | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Density | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Mass per unit volume |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | **10050 N/m³** | Weight per unit volume, $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| Specific Gravity | $S$ | **1.025** | Ratio of fluid density to that of fresh water at **4 °C** |

Where the standard acceleration due to gravity is:

$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

For KTU problems, unless otherwise stated, the working fluid is **fresh water** with:

$$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — The Starting Point

Fluid statics is governed by a single, elegant differential equation derived from Newton's second law applied to a fluid element at rest. Since the fluid has no shear (it cannot resist shear when stationary), the **pressure varies only with depth**.

### 2.1.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (Logic Steps)

- **Step 1 — Force Balance on a Fluid Element:** Consider a small fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ in static equilibrium. The only forces acting on it are the surface pressure forces and its own body weight.
- **Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium:** Summing forces in the vertical ($z$) direction and setting the sum to zero (since the element is at rest, acceleration = 0).
- **Step 3 — Cancellation of Horizontal Terms:** The horizontal pressure gradients must also be zero, proving that pressure at a point is the same in *all directions* (Pascal's principle).
- **Step 4 — Integration:** Integrate the resulting differential equation from the free surface (where $p = p_{atm}$) down to a depth $h$.

The final integrated form is the **fundamental hydrostatic equation**:

$$\boxed{p = p_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

Where:
- $p$ = absolute pressure at depth $h$ (in Pa or N/m²)
- $p_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface (**101.325 kPa** at standard sea level)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = vertical depth below the free surface (**m**)

### 2.1.2 Gauge Pressure vs. Absolute Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In almost all undergraduate problems, you are expected to work in **gauge pressure**, not absolute pressure. This is because the atmospheric pressure acts on *both sides* of any submerged structure, so it cancels out in force calculations.

$$p_{gauge} = p_{absolute} - p_{atm} = \rho g h$$

## 2.2 Hydrostatic Thrust Force on a Submerged Plane Surface

This is the **most frequently tested topic** in Module 4. When a plane surface of area $A$ is submerged in a static fluid, the resultant hydrostatic force $F$ is given by:

$$\boxed{F = \rho g \bar{h} A}$$

Where $\bar{h}$ is the **vertical depth of the centroid** of the submerged surface from the free liquid surface.

## 2.3 Centre of Pressure (The Point of Application)

The resultant force $F$ does *not* act at the centroid — it acts at a point **lower than the centroid** (because pressure is higher at greater depth). This point is called the **Centre of Pressure (CP)**.

The depth of the centre of pressure is given by:

$$\boxed{h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}}}$$

For a **vertical surface** ($\theta = 90°$, so $\sin\theta = 1$), this simplifies to:

$$h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}}$$

Where $I_{xx,c}$ is the **second moment of area** of the submerged surface about its horizontal centroidal axis, and $\theta$ is the angle the surface makes with the horizontal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers ~70% of Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $p = \rho g h$ | Gauge pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | Total hydrostatic thrust on a plane surface | N |
| 3 | $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}}$ | Depth of centre of pressure (vertical surface) | m |
| 4 | $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ | Rectangle of width $b$, depth $d$ (vertical) | m⁴ |
| 5 | $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Circle of diameter $d$ | m⁴ |
| 6 | $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{b d^3}{36}$ | Triangle (vertex up) of base $b$, height $d$ | m⁴ |
| 7 | $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{b d^3}{36}$ | Triangle (vertex down) of base $b$, height $d$ | m⁴ |
| 8 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight | N/m³ |
| 9 | $S = \rho / \rho_{water}$ | Specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute pressure conversion | Pa |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a naval architect care about $F$ and $h_{cp}$?**
> - The **total thrust $F$** is used to size the **scantlings** (thickness) of the ship's shell plating, watertight bulkheads, and dock gate structures.
> - The **location of the centre of pressure $h_{cp}$** is critical for computing the **bending moment** $M = F \times (\text{moment arm})$, which is then used to select appropriate stiffener spacing and profile.
> - In **submarine design**, $h_{cp}$ is the reference point for pressure hull collapse calculations under hydrostatic loading at operational depths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Hydrostatic Force on a Vertical Rectangular Gate

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **vertical rectangular gate** of width $b$ and height $d$, with its **top edge** lying at a depth $h_1$ below the free water surface and its **bottom edge** at a depth $h_2$ below the free surface.

We need to find:
1. The total hydrostatic thrust force $F$ on the gate.
2. The depth of the centre of pressure $h_{cp}$ from the free surface.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pressure at a General Depth**
The gauge pressure at any vertical depth $h$ below the free surface is given by the hydrostatic equation:

$$p(h) = \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Infinitesimal Force on a Horizontal Strip**
Consider an infinitesimal horizontal strip on the gate at depth $h$, having a vertical thickness $dh$ and width $b$. Its area is:

$$dA = b \, dh$$

The differential force on this strip is:

$$dF = p(h) \cdot dA = \rho g h \cdot b \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to Find Total Force $F$**
Integrate from the top of the gate ($h = h_1$) to the bottom of the gate ($h = h_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \rho g b h \, dh \\
&= \rho g b \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \, dh \\
&= \rho g b \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{h_1}^{h_2} \\
&= \rho g b \left( \frac{h_2^2 - h_1^2}{2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the algebraic identity $h_2^2 - h_1^2 = (h_2 - h_1)(h_2 + h_1) = d \cdot (h_2 + h_1)$, and recognizing that the area $A = b \cdot d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g b \cdot \frac{d \cdot (h_2 + h_1)}{2} \\
&= \rho g A \cdot \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the centroid of a rectangle is at its geometric centre, the depth of the centroid is:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2}$$

This confirms the standard formula:

$$\boxed{F = \rho g A \bar{h}}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Centre of Pressure**
The centre of pressure is the point where the moment of the distributed pressure force about the free surface equals the moment of the resultant force $F$ about the same point. Equivalently, it is the **centroid of the pressure prism** (a triangular distribution for a vertical surface).

Taking moments about the free surface:

$$F \cdot h_{cp} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot dF = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h \cdot (\rho g b h) \, dh$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot h_{cp} &= \rho g b \int_{h_1}^{h_2} h^2 \, dh \\
&= \rho g b \left[ \frac{h^3}{3} \right]_{h_1}^{h_2} \\
&= \rho g b \left( \frac{h_2^3 - h_1^3}{3} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the expression for $F$ derived above and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \frac{1}{F} \cdot \rho g b \left( \frac{h_2^3 - h_1^3}{3} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{F} \cdot \rho g b \cdot \frac{(h_2 - h_1)(h_2^2 + h_2 h_1 + h_1^2)}{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{F} \cdot \rho g A \cdot \frac{h_2^2 + h_2 h_1 + h_1^2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute $F = \rho g A \cdot \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \frac{\rho g A}{\rho g A \cdot \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2}} \cdot \frac{h_2^2 + h_2 h_1 + h_1^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{h_2^2 + h_2 h_1 + h_1^2}{h_1 + h_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the special case where the **top edge is at the free surface** ($h_1 = 0$) and the bottom edge is at depth $\bar{h} = h_2 = d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{h_2^2 + 0 + 0}{0 + h_2} = \frac{2}{3} h_2 = \frac{2d}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classic result: for a vertical rectangle with its top edge at the free surface, the centre of pressure is at **2/3 of the height from the top** (or 1/3 from the bottom). The centroid, by contrast, is at $d/2$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — A KTU-Style Simple Problem

> **Problem:** A vertical rectangular sluice gate in a freshwater dock has a width of **1.5 m** and a height of **2.0 m**. The top of the gate is located **1.0 m** below the water surface. Calculate:
> (a) The total hydrostatic thrust force on the gate.
> (b) The depth of the centre of pressure from the free water surface.

### Solution

**Given Data:**

$$b = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad d = 2.0 \text{ m}, \quad h_1 = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Part (a) — Total Hydrostatic Force $F$**

Step 1: Find the depth of the bottom edge:
$$h_2 = h_1 + d = 1.0 + 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: Find the depth of the centroid (geometric centre of the gate):
$$\bar{h} = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2} = \frac{1.0 + 3.0}{2} = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

Step 3: Find the area of the gate:
$$A = b \times d = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 4: Apply the hydrostatic force formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g \bar{h} A \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 \times 3.0 \\
&= 58860 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 58.86 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 58.86$ kN

**Part (b) — Depth of Centre of Pressure $h_{cp}$**

Step 1: Calculate the second moment of area of the rectangle about its horizontal centroidal axis:
$$I_{xx,c} = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times (2.0)^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8.0}{12} = 1.0 \text{ m}^4$$

Step 2: Apply the centre of pressure formula for a vertical surface:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}} \\
&= 2.0 + \frac{1.0}{3.0 \times 2.0} \\
&= 2.0 + \frac{1.0}{6.0} \\
&= 2.0 + 0.1667 \\
&= 2.1667 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $h_{cp} = 2.167$ m below the free surface

### Interpretation

The resultant hydrostatic force of **58.86 kN** acts at a depth of **2.167 m**, which is *below* the centroid depth of 2.0 m — exactly as theory predicts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Problem-Solving Workflow

The diagram below maps the **logical workflow** a student should follow when solving any simple hydrostatics problem, from reading the problem statement to final numerical answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify the Geometry of the Submerged Surface]
    B --> C{Is the Surface Vertical or Inclined?}
    C -- Vertical --> D[Use sin theta equals 1 in CP formula]
    C -- Inclined --> E[Use sin theta in CP formula]
    D --> F[Extract Given Data: b, d, h1, h2, rho, g]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Area A and Centroid Depth h-bar]
    G --> H[Apply Force Formula: F equals rho g h-bar A]
    H --> I[Compute Second Moment of Area I_xx_c about Centroidal Axis]
    I --> J[Apply CP Formula: h_cp equals h-bar plus I_xx_c over A h-bar]
    J --> K[Cross-Check: h_cp is greater than h-bar]
    K --> L[State Final Answer with Correct Units]
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Distribution on a Vertical Gate

The diagram below illustrates how **gauge pressure varies linearly** with depth on a vertical rectangular gate, and how the **resultant force $F$** acts at the centre of pressure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FreeSurface[Free Water Surface]
        A1[Atmospheric Pressure p_atm]
    end
    subgraph TopEdge[Top of Gate at Depth h1]
        A2[Pressure p1 equals rho g h1]
    end
    subgraph BottomEdge[Bottom of Gate at Depth h2]
        A3[Pressure p2 equals rho g h2]
    end
    subgraph ResultantForce[Resultant Force F]
        A4[F acts at Centre of Pressure h_cp]
    end
    FreeSurface --> TopEdge
    TopEdge --> BottomEdge
    BottomEdge --> ResultantForce
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Simple Hydrostatic Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[INPUT: Geometry and Fluid Properties] --> stepB[PROCESS 1: Compute Centroid Depth h-bar]
    stepB --> stepC[PROCESS 2: Compute Area A]
    stepC --> stepD[PROCESS 3: Apply F equals rho g h-bar A]
    stepD --> stepE[PROCESS 4: Compute I_xx_c]
    stepE --> stepF[PROCESS 5: Apply CP Formula]
    stepF --> stepG[OUTPUT: Force F and CP Location h_cp]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Define the following terms with one example each:
(a) Specific weight of a fluid.
(b) Centre of pressure.

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Specific weight ($\gamma$):** It is defined as the weight of the fluid per unit volume. Mathematically, $\gamma = \rho g$, where $\rho$ is the fluid density and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity. For sea water, $\gamma = 10050$ N/m³. Unit: N/m³.

(b) **Centre of pressure:** It is the point on a submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic thrust force is considered to act. For a vertical rectangular surface with its top edge at the free liquid surface, the centre of pressure lies at a depth of $\frac{2d}{3}$ from the surface, where $d$ is the height of the surface.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
State the hydrostatic pressure equation and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer:**

The hydrostatic pressure equation is:

$$p = p_{atm} + \rho g h$$

Significance of each term:
- $p$: Absolute pressure at a point located at depth $h$ below the free surface.
- $p_{atm}$: Atmospheric pressure acting on the free liquid surface (**101.325 kPa** at standard conditions).
- $\rho g h$: The pressure exerted by the column of fluid of height $h$ above the point.
- $g$: Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**).

The equation states that pressure at a point in a static fluid increases linearly with depth, which is the fundamental basis of all hydrostatic force calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A vertical rectangular gate in a fresh-water reservoir is **2.5 m wide** and **3.0 m high**. The top edge of the gate is located **2.0 m below** the free water surface. Determine:
(a) The total hydrostatic thrust force on the gate.
(b) The depth of the centre of pressure from the free water surface.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $b = 2.5$ m, $d = 3.0$ m, $h_1 = 2.0$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (a) — Total Hydrostatic Force $F$ [7 marks]**

Depth of bottom edge:
$$h_2 = h_1 + d = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

Depth of centroid:
$$\bar{h} = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2} = \frac{2.0 + 5.0}{2} = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

Area of gate:
$$A = b \times d = 2.5 \times 3.0 = 7.5 \text{ m}^2$$

Total hydrostatic force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g \bar{h} A \\
&= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 7.5 \\
&= 257512.5 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 257.51 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 257.51$ kN

**Part (b) — Centre of Pressure [7 marks]**

Second moment of area about the horizontal centroidal axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xx,c} &= \frac{b d^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times (3.0)^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times 27}{12} \\
&= 5.625 \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Depth of centre of pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}} \\
&= 3.5 + \frac{5.625}{7.5 \times 3.5} \\
&= 3.5 + \frac{5.625}{26.25} \\
&= 3.5 + 0.2143 \\
&= 3.7143 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $h_{cp} = 3.714$ m below the free water surface.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

A vertical triangular gate in a sea-water dock has its **vertex at the top** and its **base at the bottom**. The base of the triangle is **3.0 m wide** and is located **4.0 m below** the free water surface. The vertex is **1.0 m below** the free water surface. Calculate:
(a) The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
(b) The depth of the centre of pressure from the free water surface.

Take $\rho_{sea} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Base $b = 3.0$ m, height of triangle $d = h_2 - h_1 = 4.0 - 1.0 = 3.0$ m, $h_1 = 1.0$ m (vertex depth), $h_2 = 4.0$ m (base depth), $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Part (a) — Total Hydrostatic Force [7 marks]**

Area of triangle:
$$A = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times d = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.0 \times 3.0 = 4.5 \text{ m}^2$$

For a triangle with its vertex at the top, the centroid lies at a distance of $\frac{d}{3}$ from the base (or $\frac{2d}{3}$ from the vertex). Therefore, the depth of the centroid is:

$$\bar{h} = h_1 + \frac{2d}{3} = 1.0 + \frac{2 \times 3.0}{3} = 1.0 + 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

Total hydrostatic force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \rho g \bar{h} A \\
&= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3.0 \times 4.5 \\
&= 135774.375 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 135.77 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $F = 135.77$ kN

**Part (b) — Centre of Pressure [7 marks]**

Second moment of area of a triangle (vertex up) about its horizontal centroidal axis:
$$I_{xx,c} = \frac{b d^3}{36} = \frac{3.0 \times (3.0)^3}{36} = \frac{3.0 \times 27}{36} = 2.25 \text{ m}^4$$

Depth of centre of pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cp} &= \bar{h} + \frac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}} \\
&= 3.0 + \frac{2.25}{4.5 \times 3.0} \\
&= 3.0 + \frac{2.25}{13.5} \\
&= 3.0 + 0.1667 \\
&= 3.1667 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $h_{cp} = 3.167$ m below the free water surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the centroid shift for triangles:** The centroid of a triangle is NOT at $d/2$. It is at $d/3$ from the base or $2d/3$ from the vertex. Students who use $d/2$ lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong second moment of area:** Students often use $bd^3/12$ (rectangle formula) for a triangle. The correct formula is $bd^3/36$.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Final answers must be in **N** or **kN** for force and **m** for depth. Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 4. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure:** KTU 2024 questions almost always want gauge pressure in force calculations. Do not add $p_{atm}$ unless explicitly asked.
> 5. **Not verifying $h_{cp} > \bar{h}$:** If your computed $h_{cp}$ is *less* than $\bar{h}$, you have made a sign or formula error. Centre of pressure is ALWAYS below the centroid for a submerged surface with the free surface above it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Basics and Introduction to Solution of a Simple Problem**

- ☐ The **hydrostatic pressure equation** $p = \rho g h$ is the foundation of all Module 4 problems.
- ☐ **Gauge pressure** is used in KTU problems unless absolute pressure is explicitly asked.
- ☐ The **total hydrostatic thrust force** on a plane surface is $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$.
- ☐ The **centroid depth $\bar{h}$** depends on the geometry: $d/2$ for a rectangle, $d/3$ from base (or $2d/3$ from vertex) for a triangle, $d/2$ for a circle.
- ☐ The **centre of pressure** is ALWAYS *below* the centroid ($h_{cp} > \bar{h}$) for a submerged surface.
- ☐ For a **vertical** surface, $h_{cp} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{xx,c}}{A \bar{h}}$.
- ☐ For an **inclined** surface, the formula gains a $\sin^2\theta$ factor in the numerator.
- ☐ **Second moment of area** formulas to memorize:
  - Rectangle: $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$
  - Circle: $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$
  - Triangle: $I_{xx,c} = \dfrac{bd^3}{36}$
- ☐ Standard values: $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{sea} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- ☐ In naval architecture, $F$ is used for **plate thickness** design, and $h_{cp}$ is used for **bending moment** and **stiffener design**.
- ☐ Always **state the final answer with units** — this is a free mark in KTU valuation.
- ☐ **Cross-check** the result: $h_{cp}$ should be between $\bar{h}$ and $h_2$ (bottom of surface).
